topics.title: Definitions of IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definitions of IoT ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions of IoT The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is one of those rare concepts that has been formally standardized by multiple international bodies. Each definition emphasizes a slightly different facet — *connectivity*, *autonomy*, *semantics*, or *services*. For KTU examination purposes, you are expected to remember **at least three** of these authoritative definitions verbatim. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — OEERT614 / Module 1** > The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly mandates that students understand IoT as a *dynamic global network infrastructure with self-configuring capabilities based on standard and interoperable communication protocols*. The ITU-T Y.2060 definition is treated as the **gold standard** reference answer. --- #### Definition 1 — ITU-T Recommendation Y.2060 (2012) [Most Important] > *"The Internet of Things is a global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."* #### Definition 2 — IERC — European Research Cluster on IoT (2014) > *"IoT is a dynamic global network infrastructure with self-configuring capabilities based on standard and interoperable communication protocols where physical and virtual 'things' have identities, physical attributes, and virtual personalities and use intelligent interfaces, and are seamlessly integrated into the information network."* #### Definition 3 — IEEE Internet of Things Initiative > *"IoT is a self-configuring, adaptive, complex network that interconnects 'things' to the Internet through the use of standard communication protocols."* #### Definition 4 — Oxford Dictionary (Compact Form) > *"The interconnection via the Internet of computing devices embedded in everyday objects, enabling them to send and receive data."* --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Body" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy** > Imagine your **human body**. Your eyes, ears, and skin are *sensors* collecting data. Your nerves are the *network* carrying signals. Your brain is the *processing & decision-making unit*. Your hands and legs are the *actuators* that perform physical actions. > > Now scale this body to the **entire city** — streetlights become sensors, fiber cables become nerves, the cloud becomes the brain, and traffic signals become actuators. **That scaled-up, Internet-connected nervous system is the Internet of Things.** Each "thing" is no longer a dumb object; it can *sense, think, talk, and act*. --- ### 1.3 What Exactly is a "Thing" in IoT? A **"thing"** in the IoT context is **not restricted to physical objects**. According to ITU-T Y.2060, things are classified into two categories: | Category | Description | Example | |----------|-------------|---------| | **Physical Thing** | A real-world object from the physical world (device, sensor, actuator, animal, person) | A temperature sensor, a smart refrigerator, a cow with an RFID tag | | **Virtual Thing** | A digital entity existing only in the information world | A software agent, a virtual service, a cloud-hosted business process | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Identifier Property:** Each "thing" must possess a **unique identifier** (e.g., IPv6 address, EPC for RFID, URI) so that it can be unambiguously addressed within the global network. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Values The following quantitative metrics are the ones most frequently referenced in the KTU syllabus as the defining performance boundaries of any IoT system: - **Unique Addressable Things:** Approximately **$1.5 \times 10^{16}$** items (Cisco IBSG estimate, sufficient for ~**10 years**). - **Year IoT coined:** **1999** by **Kevin Ashton** (Procter & Gamble, MIT Auto-ID Lab). - **Standardization Body:** **ITU-T** Study Group 20 (IoT and smart cities). - **Key Protocol Reference Stack:** IEEE 802.15.4, 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, MQTT, HTTP, IPv6. - **Cognitive Definition Marker:** **4 defining attributes** — *Sensing, Identification, Communication, Actuation*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four-Pillar Conceptual Framework Every authoritative definition of IoT can be decomposed into **four universally accepted pillars**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to list or explain these pillars, so memorize them as a single mental model. 1. **Sensing / Identification** The "thing" must be able to perceive its environment (sensors, RFID tags, QR codes, beacons) or be uniquely identified (UID, IPv6, EPC). *Why it matters:* Without sensing, there is no data. Without identification, there is no addressing. 2. **Communication / Connectivity** The "thing" must possess the ability to connect to the Internet or to neighbouring things using standard, interoperable protocols. *Why it matters:* Connectivity is the literal "Internet" in IoT — without it, the device is just an embedded system. 3. **Processing / Computation** The data acquired must be transformed into useful information, either locally (edge) or remotely (cloud/fog). *Why it matters:* Raw sensor data is noise; processed data is information. 4. **Actuation / Response** The "thing" (or an associated system) must be able to act on the processed information (actuators, alerts, API calls). *Why it matters:* This closes the feedback loop — turning IoT from a *monitoring* tool into an *autonomous* system. > [!NOTE] > **"How" Behind the Pillar Model** > The IERC definition calls this the **Sense–Compute–Actuate** model, while the ITU-T Y.2060 framework formalizes it as the **Identification → Sensing → Communication → Computation → Actuation** pipeline. Both converge on the same underlying architecture. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > Since "Definitions of IoT" is a **conceptual module**, the "formula sheet" here is a **terminological and structural cheat sheet** rather than a numerical one. KTU examiners expect students to recall these relationships verbatim. | # | Concept | Formal Statement / Relationship | Units / Notes | |---|---------|--------------------------------|---------------| | 1 | IoT Identifier Space | $N_{\text{things}} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{16}$ for $\sim 10$ years | Items | | 2 | IPv6 Address Capacity | $2^{128} = 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ unique addresses | Addresses | | 3 | IoT Reference Model (ITU-T) | 4 Layers: Application $\to$ Service/Application Support $\to$ Network $\to$ Device | Layers | | 4 | IoT Reference Model (Cisco) | 5 Stages: Sensors $\to$ Network $\to$ Processing $\to$ Application $\to$ Business | Stages | | 5 | IoT = M2M + H2M + H2H | Set-theoretic: $IoT = M2M \cup H2M \cup H2H$ | Union | | 6 | Thing Identity Property | $\forall T_i \in \mathbb{T}, \exists \text{ID}(T_i)$ unique | Existence | | 7 | Interoperability Condition | $\vert P_i \cap P_j \vert \geq 1$ for protocols $P_i, P_j$ | Common protocol count | | 8 | Dynamic Self-Configuration | System adapts without human intervention | Boolean state | > **Notation Glossary** > $\mathbb{T}$ = Set of all "things"; ID$(T_i)$ = unique identifier of thing $T_i$; $P_i$ = protocol stack of node $i$. --- ### 2.3 The McKinsey Perspective — "Sensors + Actuators + Intelligence" A complementary definition often cited in KTU answer keys is the **McKinsey Global Institute (2013)** formulation: > *"IoT refers to a network of sensors and actuators embedded in physical objects, linked through wired and wireless networks, often using the same Internet Protocol (IP) that connects the Internet."* This definition introduces the *three structural ingredients* — **Sensors, Actuators, Networks** — that any physical IoT deployment must possess. It is the *most operationally useful* definition for engineering problem statements. --- ### 2.4 Why Definitions Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Sector | How the Definition Manifests | |-----------------|------------------------------| | **Smart Agriculture** | Soil-moisture sensors (sense), LoRaWAN (communicate), edge gateway (compute), irrigation valve (actuate) | | **Healthcare** | Wearable ECG patch (sense), BLE/Wi-Fi (communicate), cloud analytics (compute), SMS alert (actuate) | | **Smart Cities** | Traffic cameras (sense), 5G/fiber (communicate), traffic-management AI (compute), adaptive signals (actuate) | | **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Vibration sensors (sense), OPC-UA (communicate), predictive analytics (compute), robotic arm (actuate) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** No matter which definition you cite, if you cannot map a system to all four pillars (Sense, Communicate, Compute, Actuate), it is **not** a true IoT system — it is at best a *sensor network* or a *telemetry system*. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Decomposition of the ITU-T Y.2060 Definition The KTU 2024 Scheme demands that students be able to **parse** an IoT definition into its constituent semantic components. Below is the exhaustive, line-by-line analytical breakdown of the most important definition — **ITU-T Y.2060**. Let us denote the IoT as the set $\mathbb{I}$ defined recursively by: $$ \mathbb{I} = \{\, T \mid T \text{ satisfies all four defining properties of IoT } \,\} $$ For an entity $T$ to qualify as a member of $\mathbb{I}$, it must satisfy: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Property 1 (Physicality/Virtuality)} & : T \in \mathcal{P} \cup \mathcal{V} \\ \text{Property 2 (Identification)} & : \exists \, \text{UID}(T) \in \mathcal{U} \\ \text{Property 3 (Connectivity)} & : T \leftrightarrow \text{Net}, \text{ via } P \in \mathcal{P}_{\text{std}} \\ \text{Property 4 (Service-Enablement)} & : T \Rightarrow S, \text{ where } S \in \mathcal{S}_{\text{adv}} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $\mathcal{P}$ = set of all physical things, - $\mathcal{V}$ = set of all virtual things, - $\mathcal{U}$ = universal identifier space (e.g., IPv6), - $\text{Net}$ = the global information network, - $\mathcal{P}_{\text{std}}$ = set of standard interoperable protocols, - $S$ = an advanced service, - $\mathcal{S}_{\text{adv}}$ = set of advanced services. > **Conversion Logic:** Each row above is one *mandatory clause* lifted directly from the ITU-T Y.2060 sentence. A real "thing" must satisfy **all four** clauses simultaneously to be classified as an IoT member. If even one clause fails, the entity is not part of $\mathbb{I}$. --- ### 3.2 Deriving the IoT Conceptual Set Equation IoT is mathematically the **superset union** of three communication paradigms: **Machine-to-Machine (M2M)**, **Human-to-Machine (H2M)**, and **Human-to-Human (H2H)**. Let us derive this relationship step-by-step. **Step 1 — Define the three foundational communication paradigms:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{M2M} & : \text{Machines exchanging data without human intervention} \\ \text{H2M} & : \text{Humans interacting with machines (e.g., smartphone app)} \\ \text{H2H} & : \text{Humans interacting with other humans through machines} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Express each paradigm as a set of communication events:** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbb{M} & = \{\, e \mid e = (m_1, m_2, \text{msg}), \, m_1, m_2 \in \text{Machines} \,\} \\ \mathbb{H}_{2M} & = \{\, e \mid e = (h, m, \text{msg}), \, h \in \text{Humans}, \, m \in \text{Machines} \,\} \\ \mathbb{H}_{2H} & = \{\, e \mid e = (h_1, h_2, \text{msg}), \, h_1, h_2 \in \text{Humans} \,\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Form the union to obtain the IoT conceptual set:** $$ \mathbb{I} = \mathbb{M} \cup \mathbb{H}_{2M} \cup \mathbb{H}_{2H} $$ **Step 4 — Cardinality reasoning (the "IoT explosion"):** If $|\mathbb{M}| = M$, $|\mathbb{H}_{2M}| = H_m$, and $|\mathbb{H}_{2H}| = H_h$, then by the **Inclusion-Exclusion Principle**: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert \mathbb{I} \vert & = \vert \mathbb{M} \vert + \vert \mathbb{H}_{2M} \vert + \vert \mathbb{H}_{2H} \vert \\ & \quad - \vert \mathbb{M} \cap \mathbb{H}_{2M} \vert - \vert \mathbb{M} \cap \mathbb{H}_{2H} \vert - \vert \mathbb{H}_{2M} \cap \mathbb{H}_{2H} \vert \\ & \quad + \vert \mathbb{M} \cap \mathbb{H}_{2M} \cap \mathbb{H}_{2H} \vert \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** In practice, the three sets heavily overlap (a smart-home hub participates in M2M, H2M, *and* H2H via WhatsApp), so the inclusion-exclusion terms are non-negligible. This is precisely why IoT scales non-linearly beyond simple M2M. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Major Definitions This is the **single most exam-relevant matrix** in this module. Memorize it. | Dimension | **ITU-T Y.2060** | **IERC (EU Cluster)** | **Oxford Dictionary** | |-----------|------------------|----------------------|----------------------| | **Year Published** | 2012 | 2014 | 2013 | | **Scope** | Global infrastructure | Global network infrastructure | Interconnection via Internet | | **Emphasizes** | Service enablement | Self-configuration, intelligence | Everyday objects | | **Mentions Virtual Things?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only physical objects) | | **Mentions Self-Configuration?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Mentions Standard Protocols?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Implicitly | | **Best Used For** | KTU/M.Tech answers | Research papers | Layman/journalism | | **Length of Definition** | Long (~50 words) | Very long (~60 words) | Short (~15 words) | | **Recommended Marks Weight** | 7 marks | 5 marks | 2 marks | | **Cognitive Level (Bloom's)** | Understand + Apply | Remember + Analyze | Remember | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — The Conceptual Anatomy of an IoT Definition ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SRC["Authoritative Sources"] A1["ITU-T Y.2060"] A2["IERC EU Cluster"] A3["IEEE IoT Initiative"] A4["Oxford Dictionary"] A5["McKinsey Global Inst."] A6["Kevin Ashton 1999"] end subgraph CORE["Four Defining Pillars of IoT"] P1["Pillar 1: Sensing and Identification"] P2["Pillar 2: Communication and Connectivity"] P3["Pillar 3: Processing and Computation"] P4["Pillar 4: Actuation and Response"] end subgraph DOMAIN["The Thing Space"] T1["Physical Thing: Sensor, RFID, Vehicle"] T2["Virtual Thing: Software Agent, Cloud Service"] end subgraph SVC["Service Outcomes"] S1["Advanced Services: Smart Home, eHealth, Smart Grid"] S2["Global Addressable Network with Self-Config"] end A1 --> CORE A2 --> CORE A3 --> CORE A4 --> CORE A5 --> CORE A6 --> CORE CORE --> DOMAIN DOMAIN --> SVC P1 --> T1 P2 --> T1 P3 --> T2 P4 --> S1 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — The ITU-T Y.2060 Five-Layer Reference Model ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["LAYER 5: APPLICATION LAYER
Smart Home, eHealth, Smart Cities"] L2["LAYER 4: SERVICE AND APPLICATION SUPPORT LAYER
Generic Services like Data Storage, Data Mining"] L3["LAYER 3: NETWORK LAYER
Routing, Gateways, 6LoWPAN, RPL"] L4["LAYER 2: DEVICE LAYER
Sensors, Actuators, RFID Tags, Embedded Devices"] L5["LAYER 1: DEVICE HARDWARE AND SENSING
Physical Hardware and Sensing Sub-Layer"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Sense-Compute-Actuate Closed Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR SENS["SENSE
Temperature Sensor
Humidity Probe"] ID["IDENTIFY
Unique UID
IPv6 Address"] COMM["COMMUNICATE
Wi-Fi, LoRa, BLE"] PROC["PROCESS
Edge AI, Cloud Analytics"] ACT["ACTUATE
Motor, Relay, Alarm"] SENS --> ID --> COMM --> PROC --> ACT PROC -.Feedback.-> SENS ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block — IoT as a Superset of M2M, H2M, H2H ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IOT["IoT - Super Set"] M2M["M2M: Machine to Machine
Sensor to Gateway"] H2M["H2M: Human to Machine
Phone to Smart Bulb"] H2H["H2H: Human to Human
WhatsApp via Cloud"] IOTSH["Shared Overlap Region
Cross-Protocol Events"] end M2M --- IOTSH H2M --- IOTSH H2H --- IOTSH ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the Internet of Things as per the ITU-T Y.2060 recommendation. Mention any two features of IoT.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** According to ITU-T Y.2060, *"The Internet of Things is a global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."* **Two features of IoT:** 1. **Dynamic & Self-Configuring:** The network can adapt to changing topologies without manual intervention. 2. **Interoperable Communication Protocols:** Things communicate using standard protocols across heterogeneous networks. *Mark Distribution: Definition = 2 marks; Two features = ½ mark each.* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a physical thing and a virtual thing in IoT with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Physical Thing** is an object that exists in the physical world and can be sensed, identified, or actuated. *Example:* A temperature sensor deployed in a cold-storage warehouse. A **Virtual Thing** is an entity that exists only in the digital or information world, such as software agents and cloud-hosted services. *Example:* A virtual thermostat service running on AWS IoT Core. *Mark Distribution: Definition of Physical = 1; Definition of Virtual = 1; Examples = 1.* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **Important:** Per KTU 2024 ESE norms, every Module-1 Part-B question offers an internal choice. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives below. --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail any **three authoritative definitions of IoT** along with their key emphasis areas. **Model Solution:** **Definition 1 — ITU-T Y.2060 (2 Marks)** The ITU-T defines IoT as *"a global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting physical and virtual things based on interoperable ICT."* **Key Emphasis:** *Service enablement* and *interoperability*. It is treated as the formal telecom-standard reference. **Definition 2 — IERC (European Research Cluster) (2 Marks)** The IERC defines IoT as *"a dynamic global network infrastructure with self-configuring capabilities based on standard and interoperable communication protocols where physical and virtual things have identities, physical attributes, and virtual personalities."* **Key Emphasis:** *Self-configuration*, *intelligence*, and *virtual personalities* of things. **Definition 3 — McKinsey Global Institute (2013) (2 Marks)** McKinsey defines IoT as *"a network of sensors and actuators embedded in physical objects, linked through wired and wireless networks, often using the same Internet Protocol."* **Key Emphasis:** *Sensors + Actuators + Networks* — operationally oriented. **Common Themes (1 Mark):** All three emphasize *connectivity*, *interoperability*, and *heterogeneity of things*. They differ in whether they focus on infrastructure (ITU-T), intelligence (IERC), or components (McKinsey). --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **four foundational characteristics / pillars** of IoT. How do they collectively establish a system as "IoT"? **Model Solution:** The four foundational characteristics are: 1. **Sensing / Identification (2 Marks):** Things must be able to sense environmental parameters (temperature, motion, humidity) or be uniquely identified (RFID, IPv6, QR). Without this, no data enters the system. Example: RFID-tagged cattle in smart agriculture. 2. **Connectivity / Communication (2 Marks):** Things must communicate via standard interoperable protocols (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRaWAN, MQTT). The Internet Protocol serves as the unifying glue. 3. **Processing / Computation (1.5 Marks):** Raw data is processed locally (edge) or remotely (cloud/fog) to extract actionable information using AI/ML or rule-based engines. 4. **Actuation / Response (1.5 Marks):** The processed information triggers physical or digital actions — motors spin, alarms ring, APIs are called. This closes the feedback loop. > **Synthesis:** A system qualifies as IoT **only if all four pillars coexist**. A pure sensor network lacks actuation; a pure SCADA system lacks Internet Protocol — neither is true IoT. --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **evolutionary background** of IoT. Explain how the term was coined and trace its development from M2M communications. **Model Solution:** **1. Coining of the Term (2 Marks):** The term **"Internet of Things"** was coined by **Kevin Ashton** in **1999** during a presentation at **Procter & Gamble**, later popularized through his work at the **MIT Auto-ID Lab**. He used the term to describe a system where the Internet connects to the physical world via sensors — making computers aware of the *real world* through data. **2. M2M (Machine-to-Machine) Foundation (2 Marks):** IoT evolved from **M2M communications** — point-to-point data exchange between machines using SCADA, telemetry, and PLCs in industrial settings. M2M was *closed-loop, proprietary, and non-IP-based*. **3. Transition from M2M to IoT (2 Marks):** With the advent of **IPv6** (address space of $2^{128}$), low-power wireless protocols (Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN), and cloud computing, M2M opened up to the Internet. Devices became **uniquely IP-addressable**, leading to M2M $\to$ IoT transition. **4. Modern IoT Era (1 Mark):** Today, IoT integrates **AI/ML, edge computing, 5G, and digital twins**, making it a foundational layer for Industry 4.0, smart cities, and the metaverse. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **ITU-T Y.2060 reference model** for IoT. List the function of each layer. **Model Solution:** The ITU-T Y.2060 model consists of **five layers** (some references group them into four): **Layer 1 — Device Hardware & Sensing Sub-Layer (1 Mark):** Contains physical sensors, actuators, transceivers. Converts physical phenomena (heat, motion, light) into electrical signals. **Layer 2 — Device Layer (1.5 Marks):** Includes RFID tags, embedded devices, sensor nodes. Responsible for local data acquisition and actuation. **Layer 3 — Network Layer (1.5 Marks):** Provides routing and transport using 6LoWPAN, RPL, IPv4/IPv6, and gateways. Acts as the "postal system" of IoT. **Layer 4 — Service & Application Support Layer (1.5 Marks):** Generic capabilities like data storage, retrieval, mining, and security services. Independent of specific applications. **Layer 5 — Application Layer (1.5 Marks):** Smart home, e-health, intelligent transport, smart grid. Provides user-facing services built on lower layers. **Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 above for the structural visualization.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Quoting only one definition:** Students often write only the ITU-T definition and ignore IERC/McKinsey. KTU mandates comparison; you lose **2-3 marks** for this. > 2. **Confusing "things" with "sensors":** A "thing" is a *broader* entity — sensors, actuators, RFID tags, vehicles, *and* even virtual agents qualify. > 3. **Writing P2P without IPv6 reference:** When asked about addressability, students often forget to cite **IPv6's $2^{128}$ address space** as the enabling technology. > 4. **Missing the "self-configuring" keyword:** The IERC definition's *self-configuring* property is a high-frequency KTU short-answer question — always memorize it. > 5. **Diagram labeling errors:** In the ITU-T reference model, students frequently swap the *Service* and *Network* layers — check carefully. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Definitions of IoT** - ✅ IoT was **coined by Kevin Ashton in 1999** at Procter & Gamble / MIT Auto-ID Lab. - ✅ The **ITU-T Y.2060 (2012)** definition is the most formal and the most KTU-referenced. - ✅ **IERC (2014)** adds the *self-configuring* and *virtual personalities* dimensions. - ✅ A "thing" can be **physical** (sensor, RFID) **or virtual** (software agent). - ✅ Every "thing" must have a **unique identifier** (typically IPv6 — $2^{128}$ addresses). - ✅ The **four pillars** of IoT: **Sense, Identify, Communicate, Compute, Actuate** (often listed as 4 or 5 depending on the source). - ✅ IoT = **M2M ∪ H2M ∪ H2H** (set-theoretic union). - ✅ McKinsey's definition emphasizes **sensors + actuators + networks** — the most engineering-friendly form. - ✅ The ITU-T Y.2060 reference model has **5 layers**; Cisco's has **5 stages**; the IERC has the **Sense-Compute-Actuate** loop. - ✅ Key protocol enablers: **6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, MQTT, IEEE 802.15.4, IPv6**. - ✅ Smart agriculture, smart cities, e-health, and IIoT are the four major application sectors most cited in KTU answer keys. - ✅ Differentiate from **WSN** (Wireless Sensor Network): WSN is a *subset* of IoT — WSN lacks IP-connectivity, actuation, and global addressing in its pure form. - ✅ A true IoT system **must** satisfy *all* four pillars; lacking even one disqualifies the system from being labelled "IoT".

topics.title: Characteristics of IoT Devices - Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of IoT Devices - Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 OEERT614 syllabus**, the **Power** characteristic of an IoT device refers to the quantitative and qualitative management of electrical energy consumed, harvested, stored, and dissipated by the device across its operational lifecycle. It encompasses the device's power supply architecture, average power consumption profile, peak current draw, sleep/active duty cycle, and the corresponding battery lifetime or energy harvesting feasibility.

Formally, power in an IoT node is defined as the rate at which electrical energy is consumed by the embedded subsystems (sensors, micro-controller unit, radio transceiver, and actuators) to perform sensing, computation, and communication tasks.

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

where $P_{\text{avg}}$ is the average power drawn in **Watts (W)**, $E_{\text{total}}$ is the total energy consumed per duty cycle in **Joules (J)**, and $T_{\text{cycle}}$ is the duration of one operational cycle in **seconds (s)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Power is one of the **four primary characteristics** of IoT devices, alongside *Connectivity, Sensing/Actuation, and Intelligence/Identity*. The KTU board consistently tests the student's ability to relate power budgets to *deployment lifetime*, *duty cycling*, and *energy harvesting* — not just define the term.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smartphone vs. The IoT Sensor

Imagine two devices:
1. A **smartphone** — you charge it every day, it has a screen, powerful processor, and constant connectivity. Power is *abundant* and *manually replenished*.
2. A **soil moisture sensor** buried in a farm — no screen, tiny processor, sleeps 99% of the time, and runs on a coin cell battery for **5+ years**.

The IoT sensor is the perfect analogy. Most IoT edge devices are **untethered, deployed in remote or hostile environments, and expected to run for years without human intervention**. This makes power not just a feature — it is a **design constraint that dictates everything else**: the choice of MCU, the radio protocol, the data transmission frequency, and even the physical casing.

## 1.3 Key Power Metrics Used in IoT

The following standard metrics are universally applied in KTU-board level questions:

- **Active Current ($I_{\text{active}}$):** Current drawn when the MCU, sensor, and radio are fully operational.
- **Sleep Current ($I_{\text{sleep}}$):** Current drawn in deep-sleep / standby mode (often in **µA** or **nA**).
- **Duty Cycle ($D$):** The fraction of time the device is active.
- **Average Current ($I_{\text{avg}}$):** The effective current drawn over a full cycle, which determines battery life.
- **Battery Capacity ($C_{\text{bat}}$):** Usually rated in **mAh** (milliampere-hours).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** For battery-powered IoT devices, the **sleep current** is often more critical than the active current, because the device spends the overwhelming majority of its life in sleep mode. A poorly designed sleep state can drain a coin cell in weeks instead of years.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Power Flows in an IoT Node

A typical IoT device is composed of **four power-consuming subsystems**, and power management involves orchestrating all of them:

1. **Power Source (Supply Stage):**
   - Primary sources: Batteries (Li-ion, Alkaline, Li-SOCl₂), Energy Harvesters (solar, vibration, thermal, RF), USB/Mains.
   - Each source has a characteristic energy density (e.g., a CR2032 coin cell holds roughly **220 mAh at 3 V = 2.4 kJ**).

2. **Power Management Unit (PMU / Voltage Regulator):**
   - DC-DC converters, LDO regulators.
   - Must maintain a stable rail (e.g., 3.3 V or 1.8 V) for digital logic.
   - Quiescent current ($I_{\text{q}}$) of the regulator is itself a power loss that must be accounted for.

3. **Processing & Sensing Subsystem:**
   - MCU (e.g., ESP32, ARM Cortex-M0+), sensors (temperature, humidity, accelerometer).
   - Power scales with clock frequency, supply voltage, and active peripherals.

4. **Communication Subsystem (Radio):**
   - This is the **single largest power sink** in most IoT nodes.
   - Wi-Fi peak: ~**250 mA**; BLE: ~**7.5 mA**; LoRa: ~**40–100 mA** (transmit), Zigbee: ~**30 mA**.
   - Radio is **bursty** — active only during transmit/receive events.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Power Management Techniques

| Technique | How it Works | Why it Matters for KTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Duty Cycling** | Device sleeps for $T_{\text{sleep}}$ and wakes for $T_{\text{active}}$; repeats every $T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Reduces average power by 2–4 orders of magnitude |
| **Sleep Modes** | Deep sleep shuts down CPU, RAM retention disabled, peripherals off | Lowers current from **mA → µA** |
| **Energy Harvesting** | Converts ambient energy (light, heat, motion) into electrical energy | Enables *energy-neutral* or *self-sustaining* IoT |
| **Power Gating** | Disconnects power to unused circuit blocks via MOSFET switches | Eliminates leakage current in inactive sub-circuits |
| **Dynamic Voltage & Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** | Lowers V/F when full performance is not needed | Power scales quadratically with voltage ($P \propto V^2 f$) |
| **Protocol Selection** | Choosing BLE/LoRa over Wi-Fi for low-rate uplink | Cuts radio energy per bit drastically |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - D) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}$ | Average current over one duty cycle | mA |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$ | Duty cycle ratio | dimensionless (0–1) or % |
| 3 | $T_{\text{lifetime}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$ | Battery lifetime in hours | hours |
| 4 | $T_{\text{lifetime}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}} \times 8760}$ | Battery lifetime converted to years | years |
| 5 | $P_{\text{avg}} = V_{\text{supply}} \cdot I_{\text{avg}}$ | Average power from average current | W or mW |
| 6 | $E_{\text{bit}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{avg}}}{R_{\text{data}}}$ | Energy per transmitted bit (J/bit) | J/bit or µJ/bit |
| 7 | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic CMOS power dissipation | W |
| 8 | $E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}}$ | Total energy per duty cycle | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula 6 — *Energy per bit ($E_{\text{bit}}$)* — is the metric used by KTU to compare wireless protocols. BLE achieves roughly **50 nJ/bit**, Wi-Fi about **10 µJ/bit**, and LoRa **~1 µJ/bit** at long range. Lower $E_{\text{bit}}$ = longer battery life for the same data throughput $R_{\text{data}}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil sensors in a vineyard use solar harvesting + duty cycling to achieve >10 years maintenance-free operation.
- **Smart Water Metering:** AMR (Automatic Meter Reading) devices use a primary Li-SOCl₂ cell with 10+ year life — entirely determined by the power calculation above.
- **Wearable Health Monitors:** Use energy harvesting from body heat / motion to extend battery life beyond single-charge use.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration sensors on rotating machinery harvest from the very vibration they are monitoring — a closed-loop power design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Current and Battery Lifetime

**Statement:** An IoT temperature sensor wakes every 10 minutes, takes a reading, transmits it over BLE, and returns to deep sleep. The active current is **7.5 mA** for **80 ms**, and the sleep current is **8 µA**. The device is powered by a CR2032 coin cell rated at **220 mAh**. Calculate the expected battery lifetime in years.

**Step 1 — Identify the cycle parameters.**
The cycle period is $T_{\text{cycle}} = 10\ \text{min} = 600\ \text{s}$.
The active time is $T_{\text{active}} = 80\ \text{ms} = 0.080\ \text{s}$.
The sleep time is $T_{\text{sleep}} = 600 - 0.080 = 599.92\ \text{s}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the duty cycle.**

$$D = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{0.080}{600} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.0133\ \%$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average current using the KTU formula.**

$$I_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - D) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = (1.333 \times 10^{-4})(7.5\ \text{mA}) + (0.9998667)(0.008\ \text{mA})$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mA} + 7.999 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mA}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 9.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mA} = 9.0\ \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Convert battery capacity from mAh to µAh for direct comparison.**

$$C_{\text{bat}} = 220\ \text{mAh} = 220{,}000\ \mu\text{Ah}$$

**Step 5 — Compute battery lifetime in hours.**

$$T_{\text{lifetime, hours}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{220{,}000\ \mu\text{Ah}}{9.0\ \mu\text{A}} = 24{,}444\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 6 — Convert hours to years.**

$$T_{\text{lifetime, years}} = \frac{24{,}444}{8760\ \text{h/year}} = 2.79\ \text{years}$$

**Conclusion:** The CR2032 cell can power the node for **~2.79 years**. This is a typical KTU 14-mark style numerical — examiners love it because it forces the student to handle unit conversions between **mAh, µA, mA, s, min, h, year** without making dimensional errors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Per Bit for Protocol Comparison

**Statement:** A wireless sensor transmits **1 packet (20 bytes)** every **30 seconds** over a radio whose average power draw during the cycle is **9.0 µW** (from the previous problem, using a 1 V rail for simplicity). Calculate the energy per bit and compare it to a Wi-Fi link transmitting the same packet using **50 mW** of average power.

**Step 1 — Convert bytes to bits.**

$$N_{\text{bits}} = 20\ \text{bytes} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte} = 160\ \text{bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the data rate in bits per second for the BLE-class link.**

$$R_{\text{data}} = \frac{160\ \text{bits}}{30\ \text{s}} = 5.33\ \text{bits/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the energy-per-bit formula.**

$$E_{\text{bit}} = \frac{P_{\text{avg}}}{R_{\text{data}}} = \frac{9.0\ \mu\text{W}}{5.33\ \text{bits/s}} = 1.6875\ \mu\text{J/bit}$$

> ⚠️ **Correction Note:** This high $E_{\text{bit}}$ reflects the *effective* energy per bit *averaged over the entire 30 s cycle*, not just the transmission interval. Real on-air $E_{\text{bit}}$ for BLE is closer to 50 nJ/bit when measured only over the active radio window.

**Step 4 — Recompute the Wi-Fi equivalent.**

$$E_{\text{bit, Wi-Fi}} = \frac{50{,}000\ \mu\text{W}}{5.33\ \text{bits/s}} = 9{,}375\ \mu\text{J/bit} = 9.375\ \text{mJ/bit}$$

**Step 5 — Compare ratios.**

$$\frac{E_{\text{bit, Wi-Fi}}}{E_{\text{bit, BLE-avg}}} = \frac{9{,}375}{1.6875} = 5555$$

**Conclusion:** The BLE-class link is roughly **~5500× more energy-efficient** per bit than Wi-Fi for this low-throughput scenario — a direct quantitative justification for KTU questions that ask *"Why is BLE preferred over Wi-Fi for battery IoT?"*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Power Characteristics of Common IoT Radios

| Radio Protocol | Peak TX Current | Active Time per Packet | Sleep Current | Effective $E_{\text{bit}}$ | Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11n)** | 250 mA | ~5 ms | 10 µA | ~10 µJ/bit | ~50 m |
| **BLE 5.0** | 7.5 mA | ~3 ms | 1 µA | ~50 nJ/bit | ~75 m |
| **LoRa (SX1276)** | 100 mA | ~300 ms | 0.2 µA | ~1 µJ/bit | ~10 km |
| **Zigbee (802.15.4)** | 30 mA | ~4 ms | 1 µA | ~100 nJ/bit | ~100 m |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | 220 mA | ~1 s (DRX) | 3 µA | ~3 µJ/bit | ~15 km |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** LoRa has *high* $E_{\text{bit}}$ but is still the best choice for *long-range, low-rate* monitoring (one packet per hour). Why? Because **per-packet energy** matters more than $E_{\text{bit}}$ when you are range-limited. This is a common viva trap.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Effect of Voltage Scaling (DVFS)

**Step 1 — Start with dynamic CMOS power equation.**

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$$

**Step 2 — If voltage is reduced from $V_{1}$ to $V_{2} = k \cdot V_{1}$ (where $k < 1$), and frequency is also scaled linearly to $f_{2} = k \cdot f_{1}$, find the new power.**

$$P_{\text{dyn,2}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot (k V_{1})^{2} \cdot (k f_{1}) = k^{3} \cdot P_{\text{dyn,1}}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**
- A **50% voltage and frequency reduction** ($k = 0.5$) yields a power reduction of $0.5^{3} = 0.125$, i.e., **87.5% power savings**.
- This is the *physical* reason why KTU emphasizes DVFS — it is *cubic* in voltage, making it one of the most powerful knobs in IoT power management.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Power-Management Architecture of an IoT Node

The following Mermaid block depicts the **block-level functional flow** of energy and control signals inside a typical battery-powered IoT device. This is the diagram the KTU board expects a student to draw under the question *"Explain the power architecture of an IoT device."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Power Source Battery or Harvester] --> pmuA[Power Management Unit PMU]
    srcB[Solar Cell Harvester] --> pmuA
    srcC[Vibration Piezo Harvester] --> pmuA
    pmuA --> regA[LDO Regulator 3V3 Rail]
    pmuA --> regB[DC-DC Buck 1V8 Rail]
    regA --> mcuA[Microcontroller Unit]
    regB --> sensA[Sensor Array]
    mcuA --> ctrlA[Power Gate Controller]
    ctrlA --> gateA[Switch to Radio]
    ctrlA --> gateB[Switch to Sensors]
    mcuA --> radioA[Radio Transceiver]
    sensA --> mcuA
    radioA --> antA[Antenna]
    mcuA --> monA[Battery Monitor ADC]
    monA --> pmuA
```

**How to read this diagram for your KTU answer:**
- *Energy arrows* (left side): From source to rails.
- *Control arrows* (centre): From MCU's GPIO/PMIC interface to power gates.
- *Feedback arrow* (bottom): Battery monitor senses voltage and feeds back to PMU to throttle or alert.

## 4.2 Duty-Cycle State Machine

This diagram shows the time-domain behaviour of the node — *active* vs. *sleep* — and the power state transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateSleep
    StateSleep --> StateActive: Timer Wake Interrupt
    StateActive --> StateRadioTX: Data Ready
    StateRadioTX --> StateActive: TX Complete ACK Received
    StateActive --> StateSleep: Task Complete
    StateSleep --> StateSleep: Low Battery
    note right of StateSleep: I_sleep approx 1 to 10 microA
    note right of StateActive: I_active approx 7.5 mA MCU plus sensors
    note right of StateRadioTX: I_TX approx 30 to 250 mA burst
```

## 4.3 Energy-Harvesting Decision Flow

The following block diagram captures the KTU-favoured *energy-neutral IoT* design philosophy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ambA[Ambient Source Light Heat Motion RF] --> trA[Transducer Solar PV Thermoelectric Piezo Rectenna]
    trA --> condA[Energy Conditioning Circuit]
    condA --> stA[Storage Element Supercap or Thin-Film Battery]
    stA --> regA[Voltage Regulator]
    regA --> iotA[IoT Node Load]
    iotA -.-> fbA[Energy Budget Feedback]
    fbA --> condA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva Question You Should Expect:** *"Can an energy-harvesting IoT node still run out of power?"* — **Yes**, if the average harvested power $P_{\text{harvest}}$ is less than the average consumed power $P_{\text{avg}}$. The condition for *energy neutrality* is $P_{\text{harvest}} \geq P_{\text{avg}}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three power-management techniques used in IoT devices and explain how duty cycling reduces power consumption.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three power-management techniques in IoT devices are:
1. **Duty Cycling** — The device alternates between a high-power *active* state and a low-power *sleep* state.
2. **Sleep Modes / Power Gating** — Unused peripherals and clock domains are shut down to cut leakage.
3. **Energy Harvesting** — Ambient energy is converted to electrical energy to supplement or replace the battery.

**Duty cycling** reduces power consumption by ensuring the radio, MCU, and sensors are powered **only for the short duration** when meaningful work is being done (sensing + transmission). For the remainder of the cycle, the device is in deep sleep drawing µA-level current. Since power is consumed only during $T_{\text{active}} \ll T_{\text{cycle}}$, the *average* power is reduced by orders of magnitude, directly extending battery life.

> **Marking:** 1 mark for listing three techniques correctly; 2 marks for the duty-cycling explanation with a clear cause-effect statement.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between active current and sleep current in an IoT node. Why is sleep current critical for battery lifetime?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Active Current ($I_{\text{active}}$) | Sleep Current ($I_{\text{sleep}}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Magnitude | mA range | µA to nA range |
| Duration per cycle | Short (ms to s) | Long (minutes to hours) |
| Subsystems ON | CPU, Radio, Sensors | Almost all OFF; only RTC running |

**Why sleep current is critical:** Because the device spends **>99%** of its lifetime in sleep mode, even a small sleep current (e.g., 50 µA instead of 1 µA) can dominate the average current and drastically reduce battery life. Optimising sleep current is the single most effective lever for years-long deployment.

> **Marking:** 1 mark for the table; 1 mark for the *duration* insight; 1 mark for the numerical dominance argument.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **power supply architecture** of an IoT device, with a neat block diagram covering energy source, PMU, regulator, and load subsystems. Discuss the role of energy harvesting in enabling maintenance-free IoT deployments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT air-quality monitor operates with the following parameters:
- $I_{\text{active}} = 12\ \text{mA}$ for $T_{\text{active}} = 150\ \text{ms}$
- $I_{\text{sleep}} = 5\ \mu\text{A}$ during the remaining time
- Measurement interval: once every **5 minutes**
- Battery: **2 × AA Alkaline (each 2500 mAh) in series**, so total capacity = 2500 mAh at 3 V

Compute the **duty cycle, average current, and expected battery lifetime in years**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**
A typical IoT power architecture has four layers:

1. **Energy Source Layer:** Primary battery (Li-SOCl₂, Alkaline), secondary (Li-ion), or harvester (solar, vibration, thermal). Selection depends on *energy density* vs. *deployment lifetime* trade-off.
2. **Power Management Unit (PMU):** A chip like TI BQ25570 or ADP5090 that handles maximum power point tracking (MPPT) for harvesters, battery charging, and source switching.
3. **Voltage Regulation Layer:** LDOs for low-noise rails, buck converters for high-current rails (e.g., radio).
4. **Load Layer:** MCU, sensor, radio — each power-gated by the MCU's GPIO-controlled MOSFETs.

**Role of energy harvesting:** It enables **energy-neutral operation** ($P_{\text{harvest}} \geq P_{\text{avg}}$), where the device never needs battery replacement — crucial for sensors in pipelines, structural health, agriculture, and remote wildlife tracking. Harvesters convert ambient energy (solar ~ 100 mW/cm², vibration ~ 10–100 µW, thermal gradients ~ 50 µW/cm²·K) into usable DC.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Cycle time:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = 5\ \text{min} = 300\ \text{s}$$

Step 2 — Sleep time:

$$T_{\text{sleep}} = 300 - 0.150 = 299.85\ \text{s}$$

Step 3 — Duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{0.150}{300} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.05\ \%$$

Step 4 — Average current:

$$I_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - D) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = (5.0 \times 10^{-4})(12\ \text{mA}) + (0.9995)(0.005\ \text{mA})$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} + 4.9975 \times 10^{-3} = 1.09975 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mA} \approx 11.0\ \mu\text{A}$$

Step 5 — Battery lifetime in hours:

$$T_{\text{h}} = \frac{2500\ \text{mAh}}{0.011\ \text{mA}} = 227{,}273\ \text{hours}$$

Step 6 — Convert to years:

$$T_{\text{years}} = \frac{227{,}273}{8760} = 25.94\ \text{years}$$

**Final Answer:** $D = 0.05\ \%,\ I_{\text{avg}} = 11.0\ \mu\text{A},\ \text{Lifetime} \approx 25.9\ \text{years}$.

> **Marking allocation:** 1 mark for identifying all four architecture blocks; 2 marks for energy-harvesting explanation with one numeric example; 1 mark for duty cycle; 2 marks for $I_{\text{avg}}$; 1 mark for unit conversion to years.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Students use the *combined* capacity of batteries in series (correct) but confuse the *voltage* with the *capacity*. Series adds voltage; capacity in Ah remains the **same** as one cell.
> 2. **Common mistake:** Using the *peak* active current instead of the *average* over the active window.
> 3. **Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert mA → µA or hours → years. The 8760 hours/year constant is mandatory.
> 4. **Mark-loss alert:** If the student does not state that $I_{\text{avg}}$ is dominated by sleep current, **−1 mark**.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of relevant equations, explain how **duty cycling and dynamic voltage-frequency scaling (DVFS)** contribute to power reduction in IoT microcontrollers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LoRa-based soil sensor transmits a 32-byte packet every hour. The LoRa radio draws **80 mA at 3.3 V during TX** for **1.2 s per transmission**. The MCU draws **8 mA** while awake for **0.5 s** to read sensors and prepare the packet, then sleeps at **3 µA** for the rest of the hour. Compute the **average power consumption, the energy per packet, and the energy per bit.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — DVFS + Duty Cycling (7 marks):**

**Duty Cycling:**
$$I_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - D) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}$$

Duty cycling reduces average current because the device spends most of its time at $I_{\text{sleep}} \ll I_{\text{active}}$. For a duty of 0.1%, the average current is virtually the sleep current — a **3-order-of-magnitude reduction**.

**DVFS:**
$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$$

Scaling voltage by $k$ and frequency by $k$ gives $P_{\text{new}} = k^{3} P_{\text{old}}$. Halving both yields **87.5% power saving**. DVFS is effective when the workload is variable — e.g., the MCU runs at full speed for packet encryption but drops to low frequency for routine polling.

**Synergy:** Duty cycling handles the *temporal* dimension (when to be on), while DVFS handles the *intensity* dimension (how hard to work when on). Together they cover the full power-management envelope.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Total active time per cycle:

$$T_{\text{active,total}} = 0.5\ \text{s (MCU)} + 1.2\ \text{s (Radio TX)} = 1.7\ \text{s}$$

Step 2 — Sleep time:

$$T_{\text{sleep}} = 3600 - 1.7 = 3598.3\ \text{s}$$

Step 3 — Total charge consumed per cycle:

$$Q = (0.5\ \text{s})(8\ \text{mA}) + (1.2\ \text{s})(80\ \text{mA}) + (3598.3\ \text{s})(0.003\ \text{mA})$$

$$Q = 4.0\ \text{mC} + 96.0\ \text{mC} + 10.795\ \text{mC} = 110.795\ \text{mC}$$

Step 4 — Average current:

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{110.795\ \text{mC}}{3600\ \text{s}} = 0.03078\ \text{mA} = 30.78\ \mu\text{A}$$

Step 5 — Average power:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 3.3\ \text{V} \times 30.78\ \mu\text{A} = 101.6\ \mu\text{W}$$

Step 6 — Energy per packet (one cycle = one packet):

$$E_{\text{packet}} = 110.795\ \text{mC} \times 3.3\ \text{V} = 365.6\ \text{mJ}$$

Step 7 — Energy per bit:

$$N_{\text{bits}} = 32 \times 8 = 256\ \text{bits}$$

$$E_{\text{bit}} = \frac{365.6\ \text{mJ}}{256} = 1.428\ \text{mJ/bit} = 1428\ \mu\text{J/bit}$$

**Final Answer:** $I_{\text{avg}} = 30.78\ \mu\text{A},\ P_{\text{avg}} = 101.6\ \mu\text{W},\ E_{\text{packet}} = 365.6\ \text{mJ},\ E_{\text{bit}} = 1.43\ \text{mJ/bit}$.

> **Marking allocation:** 2 marks each for duty cycling and DVFS equations with physical interpretation; 1 mark for synergy statement; 2 marks for $P_{\text{avg}}$; 1 mark for $E_{\text{packet}}$; 1 mark for $E_{\text{bit}}$ and final numeric with units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B:**
> 1. **Critical pitfall:** Treating MCU active current and Radio TX current as occurring *simultaneously*. In most designs, the MCU *finishes* before the radio starts. Always check the question's wording — here they were given as **separate windows**, so the time addition is correct, but in some KTU questions they overlap, in which case the *higher* of the two currents is used over the combined window.
> 2. **Mark-loss alert:** Forgetting the **1.2 s radio window** when computing average power — students often only consider the MCU window. −2 marks.
> 3. **Mark-loss alert:** Not converting µA to mA or mC to C consistently. Keep all units in the **SI prefix family** (mA, mC, mJ) until the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Power in IoT is a *constraint*, not a feature.** It dictates the choice of MCU, radio, battery, and even deployment location.
- **Sleep current > Active current** in importance for long-life battery nodes, because the device sleeps 99.9%+ of the time.
- **Master these three formulas — they appear in every KTU paper:**
  1. $I_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - D) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}$
  2. $T_{\text{lifetime}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$ (in hours)
  3. $E_{\text{bit}} = P_{\text{avg}} / R_{\text{data}}$
- **DVFS gives cubic power reduction** with voltage scaling: $P_{\text{new}} = k^{3} P_{\text{old}}$.
- **Duty cycling is the *first* technique** to apply for any battery-powered IoT device.
- **Energy harvesting** enables *energy-neutral* operation when $P_{\text{harvest}} \geq P_{\text{avg}}$.
- **Radio is the dominant power sink** — choose protocol by *data rate, range, and $E_{\text{bit}}$*, not by convenience.
- **Series-connected batteries:** voltage adds, **capacity stays the same** as one cell.
- **Conversion constant:** 1 year = **8760 hours** = 525,600 minutes.
- **Common KTU traps:**
  - Confusing mAh with mA (capacity vs. current).
  - Forgetting to convert mA to µA when sleep current is given in µA.
  - Using 1 year = 365 days (correct) but **failing to multiply by 24** to get hours.
  - Adding capacities instead of voltages for series battery stacks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computational Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Computational Constraints** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refer to the inherent physical, architectural, and resource limitations imposed on embedded sensing and actuating devices that restrict their ability to perform complex computational tasks. These constraints govern the maximum permissible throughput, storage capacity, energy budget, communication bandwidth, and responsiveness of a node, and consequently shape the entire IoT stack — from hardware selection to protocol design and application-layer logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEERT614 - Module 1):**
> Computational Constraints are a foundational concept because every subsequent topic — communication protocols (MQTT, CoAP, 6LoWPAN), edge computing, lightweight security, and operating systems (Contiki, RIOT, TinyOS) — is a *direct response* to these limitations. Mastering this topic is essential for solving design-level and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Key Constraint Categories (as defined in KTU syllabus)

| # | Constraint Category | Typical Magnitude (IoT Class) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Processing / CPU Capability | **8-bit to 32-bit MCU**, **1–200 MHz** clock |
| 2 | Memory (RAM) | **2 KB – 512 KB** SRAM |
| 3 | Storage (Flash) | **32 KB – 4 MB** |
| 4 | Energy / Power Budget | **0.5 mAh – 2500 mAh**, often battery/scavenged |
| 5 | Communication Bandwidth | **< 250 kbps** (typical LPWAN/802.15.4) |
| 6 | Form Factor & Cost | Often **< $10** BOM, sub-cm³ volume |
| 7 | Real-Time Responsiveness | Hard deadlines (ms–µs) |
| 8 | Thermal Dissipation | Passive cooling only, **< 100 mW** average |

### Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Travelling Salesman with a Tiny Backpack":**
> Imagine a field agent (the IoT sensor) sent into a remote village to collect, process, and forward data. The agent cannot carry a large server, a generator, or a fiber link — only a **tiny backpack containing a calculator, a notepad the size of a postage stamp, a small battery, and a short-range walkie-talkie**. Every decision the agent makes (what to compute locally, what to send, what to compress, what to discard) is dictated by the size of that backpack.
>
> That tiny backpack *is* the set of **computational constraints**. The strategies used to work within them (duty cycling, edge aggregation, lightweight protocols, lossy compression) are the *responses* studied throughout this course.

### Why Constraints Exist in IoT (The "Why")

1. **Physical Size:** Nodes must fit into tight enclosures (wearables, smart meters, soil sensors).
2. **Energy Scarcity:** Many nodes are battery-operated for years or harvest energy from ambient sources (solar, vibration, RF).
3. **Cost Pressure:** Billions of nodes are deployed; per-unit cost must drop below a few dollars.
4. **Communication Bottleneck:** Wireless spectrum is shared and lossy, so transmitting 1 bit can consume more energy than executing 1000 CPU instructions.
5. **Heterogeneity:** Devices from multiple vendors with wildly different capabilities must interoperate.

### Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Computational Performance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three *canonical* metrics define the performance envelope of any IoT node:
>
> - **MIPS / DMIPS** — Millions of Instructions Per Second (CPU throughput)
> - **mW/MHz** — Energy efficiency of the processor
> - **µJ/bit** — Energy cost of transmitting one bit (often **> 1000×** the energy cost of computing it)

These metrics appear routinely in KTU numerical and short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Computational Constraints in IoT

The constraints are best understood as a **five-pillar architecture**. Each pillar pulls against the others, and the IoT system designer must find a feasible operating point in the intersection of all five.

#### Pillar 1 — Processing Constraints (CPU/MCU)

The microcontroller is the brain of the IoT node. Constraints here are:

- **Word size:** 8-bit (e.g., ATmega328), 16-bit (MSP430), 32-bit (ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4).
- **Clock frequency ($f_{clk}$):** Determines instructions per second, but scales linearly with power.
- **Architecture class:** RISC vs CISC; in-order pipelines dominate IoT.
- **FPU / DSP presence:** Rare in low-end IoT; floating-point arithmetic is emulated in software at high energy cost.

**Key insight:** A Cortex-M0+ running at **48 MHz** delivers ~0.9 DMIPS/mW — the energy-efficiency frontier for IoT.

#### Pillar 2 — Memory Constraints (RAM + ROM)

- **SRAM (volatile):** Holds stack, heap, transient buffers. Tiny — often **< 64 KB**.
- **Flash (non-volatile):** Stores firmware and configuration. Slower write, finite endurance (~10k–100k cycles).
- **No Virtual Memory:** No MMU on most IoT MCUs → no paging, no swap, no demand loading.
- **Code must fit ROM; data must fit RAM** simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** A simple HTTP GET request can consume **> 20 KB of RAM** — larger than the entire SRAM of an Arduino-class node. This is precisely why **CoAP over UDP (typically < 1 KB RAM)** replaced HTTP for constrained devices.

#### Pillar 3 — Energy / Power Constraints

Energy is the **dominant** constraint for untethered IoT nodes.

- **Battery capacity ($C_{bat}$)**: measured in mAh, multiplied by nominal voltage gives Wh.
- **Average power budget ($P_{avg}$)**: For a 10-year lifetime on a 2400 mAh coin cell at 3 V:
  
  $$E_{total} = C_{bat} \times V_{nom}$$
  
  $$T_{life} = \frac{E_{total}}{P_{avg}}$$
  
- **Duty cycling:** Node sleeps $\tau_{sleep}$ and is active $\tau_{active}$. Effective duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{\tau_{active}}{\tau_{active} + \tau_{sleep}}$$

- **Energy harvesting:** Solar, piezoelectric, thermal, RF. Power is stochastic; system must adapt.

#### Pillar 4 — Communication / Bandwidth Constraints

- **Duty-cycled MAC** (e.g., 802.15.4, LoRaWAN) → effective throughput is a tiny fraction of the raw rate.
- **Packet loss** of 10–30% is common in lossy IoT networks (LLNs).
- **Energy per bit** ($E_{bit}$) is the canonical metric to compare radios.
- **Asymmetric links** (uplink heavy in sensor nets, downlink heavy in actuators).

#### Pillar 5 — Real-Time and Reliability Constraints

- **Hard real-time deadlines:** Industrial control loops (≤ 1 ms), automotive (≤ 100 µs).
- **Soft real-time:** Environmental monitoring (seconds to minutes acceptable).
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET):** Must be bounded and smaller than the deadline. WCET analysis replaces average-case benchmarking used in general-purpose computing.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Domain | Formula / Relation | Variable Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy stored in a battery | $E = V \times C$ | $V$ = nominal voltage, $C$ = capacity | Joule or Wh |
| Battery lifetime | $T_{life} = E_{total} / P_{avg}$ | Total energy / average power | seconds / years |
| Duty cycle | $D = \tau_{on} / (\tau_{on} + \tau_{off})$ | Active fraction | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Active energy per cycle | $E_{active} = P_{active} \cdot \tau_{on}$ | Power × on-time | Joules |
| Sleep current ratio | $I_{sleep} / I_{active}$ | Quiescent vs active current | unitless |
| CPU execution time | $t_{exec} = N_{cycles} / f_{clk}$ | $N_{cycles}$ = instruction count, $f_{clk}$ = clock | seconds |
| Energy per bit (radio) | $E_{bit} = (P_{tx} \cdot t_{bit}) + E_{startup}$ | Transmit power × bit duration + startup | Joules/bit |
| Link capacity (Shannon) | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = SNR | bits/second |
| Energy efficiency (compute) | $\eta_{CPU} = \text{MIPS} / P_{active}$ | Throughput per watt | MIPS/W |
| Memory footprint | $M_{tot} = M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{stack}$ | Flash + RAM usage | bytes |
| Compression gain | $CR = M_{orig} / M_{comp}$ | Compression ratio | unitless |
| Energy–latency trade-off | $E_{tot} = P_{comp} \cdot t_{comp} + E_{tx}(L)$ | Compute energy + transmission energy | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL ESCAPING RULE (for table integrity):**
> Inside the table above, every mathematical `|x|` (absolute value) is rendered as `\vert x \vert`. Never write the literal pipe character inside a table row — it breaks markdown parsing.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding computational constraints is not academic — it directly enables:

1. **Selecting the right MCU class** for a given application (e.g., Cortex-M0+ for soil sensors vs Cortex-M7 for video analytics).
2. **Designing energy-aware firmware** that meets multi-year battery budgets.
3. **Choosing between edge vs cloud computing** for latency-sensitive workloads.
4. **Justifying the use of lightweight protocols** (CoAP, MQTT-SN) over full HTTP/TCP.
5. **Sizing energy harvesters** to guarantee perpetual operation.
6. **Performing WCET analysis** for safety-critical IoT (ISO 26262, IEC 61508).

### 2.4 The Fundamental Asymmetry — *Why This Matters Most*

> [!WARNING]
> **The Single Most Tested Insight in KTU Paper:**
> In a wireless IoT node, the energy cost of transmitting **1 bit** is roughly **1000 to 10,000×** the energy cost of computing 1 bit locally. Therefore, **local computation is essentially free** compared to communication.
>
> *Implication:* The optimal design is almost always **"compute locally, transmit minimally"** — the philosophical foundation of **edge computing, fog computing, and in-network data aggregation**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are presented in *examination-ready form*. Each transition is fully expanded; no steps are skipped, as required by KTU ESE valuation expectations for 14-mark analytical questions.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Battery Lifetime from Power Budget

**Problem Setup (canonical KTU-style):** A wireless sensor node is powered by a 3.0 V, 2400 mAh Li-ion coin cell. The node's average current draw in *active mode* is 25 mA for 50 ms every 60 seconds; the sleep current is 8 µA.

**Goal:** Estimate the node's operating lifetime in days and in years.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the active energy per event.**

The active mode consumes a current $I_{act}$ for a duration $\tau_{act}$. The energy consumed in one active event is:

$$
E_{act} = V_{nom} \cdot I_{act} \cdot \tau_{act}
$$

Substituting numerical values (converting mA to A and ms to s):

$$
E_{act} = 3.0 \, \text{V} \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{A} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}
$$

$$
E_{act} = 3.0 \times 25 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{J}
$$

$$
E_{act} = 3750 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{J} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}
$$

This is the energy of a single active burst.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the sleep energy per event.**

The sleep energy between two active events is:

$$
E_{sleep} = V_{nom} \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot \tau_{sleep}
$$

where $\tau_{sleep} = 60 \, \text{s} - 50 \, \text{ms} \approx 59.95 \, \text{s}$.

$$
E_{sleep} = 3.0 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 59.95
$$

$$
E_{sleep} = 24 \times 10^{-6} \times 59.95 = 1438.8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{J}
$$

$$
E_{sleep} \approx 1.439 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Total energy per duty-cycle period.**

$$
E_{period} = E_{act} + E_{sleep}
$$

$$
E_{period} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} + 1.439 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
E_{period} = 5.189 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}
$$

The active mode dominates — even though it is "on" for less than 0.1% of the time, it accounts for **72%** of the total energy. This is a common pitfall.

---

**Step 4 — Average power consumption.**

The average power is the total energy per period divided by the period:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{E_{period}}{T_{period}}
$$

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{5.189 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}}{60 \, \text{s}} \approx 8.65 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{avg} \approx 86.5 \, \mu\text{W}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute total energy stored in the battery.**

The battery capacity $C_{bat} = 2400 \, \text{mAh}$ at $V_{nom} = 3.0 \, \text{V}$ stores:

$$
E_{bat} = V_{nom} \times C_{bat}
$$

$$
E_{bat} = 3.0 \, \text{V} \times 2400 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Ah}
$$

$$
E_{bat} = 7.2 \, \text{Wh} = 7.2 \times 3600 \, \text{J} = 25{,}920 \, \text{J}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Compute the operating lifetime.**

$$
T_{life} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}}
$$

$$
T_{life} = \frac{25{,}920 \, \text{J}}{8.65 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{J/s}}
$$

$$
T_{life} \approx 2.996 \times 10^{8} \, \text{s}
$$

Convert to days (divide by $86{,}400$) and years:

$$
T_{life} \approx 3468 \, \text{days} \approx 9.5 \, \text{years}
$$

---

**Step 7 — Verify the duty cycle.**

$$
D = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \approx 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.083\%
$$

A duty cycle below 0.1% is typical for environmental IoT monitoring and is what enables multi-year battery life.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CPU Execution Time vs Clock Frequency (WCET Boundary)

**Problem Setup:** A real-time IoT control loop requires the worst-case execution of 480,000 instructions. The MCU clock can be set to either 16 MHz, 32 MHz, or 64 MHz. Each instruction takes 1 clock cycle (RISC assumption). The control deadline is **10 ms**.

**Goal:** Identify the minimum acceptable clock frequency and explain the energy trade-off.

---

**Step 1 — Compute execution time at each frequency.**

$$
t_{exec} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}
$$

At $f_{clk} = 16 \, \text{MHz}$:

$$
t_{16} = \frac{480{,}000}{16 \times 10^{6}} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 30 \, \text{ms}
$$

At $f_{clk} = 32 \, \text{MHz}$:

$$
t_{32} = \frac{480{,}000}{32 \times 10^{6}} = 15 \, \text{ms}
$$

At $f_{clk} = 64 \, \text{MHz}$:

$$
t_{64} = \frac{480{,}000}{64 \times 10^{6}} = 7.5 \, \text{ms}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Apply the deadline constraint.**

The deadline is $t_{d} = 10 \, \text{ms}$.

The condition $t_{exec} \leq t_{d}$ is satisfied **only at 64 MHz**.

Therefore, the *minimum* acceptable clock frequency is **64 MHz**.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the dynamic power trade-off.**

Dynamic power scales linearly with frequency and with $V_{dd}^2$:

$$
P_{dyn} \propto f_{clk} \cdot V_{dd}^{2}
$$

Assuming the same $V_{dd}$, doubling the clock frequency **doubles** the dynamic power. Hence, 64 MHz consumes **4× more power** than 16 MHz and **2× more** than 32 MHz. The system spends more energy per unit time, but finishes sooner and can return to sleep faster.

**Optimal Strategy:** Run the MCU at the *minimum* frequency that meets the deadline, then enter deep sleep for the remainder of the cycle.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Cost of Local Computation vs Transmission

**Problem Setup:** A temperature sensor generates 16-bit samples at 10 Hz for 1 hour. The raw data must either be (a) transmitted uncompressed, or (b) compressed locally (compression ratio = 4) and then transmitted.

**Transmitter:** $P_{tx} = 50 \, \text{mW}$, link rate $R = 50 \, \text{kbps}$.
**Compression CPU cost:** $E_{comp} = 5 \, \mu\text{J}$ per sample (measured on the MCU).

**Goal:** Determine which strategy uses less energy.

---

**Step 1 — Raw data volume.**

$$
L_{raw} = 16 \, \text{bits} \times 10 \, \text{Hz} \times 3600 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
L_{raw} = 576{,}000 \, \text{bits} = 576 \, \text{kbits}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Transmission time and energy (uncompressed).**

$$
t_{tx,raw} = \frac{L_{raw}}{R} = \frac{576{,}000}{50{,}000} = 11.52 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
E_{tx,raw} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx,raw} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 11.52 = 0.576 \, \text{J}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Compressed data volume and energy.**

$$
L_{comp} = \frac{L_{raw}}{CR} = \frac{576{,}000}{4} = 144{,}000 \, \text{bits}
$$

$$
t_{tx,comp} = \frac{144{,}000}{50{,}000} = 2.88 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
E_{tx,comp} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.88 = 0.144 \, \text{J}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Compression energy cost.**

Number of samples in 1 hour:

$$
N_{samp} = 10 \times 3600 = 36{,}000
$$

$$
E_{comp} = 36{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.18 \, \text{J}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Total energy for the compressed strategy.**

$$
E_{tot,comp} = E_{comp} + E_{tx,comp} = 0.18 + 0.144 = 0.324 \, \text{J}
$$

Compare with raw transmission:

$$
E_{tot,raw} = 0.576 \, \text{J}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

$$
\Delta E = E_{tot,raw} - E_{tot,comp} = 0.252 \, \text{J}
$$

Savings:

$$
\frac{\Delta E}{E_{tot,raw}} = \frac{0.252}{0.576} \approx 43.75\%
$$

**Local compression is energetically favourable.** This single derivation is the cornerstone of *edge computing* — local processing saves more energy than the cost of performing it, because the radio is so much more expensive than the CPU.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Constraint Trade-offs

| Design Lever | Increases ↓ / Decreases ↑ | Processing | Memory | Energy | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Higher clock $f_{clk}$ | ↑ Speed | ✓ Less time, ↑ power | – | ✗ Higher dynamic power | – |
| Local compression | ↑ RAM & cycles | ✗ More compute | ✓ Lower data | ✓ Net win | ✓ Less traffic |
| Duty cycling | ↑ Wake latency | – | – | ✓ Saves energy | ✓ Less contention |
| Edge aggregation | ↑ Buffer RAM | – | ✗ Needs buffer | ✓ Fewer tx | ✓ Saves bandwidth |
| Stronger crypto (AES-128) | ↑ Cycles | ✗ Heavy | – | ✗ More compute | – |
| Use of LPWAN (LoRa) | ↓ Data rate | – | – | ✓ Very low $P_{tx}$ | ✗ Tiny bandwidth |
| Sleep modes (deep sleep) | ↑ Wake latency | – | – | ✓ Saves energy | – |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Node Architecture — *Where the Constraints Live*

The following block diagram maps the five pillars of computational constraints to the physical subsystems of an IoT node. Each subsystem is annotated with its dominant constraint, the typical magnitude, and the design response used in industry.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IoT Node] --> B[MCU Core]
    A --> C[Memory]
    A --> D[Radio]
    A --> E[Power Unit]
    A --> F[Sensor/Actuator]

    B --> B1["Processing Constraint\n8 to 32 bit\n1 to 200 MHz"]
    B --> B2["Response\nRISC, RTOS, event driven"]

    C --> C1["Memory Constraint\n2 KB to 512 KB SRAM"]
    C --> C2["Response\nStatic allocation, no paging"]

    D --> D1["Bandwidth Constraint\nless than 250 kbps typical"]
    D --> D2["Response\nDuty cycled MAC, CoAP"]

    E --> E1["Energy Constraint\n0.5 to 2500 mAh"]
    E --> E2["Response\nSleep modes, harvesting"]

    F --> F1["Form Factor Constraint\nsub cm cubed"]
    F2["Response\nSystem on chip, passive cooling"]

    B1 --- B2
    C1 --- C2
    D1 --- D2
    E1 --- E2
    F1 --- F2
```

### 4.2 Constraint Hierarchy — *Decision Flow When Designing an IoT Node*

This diagram shows the order in which a system designer resolves the five pillars, from the *most binding* (energy) to the *least binding* (real-time) for a typical environmental sensor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Design]) --> q1{Energy Budget?}
    q1 -->|less than 1 mW avg| e1[Choose low power MCU]
    q1 -->|greater than 100 mW| e2[Choose MPU + active cooling]
    e1 --> q2{Memory Footprint?}
    e2 --> q2
    q2 -->|less than 64 KB| m1[Static allocation, no OS]
    q2 -->|more than 1 MB| m2[Linux RTOS, dynamic alloc]
    m1 --> q3{Real Time Deadline?}
    m2 --> q3
    q3 -->|less than 1 ms| r1[RTOS + WCET analysis]
    q3 -->|seconds acceptable| r2[Best effort firmware]
    r1 --> q4{Bandwidth Available?}
    r2 --> q4
    q4 -->|less than 50 kbps| bw1[Use CoAP, MQTT SN]
    q4 -->|greater than 1 Mbps| bw2[HTTP, full TCP IP]
    bw1 --> done([Feasible Design])
    bw2 --> done
```

### 4.3 The Energy–Compute–Bandwidth Triangle

Every IoT design trades energy against compute against bandwidth. The following diagram captures the central tension that drives nearly every architectural decision in constrained IoT.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Energy Budget] -- more compute --> C[Compute Capability]
    C -- more results --> B[Bandwidth Used]
    B -- less compute --> E
    E -- less radio time --> B
    B -- fewer bits --> C
    C -- longer awake --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> Reading the diagram: *increasing compute raises energy use*; *increasing bandwidth also raises energy use*. The only free direction in the triangle is **"do less work and transmit less"** — which is precisely why **data reduction at the edge** is the dominant IoT design pattern.

### 4.4 Memory Layout of a Typical Constrained Node

A common KTU-style diagram question asks the student to draw the memory map of a microcontroller. Below is the canonical layout.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M["Memory Map of a Constrained IoT MCU"]
        direction TB
        V1["0x0000 0000 - Interrupt Vector Table"]
        V2["0x0000 0100 - Bootloader"]
        V3["0x0000 8000 - Application Code (Flash)"]
        V4["0x0001 FFFF - Reserved / Configuration"]
        V5["0x2000 0000 - SRAM (Heap + Stack + Data)"]
        V6["0x4000 0000 - Peripheral Registers"]
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define computational constraints in IoT. List any four major categories of computational constraints with one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Computational constraints in IoT are the inherent physical and architectural limitations on the processing, memory, energy, and communication capabilities of IoT nodes that restrict the complexity of tasks they can perform.

Four major categories:

1. **Processing constraint** — limited CPU/MCU capability, e.g., 8-bit ATmega328 at 16 MHz.
2. **Memory constraint** — small RAM and Flash, e.g., 2 KB SRAM, 32 KB Flash.
3. **Energy constraint** — battery-powered or energy-harvested, e.g., 2400 mAh coin cell for multi-year life.
4. **Bandwidth constraint** — low-rate lossy wireless, e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 at 250 kbps.

*(Each category with example carries 0.5 marks; the introductory definition carries 1 mark.)*

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
*Explain the statement: "In wireless IoT nodes, transmitting 1 bit costs more energy than computing 1000 bits locally." Justify with numerical reasoning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A typical radio consumes $P_{tx} \approx 50 \, \text{mW}$ at 50 kbps, giving:

$$
E_{bit} = \frac{P_{tx}}{R} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{3}} = 1 \, \mu\text{J/bit}
$$

A 32-bit MCU running at 32 MHz consumes ~$10 \, \text{mW}$, executing roughly $32 \times 10^{6}$ instructions per second, so:

$$
E_{inst} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{32 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.3 \, \text{nJ/instruction}
$$

Ratio:

$$
\frac{E_{bit}}{E_{inst}} \approx \frac{1 \, \mu\text{J}}{0.3 \, \text{nJ}} \approx 3300
$$

Hence transmitting 1 bit costs ~$3300\times$ the energy of one CPU instruction, validating the statement. This is the *fundamental asymmetry* that justifies edge computing.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**B-Option 1 — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five major computational constraints** faced by IoT devices. For each, state one engineering response used in practice. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wireless sensor node transmits 1 KB of sensor data every minute over an 802.15.4 radio at 250 kbps. The MCU is a Cortex-M0+ running at 48 MHz at 3.0 V drawing 5 mA while active. Estimate (i) transmission energy, (ii) the energy stored in a 2400 mAh battery, and (iii) how long the battery would last if the node is otherwise idle. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

Provide an expanded explanation of the five pillars using the table from Section 2.1:

1. **Processing (CPU/MCU) constraint** — limited clock, no FPU → response: use RISC MCUs, offload to edge gateway. *(1.4 marks)*
2. **Memory (RAM/Flash) constraint** — no MMU, no paging → response: static allocation, lightweight protocols. *(1.4 marks)*
3. **Energy/Power constraint** — battery-limited → response: duty cycling, sleep modes, energy harvesting. *(1.4 marks)*
4. **Communication/Bandwidth constraint** — duty-cycled MAC, lossy links → response: aggregation, CoAP over UDP. *(1.4 marks)*
5. **Real-time/Reliability constraint** — bounded WCET required → response: RTOS, pre-emptive scheduling, WCET analysis. *(1.4 marks)*

**(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Transmission energy — 3 marks**

Data size $L = 1 \, \text{KB} = 8192 \, \text{bits}$.

Transmission time:

$$
t_{tx} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8192}{250{,}000} \approx 0.0328 \, \text{s}
$$

Radio power (typical 802.15.4) $P_{tx} \approx 30 \, \text{mW}$ at 0 dBm:

$$
E_{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0328 \approx 9.83 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{J}
$$

**(ii) Battery energy — 2 marks**

$$
E_{bat} = V \times C = 3.0 \times 2400 \times 10^{-3} = 7.2 \, \text{Wh} = 25{,}920 \, \text{J}
$$

**(iii) Battery lifetime — 2 marks**

Assume MCU active only during transmission (worst case) and zero sleep current:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{E_{tx}}{60 \, \text{s}} = \frac{9.83 \times 10^{-4}}{60} \approx 1.64 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{W}
$$

$$
T_{life} = \frac{25{,}920}{1.64 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.58 \times 10^{9} \, \text{s} \approx 50 \, \text{years}
$$

In practice, sleep current of 1–10 µA dominates and limits lifetime to 5–10 years. *(This is the discussion point that earns the final 0.5 marks.)*

---

**B-Option 2 — Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice to Question A*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With suitable diagrams and formulas, derive the **battery lifetime** of a duty-cycled IoT sensor node. Assume a 3.0 V, 2400 mAh battery; active current 20 mA for 100 ms every 5 s; sleep current 5 µA. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **energy-asymmetry principle** between local computation and radio transmission. Show mathematically why edge computing is almost always energetically superior to cloud-only processing for simple aggregations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks** *(Detailed derivation mirroring Section 3.1)*

Step 1 — Active energy per event:

$$
E_{act} = V \cdot I_{act} \cdot \tau_{act} = 3.0 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 = 6 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}
$$

Step 2 — Sleep energy per event ($\tau_{sleep} \approx 4.9 \, \text{s}$):

$$
E_{sleep} = 3.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.9 = 7.35 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{J}
$$

Step 3 — Total energy per period (5 s):

$$
E_{period} = 6 \times 10^{-3} + 7.35 \times 10^{-5} \approx 6.0735 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}
$$

Step 4 — Average power:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{6.0735 \times 10^{-3}}{5} = 1.2147 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W} \approx 1.215 \, \text{mW}
$$

Step 5 — Battery energy:

$$
E_{bat} = 3.0 \times 2400 \times 10^{-3} = 7.2 \, \text{Wh} = 25{,}920 \, \text{J}
$$

Step 6 — Lifetime:

$$
T_{life} = \frac{25{,}920}{1.215 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.13 \times 10^{7} \, \text{s} \approx 247 \, \text{days} \approx 8.1 \, \text{months}
$$

Step 7 — Duty cycle:

$$
D = \frac{0.1}{5} = 2\%
$$
