topics.title: Typical Application Areas of IoT Technology (Case Studies of at Least Four Domains) - Energy Management and Smart Grid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Application Areas of IoT Technology (Case Studies of at Least Four Domains) - Energy Management and Smart Grid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is IoT in the Energy Management & Smart Grid Context?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The Internet of Things (IoT) in Energy Management refers to the networked interconnection of smart sensors, actuators, smart meters, and energy management systems that collectively monitor, measure, analyze, and control the generation, transmission, distribution, and consumption of electrical energy in real-time to achieve optimal efficiency, reliability, and sustainability.

In simpler language: imagine your home's electricity meter could **talk** to your phone, your local power station, and the national grid all at once. It could tell the grid, "Hey, I'm not using much power right now — go ahead and reduce generation," and it could tell you, "Hey, your AC is the biggest power hog — maybe turn it down between 2 PM and 4 PM when rates are high." That whole "talking" network is **IoT for Energy Management**, and the larger intelligent electrical network it sits on is the **Smart Grid**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of the **traditional power grid** as a **one-way highway** with no traffic signals:
- Power flows only **from** the power plant **to** your home.
- The utility has **no real-time visibility** into what you are using, and you have **no awareness** of what it costs to generate power at any given moment.

Now think of the **Smart Grid** as a **multi-lane smart highway with sensors, GPS, dynamic tolls, and traffic lights everywhere**:
- Power flows **both ways** (you can sell your rooftop solar back to the grid).
- Every node — from the transformer on your street to the wind farm 200 km away — has **sensors, IP addresses, and decision-making ability**.
- Prices, demand, and supply adjust **dynamically** based on real-time data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The smart grid is not just "IoT applied to electricity." It is a **cyber-physical system** that fuses communication infrastructure (ICT), sensing (IoT), and control theory (automation) into one unified energy ecosystem.

## 1.3 The Four Canonical IoT Application Domains (Case Studies)

KTU 2024 Scheme mandates case studies of **at least four** IoT domains. The four primary verticals are:

| # | Domain | Core IoT Function | Key Performance Metrics |
|---|--------|------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Smart Home / Building** | Appliance control, occupancy sensing, HVAC automation | Energy savings (**15–30%**), occupant comfort index |
| 2 | **Smart City** | Streetlight control, waste management, traffic signaling | Service efficiency (**20–40%**), citizen response time |
| 3 | **Smart Transportation** | Fleet tracking, autonomous vehicles, toll collection | Fuel efficiency, accident reduction rate |
| 4 | **Smart Energy / Smart Grid** | Smart metering, demand response, DER integration | Grid reliability (**SAIDI**), peak-to-average ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Recap:**
> - **SAIDI** = System Average Interruption Duration Index (minutes/customer/year)
> - **SAIFI** = System Average Interruption Frequency Index (interruptions/customer/year)
> - **CAIDI** = Customer Average Interruption Duration Index
> - **PAR** = Peak-to-Average Ratio of power demand

This module focuses deeply on **Domain 4: Smart Energy / Smart Grid**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Smart Grid Architecture — The Conceptual Stack

The smart grid is best understood as a **layered architecture** (often referenced as the **NIST Smart Grid Conceptual Model**). The layers, from bottom to top, are:

### Layer 1 — Physical / Generation Layer
- Conventional generators, **renewable DERs** (solar PV, wind turbines, small hydro), and energy storage systems (**BESS** — Battery Energy Storage Systems).
- IoT sensors here: turbine vibration sensors, solar irradiance pyranometers, battery State-of-Charge (SoC) sensors.

### Layer 2 — Transmission Layer
- High-voltage (HV) lines, transformers, and **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)**.
- PMUs sample voltage and current waveforms at **30–60 samples/second** (vs. 1 sample/2–4 sec in legacy SCADA).

### Layer 3 — Distribution Layer
- **Distribution Automation (DA)** equipment: reclosers, sectionalizers, capacitor banks.
- IoT enables **FLISR** — Fault Location, Isolation, and Service Restoration in **seconds** rather than hours.

### Layer 4 — Customer / Premise Layer
- **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** — smart meters, communication gateways, head-end systems.
- **Home Energy Management Systems (HEMS)** — in-home displays, smart thermostats, smart plugs.

### Layer 5 — Control & Communication Layer
- Protocols: **DNP3, IEC 61850, Modbus, MQTT, CoAP, LoRaWAN**.
- Backhaul: fiber, cellular (4G/5G), RF mesh, PLC (Power Line Communication).

### Layer 6 — Application / Service Layer
- Demand Response (DR), Outage Management System (OMS), Distributed Energy Resource Management System (DERMS), Analytics & Billing.

## 2.2 Smart Metering — The Heartbeat of Smart Grid IoT

A **smart meter** is an electronic device that records consumption of electric energy in intervals of an hour or less and communicates that information at least daily back to the utility for monitoring and billing.

**Smart Meter Block Components:**
1. **Metrology MCU** — measures V, I, P, Q, PF.
2. **Communication Module** — Wi-Fi/RF Mesh/PLC/NB-IoT.
3. **Tamper Detection Circuit** — magnetic, cover-open, reverse-energy.
4. **Local Display (HMI)** — for consumer feedback.
5. **Secure Element (SE)** — cryptographic keys for authentication.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table — these are the high-yield KTU formulas for energy management problems.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary | KTU Use Case |
|---|------------------|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Active Power (single-phase AC) | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | **Watts (W)** | Load estimation |
| 2 | Reactive Power | $Q = V \cdot I \cdot \sin\phi$ | **VAR** | Power factor correction |
| 3 | Apparent Power | $S = V \cdot I$ | **VA** | Transformer sizing |
| 4 | Power Factor | $\text{PF} = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | **0 to 1** | Penalty calculations |
| 5 | Energy Consumed | $E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P(t)\, dt$ | **kWh** | Billing |
| 6 | Peak-to-Average Ratio | $\text{PAR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$ | **dimensionless, $\geq 1$** | Grid stability |
| 7 | Demand Response Shift | $\Delta P_{\text{shift}} = P_{\text{peak}} - P_{\text{new,peak}}$ | **kW** | Load flattening |
| 8 | Renewable Penetration Ratio | $\text{RPR} = \dfrac{E_{\text{renewable}}}{E_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Sustainability index |
| 9 | Smart Meter Energy Error | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{E_{\text{measured}} - E_{\text{true}}}{E_{\text{true}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Class accuracy (0.2/0.5/1.0/2.0) |
| 10 | Capacity Factor (generation) | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{rated}} \times T} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Solar/wind efficiency |
| 11 | Transformer Loading | $\%\text{Load} = \dfrac{S_{\text{actual}}}{S_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Asset health |
| 12 | Energy Storage SoC | $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}(t_0) + \dfrac{1}{C_{\text{bat}}} \int \eta \cdot I(t)\, dt$ | **%** | BESS scheduling |
| 13 | Voltage Drop (line) | $\Delta V = I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \cdot L$ | **Volts** | Distribution planning |
| 14 | Carbon Intensity | $CI = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emitted}}{\text{kWh generated}}$ | **kg CO₂/kWh** | Green metrics |
| 15 | SAIDI | $\text{SAIDI} = \dfrac{\sum r_i N_i}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | **min/cust/yr** | Reliability KPI |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In all handwritten exams and notes, **never** confuse $\cos\phi$ (power factor angle) with $\theta$ (transmission line angle). They are mathematically related but operationally distinct.

## 2.4 Why Smart Grid Needs IoT — The Engineering Motivation

- **Real-time visibility:** Legacy SCADA polls every 2–4 seconds; IoT-PMUs poll 30–60 times/second → enables **Wide-Area Monitoring, Protection, and Control (WAMPAC)**.
- **Bidirectional power flow:** Rooftop solar + EVs turn consumers into "prosumers" — traditional grids cannot handle reverse flow safely without IoT coordination.
- **Demand-side flexibility:** Time-of-Use (ToU) pricing requires communication **back** to consumers — impossible without IoT.
- **Asset health:** Transformer Dissolved Gas Analysis (DGA) sensors detect faults weeks before failure.
- **Renewable variability:** Solar/wind output fluctuates — IoT forecasting + storage coordination smooths the curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility insight:** India's **Saubhagya scheme** and the **Smart Meter National Programme (SMNP)** aim to deploy **250 million+ smart meters** by 2025–26. This is one of the largest IoT rollouts on the planet.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR) and Demand Response Savings

**Problem Setup:** A residential community has 100 homes. The average power demand of each home is $P_{\text{avg}} = 2\text{ kW}$. During evening peak (6 PM – 9 PM), the demand spikes to $P_{\text{peak}} = 5\text{ kW}$ per home.

**Step 1 — Compute the community peak load.**

$$P_{\text{peak,total}} = N \cdot P_{\text{peak}} = 100 \cdot 5\text{ kW} = 500\text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the community average load.**

$$P_{\text{avg,total}} = N \cdot P_{\text{avg}} = 100 \cdot 2\text{ kW} = 200\text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Peak-to-Average Ratio.**

$$\text{PAR} = \frac{P_{\text{peak,total}}}{P_{\text{avg,total}}} = \frac{500}{200} = 2.5$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the PAR.**

A PAR of **2.5** means the grid must be sized to deliver **2.5× the average demand** for only a few hours per day — massively capital-inefficient.

**Step 5 — Apply Demand Response (DR) — shift 30% of peak load.**

$$\Delta P = 0.30 \cdot P_{\text{peak,total}} = 0.30 \cdot 500 = 150\text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — New peak load after DR.**

$$P_{\text{new,peak}} = 500 - 150 = 350\text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — New PAR after DR.**

$$\text{PAR}_{\text{new}} = \frac{350}{200} = 1.75$$

**Step 8 — Compute grid infrastructure capital deferral (illustration).**

If the utility's cost to provision 1 kW of peak generation capacity is **₹50,000**, the deferred capital is:

$$\text{CapEx}_{\text{deferred}} = \Delta P \cdot \text{Cost/kW} = 150 \cdot 50{,}000 = \text{₹}\,7{,}500{,}000$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** IoT-driven Demand Response reduced PAR from **2.5 → 1.75** and saved **₹75 lakh** in capacity capex — without building a single new power plant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Smart Meter Class-Accuracy Energy Error

**Problem:** A smart meter is rated **Class 1.0** (max permissible error = ±1%). During testing, the true energy is $E_{\text{true}} = 1000\text{ kWh}$. The meter reports $E_{\text{measured}} = 1012\text{ kWh}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the percentage error formula.**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{E_{\text{measured}} - E_{\text{true}}}{E_{\text{true}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{1012 - 1000}{1000} \times 100 = \frac{12}{1000} \times 100 = 1.2\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the rated class.**

$$1.2\% > 1.0\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Meter FAILS the Class 1.0 test.}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the over-billing (or under-billing) impact.**

If the tariff is **₹7/kWh**, the customer's bill error is:

$$\Delta \text{Bill} = (E_{\text{measured}} - E_{\text{true}}) \cdot \text{Tariff} = 12 \cdot 7 = \text{₹}\,84$$

For 10 million such meters, the cumulative billing error becomes **₹84 crore** — a real-world risk that IoT-based remote calibration monitoring helps prevent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) State-of-Charge

**Problem:** A 500 kWh community BESS has $\text{SoC}(t_0) = 40\%$. It charges at a constant current $I = 100\text{ A}$ at voltage $V = 400\text{ V}$ for 1 hour with round-trip efficiency $\eta = 0.92$.

**Step 1 — Energy input during charging (theoretical).**

$$E_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I \cdot t = 400 \cdot 100 \cdot 3600 = 1.44 \times 10^{8}\text{ J} = 40\text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored after losses.**

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{in}} = 0.92 \cdot 40 = 36.8\text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Update State of Charge.**

$$\text{SoC}(t_1) = \text{SoC}(t_0) + \frac{E_{\text{stored}}}{C_{\text{bat}}} \times 100\% = 40 + \frac{36.8}{500} \times 100 = 40 + 7.36 = 47.36\%$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Insight.**

The SoC increases by only **7.36 percentage points** despite adding 40 kWh gross input — because **8% of the energy is lost** to heat, internal resistance, and conversion. This is why IoT-based Battery Management Systems (BMS) are critical: they prevent overcharge, thermal runaway, and depth-of-discharge damage.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Legacy Grid vs Smart Grid

| Parameter | Legacy Grid | Smart Grid (IoT-enabled) | Improvement |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Meter reading | Manual, monthly | Automated, **15-min interval** | **~3000×** data density |
| Outage detection | Customer call | **Automatic via AMI alarms** | Hours → seconds |
| Power flow direction | Unidirectional | Bidirectional | Enables prosumer model |
| Demand response | None | **Real-time price signals** | 10–15% peak reduction |
| Renewable integration | Limited, curtailment | **Forecast-driven dispatch** | 30–50% higher penetration |
| Asset monitoring | Periodic inspection | **Continuous sensor monitoring** | 50% fewer failures |
| Billing accuracy | Estimated | **Actual consumption** | Class 0.5 / 0.2 metering |
| Cyber-physical risk | Low | **High — needs security** | New attack surface |

---

## 3.5 Real-World Deployment Numbers (Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Internet of Energy" by numbers (approximate, 2023–2024 figures):**
> - Global smart meter installed base: **> 1 billion units**
> - Annual smart meter shipments: **~200 million units/year**
> - Global smart grid IoT market: **USD 60+ billion** (growing at ~20% CAGR)
> - Typical home energy savings from smart meters + ToU pricing: **5–15%**
> - Industrial IoT energy savings (factories, buildings): **10–25%**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Smart Grid End-to-End IoT Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GEN["GENERATION LAYER - Power Plants and DERs"]
        G1["Conventional Thermal Plant"]
        G2["Solar PV Farm"]
        G3["Wind Farm"]
        G4["Hydro Plant"]
        G5["Battery Energy Storage BESS"]
    end

    subgraph TRAN["TRANSMISSION LAYER - HV Grid 400kV 220kV"]
        T1["Step Up Transformer"]
        T2["PMU Phasor Measurement Unit"]
        T3["HV Transmission Line"]
        T4["Substation SCADA"]
    end

    subgraph DISTR["DISTRIBUTION LAYER - MV 11kV LV 415V"]
        D1["Primary Distribution Feeder"]
        D2["Distribution Transformer with DGA Sensor"]
        D3["Recloser and Sectionalizer"]
        D4["Capacitor Bank for VAR"]
    end

    subgraph CONS["CUSTOMER LAYER - Premises"]
        C1["Smart Meter AMI"]
        C2["Home Energy Management HEMS"]
        C3["Rooftop Solar Inverter"]
        C4["EV Charging Station"]
        C5["Smart Appliance IoT"]
    end

    subgraph COMM["COMMUNICATION LAYER - IoT Backhaul"]
        N1["RF Mesh Network"]
        N2["Cellular 4G 5G NB-IoT"]
        N3["Power Line Communication PLC"]
        N4["Fiber Backbone"]
        N5["LoRaWAN Gateway"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL AND APPLICATION LAYER"]
        A1["Head End System HES"]
        A2["Meter Data Management MDMS"]
        A3["Outage Management OMS"]
        A4["Demand Response Platform"]
        A5["DERMS and Analytics"]
        A6["Customer Portal Mobile App"]
    end

    G1 --> T1
    G2 --> T1
    G3 --> T1
    G4 --> T1
    G5 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C3 --> C1
    C4 --> C1
    C5 --> C2

    C1 <--> N1
    C1 <--> N2
    C2 <--> N5
    D2 <--> N3
    T2 <--> N4
    D3 <--> N2

    N1 --> A1
    N2 --> A1
    N3 --> A1
    N4 --> A1
    N5 --> A1

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A2 --> A4
    A2 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A4 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Energy flows **top → bottom** (Generation → Transmission → Distribution → Customer). Information flows **bidirectionally** via the **Communication Layer** into the **Control & Application Layer**, which sends control signals back down. This bi-directional energy + data flow is the **defining characteristic** of a smart grid.

---

## 4.2 Demand Response Sequence Diagram (Time-of-Use Pricing Event)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Utility DERMS
    participant MD as MDMS Platform
    participant SM as Smart Meter at Home
    participant HEMS as Home Energy Mgmt Sys
    participant AC as Smart Air Conditioner
    participant WH as Smart Water Heater

    U->>MD: Forecast peak 6PM to 9PM, 1hr ahead
    MD->>SM: Broadcast ToU price signal HIGH 12 per kWh
    SM->>HEMS: Forward price signal over HAN
    HEMS->>AC: Setpoint raise 2 deg C for 3 hours
    HEMS->>WH: Delay heating cycle by 90 minutes
    AC-->>HEMS: Ack setpoint applied, draw 1.2kW
    WH-->>HEMS: Ack delay accepted
    HEMS-->>SM: Total household load 2.8kW
    SM-->>MD: 15min interval reading 2.8kW
    MD->>U: Aggregated community load down 18 percent
    U-->>U: Issue rebate 200 INR to consumer
```

---

## 4.3 Smart Meter Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SM["SMART METER MODULE"]
        A["Voltage Divider and CT Sensors"]
        B["Metrology MCU ADC"]
        C["Real Time Clock RTC"]
        D["Tamper Detection Mag Cover Reverse"]
        E["Secure Element Crypto Chip"]
        F["Local LCD Display"]
        G["Communication Module RF Cellular PLC"]
        H["Local Memory Flash 4 to 16MB"]
    end

    A --> B
    C --> B
    B --> H
    B --> G
    D --> B
    E --> G
    B --> F
    G <-->|"HAN / WAN"| EXT["Utility HES"]
```

---

## 4.4 Microgrid with IoT Coordination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MICRO["COMMUNITY MICROGRID"]
        SOL["Rooftop Solar PV 50kWp"]
        WIND["Small Wind 20kW"]
        BESS["Battery Storage 100kWh"]
        DG["Diesel Genset Backup"]
        LOAD["Residential Load Peak 80kW"]
        EMS["Microgrid EMS Controller"]
        PCC["Point of Common Coupling to Main Grid"]
    end

    SOL --> EMS
    WIND --> EMS
    BESS --> EMS
    DG --> EMS
    EMS --> LOAD
    EMS <--> PCC
    PCC <-->|"Bidirectional import export"| MAINGRID["Main Utility Grid"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The **Microgrid EMS** is essentially a **local IoT edge controller** that runs optimization algorithms (often Model Predictive Control) every 1–15 minutes to balance generation, storage, and load — and decides whether to **island** (disconnect from the main grid) during faults.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define the term "Smart Grid". List any four features that distinguish a smart grid from a conventional electrical grid.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Smart Grid** is an electricity network that uses digital communication, IoT sensors, and intelligent control systems to monitor and manage the flow of electricity from **all generation sources to meet the varying electricity demands** of end users in real-time.

**Four distinguishing features:**

1. **Bidirectional power and information flow** — supports distributed prosumers (e.g., rooftop solar owners).
2. **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** — automatic, remote, real-time consumption reading.
3. **Self-healing capability** — automatically detects, isolates, and restores faults (FLISR).
4. **Demand Response integration** — dynamic pricing signals that shift consumer load away from peak hours.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**What is Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)? Mention its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

> **AMI** is the integrated system of smart meters, communication networks, and data management systems that enables **two-way communication** between the consumer's premises and the utility.

**Main Components:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Smart Meter** | Records consumption at 15-min or hourly intervals |
| 2 | **Communication Network** | RF Mesh / PLC / Cellular to transport data |
| 3 | **Head-End System (HES)** | Aggregates and manages all meters in a region |
| 4 | **Meter Data Management System (MDMS)** | Validates, stores, and processes meter data |
| 5 | **Customer Gateway / In-Home Display** | Provides real-time feedback to consumer |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question has a sub-part (a) for **7 marks** and sub-part (b) for **7 marks**. The two sub-parts together make **14 marks**. Students may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the layered architecture of the Smart Grid with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of IoT in each layer.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The Smart Grid architecture, as defined in the **NIST Smart Grid Conceptual Model**, is organized into **seven logical layers**:

1. **Bulk Generation Layer** — Large centralized power plants (thermal, hydro, nuclear) and distributed energy resources (DERs) like solar, wind, biomass. **IoT role:** Vibration, temperature, and emissions sensors on turbines; weather sensors for forecasting renewable output.

2. **Transmission Layer** — High-voltage lines (400 kV, 220 kV in India) and substations. **IoT role:** **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** at 30–60 Hz sampling; enables **Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS)** to detect grid instabilities in real time.

3. **Distribution Layer** — Medium- and low-voltage feeders reaching homes and businesses. **IoT role:** **Distribution Automation (DA)** devices — reclosers, sectionalizers, capacitor bank controllers with **FLISR** (Fault Location, Isolation, Service Restoration).

4. **Customer / Premise Layer** — End consumers with smart meters, rooftop solar, EVs, smart appliances, **Home Energy Management Systems (HEMS)**. **IoT role:** Bi-directional metering, load control, in-home displays.

5. **Control / Operations Layer** — Utility control centers running **SCADA**, **Energy Management System (EMS)**, **Distribution Management System (DMS)**. **IoT role:** Real-time data ingestion, predictive analytics.

6. **Communication Layer** — The IoT **backhaul**: RF mesh, cellular (4G/5G/NB-IoT), PLC, fiber, LoRaWAN, satellite. **IoT role:** The **nervous system** of the grid.

7. **Application / Service Layer** — Demand Response, Outage Management System (OMS), DERMS, billing, customer portals, mobile apps, and analytics dashboards. **IoT role:** Translates raw data into consumer-facing services.

> **Conclusion:** Each layer integrates IoT devices whose **data** flows up to the application layer, and whose **control commands** flow back down — closing the cyber-physical loop.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the working of a Home Energy Management System (HEMS) integrated with smart meters and IoT-enabled appliances. Explain how it reduces the Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR).

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

A **Home Energy Management System (HEMS)** is an IoT-based residential platform that monitors and controls the energy consumption of household appliances to **minimize cost and peak demand** while **maintaining user comfort**.

**Block diagram (textual):**

```
[Smart Meter (AMI)] ↔ [HEM Gateway / Controller] ↔ [Smart Appliances]
                                  ↕
                        [Cloud / Mobile App]
                                  ↕
                    [Utility Demand Response Signal]
```

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Smart Meter** records household consumption at 15-min intervals and sends it to the utility's **MDMS** over the AMI network.
2. The **HEMS controller** (often a smart speaker, dedicated device, or router) receives the same data plus **Time-of-Use (ToU) price signals** from the utility.
3. Based on the price, occupancy sensors, and user-defined preferences, the controller **schedules deferrable loads**:
   - **Water heater** → heated during off-peak (11 PM – 6 AM).
   - **Washing machine / dishwasher** → started when ToU rate drops.
   - **EV charger** → starts only when SoC target and tariff both allow.
   - **AC / Thermostat** → setpoint raised by 1–2°C during peak price windows.
4. Real-time consumption and cost are shown on the **in-home display or mobile app**, giving the consumer direct visibility.
5. Aggregated HEMS data from thousands of homes gives the utility the **demand flexibility** to flatten the community load curve.

**Mathematical impact on PAR:**

If before HEMS: $P_{\text{peak}} = 5\text{ kW}$, $P_{\text{avg}} = 2\text{ kW}$

$$\text{PAR}_{\text{before}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$

After HEMS shifts 1.5 kW of load from peak to off-peak: $P_{\text{new,peak}} = 3.5\text{ kW}$

$$\text{PAR}_{\text{after}} = \frac{3.5}{2} = 1.75$$

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{2.5 - 1.75}{2.5} \times 100 = 30\%\text{ reduction in PAR}$$

> **Conclusion:** HEMS reduces the **infrastructure stress** on the grid, defers costly capacity additions, and **lowers consumer electricity bills** — all enabled by IoT data exchange.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the role of IoT in integrating renewable energy sources into the smart grid. Explain the concept of Distributed Energy Resources (DER) and the challenges involved.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Distributed Energy Resources (DERs)** are smaller-scale power generation or storage units (typically **less than 10 MW**) located **close to the point of consumption**. They include:

- **Solar PV** (rooftop and ground-mounted)
- **Wind turbines** (small and medium)
- **Micro-hydro**
- **Biomass / Biogas plants**
- **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)**
- **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** capable EVs

**Role of IoT in Renewable Integration:**

| IoT Function | Specific Application |
|--------------|----------------------|
| **Real-time monitoring** | Irradiance sensors, anemometers, panel temperature |
| **Forecasting** | ML-based solar/wind prediction using weather + sensor data |
| **Inverter control** | Remote setpoint for reactive power, Volt-VAR, Volt-Watt |
| **Storage coordination** | IoT-based BMS for BESS charge/discharge optimization |
| **Bidirectional metering** | Net-metering accuracy for prosumers |
| **Grid code compliance** | Auto-curtailment during frequency/voltage excursions |

**Major Challenges:**

1. **Intermittency and variability** — solar drops with clouds, wind is unpredictable. Requires forecasting + storage.
2. **Reverse power flow** — distribution feeders designed for unidirectional flow suffer voltage rise, protection mis-coordination.
3. **Power quality issues** — harmonics from inverters; voltage flicker from cloud transients.
4. **Cybersecurity** — millions of distributed endpoints vastly expand the **attack surface**.
5. **Standards fragmentation** — IEC 61727, IEEE 1547, IS 16221 — protocol mismatches.
6. **Economic and regulatory** — net-metering policy, tariff design, grid code enforcement.

> **Conclusion:** IoT is **not optional** for renewable integration; it is the **enabling technology** that turns a chaotic, distributed energy source into a **dispatchable, predictable, grid-friendly** resource.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Demand Response (DR). Differentiate between **Time-of-Use (ToU)** and **Real-Time Pricing (RTP)** strategies with suitable examples.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Demand Response (DR)** is a set of programs and mechanisms that **incentivize electricity consumers to voluntarily change their consumption patterns** in response to **price signals or reliability emergencies**.

**Types of DR Programs:**

- **Price-based DR:** ToU, RTP, Critical Peak Pricing (CPP)
- **Incentive-based DR:** Direct Load Control (DLC), Interruptible Load, Emergency DR, Demand Bidding

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Time-of-Use (ToU) | Real-Time Pricing (RTP) |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Price update frequency | **Pre-defined blocks** (e.g., 3 slots/day) | **Hourly or 15-min** intervals |
| Customer awareness needed | Moderate | **High** (must track every hour) |
| Implementation complexity | **Low** | **High** (requires AMI + smart meter) |
| Risk to consumer | **Low** (predictable) | **High** (volatile) |
| Suited for | Residential, small commercial | Large industrial, commercial |
| Example (India) | Maharashtra ToU: 0600–0900 ₹7, 0900–1800 ₹9, 1800–2200 ₹11, 2200–0600 ₹5/kWh | Industrial RTP: ₹/kWh changes every 15 min based on IEX market |
| Example (Global) | Ontario, CA ToU | ERCOT (Texas) RTP for large users |

**Example Calculation:**

A factory consumes **1000 kWh/day**. Under flat tariff (₹8/kWh):

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{flat}} = 1000 \times 8 = \text{₹}\,8{,}000$$

Under ToU where **60% of load is shifted** from peak (₹11) to off-peak (₹5):

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{ToU}} = (400 \times 11) + (600 \times 5) = 4400 + 3000 = \text{₹}\,7{,}400$$

$$\text{Savings} = 8000 - 7400 = \text{₹}\,600\text{/day} = \text{₹}\,2{,}19{,}000\text{/year}$$

> **Conclusion:** IoT-enabled AMI is the **prerequisite** for both ToU and RTP, because it is the only economically viable way to **measure**, **communicate**, and **bill** consumption at sub-hourly granularity.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Smart Grid" with "IoT":** The smart grid is the **infrastructure**; IoT is one of the **enabling technologies** (along with AI, big data, advanced controls). Examiners mark you down if you treat them as synonyms.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark question on "Smart Grid architecture" **always** expects a labelled block diagram. A textual answer without it caps you at ~4–5 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always write **kW, kWh, kVA, kg CO₂/kWh**. Marks are explicitly allocated to correct units in derivations.
> 4. **No numerical example:** A PAR or demand response question without a numerical illustration loses at least 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Confusing DR with Energy Efficiency:** **DR = shifting load in time**; **EE = reducing total consumption**. They are different concepts.
> 6. **Missing two-way flow:** Any answer on smart grid that doesn't mention **bidirectional** power and information flow is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 1 — Energy Management & Smart Grid:**

- ✅ **Smart Grid** = Cyber-physical energy network with bidirectional power + information flow.
- ✅ **Four canonical IoT domains** = Smart Home, Smart City, Smart Transportation, Smart Energy (focus here).
- ✅ **AMI** = Smart Meter + Communication + HES + MDMS + Customer Gateway.
- ✅ **Smart meter accuracy classes** = 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 (IEC 62053-22/23) — memorize the formula.
- ✅ **PAR formula:** $\text{PAR} = P_{\text{peak}} / P_{\text{avg}}$ — always $\geq 1$.
- ✅ **DR types:** ToU (pre-defined) vs RTP (hourly/15-min) — IoT is mandatory for both.
- ✅ **DERs** = small-scale, distributed, IoT-monitored generation/storage (solar, wind, BESS, V2G).
- ✅ **PMUs** sample at 30–60 Hz vs legacy SCADA at 0.25–0.5 Hz — 100× faster.
- ✅ **FLISR** = Fault Location, Isolation, Service Restoration in seconds (vs hours manually).
- ✅ **HEMS** shifts deferrable loads (water heater, EV, washer) from peak to off-peak.
- ✅ **Reliability KPIs:** SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI — all reduce significantly with smart grid IoT.
- ✅ **India context:** Saubhagya scheme, Smart Meter National Programme (SMNP), 250M+ meter target.
- ✅ **Cyber-physical risk** of smart grid IoT is **real** — must include security (encryption, PKI, secure boot).
- ✅ **Communication protocols** to mention: DNP3, IEC 61850, Modbus, MQTT, CoAP, LoRaWAN.
- ✅ **Always include a labelled diagram** in any 7-mark architecture question.
- ✅ **Always include a numerical example** in any DR / PAR / energy calculation question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT for Home
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT for Home

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoT for Home**, commonly referred to as the **Smart Home** or **Connected Home** paradigm, is the deployment of an Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem within a domestic residential environment to enable intelligent monitoring, control, and automation of household appliances, environmental conditions, security systems, and energy management infrastructure. It integrates physical sensing devices, wireless communication protocols, cloud-edge computing platforms, and actuator mechanisms to transform a passive dwelling into a context-aware, responsive, and user-centric habitat.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, IoT for Home is classified as a domain-specific application of Consumer IoT, emphasizing interoperability, low-power communication, and human-machine interface design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Domain-Specific IoTs — Home, City, Environment, Energy, Retail, Logistics, Health, Industry. Home IoT is the foundational use-case that demonstrates the complete Perception–Network–Application stack.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an ordinary house as a **body without a nervous system**. The lights, fan, door, and refrigerator are independent organs — they work but cannot "talk" to each other or to you when you are away. Now, imagine embedding a **digital nervous system** throughout the house:

- **Nerves** → Sensors (temperature, motion, gas, light)
- **Spinal Cord** → Local Gateway / Hub (Raspberry Pi, ESP32, Smart Speaker)
- **Brain** → Cloud Server / AI Engine
- **Muscles** → Actuators (smart switches, motors, valves)
- **Voice/Senses** → Mobile App / Voice Assistant

When a sensor (nerve) detects something, the hub (spinal cord) processes it and commands an actuator (muscle) — *all without the user lifting a finger*. That is the essence of **IoT for Home**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics in Smart Home IoT:**
> - **Wi-Fi** Range: ~**50 m (indoor)**
> - **Zigbee** Power: ~**30 mA peak**, Mesh-capable
> - **MQTT** Default Port: **1883** (TCP)
> - **Cloud Latency Target:** < **500 ms** for control commands

### 1.3 Objectives of Smart Home IoT

1. **Automation** — Rule-based and AI-driven task execution.
2. **Remote Monitoring** — Real-time telemetry from anywhere.
3. **Energy Efficiency** — Demand-response load balancing.
4. **Safety & Security** — Intrusion, fire, and gas-leak detection.
5. **Comfort & Convenience** — Personalized environmental settings.
6. **Health Assistance** — Elderly fall detection, medication reminders.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of IoT for Home

The smart home ecosystem is structured into **five canonical layers** as defined in the KTU OEERT614 syllabus:

**Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer**
- Physical devices that capture data: **DHT22** (temperature/humidity), **PIR** (motion), **MQ-2** (gas), **LDR** (light), **current sensors**.
- Smart meters monitor household energy at the appliance level.

**Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer**
- Short-range: **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**, **Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4)**, **Z-Wave**.
- Long-range backhaul: **MQTT over TCP/IP**, **CoAP over UDP**, **HTTP/REST**.

**Layer 3 — Edge / Gateway Layer**
- Local processors: **ESP32, ESP8266, Raspberry Pi, Arduino**.
- Performs protocol translation (e.g., Zigbee → MQTT), data aggregation, and local rule execution (e.g., "if temp > 30 °C, switch ON AC").

**Layer 4 — Cloud / Application Layer**
- **AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Blynk, ThingSpeak**.
- Data storage, analytics dashboards, ML inference, and firmware OTA updates.

**Layer 5 — Interface / User Layer**
- **Mobile apps, Voice assistants (Alexa, Google Home), Web dashboards, HMI panels**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Flow

- **Step 1: Sensing** — A PIR motion sensor detects human presence in the living room.
- **Step 2: Signal Conditioning** — The microcontroller converts the analog/digital signal into a structured JSON payload.
- **Step 3: Wireless Transmission** — The gateway publishes the payload to a **MQTT broker** (e.g., Mosquitto) over Wi-Fi.
- **Step 4: Cloud Ingestion** — The MQTT broker routes the message to the cloud via **topic-based subscription** (e.g., `home/livingroom/motion`).
- **Step 5: Rule Engine Execution** — A cloud rule (or local Node-RED flow) checks the time of day and lighting state.
- **Step 6: Actuation** — A command is sent back through the broker to a smart relay, switching ON the lamp.
- **Step 7: Feedback & Logging** — The state change is logged and pushed to the user's mobile app via push notification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MQTT for Home IoT?** It is a **lightweight, publish-subscribe protocol** designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks — perfect for home Wi-Fi where bandwidth is shared across many devices.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept / Parameter** | **Formula or Standard Value** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB | Sensor data quality assessment |
| Path Loss (Log-Distance) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | dB | Indoor RF coverage planning |
| Data Rate (Shannon) | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | Max throughput of home Wi-Fi channel |
| Energy of a Packet | $E_{\text{tx}} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{tx}}$ | Joules | Battery life estimation |
| Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling for sensors |
| MQTT QoS Levels | 0, 1, 2 | — | Delivery guarantees |
| Zigbee Channel Count | 16 (2.4 GHz ISM) | channels | Mesh network deployment |
| Latency Budget | $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{tx}} + L_{\text{prop}} + L_{\text{proc}} + L_{\text{queue}}$ | ms | End-to-end responsiveness |

> [!NOTE]
> Use **\vert** or **\mid** in written exam answers to avoid markdown table corruption, e.g., $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(P_{\text{signal}} \big/ P_{\text{noise}}\right)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart home IoT engineering is foundational to:
- **Energy sector** — Demand-side management in smart grids.
- **Healthcare** — Ambient Assisted Living (AAL) for the elderly.
- **Insurance** — Usage-based premiums via telemetry.
- **Civil Engineering** — Smart building HVAC and structural health monitoring.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Communication Protocol Selection

For a 2-BHK apartment (Area ≈ **90 m²**, 6 rooms), the engineer must select an appropriate protocol. Let us derive the **energy cost per transmitted byte** and compare.

**Given Constants (from IEEE 802.15.4 / Wi-Fi datasheets):**

- Zigbee: $V = 3.3$ V, $I_{\text{tx}} = 30$ mA, $t_{\text{tx per byte}} = 32$ µs.
- Wi-Fi (ESP8266): $V = 3.3$ V, $I_{\text{tx}} = 170$ mA, $t_{\text{tx per byte}} = 8$ µs.

**Step 1 — Energy per Byte for Zigbee**

$$
E_{\text{Zigbee}} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{tx}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{Zigbee}} = 3.3 \;\text{V} \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{A} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s}
$$

$$
E_{\text{Zigbee}} = 3.3 \times 30 \times 32 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{Zigbee}} = 3168 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{J} = 3.168 \; \mu\text{J/byte}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy per Byte for Wi-Fi**

$$
E_{\text{WiFi}} = 3.3 \;\text{V} \times 170 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{A} \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s}
$$

$$
E_{\text{WiFi}} = 3.3 \times 170 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{WiFi}} = 4488 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{J} = 4.488 \; \mu\text{J/byte}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparative Ratio**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_{\text{Zigbee}}}{E_{\text{WiFi}}} = \frac{3.168}{4.488} \approx 0.706
$$

**Interpretation:** Zigbee consumes **~70.6 %** of Wi-Fi's energy per byte — making it the preferred choice for **battery-powered** end nodes (e.g., door sensors), while Wi-Fi dominates where higher bandwidth and direct cloud access are required (e.g., cameras).

### 3.2 Derivation — Path Loss for Indoor Smart Home

**Problem:** Estimate the path loss at 10 m from a Wi-Fi AP in a typical home. Use $PL(d_0) = 40$ dB at $d_0 = 1$ m, path loss exponent $n = 3$ (two walls + floor attenuation).

**Step 1 — Apply Log-Distance Model**

$$
PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$
PL(10) = 40 + 10 \times 3 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Logarithm**

$$
PL(10) = 40 + 30 \times \log_{10}(10) = 40 + 30 \times 1
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result**

$$
PL(10) = 70 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Engineering Verdict:** If AP transmit power is **20 dBm** and the receiver sensitivity is **-75 dBm**, link margin is $20 - 70 - (-75) = 25$ dB → **Reliable link**.

### 3.3 Energy Savings Calculation — Smart Lighting

A household has **10 incandescent bulbs (60 W each)**, used **5 hours/day**. Replacing with **9 W smart LEDs** with occupancy-based switching (saving 25 % additional energy).

**Step 1 — Daily Energy (Incandescent)**

$$
E_{\text{old}} = 10 \times 60 \;\text{W} \times 5 \;\text{h} = 3000 \;\text{Wh} = 3.0 \;\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily Energy (LED, no automation)**

$$
E_{\text{LED}} = 10 \times 9 \times 5 = 450 \;\text{Wh} = 0.45 \;\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply 25 % Smart Switching Saving**

$$
E_{\text{final}} = 0.45 \times (1 - 0.25) = 0.3375 \;\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily Savings**

$$
S = 3.0 - 0.3375 = 2.6625 \;\text{kWh/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Savings (₹8 per kWh)**

$$
S_{\text{annual}} = 2.6625 \times 365 \times 8 = \text{₹}\;7774.5
$$

This explains the **measurable economic utility** of IoT-driven home energy management.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of IoT for Home

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PER["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        S1["TEMP SENSOR DHT22"]
        S2["MOTION SENSOR PIR"]
        S3["GAS SENSOR MQ2"]
        S4["LDR LIGHT SENSOR"]
        S5["SMART METER"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE GATEWAY LAYER"]
        GW["ESP32 RASPBERRY PI GATEWAY"]
        RT["NODE RED LOCAL RULES"]
    end

    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"]
        W1["WIFI 80211"]
        W2["ZIGBEE 802154"]
        W3["BLE 50"]
        W4["MQTT BROKER MOSQUITTO"]
    end

    subgraph CLD["CLOUD APPLICATION LAYER"]
        C1["AWS IOT CORE"]
        C2["DATABASE TIMESTREAM"]
        C3["ML ANALYTICS ENGINE"]
        C4["DASHBOARD GRAFANA"]
    end

    subgraph UI["USER INTERFACE LAYER"]

    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        A1["SMART RELAY LAMP"]
        A2["SMART FAN MOTOR"]

        A4["DOOR LOCK SERVO"]
        A5["HVAC THERMOSTAT"]
    end

    S1 --> GW
    S2 --> GW
    S3 --> GW
    S4 --> GW

    GW --> W2
    GW --> W3

    W3 --> W4

    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C4

    C4 --> U2
    C4 --> U3

    U2 --> C1

    C1 --> A3
    C1 --> A4
    C1 --> A5
```
