```mermaid
    RT --> A2
    RT --> A3
    RT --> A4
    RT --> A5
```

### 4.2 Control Flow Diagram — Motion-Activated Lighting Use Case

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["START SYSTEM BOOT"] --> P2["INIT SENSORS AND MQTT"]
    P2 --> P3["PIR SENSE MOTION"]
    P3 --> P4{"MOTION DETECTED"}
    P4 -->|NO| P5["WAIT 5 SECONDS"]
    P5 --> P3
    P4 -->|YES| P6["READ LDR VALUE"]
    P6 --> P7{"AMBIENT LIGHT LESS THAN 200 LUX"}
    P7 -->|NO| P5
    P7 -->|YES| P8["PUBLISH MQTT TOPIC HOME LIVINGROOM LAMP ON"]
    P8 --> P9["SMART RELAY SWITCHES ON LAMP"]
    P9 --> P10["WAIT 60 SECONDS NO MOTION"]
    P10 --> P11["PUBLISH MQTT TOPIC HOME LIVINGROOM LAMP OFF"]
    P11 --> P12["LAMP OFF RETURN TO LOOP"]
    P12 --> P3
```

### 4.3 Data Flow — MQTT Topic Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1["HOME LIVINGROOM TEMP"] --> BROKER["MQTT BROKER PORT 1883"]
    T2["HOME LIVINGROOM MOTION"] --> BROKER
    T3["HOME KITCHEN GAS"] --> BROKER
    T4["HOME BEDROOM LAMP"] --> BROKER
    T5["HOME ENERGY TOTAL"] --> BROKER
    BROKER --> SUB1["MOBILE APP SUBSCRIBER"]
    BROKER --> SUB2["VOICE ASSISTANT SUBSCRIBER"]
    BROKER --> SUB3["ACTUATOR CONTROLLER SUBSCRIBER"]
    BROKER --> SUB4["CLOUD DATABASE SUBSCRIBER"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Q1.** Define IoT for Home. List any four objectives of a smart home system.

**Model Answer:**
IoT for Home, or Smart Home, refers to the integration of Internet-connected sensors, actuators, gateways, and cloud services within a residential environment to enable automated monitoring and control of household functions.
**Four objectives:**
1. Automation of daily tasks (lighting, climate).
2. Remote monitoring via mobile/web apps.
3. Energy efficiency through intelligent switching.
4. Enhanced safety via intrusion, fire, and gas detection.

*(Self-explanatory; no diagram required.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Q2.** Compare Zigbee and Wi-Fi as communication protocols for a smart home. Mention any three parameters.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Zigbee** | **Wi-Fi** |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | IEEE 802.15.4 | IEEE 802.11 |
| Range | ~10–30 m indoor | ~50 m indoor |
| Power | Low (~30 mA) | High (~170 mA) |
| Topology | Mesh | Star |
| Data Rate | 250 kbps | Up to 600 Mbps |
| Best Use | Battery sensors | Cameras, hubs |

**Conclusion:** Zigbee suits low-power sensors; Wi-Fi suits bandwidth-heavy nodes.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer architecture of an IoT-based smart home system**. Describe the function of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five-layer architecture of an IoT smart home is:

1. **Perception Layer:** Contains physical sensors — DHT22 (temp/humidity), PIR (motion), MQ-2 (gas), LDR (light), smart meters. They convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.
2. **Network Layer:** Handles data transmission using **Wi-Fi, Zigbee, BLE, LoRa**. Uses protocols like **MQTT (port 1883)** and **CoAP (port 5683)** for IP-based messaging.
3. **Edge/Gateway Layer:** Devices like **ESP32, Raspberry Pi** perform local processing, protocol translation, and execute local rules (e.g., Node-RED).
4. **Cloud/Application Layer:** Hosts data storage (InfluxDB, AWS Timestream), analytics, ML inference, dashboards, and OTA firmware management.
5. **User Interface Layer:** Provides interaction via mobile apps (Blynk), voice assistants (Alexa, Google Home), and web dashboards.

The block diagram (as in Section 4.1) shows data flow: Sensors → Gateway → Network → Cloud → UI → Actuation feedback.

**(b)** Design a **smart energy meter** for a home. Show the hardware block diagram, list the components, and explain the working. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Current Sensor ACS712] → [Microcontroller ESP32] → [Wi-Fi Module] → [Cloud Server] → [Mobile App]
            ↓                       ↓
       [Voltage Divider]      [LCD Display]
```

**Components:**
- ACS712 Current Sensor (±5 A / ±20 A variant).
- ZMPT101B Voltage Sensor.
- ESP32 microcontroller (Wi-Fi + BLE + dual-core).
- 16×2 I2C LCD.
- Relay module for load control.
- Blynk/ThingSpeak cloud platform.

**Working Principle:**
1. The current sensor outputs an analog voltage proportional to line current.
2. The voltage sensor measures AC mains (stepped down to 0–5 V).
3. ESP32 samples both at >**2 kHz** (satisfying Nyquist for 50 Hz mains).
4. Compute **Real Power** = $V_{\text{rms}} \times I_{\text{rms}} \times \cos\phi$.
5. Energy (kWh) is integrated over time: $E = \int P(t) \, dt$.
6. Data published to cloud via **MQTT topic `home/energy/total`**.
7. Mobile app displays real-time and historical consumption; user can set alerts.

**Engineering Utility:** Identifies power-hungry appliances, enables **time-of-use billing**, and supports demand-response programs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **MQTT protocol** with a neat diagram. Why is it preferred over HTTP for home IoT applications? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight **publish-subscribe** messaging protocol standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**.

**Architecture:**

```
[Publisher Sensor Node] → [MQTT Broker Mosquitto] → [Subscriber Mobile App]
                                       ↓
                              [Subscriber Cloud DB]
```

- **Topics** are hierarchical, e.g., `home/livingroom/temp`.
- **QoS Levels:** 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once).
- **Default Port:** **1883 (TCP)**, **8883 (TLS)**.

**Why MQTT over HTTP for Home IoT?**

| **Parameter** | **MQTT** | **HTTP** |
|---|---|---|
| Header Size | 2 bytes | Hundreds of bytes |
| Connection | Persistent | Per-request |
| Power | Low | High |
| Push Support | Native (Pub/Sub) | Requires polling |
| Suitable for | Constrained devices | Web browsers |

**Conclusion:** MQTT's small footprint, persistent session, and event-driven push make it ideal for resource-constrained smart home sensors.

**(b)** A smart home gateway transmits a **2 KB JSON payload** every 30 seconds over Wi-Fi. ESP32 draws **170 mA at 3.3 V** during transmission, taking **2 ms per KB**. Calculate:
(i) Energy per transmission.
(ii) Daily energy consumed.
(iii) Battery life if powered by a **2000 mAh, 3.7 V Li-Po battery**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Payload = 2 KB, $t_{\text{tx}} = 2 \;\text{ms/KB}$ → $t_{\text{tx}} = 4 \;\text{ms}$ per transmission.
- $V = 3.3$ V, $I = 170$ mA = 0.17 A.
- Frequency = every 30 s → $2880$ transmissions/day.

**(i) Energy per Transmission:**

$$
E_{\text{tx}} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{tx}} = 3.3 \times 0.17 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
E_{\text{tx}} = 2.244 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{J} = 2.244 \;\text{mJ}
$$

**(ii) Daily Energy:**

$$
E_{\text{daily}} = 2.244 \times 10^{-3} \times 2880 = 6.46272 \;\text{J}
$$

**(iii) Battery Life:**

Battery energy:

$$
E_{\text{bat}} = 3.7 \;\text{V} \times 2000 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{Ah} \times 3600 \;\text{s/h}
$$

$$
E_{\text{bat}} = 3.7 \times 2 \times 3600 = 26640 \;\text{J}
$$

Battery life (assuming ONLY Wi-Fi consumption):

$$
\text{Days} = \frac{26640}{6.46272} \approx 4121 \;\text{days} \approx 11.3 \;\text{years}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert mA → A and ms → s, leading to off-by-1000× errors. Always show unit conversions.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using HTTP instead of MQTT in the question stem; **MQTT is the KTU-suggested protocol** for IoT applications — using HTTP in a smart home context loses marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the block diagram in (a) — KTU awards **2 marks** for a neat, labeled diagram. Always draw even a simple one.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In (b), not considering **idle/sleep current** of ESP32 (~**10 µA**), which dominates long-term battery drain. For full credit, mention sleep-mode optimization.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Smart Home = Perception + Network + Edge + Cloud + UI** — five-layer architecture is **mandatory** for KTU 14-mark answers.
- **MQTT** (Port **1883**) is the **default IoT protocol** for home applications — know its **Pub/Sub model, topics, QoS levels**.
- **Wi-Fi** = high bandwidth, high power; **Zigbee** = low power, mesh, ideal for battery sensors.
- **Path Loss Model:** $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$ — used for indoor coverage.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ — apply when sampling AC mains (≥ 2 kHz for 50 Hz).
- **Energy of transmission:** $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ — always show **unit conversions** explicitly.
- **Standard Sensors:** DHT22 (temp), PIR (motion), MQ-2 (gas), LDR (light), ACS712 (current).
- **Cloud Platforms:** AWS IoT Core, Blynk, ThingSpeak, Azure IoT Hub — mention at least one in answers.
- **Security:** Always mention **TLS/SSL** for MQTT (port **8883**) and **device authentication** as a 2-mark differentiator.
- **Use $\vert$ or \mid in LaTeX** within table cells; never use `|` directly.
- **Diagrams** carry **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions — never skip them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Smart City** is an urban ecosystem that leverages **Internet of Things (IoT)** technologies, information and communication infrastructure, and data analytics to deliver sustainable, efficient, and citizen-centric services. According to the **ISO 37120 (Sustainable Cities and Communities)** standard framework adopted in modern KTU 2024 syllabus, a smart city integrates physical infrastructure, digital connectivity, and human capital to optimize urban operations including transportation, energy, water, waste, healthcare, and governance.

In KTU 2024 OEERT614 terminology, "Cities" as an IoT application domain refers to the deployment of interconnected sensors, actuators, edge/fog devices, and cloud platforms that collectively monitor, analyze, and manage urban systems in real-time.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The City's Nervous System"**
>
> Think of a smart city as a **human body**. Just as your nervous system has:
> - **Sensors** (nerves in skin) → detect temperature, pressure, pain
> - **Brain (Cloud/Data Center)** → processes and decides
> - **Muscles (Actuators)** → take action (move hand away from fire)
> - **Communication (Spinal Cord)** → connects sensors to brain
>
> A smart city does the same: traffic sensors detect congestion, sends data to cloud, cloud decides green wave timing, signals change → traffic flows smoothly!

### Key Smart City Metrics & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Standards & Constants for KTU 2024:**
> - **ISO 37120** — Sustainable city indicators (**17** core themes)
> - **Population Threshold for "City"** as per Census of India: **≥ 5,000** population, **≥ 4,000 persons/km²** density, **≥ 75%** male non-agricultural workforce
> - **UN Projection**: **68%** of world population will live in urban areas by **2050**
> - **Smart City India Mission (2015)**: **100 cities** selected, ₹**48,000 crore** central funding
> - **Average data generated per smart city/day**: ≈ **2.5 zettabytes** (estimate by IDC)
> - **IoT device density target**: **~1 million sensors/km²** in mature smart cities

### Core Definition Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *"A Smart City is an urban area that uses different types of electronic IoT sensors to collect data and use insights from that data to manage assets, resources, and services efficiently; thereby improving operations across the city including but not limited to transportation, water distribution, waste management, law enforcement, healthcare, schools, libraries, and other community services."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of a Smart City (Six 'S' Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus and **Smart Cities Mission (India)** define a smart city through **six interlinked pillars**:

| # | Pillar | IoT Function | Example |
|---|--------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | **Smart Economy** | Business analytics, IoT marketplaces | Startup incubation platforms |
| 2 | **Smart Mobility** | GPS, RFID, ANPR cameras, V2X | Adaptive traffic signals |
| 3 | **Smart Environment** | Air/water quality sensors, weather stations | AQI monitoring |
| 4 | **Smart People** | EdTech platforms, citizen engagement apps | Digital literacy programs |
| 5 | **Smart Living** | Healthcare IoT, smart homes, safety alerts | Remote patient monitoring |
| 6 | **Smart Governance** | e-Governance portals, transparent data flow | Online municipal services |

### 2.2 Layered IoT Architecture for Smart Cities

The operational architecture of a smart city IoT deployment follows the standard **5-layer IoT Reference Model** (modified per **oneM2M / ITU-T Y.2060**):

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors & Actuators)**
   - Physical devices: temperature, humidity, gas, PM2.5, vibration, cameras, RFID tags
   - Converts physical phenomena → electrical signals → digital data
   - *Example: Smart streetlights with LDR + motion + air quality sensors*

2. **Network Layer (Communication)**
   - Wired: Fiber-optic backbones (10–100 Gbps), Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)
   - Wireless: **LoRaWAN** (long range, low power), **NB-IoT** (cellular), **ZigBee** (802.15.4), **Wi-Fi HaLow** (802.11ah), **5G mMTC**
   - Selection criteria: bandwidth, range, power, latency

3. **Edge / Fog Layer (Pre-processing)**
   - Local computation at gateways, reduces cloud load
   - Latency-sensitive tasks: traffic signal control, emergency dispatch
   - Typical edge latency: **< 10 ms**

4. **Processing Layer (Cloud / Data Center)**
   - Big data analytics, ML/DL model inference
   - Long-term storage, dashboards
   - Tools: Apache Kafka, Hadoop, Spark, TensorFlow, AWS IoT Core

5. **Application Layer (End-User Services)**
   - Mobile/web apps, control rooms, public displays
   - Domain-specific: traffic, healthcare, energy

### 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet for KTU Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations & Metrics (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE):**

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units | Notes |
|---------|------------------|-------|-------|
| Population Density | $$\rho = \frac{N}{A}$$ | persons/km² | $N$ = population, $A$ = area in km² |
| Smart City Index Score (SCI) | $$\text{SCI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$$ | dimensionless | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = sub-score, $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| Sensor Data Rate | $$R_b = n \cdot f_s \cdot b$$ | bits/s (bps) | $n$ = sensors, $f_s$ = sampling freq, $b$ = bits/sample |
| Coverage Area (Wireless) | $$A_{cov} = \pi R^2$$ | km² | For a single LoRa/5G tower, $R$ = radius |
| Network Latency (end-to-end) | $$T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$$ | seconds | Sum of all delay components |
| Data Volume per City/day | $$V = R_b \times 86400$$ | bits/day | Convert to TB/ZB using $8$ and $10^{12}$ |
| Energy Efficiency (Smart Grid) | $$\eta = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{total}} \times 100\%$$ | % | KPI for smart energy pillar |
| Cost-Benefit Ratio | $$\text{BCR} = \frac{\sum \text{Benefits}_{PV}}{\sum \text{Costs}_{PV}}$$ | dimensionless | Present value; if $\text{BCR} > 1$, project viable |
| AQI Computation (Indian CPCB) | $$\text{AQI} = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} (C - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$ | 0–500 scale | Sub-index formula for pollutants |
| **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ | bps | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear directly in KTU ESE problems on smart city planning, sensor network design, and economic feasibility. The **Shannon Capacity** formula is especially important for 5G/LoRa network dimensioning questions.

### 2.4 Smart City Stakeholders

- **Citizens** — primary beneficiaries
- **Municipal Corporation** — service delivery
- **Technology Providers** — Cisco, IBM, Siemens, TCS
- **Utility Companies** — power, water
- **Policy Makers** — government regulators
- **Researchers & Academia** — KTU, IITs, NITs

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1: Data Volume Estimation for Smart City

**Problem (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**
A smart city deploys **10,000 air quality sensors**, each sampling **PM2.5, PM10, CO, NO₂, SO₂, O₃** (i.e., **6 pollutants**) at a rate of **once per minute**, with each sample encoded in **16 bits**. Estimate the **total data generated per day**. Also calculate the **daily storage** in **TB** assuming only **30%** is retained after preprocessing.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given parameters:**

- Number of sensors: $n = 10{,}000$
- Pollutants per sensor: $6$
- Sampling frequency: $f_s = 1 \text{ sample/min}$
- Bits per sample: $b = 16 \text{ bits}$

**Step 2 — Calculate per-sensor per-second bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per sensor rate} &= f_s \times b \times (\text{pollutants}) \\
&= \frac{1}{60} \text{ samples/s} \times 16 \text{ bits} \times 6 \\
&= \frac{96}{60} \text{ bits/s} \\
&= 1.6 \text{ bits/s per sensor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total aggregate rate for all sensors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= n \times \text{per-sensor rate} \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 1.6 \\
&= 16{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= 1.6 \times 10^4 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bits per day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{raw} &= R_b \times 86400 \text{ s/day} \\
&= 1.6 \times 10^4 \times 8.64 \times 10^4 \\
&= 1.3824 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert bits to bytes (divide by 8):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{raw} &= \frac{1.3824 \times 10^9}{8} \\
&= 1.728 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes/day} \\
&= 172.8 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply 30% retention:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{retained} &= 0.30 \times 172.8 \text{ MB} \\
&= 51.84 \text{ MB/day} \\
&= 5.184 \times 10^{-5} \text{ TB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The city generates **≈ 172.8 MB/day raw**, of which **≈ 51.84 MB/day (≈ 0.052 GB)** is stored long-term per day. This is why **edge computing** is critical — sending every bit to cloud is inefficient.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2: AQI Calculation

**Problem:** A monitoring station reports PM2.5 concentration of **180 μg/m³**. The CPCB breakpoints for PM2.5 (24-hr) are: $C_{Lo} = 90$, $C_{Hi} = 180$, $I_{Lo} = 101$, $I_{Hi} = 200$. Compute the AQI.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AQI} &= \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} \cdot (C - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo} \\
&= \frac{200 - 101}{180 - 90} \cdot (180 - 90) + 101 \\
&= \frac{99}{90} \cdot 90 + 101 \\
&= 99 + 101 \\
&= 200
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** AQI = **200** → falls in the **"Poor"** category (CPCB bands: 0–50 Good, 51–100 Satisfactory, 101–200 Moderate, 201–300 Poor, 301–400 Very Poor, 401–500 Severe).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Feature | Traditional City | Smart City |
|---------|------------------|------------|
| Data collection | Manual, periodic | Real-time, sensor-based |
| Decision making | Top-down, delayed | Data-driven, automated |
| Citizen feedback | Slow (paper/offline) | Mobile apps, social media |
| Resource use | Inefficient, wastage | Optimized, demand-driven |
| Infrastructure | Static, fixed | Adaptive, self-healing |
| Cost over time | High (reactive) | High initial, low operational |
| Environmental impact | High carbon footprint | Lower via monitoring |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Smart City Reference Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL1["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        A1["Air Quality Sensors"]
        A2["Traffic Cameras & Loop Detectors"]
        A3["Smart Meters Electricity Water Gas"]
        A4["Waste Bin Level Sensors"]
        A5["Streetlight LDR and Motion Sensors"]
    end

    subgraph SL2["NETWORK LAYER"]
        B1["5G and NB IoT Cellular"]
        B2["LoRaWAN Gateway Network"]
        B3["Fiber Optic Backbone"]
        B4["Wi-Fi HaLow and ZigBee Mesh"]
    end

    subgraph SL3["EDGE FOG LAYER"]
        C1["Edge Gateways Local Analytics"]
        C2["Latency Critical Controllers"]
        C3["Data Filtering and Compression"]
    end

    subgraph SL4["CLOUD PROCESSING LAYER"]
        D1["Big Data Lake Storage"]
        D2["AI and ML Analytics Engine"]
        D3["Digital Twin City Model"]
        D4["Dashboard and Visualization"]
    end

    subgraph SL5["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        E1["Smart Traffic Management"]
        E2["Smart Energy Grid"]
        E3["Citizen Mobile Apps"]
        E4["Emergency Response System"]
        E5["Municipal E Governance"]
    end

    SL1 --> SL2
    SL2 --> SL3
    SL3 --> SL4
    SL4 --> SL5

    E1 -.feedback.-> A2
    E2 -.feedback.-> A3
    E5 -.feedback.-> A4
```

### 4.2 Smart City Use-Case Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOM1["SMART MOBILITY"]
        M1["Adaptive Traffic Signal"]
        M2["Smart Parking System"]
        M3["Public Transit Tracker"]
        M4["EV Charging Network"]
    end

    subgraph DOM2["SMART ENVIRONMENT"]
        N1["Air Pollution Monitor"]
        N2["Noise Level Sensor"]
        N3["Water Quality Monitor"]
        N4["Weather Station"]
    end

    subgraph DOM3["SMART SAFETY"]
        P1["CCTV with AI Analytics"]
        P2["Disaster Early Warning"]
        P3["Fire Detection Sensors"]
        P4["Crowd Density Monitor"]
    end

    M1 --- N1
    M2 --- P1
    M3 --- N4
    P2 --- P3

    CC["CENTRAL COMMAND AND CONTROL CENTER"]

    M1 --> CC
    N1 --> CC
    P1 --> CC
    CC --> E1["Emergency Dispatch"]
    CC --> CIT["Citizens via Mobile App"]
```

### 4.3 Smart City Implementation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Stage 1 Assessment and Planning"] --> s2["Stage 2 Infrastructure Deployment"]
    s2 --> s3["Stage 3 Sensor and Device Installation"]
    s3 --> s4["Stage 4 Network and Connectivity Setup"]
    s4 --> s5["Stage 5 Platform and Analytics Integration"]
    s5 --> s6["Stage 6 Citizen Service Launch"]
    s6 --> s7["Stage 7 Continuous Monitoring and Optimization"]
    s7 -.feedback.-> s1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw and explain" the smart city architecture, label each layer with **2–3 example devices/protocols** to earn full marks. Examiners explicitly award marks for "labeled examples."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q1. Define a Smart City. List any FOUR core characteristics.** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A Smart City is an urban area that uses **IoT sensors, data analytics, and ICT infrastructure** to efficiently manage resources, infrastructure, and services, while improving the **quality of life** of its citizens.
Four core characteristics:
1. **Smart Economy** — innovation, entrepreneurship, IoT-driven jobs
2. **Smart Mobility** — intelligent transport, real-time traffic control
3. **Smart Environment** — pollution monitoring, green energy
4. **Smart Governance** — transparent e-governance and citizen participation

> [!NOTE]
> Alternative 2 pillars (Smart People, Smart Living) are equally valid.

---

**Q2. Mention any THREE challenges in implementing IoT-based smart cities.** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Data Privacy and Security** — large-scale citizen data vulnerable to cyber-attacks
2. **Interoperability** — heterogeneous devices from multiple vendors use different protocols
3. **High Initial Capital Cost** — sensor deployment, 5G infrastructure, and platform setup require massive upfront investment
*(Additional accepted: lack of skilled workforce, power consumption of sensors, regulatory hurdles)*

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the layered architecture of IoT in smart cities with a neat diagram. List at least TWO applications for each layer. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IoT architecture for smart cities is organized into **5 layers**:

1. **Perception Layer** — physical sensors/actuators
   - *Apps:* air quality sensors, smart water meters, RFID toll tags

2. **Network Layer** — wired + wireless communication
   - *Apps:* 5G mMTC for autonomous vehicles, LoRaWAN for waste bin monitoring

3. **Edge/Fog Layer** — local processing, low latency
   - *Apps:* real-time traffic signal controller, edge-based video analytics for crime detection

4. **Cloud Processing Layer** — long-term storage, AI/ML
   - *Apps:* predictive analytics for energy demand, digital twin of city

5. **Application Layer** — end-user services
   - *Apps:* citizen mobile app for complaint filing, smart parking app

> [!NOTE]
> Drawing the layered block diagram (as in Section 4.1) earns **2 marks** automatically. Labeling each layer earns the rest.

---

**(b) Discuss the role of IoT in Smart Transportation. Explain at least FOUR real-world use cases with their benefits. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Smart Transportation is one of the **highest-impact** smart city verticals. IoT enables:

1. **Adaptive Traffic Signal Control**
   - *Tech:* Camera + loop detectors at junctions → real-time density analysis → green-wave optimization
   - *Benefit:* Reduces average commute time by **20–30%**; lowers emissions

2. **Smart Parking System**
   - *Tech:* Ultrasonic/IR sensors embedded in each parking slot → cloud dashboard
   - *Benefit:* Reduces time spent searching for parking by **up to 40%**, cuts fuel wastage

3. **Public Transit Tracking (e-Governance)**
   - *Tech:* GPS + GSM in buses/trains; RFID ticketing
   - *Benefit:* Live arrival info, increased ridership, fare transparency

4. **EV (Electric Vehicle) Charging Network**
   - *Tech:* Smart chargers with OCPP protocol, mobile app booking
   - *Benefit:* Optimized energy distribution, lower grid load during peak hours

5. **Accident Detection and Emergency Response**
   - *Tech:* Accelerometer + GPS in vehicles → automatic SOS to control room
   - *Benefit:* Faster medical response, potentially saving lives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Intro (1) + 4 cases × 1.5 marks each (6) = 7.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Describe the concept of a Smart Grid. How does IoT enable efficient energy management in cities? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Smart Grid** is an electricity supply network that uses **IoT sensors, two-way communication, and automation** to monitor, predict, and manage energy generation, distribution, and consumption in real time.

**IoT's role in smart energy management:**

1. **Smart Metering (AMI — Advanced Metering Infrastructure)**
   - Real-time consumption data, dynamic pricing, theft detection
2. **Demand Response**
   - Sensors in HVAC/appliances auto-adjust during peak hours based on price signals
3. **Renewable Integration**
   - IoT weather sensors + solar/wind forecasting = stable grid with renewables
4. **Predictive Maintenance**
   - Vibration/temperature sensors on transformers detect faults before failure
5. **Outage Management**
   - Smart sensors auto-report outages, crews dispatched faster

> [!NOTE]
> Use keywords like **AMI, SCADA, DER, microgrid** for full marks.

---

**(b) What are the major security and privacy challenges in smart city IoT deployments? Suggest suitable mitigation strategies. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Key Challenges:**

| Challenge | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Data Privacy** | Citizens' location, health, behavior data is collected continuously |
| **Cyber-Attacks** | Botnets (e.g., Mirai), DDoS on city infrastructure |
| **Device Tampering** | Physical access to streetlight/camera sensors |
| **Insecure Communication** | Unencrypted sensor links can be intercepted |
| **Insider Threats** | Rogue municipal employees misusing data |
| **Lack of Standards** | Vendor-specific protocols; weak interoperability |

**Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **End-to-End Encryption** — TLS/DTLS for all IoT traffic
2. **Strong Authentication** — PKI-based digital certificates per device
3. **Blockchain** — tamper-proof, decentralized data logging
4. **Edge AI Anomaly Detection** — detect attacks at gateway level
5. **Regular Firmware Updates** — patch vulnerabilities
6. **Compliance with GDPR / India's DPDP Act 2023** — lawful data processing

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **No diagram or poorly labeled diagram** → -2 to -3 marks
> 2. **Listing without explanation** — e.g., "5G, LoRa" without saying *why* (latency/range)
> 3. **Confusing "Smart City" with "Digital City"** — smart = sensors + analytics + automation
> 4. **Ignoring the 5-layer architecture** — most ESE answers need it
> 5. **Mixing up IS standards** — ISO 37120 ≠ 37122 (different scopes)
> 6. **Skipping numerical units** in formulas → loses easy 0.5 marks per error

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Smart City = IoT + ICT + Data Analytics for sustainable urban living
- [x] **Six Pillars:** Economy, Mobility, Environment, People, Living, Governance
- [x] **Five-Layer Architecture:** Perception → Network → Edge → Cloud → Application
- [x] **Key Indian Mission:** Smart Cities Mission launched **2015**, **100 cities**, ₹**48,000 Cr**
- [x] **Standards to remember:** ISO 37120, oneM2M, ITU-T Y.2060, IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee)
- [x] **Protocols:** 5G mMTC, NB-IoT, LoRaWAN, ZigBee, Wi-Fi HaLow
- [x] **Formulae:** $\rho = N/A$, $\text{AQI}$ sub-index, Shannon $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$
- [x] **AQI Categories (CPCB):** 0–50 Good → 401–500 Severe
- [x] **Smart Grid buzzwords:** AMI, SCADA, DER, microgrid, demand response
- [x] **Security mantra:** Encryption + PKI + Blockchain + Edge AI + Compliance
- [x] **Most-tested example:** Smart transportation (parking, traffic, transit, EV)
- [x] **DPDP Act 2023** — India's data protection law, frequently cited in privacy questions
- [x] **Draw the 5-layer diagram** in every architecture question — easy 2 marks!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environment Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environment Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoT-based Environment Monitoring** is the systematic deployment of interconnected sensing devices, communication networks, and computational platforms to continuously observe, measure, transmit, and analyse physical, chemical, and biological parameters of a natural or built environment. It is a vertical application domain of the Internet of Things where heterogeneous sensor nodes form a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** that bridges real-world environmental phenomena with cloud-scale analytics.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, environment monitoring encompasses monitoring of:

- **Air quality** (PM2.5, PM10, CO₂, NO₂, SO₂, O₃)
- **Water quality** (pH, turbidity, dissolved oxygen, TDS)
- **Soil parameters** (moisture, NPK, temperature)
- **Ambient conditions** (temperature, humidity, noise, light, UV index)
- **Weather/climatic variables** (wind speed, rainfall, atmospheric pressure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Environment monitoring is positioned as a flagship IoT use-case demonstrating the **perception → network → application** layered model. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to map a real-world monitoring requirement onto IoT architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **doctor monitoring a patient**:
- The patient = the environment (air, water, soil).
- The medical instruments (stethoscope, BP cuff, thermometer) = the **sensors**.
- The wires/cables carrying signals = the **communication network**.
- The doctor's brain interpreting readings = the **cloud/edge analytics**.
- The prescription/treatment = the **actuation response** (alerts, sprinklers, ventilation).

Just as a doctor cannot be physically present 24/7 but still monitors continuously through devices, IoT allows **continuous, remote, automated, and intelligent** environmental monitoring without human intervention.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (must memorise for KTU)

| Standard / Metric | Significance |
|---|---|
| **PM2.5 ≤ 60 µg/m³ (24-hr)** | National Ambient Air Quality Standard (NAAQS, CPCB India) |
| **AQI Categories: 0–500 scale** | CPCB India, 6-category bucket system |
| **pH range 6.5–8.5** | Potable water (BIS 10500:2012) |
| **LoRaWAN range: 2–15 km** | Long-range, low-power outdoor monitoring |
| **MQTT QoS levels: 0, 1, 2** | Quality of Service for sensor messaging |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is the single most tested parameter in KTU board exams for this topic. Memorise the CPCB 6-bucket AQI scale: *Good (0–50), Satisfactory (51–100), Moderately Polluted (101–200), Poor (201–300), Very Poor (301–400), Severe (401–500)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 4-Layer Functional Architecture of IoT Environment Monitoring

The architecture can be decomposed into four interacting logical layers:

**Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer**
- Comprises physical and chemical sensors (electrochemical, optical, MEMS, semiconductor).
- Performs **analog-to-digital conversion (ADC)** and local signal conditioning.
- Sensor selection criteria: **selectivity, sensitivity, range, accuracy, response time, drift**.

**Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer**
- Handles data transmission via short-range (BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi) or long-range (LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Sigfox) protocols.
- Implements **gateways** for protocol translation (e.g., ZigBee ↔ IP/MQTT).
- For KTU: emphasise **MQTT** (publish/subscribe, lightweight, TCP-based) and **CoAP** (RESTful, UDP-based, low-power).

**Layer 3 — Processing / Edge–Cloud Layer**
- Performs **data aggregation, filtering, anomaly detection, ML-based prediction**.
- Fog/Edge computing reduces latency; Cloud provides scalability and storage.

**Layer 4 — Application / Presentation Layer**
- Dashboards, mobile apps, alert systems, actuator triggers (e.g., traffic diversion during AQI > 300).
- Visualisation tools: Grafana, Kibana, ThingSpeak, custom React/Angular dashboards.

### 2.2 Why Each Layer Matters (the "Why")

- **Why perception?** Sensors are the only interface with physical reality. Garbage-in → garbage-out.
- **Why network?** Sensor data is meaningless if not transmitted reliably and timely.
- **Why edge computing?** A forest fire sensor cannot wait for cloud round-trip; decisions must be sub-second.
- **Why application layer?** End-users (citizens, farmers, regulators) need actionable insights, not raw voltages.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars in formulas below use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. **Never use `|` inside a table cell.**

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AQI Sub-Index for pollutant $p$ | $I_p = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \cdot (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Linear interpolation between CPCB breakpoints |
| 2 | Overall AQI | $\text{AQI} = \max(I_{PM2.5}, I_{PM10}, I_{NO_2}, I_{SO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3})$ | Worst-sub-index rule |
| 3 | Sensor Resolution | $R = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | n-bit ADC with reference $V_{ref}$ |
| 4 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Sensor fidelity metric |
| 5 | Free-Space Path Loss | $\text{FSPL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | d in km, f in MHz |
| 6 | Link Budget | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX}$ | Wireless range feasibility |
| 7 | Battery Lifetime (years) | $T = \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{nom}}{I_{avg} \cdot V_{load}}$ | Energy budgeting for remote nodes |
| 8 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion |
| 9 | pH Definition | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}\!\left[\text{H}^+\right]$ | Acidity/alkalinity of water |
| 10 | TDS to Conductivity | $\text{TDS(ppm)} \approx 0.55 \cdot \text{EC}(\mu S/cm)$ | Approximate conversion |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

These formulas directly enable a KTU student to design and justify a real monitoring system:
- **AQI formula** → pollutant concentration dashboards for smart cities (Delhi, Bengaluru).
- **FSPL + Link Budget** → choosing LoRaWAN vs. NB-IoT for a forest deployment.
- **Battery Lifetime** → sizing solar panel for unattended sensor nodes.
- **Sampling Theorem** → selecting ADC rate for vibration/seismic monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CPCB Air Quality Index (AQI) Sub-Index

The **CPCB AQI** converts a measured pollutant concentration $C_p$ into a normalised integer between **0 and 500** using piecewise linear interpolation between predefined concentration breakpoints.

**Step 1 — Identify the applicable breakpoint range.**

For pollutant $p$, the CPCB table provides ordered pairs $(BP_{Lo}, I_{Lo})$ and $(BP_{Hi}, I_{Hi})$ such that:

$$BP_{Lo} \leq C_p \leq BP_{Hi}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear interpolation formula.**

$$I_p = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \cdot (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

**Step 3 — Round to the nearest integer** (CPCB convention).

$$I_p^{final} = \text{round}(I_p)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the overall AQI** as the maximum of all sub-indices:

$$\text{AQI} = \max_{p \in \{PM2.5, PM10, NO_2, SO_2, CO, O_3, NH_3, Pb\}} I_p$$

**Step 5 — Identify the dominant pollutant** that determined the AQI for public reporting.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Standard)

> **Problem:** PM2.5 concentration measured = **78 µg/m³**. Compute AQI sub-index using CPCB breakpoints: $(BP_{Lo}, I_{Lo}) = (60, 101)$ and $(BP_{Hi}, I_{Hi}) = (90, 200)$.

**Step 1 — Identify range.** $60 \leq 78 \leq 90$ ✓ (Moderately Polluted band)

**Step 2 — Substitute into the linear formula.**

$$I_{PM2.5} = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 60} \cdot (78 - 60) + 101$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the slope.**

$$\frac{200 - 101}{90 - 60} = \frac{99}{30} = 3.3$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by concentration offset.**

$$3.3 \cdot (78 - 60) = 3.3 \cdot 18 = 59.4$$

**Step 5 — Add lower index.**

$$I_{PM2.5} = 59.4 + 101 = 160.4$$

**Step 6 — Round.**

$$I_{PM2.5}^{final} = 160$$

> **Result:** AQI sub-index for PM2.5 = **160 (Moderately Polluted)**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Protocol Selection for Environment Monitoring

This matrix is a **favourite KTU exam table** because it tests applied understanding.

| Parameter | Wi-Fi | ZigBee | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | ~50 m | ~100 m | 2–15 km | 1–10 km |
| **Data Rate** | 54 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps | 26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL |
| **Power** | High | Low | Very Low | Medium |
| **Topology** | Star | Mesh | Star-of-stars | Star |
| **Use Case Fit** | Indoor lab | Home/building | Forest, farm | Urban metering |
| **Best for Env. Monit.?** | ❌ | ✅ (short) | ✅✅ (rural) | ✅ (urban) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** "**W**i-Fi **Z**ips **L**ocally, **L**oRa **N**etworks **L**ong." (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT — increasing outdoor range).

---

### 3.4 Sensor-to-Cloud Latency Budget (Derivation Style)

For a **forest fire detection** use-case, total system latency $T_{total}$ is:

$$T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{tx} + T_{net} + T_{cloud}$$

A typical budget:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sense} &\approx 1 \text{ s} \quad \text{(MQ-2 gas sensor warm-up + ADC)} \\
T_{proc}  &\approx 0.05 \text{ s} \quad \text{(microcontroller filtering)} \\
T_{tx}    &\approx 0.5 \text{ s} \quad \text{(LoRaWAN uplink on SF7)} \\
T_{net}   &\approx 0.1 \text{ s} \quad \text{(gateway to broker)} \\
T_{cloud} &\approx 0.3 \text{ s} \quad \text{(AWS IoT rule + alert)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$T_{total} = 1 + 0.05 + 0.5 + 0.1 + 0.3 = 1.95 \text{ s}$$

**Conclusion:** Sub-2-second end-to-end latency is achievable — well within fire-response thresholds (~30 s).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End IoT Environment Monitoring Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENVP["LAYER 1: PERCEPTION SENSORS"]
        sensA["AIR SENSOR PM2.5 PM10 CO2"]
        sensB["WATER SENSOR PH TDS TURBIDITY"]
        sensC["SOIL SENSOR MOISTURE NPK TEMP"]
        sensD["WEATHER SENSOR HUMIDITY RAIN WIND"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["LAYER 2: EDGE GATEWAY"]
        edgeNode["MCU ARDUINO OR ESP32 OR RPI"]
        adcBlock["ADC SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
        protoStack["PROTOCOL STACK MQTT OR COAP OR LORAWAN"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["LAYER 3: CLOUD PLATFORM"]
        broker["MQTT BROKER OR AWS IOT CORE"]
        storage["TIME SERIES DB INFLUXDB OR TIMESTREAM"]
        analytics["STREAM ANALYTICS ML ANOMALY DETECTION"]
    end

    subgraph APPL["LAYER 4: APPLICATION"]
        dash["WEB DASHBOARD GRAFANA"]
        mobile["MOBILE APP CITIZEN ALERTS"]
        actr["ACTUATOR ALARM SPRINKLER VALVE"]
    end

    sensA --> edgeNode
    sensB --> edgeNode
    sensC --> edgeNode
    sensD --> edgeNode
    edgeNode --> adcBlock
    adcBlock --> protoStack
    protoStack -->|WIRELESS UPLINK| broker
    broker --> storage
    storage --> analytics
    analytics --> dash
    analytics --> mobile
    analytics -->|THRESHOLD BREACH| actr
```

### 4.2 Data Flow Sequence: Sensor Reading → User Alert

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SensorNode as Sensor Node
    participant Gateway as IoT Gateway
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker
    participant Cloud as Cloud Analytics
    participant UserApp as User Dashboard
    participant Alarm as Alert Actuator

    SensorNode->>SensorNode: Read PM2.5 every 60 sec
    SensorNode->>Gateway: Publish topic env/air/pm25 value 78
    Gateway->>Broker: Forward via LoRaWAN or Wi-Fi
    Broker->>Cloud: Subscribed topic delivers payload
    Cloud->>Cloud: Compute AQI sub-index equals 160
    Cloud->>UserApp: Push AQI update to dashboard
    Cloud->>Alarm: Trigger if AQI greater than 300
    Alarm-->>UserApp: Acknowledge alert sent
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Functional Architecture (Block Diagram Fallback)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["PHYSICAL LAYER AIR WATER SOIL NOISE"] --> L2["SENSING LAYER TRANSDUCER SIGNAL CONDITIONER"]
    L2 --> L3["COMMUNICATION LAYER WIRED WIRELESS"]
    L3 --> L4["EDGE COMPUTING LAYER LOCAL INFERENCE"]
    L4 --> L5["CLOUD LAYER STORAGE PROCESSING ML"]
    L5 --> L6["APPLICATION LAYER DASHBOARD ALERT ACTUATOR"]
    L6 -.FEEDBACK CONTROL.-> L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **feedback loop** from Layer 6 back to Layer 1 is what differentiates a true **IoT system** from a mere **monitoring system**. KTU examiners reward students who identify this closed-loop nature.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1.a]**

**Q1.** Define the term **Air Quality Index (AQI)** as per CPCB. List the **six broad AQI categories** along with their numerical ranges.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a numerical scale (0–500) used by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India, to communicate ambient air pollution levels to the general public in a simple, understandable form. It is computed as the **maximum of sub-indices** computed for individual pollutants: *PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, SO₂, CO, and O₃*.
>
> **Six AQI Categories:**
>
> 1. **Good:** 0–50
> 2. **Satisfactory:** 51–100
> 3. **Moderately Polluted:** 101–200
> 4. **Poor:** 201–300
> 5. **Very Poor:** 301–400
> 6. **Severe:** 401–500
>
> *(3 marks — definition 1 mark, six categories 2 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1.b]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **MQTT** and **CoAP** protocols used in IoT environment monitoring.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (port 1883/8883) | UDP (port 5683) |
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Response (REST) |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes minimum | 4 bytes |
| **QoS Levels** | 3 (0, 1, 2) | 2 (CON/NON) |
| **Power Usage** | Moderate | Very Low |
| **Best For** | Reliable cloud telemetry | Constrained lossy networks |
| **KTU Use Case** | Smart city dashboards | Battery forest sensors |

*(3 marks — three valid differences with examples)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Q4 — 14 Marks]**

#### ⭐ Question A: 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four-layer architecture** of an IoT-based environment monitoring system. For each layer, list two real-world components/technologies used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A region reports **SO₂ concentration of 92 µg/m³**. Using CPCB breakpoints for SO₂: $(BP_{Lo}=62, I_{Lo}=101)$ and $(BP_{Hi}=108, I_{Hi}=200)$, compute the AQI sub-index. State the pollution category.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The four-layer architecture of IoT-based environment monitoring is:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors):** Converts physical/chemical phenomena into electrical signals.
   - Examples: **MQ-135 gas sensor**, **DHT22 humidity sensor**, **TSL2561 lux sensor**, **pH electrode**, **MPX pressure sensor**.

2. **Network/Communication Layer:** Transmits sensor data to gateways/cloud.
   - Examples: **Wi-Fi (ESP8266)**, **LoRaWAN (SX1276)**, **ZigBee**, **NB-IoT (Quectel BC95)**, **MQTT broker (Mosquitto)**, **CoAP server**.

3. **Processing/Edge-Cloud Layer:** Aggregates, filters, and analyses data.
   - Examples: **AWS IoT Core + Lambda**, **Azure IoT Hub**, **GCP Pub/Sub**, **InfluxDB + Grafana**, **TensorFlow Lite on Raspberry Pi**.

4. **Application/Presentation Layer:** End-user interaction and actuation.
   - Examples: **Web dashboards (React/Angular)**, **mobile apps (Flutter)**, **SMS/email alerts via Twilio**, **actuators (relays, solenoid valves, sirens)**.

*(7 marks — 4 layers × 1.5 marks ≈ 6, plus 1 mark for examples integration)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the breakpoint range.** $62 \leq 92 \leq 108$ ✓

**Step 2 — Apply the CPCB AQI formula.**

$$I_{SO_2} = \frac{200 - 101}{108 - 62} \cdot (92 - 62) + 101$$

**Step 3 — Compute slope.**

$$\frac{99}{46} \approx 2.1522$$

**Step 4 — Compute offset contribution.**

$$2.1522 \cdot 30 \approx 64.57$$

**Step 5 — Final sub-index.**

$$I_{SO_2} = 64.57 + 101 \approx 165.57 \rightarrow \text{round to } 166$$

**Step 6 — Category identification.**

$$101 \leq 166 \leq 200 \Rightarrow \textbf{Moderately Polluted}$$

*(7 marks — formula 2, substitution 2, arithmetic 2, category 1)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Alternative Choice): 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **three real-world use cases** of IoT environment monitoring (e.g., smart city air quality, precision agriculture, industrial emission monitoring). For each, identify the **sensors, communication protocol, and one benefit**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sensor node transmits with $P_{TX} = 14$ dBm, $G_{TX} = 2$ dBi, $G_{RX} = 2$ dBi. Distance = **5 km**, frequency = **868 MHz**. Compute received power $P_{RX}$ using free-space path loss. Is the link viable if receiver sensitivity = **−120 dBm**?

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Use Case 1 — Smart City Air Quality (Delhi).**
- Sensors: **SDS011 (PM2.5/PM10), MQ-7 (CO), DHT22**.
- Protocol: **Wi-Fi/MQTT** to AWS IoT.
- Benefit: Real-time AQI dissemination to citizens via mobile app → reduced exposure.

**Use Case 2 — Precision Agriculture (Soil Monitoring).**
- Sensors: **Capacitive soil moisture, DS18B20 temperature, NPK sensor**.
- Protocol: **LoRaWAN** for multi-hectare range.
- Benefit: Optimised irrigation → 30–40% water saving, higher crop yield.

**Use Case 3 — Industrial Stack Emission Monitoring.**
- Sensors: **Electrochemical NO₂, SO₂, NDIR CO₂**.
- Protocol: **NB-IoT** for reliable backhaul in industrial zones.
- Benefit: Regulatory compliance, automatic reporting to CPCB, fines avoided.

*(7 marks — 3 use cases × 2 marks + 1 integration mark)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Free-space path loss formula.**

$$\text{FSPL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

with $d = 5$ km, $f = 868$ MHz.

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$20\log_{10}(5) = 20 \cdot 0.6990 = 13.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(868) = 20 \cdot 2.9385 = 58.77 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Sum.**

$$\text{FSPL} = 13.98 + 58.77 + 32.44 = 105.19 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Link budget.**

$$P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX}$$

$$P_{RX} = 14 + 2 - 105.19 + 2 = -87.19 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 5 — Link feasibility check.**

$$-87.19 \text{ dBm} \gg -120 \text{ dBm}$$

Since $P_{RX}$ is **32.81 dB above** the sensitivity threshold, the link is **highly viable** with substantial fade margin. ✅

*(7 marks — FSPL 3, link budget 2, feasibility 2)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing 1–3 marks each:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in FSPL:** Using $d$ in **metres** instead of **kilometres**, or $f$ in **Hz** instead of **MHz**. The constant 32.44 assumes both. Wrong units → 0 marks.
> 2. **AQI rounding error:** Forgetting the final `round()` step loses the AQI category identification mark.
> 3. **Confusing AQI with raw concentration:** Examiners explicitly deduct when students report "AQI = 92 µg/m³" — units must be **dimensionless index**, not concentration units.
> 4. **MQTT port confusion:** 1883 = unsecured, 8883 = TLS. Mixing them up = −½ mark.
> 5. **Protocol-relevance mismatch:** Suggesting Wi-Fi for forest monitoring loses applicability marks; examiners expect LoRaWAN/NB-IoT there.
> 6. **Omitting feedback/actuation:** A monitoring system that only "observes" is not a complete IoT system — always mention actuation/control loop for full marks.

---

### 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Environment monitoring** = sensing + network + analytics + actuation for air, water, soil, weather, noise.
- ✅ **Four-layer architecture:** Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- ✅ **AQI formula** is the most tested formula — memorise the **CPCB piecewise linear interpolation** form.
- ✅ **Six AQI categories** (0–50, 51–100, 101–200, 201–300, 301–400, 401–500) — must know the names *and* numerical ranges.
- ✅ **Protocol selection rule of thumb:** Wi-Fi/ZigBee (indoor), LoRaWAN (rural long-range), NB-IoT (urban cellular), BLE (wearable).
- ✅ **MQTT vs CoAP:** TCP/Pub-Sub vs UDP/Request-Response — three differences suffice.
- ✅ **FSPL constant 32.44** requires $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz** — units are examinable.
- ✅ **Sensors to remember:** MQ-series (gas), DHT22 (T/H), SDS011 (PM), pH electrode, DS18B20 (temperature), capacitive moisture.
- ✅ **Standards:** CPCB (India AQI), BIS 10500 (drinking water), WHO (global air quality).
- ✅ **Feedback loop** (actuator → physical layer) is what makes it **IoT**, not just SCADA/monitoring.
- ✅ **End-to-end latency** in critical applications (fire, gas leak) must be < 5 seconds — justify with a latency budget.
- ✅ **Edge computing** is preferred over pure cloud for time-sensitive and bandwidth-constrained scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IoT in Agriculture: Formal Academic Definition

**Internet of Things (IoT) in Agriculture** refers to the deployment of interconnected sensors, actuators, communication networks, and data analytics platforms to monitor, manage, and optimize agricultural operations — encompassing crop production, livestock rearing, soil management, irrigation, and post-harvest handling — through real-time data acquisition, transmission, and intelligent decision-making.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this discipline is most commonly referred to as **Smart Agriculture** (or **Precision Agriculture / Precision Farming**), which integrates the four pillars of modern agritech:

1. **Sensing Layer** – physical/chemical sensors in the field
2. **Communication Layer** – wireless protocols (LoRaWAN, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, NB-IoT, 5G)
3. **Processing Layer** – cloud/edge computing and analytics
4. **Actuation Layer** – automated responses (motors, valves, drones, robots)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Agriculture):**
> The syllabus explicitly expects students to demonstrate awareness of **IoT-based smart agriculture systems**, including architecture, sensor types (soil, weather, crop, livestock), communication technologies, and real-world applications such as **smart irrigation and greenhouse automation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Nervous System of a Farm"

Imagine a traditional farmer walking through his field every morning. He bends down, touches the soil, looks at the sky, checks the leaf colour, and decides whether to water the field that day. He uses his **five senses** (touch, sight, smell, hearing, taste) and his **memory + experience** to make decisions.

Now imagine if the farmer had **millions of tiny digital "nerves"** planted all across the field — in the soil, on the leaves, in the air, and even on the cattle. These nerves:

- **Sense** the environment (just like his fingers feel soil moisture)
- **Talk** to a central "brain" in the cloud (just like he calls other farmers for advice)
- **Remember** every reading for years (unlike his imperfect memory)
- **Act** automatically (turning on a water pump without him walking to the switch)

This digital nervous system is exactly what **IoT in Agriculture** does. It transforms a passive, intuition-based farm into a **self-aware, data-driven, and automated** ecosystem.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardized Terms Used in Smart Agriculture (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary):**
> - **Precision Agriculture (PA)** – Site-specific crop management using data, ensuring the right input is given at the right place and right time.
> - **Variable Rate Technology (VRT)** – Applying inputs (water, fertilizer, pesticide) at varying rates across a field based on sensor data.
> - **Yield** – Crop output measured in **tonnes per hectare (t/ha)** or **kg/m²**.
> - **Evapotranspiration (ET)** – Combined water loss from soil evaporation and plant transpiration, measured in **mm/day**.
> - **Soil Moisture Content (θ)** – Expressed as a **percentage (%)** by volume or by weight.
> - **Crop Water Requirement (CWR)** – Total water a crop needs, expressed in **mm** or **litres/m²/day**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an IoT-Agriculture System

The system functions in a **Sense → Transmit → Analyse → Actuate → Feedback** loop. Let us break it down into structured, sequential logic steps:

### Step 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensing Layer)
- Physical/chemical sensors distributed in the field continuously measure parameters.
- Each sensor node has a **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** — typically ESP32, Arduino Uno, or STM32 — that converts analog sensor signals into digital values via an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.

### Step 2 — Local Processing (Edge Computing)
- Before transmission, the MCU performs basic filtering, threshold checks, and timestamping.
- Edge processing reduces bandwidth consumption and improves response latency.

### Step 3 — Wireless Transmission (Network Layer)
- Data is sent via short-range (ZigBee, BLE), medium-range (Wi-Fi), or long-range (LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Sigfox) protocols to a **gateway**.
- The gateway forwards data to the **cloud server** over cellular/4G/5G/satellite backhaul.

### Step 4 — Cloud Analytics & Decision Engine
- Cloud platform (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT) stores data in time-series databases.
- **Machine Learning models** predict soil moisture trends, detect crop diseases, and forecast yield.

### Step 5 — Actuation & Feedback
- Based on analytics, commands are sent back to field actuators — solenoid valves, DC motors, drone sprayers, greenhouse fans, or SMS alerts to farmers.

### Step 6 — Visualization
- A dashboard (web/mobile app) displays real-time readings, historical trends, and predictive insights for the farmer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This architecture is the foundation of all modern **agricultural cyber-physical systems (ACPS)**. The same template applies to smart cities, healthcare, and industrial IoT — understanding agriculture makes you fluent in all CPS domains.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soil Moisture (Volumetric) | $\theta_v = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100\%$ | $V_{water}$ = volume of water (m³); $V_{soil}$ = total soil volume (m³) | Irrigation scheduling |
| Crop Water Requirement | $CWR = ET_c - P_{eff}$ | $ET_c$ = crop evapotranspiration (mm/day); $P_{eff}$ = effective rainfall (mm/day) | Deciding when to irrigate |
| Reference Evapotranspiration (Penman-Monteith) | $ET_o = \dfrac{0.408 \, \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \, \dfrac{900}{T+273} \, u_2 \, (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma(1 + 0.34 \, u_2)}$ | $R_n$ = net radiation (MJ/m²/day); $G$ = soil heat flux (MJ/m²/day); $T$ = temperature (°C); $u_2$ = wind speed (m/s); $e_s, e_a$ = saturation & actual vapour pressure (kPa); $\Delta$ = slope of vapour pressure curve; $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant (kPa/°C) | FAO-56 standard for irrigation planning |
| Soil Electrical Conductivity (Salinity) | $EC = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·m); $EC$ in dS/m or mS/cm | Soil salinity monitoring |
| NPK Sensor Calibration (Nitrate) | $C_{NO_3} = k \cdot V_{out}$ | $k$ = calibration constant; $V_{out}$ = sensor output voltage (V) | Fertilizer dosing |
| Battery Lifetime (Sensor Node) | $T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$ | $C_{bat}$ = capacity (mAh); $V_{bat}$ = voltage (V); $P_{avg}$ = average power draw (mW) | Field deployment planning |
| Wireless Range (Friis Equation) | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $P_r, P_t$ = received/transmitted power (W); $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains; $\lambda$ = wavelength (m); $d$ = distance (m) | Link budget for LPWAN in farms |
| Yield Estimation (Empirical) | $Y = Y_{max} \cdot \prod_i f_i(x_i)$ | $Y_{max}$ = potential yield; $f_i(x_i)$ = limiting factor functions (water, nutrient, light, temperature) | Crop modelling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Penman-Monteith equation looks intimidating. In exams, you only need to recognize its form and identify the **inputs** — never derive it. Focus on simpler formulas like Soil Moisture θ, Crop Water Requirement, and Battery Lifetime.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Drip Irrigation Automation:** Soil moisture sensors trigger solenoid valves only when θ drops below a threshold (e.g., 30%).
- **Greenhouse Climate Control:** Temperature, humidity, CO₂, and light sensors control HVAC, shading nets, and grow lights.
- **Livestock Monitoring:** Wearable IoT collars track cattle body temperature, heart rate, and GPS location for health and grazing analysis.
- **Drone-based Crop Scouting:** UAVs equipped with multispectral cameras capture NDVI imagery for pest/disease detection.
- **Supply Chain Cold Chain Monitoring:** IoT temperature loggers ensure perishables are transported at safe temperatures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Crop Water Requirement (CWR)

The Crop Water Requirement quantifies **how much water a crop needs beyond what nature provides**. The derivation proceeds from the principle of **water balance** in the root zone.

### Step 1 — Root Zone Water Balance Equation

The water balance in the root zone over a time interval $\Delta t$ is:

$$
\Delta S = P + I - ET_c - R - D
$$

Where:
- $\Delta S$ = change in root-zone soil water storage (mm)
- $P$ = precipitation / rainfall (mm)
- $I$ = irrigation applied (mm)
- $ET_c$ = crop evapotranspiration (mm)
- $R$ = surface runoff (mm)
- $D$ = deep percolation / drainage (mm)

### Step 2 — Effective Rainfall

Not all rainfall infiltrates the root zone. A fraction is lost to runoff and deep percolation. The portion usable by the crop is:

$$
P_{eff} = P - R_{loss} - D_{excess}
$$

For rainfed agriculture, $P_{eff}$ is typically **70–80% of total rainfall**.

### Step 3 — Isolate Irrigation

We need to determine the **additional water (irrigation)** required so that the root zone remains at field capacity. Setting $\Delta S = 0$ (steady state) and rearranging for $I$:

$$
I = ET_c - P_{eff} + R + D
$$

### Step 4 — Define Crop Water Requirement

By definition, **CWR** is the net irrigation requirement under ideal conditions (no runoff, no deep percolation beyond root zone). So $R = 0$ and $D = 0$:

$$
\boxed{CWR = ET_c - P_{eff}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We simply **isolated the irrigation term** in the water balance, set the change in storage to zero (steady-state condition for a single irrigation cycle), and defined the result as the **net water the crop needs from us**.

### Step 5 — Incorporate Crop Coefficient ($K_c$)

Crop evapotranspiration differs from reference evapotranspiration:

$$
ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_o
$$

So the full working equation becomes:

$$
\boxed{CWR = K_c \cdot ET_o - P_{eff}}
$$

Where $K_c$ is the **crop coefficient** (FAO-56 tables): e.g., $K_c \approx 0.3$ for initial stage, $1.15$ for mid-season rice.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A wheat field has reference evapotranspiration $ET_o = 5 \text{ mm/day}$, crop coefficient $K_c = 1.05$, and effective rainfall $P_{eff} = 2 \text{ mm/day}$. Calculate the **daily Crop Water Requirement (CWR)** and the **monthly irrigation volume** required for a 1-hectare field.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Crop Evapotranspiration**

$$
ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_o = 1.05 \times 5 = 5.25 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CWR**

$$
CWR = ET_c - P_{eff} = 5.25 - 2 = 3.25 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert mm/day to litres/m²/day**

Since $1 \text{ mm of water over 1 m}^2 = 1 \text{ litre}$:

$$
CWR = 3.25 \text{ litres/m}^2\text{/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Area Conversion (1 hectare = 10,000 m²)**

$$
\text{Volume/day} = 3.25 \times 10{,}000 = 32{,}500 \text{ litres/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Monthly Volume (30 days)**

$$
V_{monthly} = 32{,}500 \times 30 = 9{,}75{,}000 \text{ litres} = 975 \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** CWR = **3.25 mm/day**, and the farmer must supply **975 m³ of water per month per hectare** to keep the wheat crop healthy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sensors Used in Smart Agriculture

| Sensor Type | Parameter Measured | Working Principle | Typical Range | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitive Soil Moisture | Volumetric water content | Dielectric permittivity change | 0–100% VWC | Smart irrigation |
| Resistive Soil Moisture | Soil wetness | Conductivity between probes | Relative (0–1023 ADC) | Low-cost demos |
| DHT22 / DHT11 | Air temperature + humidity | Thermistor + capacitive polymer | T: -40 to 80 °C; RH: 0–100% | Greenhouse |
| DS18B20 | Soil temperature | 1-Wire digital thermometry | -55 to 125 °C | Soil thermal mapping |
| pH Sensor | Soil/water pH | Ion-selective electrode | 0–14 pH | Hydroponics |
| NPK Sensor | Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium | Ion-selective electrochemical | mg/kg | Fertilizer dosing |
| LDR / BH1750 | Light intensity (Lux) | Photoconductivity / photodiode | 0–65535 lux | Greenhouse lighting |
| MQ-135 | CO₂ / NH₃ / air quality | Semiconductor gas sensor | ppm range | Stored grain monitoring |
| Accelerometer (ADXL345) | Cattle movement | MEMS capacitance | ±16 g | Livestock activity |
| GPS (NEO-6M) | Location of equipment/livestock | Satellite triangulation | <2.5 m accuracy | Asset tracking |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IoT-Agriculture System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fieldEnv["FIELD ENVIRONMENT LAYER"]
        soilNode["SOIL SENSOR NODES"]
        weatherNode["WEATHER STATION SENSORS"]
        cropNode["CROP CANOPY SENSORS"]
        livestockNode["LIVESTOCK WEARABLES"]
    end

    subgraph edgeLayer["EDGE / GATEWAY LAYER"]
        edgeMCU["MICROCONTROLLER ESP32"]
        preprocess["LOCAL DATA PREPROCESSING"]
        gateway["EDGE GATEWAY WITH LORAWAN OR WIFI"]
    end

    subgraph networkLayer["NETWORK LAYER"]
        lora["LONG RANGE LORA OR NB IOT"]
        cellular["4G 5G CELLULAR BACKHAUL"]
    end

    subgraph cloudLayer["CLOUD PROCESSING LAYER"]
        server["CLOUD SERVER AWS OR AZURE"]
        database["TIME SERIES DATABASE"]
        mlEngine["MACHINE LEARNING ENGINE"]
        dashboard["WEB AND MOBILE DASHBOARD"]
    end

    subgraph actuationLayer["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        valves["SOLENOID IRRIGATION VALVES"]
        motors["MOTOR PUMPS"]
        fans["GREENHOUSE FANS AND VENTS"]
        drone["DRONE SPRAYERS"]
        alert["SMS APP NOTIFICATIONS TO FARMER"]
    end

    soilNode --> edgeMCU
    weatherNode --> edgeMCU
    cropNode --> edgeMCU
    livestockNode --> edgeMCU
    edgeMCU --> preprocess
    preprocess --> gateway
    gateway --> lora
    lora --> cellular
    cellular --> server
    server --> database
    database --> mlEngine
    mlEngine --> dashboard
    dashboard --> alert
    mlEngine --> valves
    mlEngine --> motors
    mlEngine --> fans
    mlEngine --> drone
```

## 4.2 Workflow Sequence — Smart Irrigation Use Case

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Soil as Soil Moisture Sensor
    participant Edge as Edge Microcontroller
    participant Cloud as Cloud Analytics
    participant Farmer as Farmer Mobile App
    participant Valve as Solenoid Valve

    Soil->>Edge: Analog reading of moisture percentage
    Edge->>Edge: Threshold check and digital filtering
    Edge->>Cloud: Transmit timestamped moisture data over LoRa
    Cloud->>Cloud: Run prediction model and trend analysis
    Cloud->>Farmer: Push notification of irrigation recommendation
    Farmer->>Cloud: Approve or override the irrigation command
    Cloud->>Valve: Send OPEN command via gateway
    Valve->>Valve: Activate water flow for computed duration
    Valve->>Cloud: Confirm flow meter reading and water volume
    Cloud->>Farmer: Update dashboard with irrigation log
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic Flow — When to Irrigate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["READ SOIL MOISTURE SENSOR VALUE"] --> B["THETA VALUE IN PERCENT"]
    B --> C{"IS THETA LESS THAN 30 PERCENT"}
    C -->|"YES"| D["READ WEATHER FORECAST FROM CLOUD"]
    C -->|"NO"| E["NO IRRIGATION NEEDED WAIT 1 HOUR"]
    D --> F{"RAIN EXPECTED IN NEXT 6 HOURS"}
    F -->|"YES"| G["DELAY IRRIGATION LOG EVENT"]
    F -->|"NO"| H["CALCULATE CWR FROM PENMAN MONTEITH"]
    H --> I["OPEN SOLENOID VALVE FOR DURATION T"]
    I --> J["LOG WATER VOLUME TO DATABASE"]
    J --> K["CLOSE VALVE SEND ALERT TO FARMER"]
    G --> L["SLEEP MODE UNTIL NEXT CYCLE"]
    K --> L
    E --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the architecture of an IoT-based smart agriculture system," always include **at least four layers**: Sensing, Network/Communication, Cloud/Processing, and Actuation. Mention **one protocol per layer** (e.g., LoRaWAN for the field, MQTT for cloud, HTTP for dashboard).
