# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Precision Agriculture. List any four IoT-based applications of precision agriculture.**

**Model Answer:**
Precision Agriculture (PA) is an information-based farm management approach that uses IoT, GPS, sensors, and data analytics to optimize inputs (water, fertilizer, pesticides) on a site-specific basis, ensuring the **right input, right amount, right place, right time**.

**Four IoT-based applications:**
1. **Smart irrigation** using soil moisture sensors and automated valves.
2. **Greenhouse climate control** using temperature, humidity, and CO₂ sensors with actuators.
3. **Crop health monitoring** using drone-mounted multispectral cameras and NDVI analysis.
4. **Livestock health tracking** using wearable IoT collars monitoring temperature, activity, and GPS.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of soil moisture sensors in smart agriculture. Mention any two commonly used soil moisture sensing techniques.**

**Model Answer:**
Soil moisture sensors play a critical role in smart agriculture by providing **real-time volumetric water content (θ)** of the root zone, enabling **data-driven irrigation scheduling** that conserves water, prevents over-watering, and improves crop yield.

**Two commonly used techniques:**
1. **Capacitive sensing** – Measures the dielectric permittivity of soil; moisture changes the capacitance between two electrodes. Highly resistant to corrosion and preferred for long-term field deployment.
2. **Resistive sensing** – Measures electrical conductivity between two metal probes; conductivity increases with water content. Low cost but suffers from corrosion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IoT-based smart agriculture system. Discuss the role of each layer in detail.

**Model Answer:**

The architecture of an IoT-based smart agriculture system is organized into **four functional layers**:

**1. Sensing / Perception Layer:**
This is the data acquisition layer consisting of various physical, chemical, and biological sensors deployed across the field. Examples include soil moisture, pH, NPK, temperature, humidity, light intensity, and CO₂ sensors. For livestock, wearables with accelerometers and GPS modules are used. Each sensor is interfaced to a microcontroller (ESP32/Arduino) which digitizes the readings using ADC.

**2. Network / Communication Layer:**
Sensors transmit data over wireless protocols such as **LoRaWAN** (long range, low power), **ZigBee** (mesh, medium range), **Wi-Fi** (high bandwidth), or **NB-IoT** (cellular, low power). The data is routed to an edge gateway, which then forwards it to the cloud over 4G/5G/satellite links. MQTT is the most common application-layer protocol.

**3. Cloud / Processing Layer:**
The cloud server stores time-series sensor data, runs analytics, and applies ML models for prediction (e.g., predicting irrigation needs, detecting crop diseases). It hosts the decision engine and exposes APIs for the dashboard.

**4. Actuation / Application Layer:**
Based on analytics, automated responses are triggered: solenoid valves open for irrigation, fans run for greenhouse cooling, drones spray pesticides, and SMS/app alerts are sent to the farmer. The dashboard visualizes real-time and historical data.

```
[Field Sensors] → [Edge MCU + Gateway] → [Cloud + ML] → [Actuators + Dashboard]
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of an IoT-based automated drip irrigation system. Explain the role of the Penman-Monteith equation in computing crop water requirement.

**Model Answer:**

**IoT-based Automated Drip Irrigation System:**

The system uses **soil moisture sensors** inserted at multiple points in the root zone. The sensor readings are collected by an **ESP32 microcontroller**, which transmits data to the cloud via **Wi-Fi or LoRa**. The cloud runs an algorithm:

1. Reads the volumetric moisture content **θ** of the soil.
2. Compares θ with a pre-defined threshold (e.g., 30%).
3. If θ < threshold, fetches the **7-day weather forecast** from an online API.
4. Computes **Crop Water Requirement (CWR)** using:

$$
CWR = K_c \cdot ET_o - P_{eff}
$$

5. Sends a command to a **motor pump and solenoid valves** to deliver the exact computed volume of water.
6. A **flow meter** confirms the water volume, and data is logged.

**Role of the Penman-Monteith Equation:**

The FAO-56 Penman-Monteith equation is the **international standard** for estimating reference evapotranspiration $ET_o$. It combines:

- **Energy balance** (radiation $R_n$ and soil heat flux $G$)
- **Aerodynamic term** (wind speed $u_2$ and vapour pressure deficit $e_s - e_a$)

The equation is:

$$
ET_o = \dfrac{0.408 \, \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \, \dfrac{900}{T+273} \, u_2 \, (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma(1 + 0.34 \, u_2)}
$$

Multiplying $ET_o$ by the **crop coefficient $K_c$** gives the actual crop water demand $ET_c$, which is then used to schedule irrigation. This makes irrigation **scientific, weather-aware, and crop-specific** rather than calendar-based.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various sensors used in IoT-based agriculture for monitoring soil, weather, and livestock. Provide one example sensor for each category.

**Model Answer:**

**Soil Monitoring Sensors:**
These measure the chemical and physical properties of soil:
- **Soil Moisture Sensor (Capacitive type, e.g., SKU:SEN0193)** – Measures volumetric water content (θ) via dielectric permittivity.
- **pH Sensor (e.g., SKU:SEN0161)** – Measures soil acidity/alkalinity (0–14 pH) critical for nutrient availability.
- **NPK Sensor** – Ion-selective electrodes detect nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium concentrations.
- **DS18B20** – Digital soil temperature sensor for germination analysis.

**Weather / Ambient Monitoring Sensors:**
- **DHT22** – Combined temperature (-40 to 80 °C) and relative humidity (0–100%) sensor.
- **BH1750** – Digital ambient light sensor in lux.
- **Anemometer + Wind Vane** – Wind speed and direction.
- **Rain Gauge (Tipping Bucket)** – Rainfall in mm.
- **BME280** – Combined pressure, temperature, humidity sensor with high accuracy.

**Livestock Monitoring Sensors:**
- **GPS Module (NEO-6M)** – Tracks grazing patterns and prevents theft.
- **Accelerometer (ADXL345)** – Detects activity, lameness, and oestrus cycles.
- **Body Temperature Sensor (MAX30205)** – Detects fever early.
- **Heart Rate Sensor (Pulse Sensor)** – Monitors cardiac health.
- **Rumen Bolus Sensors** – Internal pH and temperature for dairy cattle.

All these sensors are integrated on a wearable collar or ear tag, with data sent to a base station via BLE or LoRa.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the communication technologies used in IoT-based smart agriculture. Compare LoRaWAN, ZigBee, and NB-IoT in terms of range, power consumption, and data rate.

**Model Answer:**

**Communication Technologies in Smart Agriculture:**

Agricultural IoT requires wireless protocols that can cover **large field areas** while operating on **battery power for years**. The three leading technologies are:

**LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network):**
- Uses **spread spectrum modulation (CSS)** in sub-GHz ISM bands (868/915 MHz).
- **Range:** Up to 10–15 km in rural open fields.
- **Data rate:** 0.3–50 kbps (very low).
- **Power consumption:** Extremely low (battery life 5–10 years).
- **Best for:** Periodic sensor readings (soil moisture, weather).

**ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4):**
- Operates at 2.4 GHz with mesh networking.
- **Range:** 10–100 m per hop; mesh extends range across farm.
- **Data rate:** 250 kbps.
- **Power consumption:** Low.
- **Best for:** Greenhouse and indoor farm sensor mesh.

**NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT):**
- Cellular technology operating in licensed LTE bands.
- **Range:** Excellent (cell-tower based, kilometers).
- **Data rate:** 26 kbps (uplink), 62 kbps (downlink).
- **Power consumption:** Low (battery life ~10 years).
- **Best for:** Direct-to-cloud farm deployments where cellular coverage exists.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | LoRaWAN | ZigBee | NB-IoT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 10–15 km | 10–100 m (mesh) | Several km |
| Data Rate | 0.3–50 kbps | 250 kbps | 26–62 kbps |
| Power | Ultra-low | Low | Low |
| Spectrum | Sub-GHz ISM | 2.4 GHz ISM | Licensed LTE |
| Topology | Star-of-stars | Mesh | Star (cellular) |
| Best Use | Open-field crops | Greenhouse | Connected rural farms |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse LoRa (physical layer) with LoRaWAN (MAC layer).** LoRa is the modulation; LoRaWAN is the protocol.
> 2. **Never say "ZigBee has long range."** It is a short-range mesh protocol — say "ZigBee uses mesh to extend effective range."
> 3. **Do NOT claim IoT sensors work on Wi-Fi alone for large farms.** Wi-Fi is limited to ~50 m; LoRa/NB-IoT are needed for hectares.
> 4. **Always mention the operating frequency band** (sub-GHz for LoRa, 2.4 GHz for ZigBee/Wi-Fi).
> 5. **Don't list applications without mapping them to specific sensors** — KTU values the *integration* of sensor + protocol + use case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** IoT in Agriculture = real-time monitoring + automation of farming using sensors, networks, cloud, and actuators.
- **Four-Layer Architecture:** Sensing → Network → Cloud/Processing → Actuation/Application.
- **Smart Agriculture Synonyms:** Precision Agriculture, Precision Farming, Smart Farming, Digital Agriculture, AgriTech 4.0.
- **Key Sensors:** Soil moisture (capacitive), DHT22 (temp+humidity), NPK, pH, BH1750 (light), MQ-135 (air quality), GPS, accelerometer.
- **Key Protocols:** LoRaWAN (long-range, low-power), ZigBee (mesh, short-range), Wi-Fi (high bandwidth, short range), NB-IoT (cellular, long range), MQTT (application layer, cloud communication).
- **Core Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $\theta_v = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100\%$
  - $CWR = K_c \cdot ET_o - P_{eff}$
  - $T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$
  - Penman-Monteith $ET_o$ formula (recognize, do not memorize the full expansion).
- **Major Applications:** Smart irrigation, greenhouse automation, crop disease detection (NDVI + drones), livestock monitoring, cold chain logistics, agro-supply-chain traceability (blockchain + IoT).
- **Benefits:** Water savings (up to 50%), fertilizer reduction (up to 30%), yield improvement (up to 25%), labour cost reduction.
- **Challenges:** Rural connectivity gaps, sensor fouling/corrosion, high initial CAPEX, need for farmer training, data privacy and cybersecurity of farm data.
- **Exam Buzzwords:** *"Sense-Transmit-Analyse-Actuate feedback loop," "right input, right place, right time," "FAO-56 Penman-Monteith standard," "NDVI," "LPWAN for long-range low-power."*
- **Diagram Mandate:** Always draw a 4-layer block diagram with **at least one labelled protocol per layer** in any 7+ mark answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For any 14-mark question on Agriculture, the perfect answer structure is **(a) Architecture diagram + layer explanation + 1 example per layer** and **(b) Pick ONE deep-dive application** (smart irrigation OR greenhouse OR livestock) and discuss its sensors, protocols, formula, and benefits. This guarantees full marks under the KTU 2024 Scheme valuation pattern.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Supply Chain and Customer Monitoring** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the integrated deployment of networked sensors, actuators, RFID/GPS tracking devices, communication gateways, and cloud-based analytics platforms across the entire value chain — from raw material procurement, manufacturing, warehousing, distribution, and retail — to achieve **real-time visibility, traceability, and intelligent decision-making** for both logistics assets and end-customer behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** IoT-enabled Supply Chain Monitoring (SCM-IoT) is a cyber-physical system where physical goods, vehicles, containers, and retail environments are instrumented with smart sensors that continuously report status (location, temperature, humidity, shock, stock level) over the Internet to centralized analytical engines, enabling **proactive exception management, demand forecasting, and personalized customer engagement.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Postal Service"

Imagine the traditional postal system of the 1990s: you dropped a parcel, got a receipt, and then had **zero visibility** until the recipient called you. Now imagine every parcel having a **miniature GPS + temperature sensor + accelerometer** that updates its live location on your phone, warns you if it's been tossed around too violently, and tells you that the freezer compartment of the truck is malfunctioning.

**That is IoT-enabled Supply Chain Monitoring in a nutshell.**

For the **customer side**: think of a smart refrigerator that *automatically* detects you are low on milk, places an order with the grocery store, the store's smart shelf *notices* the stock dipped, and a delivery drone is dispatched — all without a human clicking "Buy Now." The customer is monitored not as a person, but as a **node in a connected consumption ecosystem**.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants (KTU Board-Favored)

> [!NOTE]
> Standard Metrics Used in IoT-SCM Literature:
> - **Fill Rate**: Percentage of customer demand met from available stock — target **≥ 95%**.
> - **Order Cycle Time (OCT)**: Time from order placement to delivery — typical **3–7 days** for traditional, **< 24 hours** for IoT-optimized.
> - **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)**: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) ÷ Average Inventory.
> - **Perfect Order Rate (POR)**: % of orders delivered on-time, in-full, damage-free, with correct documentation — IoT target **≥ 90%**.
> - **OTIF (On-Time In-Full)**: Industry KPI directly improved by IoT tracking.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** *Supply Chain IoT = **S**ensors + **C**onnectivity + **A**nalytics = **SCA** (See-Control-Act)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of IoT-Enabled Supply Chain Monitoring

### Pillar 1: **Asset Tracking & Traceability**
- **Technology Stack:** RFID (passive/active), GPS/GNSS, BLE Beacons, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT.
- **Function:** Every SKU (Stock Keeping Unit), pallet, container, and vehicle is tagged.
- **Why it matters:** Eliminates the *bullwhip effect* by providing upstream/downstream visibility.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used by Amazon (Kiva robots), DHL (Smart Containers), Maersk (TradeLens blockchain-IoT).

### Pillar 2: **Condition Monitoring (Cold Chain & Sensitive Goods)**
- **Sensors Used:** Temperature, humidity, CO₂, light, shock/vibration (3-axis accelerometers), tilt.
- **Why it matters:** Pharmaceuticals, food, and chemicals require strict **GDP (Good Distribution Practice)** compliance.
- **Real-time threshold alerts** prevent spoilage — e.g., vaccines must stay at **2°C to 8°C**.

### Pillar 3: **Inventory & Warehouse Intelligence**
- **Technology:** Smart shelves with weight sensors, RFID portals, computer vision cameras.
- **Function:** Auto-replenishment triggers, real-time SKU counting, theft detection.
- **Why it matters:** Reduces **stockout probability** and **overstock carrying cost**.

### Pillar 4: **Customer Monitoring & Engagement**
- **Technology:** In-store BLE beacons, smart carts, mobile app telemetry, connected products.
- **Function:** Personalized offers, dwell-time analytics, after-sales service automation.
- **Why it matters:** Converts anonymous footfall into **actionable Customer Journey Maps (CJM)**.

## 2.2 The IoT-SCM Architecture (3-Layer Model)

| Layer | Function | Components | Example |
|-------|----------|------------|---------|
| **Perception Layer** | Physical data acquisition | RFID tags, GPS modules, temp/humidity sensors, accelerometers | A pallet tagged with an active RFID + temperature sensor |
| **Network Layer** | Data transmission & routing | Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, LoRa, NB-IoT, MQTT brokers, Edge Gateways | Teltonika gateway forwarding MQTT packets to AWS IoT Core |
| **Application Layer** | Analytics, visualization, control | Cloud platforms (AWS/Azure IoT), dashboards (Grafana), ML models | Power BI dashboard alerting warehouse manager of low stock |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these formulas — KTU frequently tests quantitative reasoning in 14-mark Part B questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | times/year | Measures efficiency of stock rotation |
| 2 | **Fill Rate (FR)** | $\text{FR} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Fulfilled from Stock}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | % | Direct measure of customer satisfaction |
| 3 | **Stockout Probability** | $P_s = 1 - \text{Service Level}$ | dimensionless | Helps set safety stock |
| 4 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $\text{ROP} = (D \times L) + SS$ | units | $D$=daily demand, $L$=lead time, $SS$=safety stock |
| 5 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = Z \times \sigma_{LT}$ | units | $Z$=service level factor (e.g., **1.65** for 95%) |
| 6 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units/order | $D$=demand, $S$=order cost, $H$=holding cost |
| 7 | **Perfect Order Rate (POR)** | $\text{POR} = \prod (\text{OTIF factors})$ | % | On-time × In-full × Damage-free × Docs |
| 8 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = C_{sensor} + C_{network} + C_{cloud} + C_{maintenance}$ | currency | Justifies IoT CapEx to management |
| 9 | **Latency Budget for Real-Time Tracking** | $T_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{tx} + t_{cloud} + t_{render}$ | ms | Must be $\leq$ **5 s** for live fleet tracking |
| 10 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of IoT Tag** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability metric for sensor selection |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma Cold Chain (Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine):** IoT temperature loggers ensured **2°C–8°C** integrity across 190+ countries.
- **Walmart RFID Mandate (2003–present):** Reduced out-of-stock items by **16–30%**.
- **Tesla Connected Cars:** Continuous OTA (Over-The-Air) telemetry enables predictive maintenance — a customer monitoring use case.
- **Zara's In-Store IoT:** RFID-tagged garments + smart fitting rooms increased conversion rates by **~20%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Reorder Point (ROP) Optimization using IoT Demand Data

> **KTU-Style Problem:** A retail store sells an IoT-tracked smart bulb. Historical demand data (collected via IoT point-of-sale sensors) shows an average daily demand $D = 120$ units. The lead time from the IoT-enabled supplier is $L = 5$ days. The standard deviation of demand during lead time is $\sigma_{LT} = 30$ units. The store wants a service level of **95%**. Calculate the Reorder Point (ROP) and the Safety Stock (SS).

### Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the Service Level Factor (Z)**
From the standard normal distribution table, a 95% service level corresponds to:
$$Z = 1.65$$
This is the number of standard deviations above the mean that we want our safety stock to cover.

**Step 2: Calculate Safety Stock (SS)**
The safety stock formula for variable demand and constant lead time is:
$$SS = Z \times \sigma_{LT}$$
Substituting the given values:
$$SS = 1.65 \times 30$$
$$SS = 49.5 \approx 50 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Average Demand During Lead Time**
The expected demand during the lead time is:
$$D_{LT} = D \times L$$
$$D_{LT} = 120 \times 5$$
$$D_{LT} = 600 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Reorder Point (ROP)**
The Reorder Point is the sum of expected lead-time demand and safety stock:
$$\text{ROP} = D_{LT} + SS$$
$$\text{ROP} = 600 + 50$$
$$\boxed{\text{ROP} = 650 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation**
When IoT sensors detect the on-hand inventory dropping to **650 units**, an automatic purchase order is triggered via the cloud platform to the supplier's ERP system, **preventing stockout with 95% confidence**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often gives Z-value for **90% = 1.28**, **95% = 1.65**, **99% = 2.33**. Memorize these three.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — IoT Latency Budget Calculation

> **Problem:** An IoT supply chain system has the following component latencies: Sensor sampling $t_{sense} = 200$ ms, LoRaWAN transmission $t_{tx} = 800$ ms, Cloud processing $t_{cloud} = 1500$ ms, and Dashboard rendering $t_{render} = 500$ ms. Calculate the total latency and determine if it meets the **real-time fleet tracking requirement of $\leq 5000$ ms**.

### Solution

**Step 1: State the Total Latency Equation**
$$T_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{tx} + t_{cloud} + t_{render}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Given Values**
$$T_{total} = 200 + 800 + 1500 + 500$$

**Step 3: Perform the Addition**
$$T_{total} = 3000 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Compare Against the SLA**
$$T_{total} = 3000 \text{ ms} \leq 5000 \text{ ms} \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**
The system meets the real-time requirement with a **2000 ms (40%) safety margin**. If LoRaWAN were replaced with 5G (latency ~10 ms), $T_{total}$ would drop to **2210 ms**, enabling even faster closed-loop control.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Traditional SCM vs IoT-Enabled SCM

| Parameter | Traditional SCM | IoT-Enabled SCM | Improvement |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Visibility** | Batch updates, manual scans | Real-time, continuous | $\sim$ **95%** more accurate |
| **Inventory Accuracy** | ~65% (per industry studies) | $\geq$ 95% (RFID) | **+30%** |
| **Out-of-Stock Rate** | 8–10% | 2–3% | **~70% reduction** |
| **Lead Time Variability** | High ($\sigma$ large) | Low (IoT predictive) | **~50% reduction** |
| **Customer Engagement** | Generic, post-purchase | Personalized, in-context | **2–3×** conversion lift |
| **Loss/Theft** | 1.5–2% of revenue | 0.3–0.5% | **~80% reduction** |
| **Data Latency** | Days (EOD sync) | Seconds (MQTT push) | **$\sim$ 10⁵× faster** |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**Q: Define IoT-enabled Supply Chain Monitoring. List any four key sensors used.**

**Model Answer:**
IoT-enabled Supply Chain Monitoring is a cyber-physical system that uses networked sensors, RFID tags, and GPS modules to provide real-time visibility and traceability of goods, assets, and vehicles across the entire supply chain — from manufacturer to end customer.

Four key sensors are:
1. **RFID Reader/Tag** — for non-contact SKU identification
2. **GPS/GNSS Module** — for vehicle and container geolocation
3. **Temperature & Humidity Sensor** (e.g., DHT22, SHT31) — for cold chain integrity
4. **3-axis Accelerometer** (e.g., ADXL345) — for shock and tilt detection during transit

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**Q: What is the role of BLE beacons in customer monitoring inside a smart retail store?**

**Model Answer:**
BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) beacons are small, battery-powered transmitters placed at strategic points inside a retail store. They continuously broadcast a unique ID (e.g., UUID) to nearby smartphones running the store's mobile app.

Their roles in customer monitoring include:
1. **Indoor Positioning** — determining the customer's aisle-level location (accuracy ~1–3 m).
2. **Dwell-Time Analytics** — measuring how long a customer lingers at a specific product shelf.
3. **Personalized Push Notifications** — sending context-aware offers (e.g., "20% off the shoes you're viewing").
4. **Heatmap Generation** — creating footfall density maps to optimize store layout and product placement.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **ONE** of the two choices.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the three-layer architecture of an IoT-based supply chain monitoring system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each layer with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical distributor uses IoT temperature sensors to monitor a vaccine shipment. The shipment must be maintained between **2°C and 8°C**. The temperature sensor reports values every 5 minutes. On a particular day, the readings (in °C) for 12 consecutive hours are: 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8. **(i)** Identify the **first violation instant**, **(ii)** Calculate the **mean temperature** and **standard deviation**, **(iii)** Determine whether the **GDP (Good Distribution Practice) compliance** is satisfied. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Three-Layer IoT-SCM Architecture:**

1. **Perception Layer (Sensor Layer):**
   - Function: Acquires physical parameters from goods, vehicles, and environment.
   - Components: RFID tags, GPS modules, temperature/humidity sensors, accelerometers, smart shelf weight sensors.
   - Example: A pallet of medicines tagged with an active RFID + temperature sensor at the loading dock.

2. **Network Layer (Transmission Layer):**
   - Function: Reliably transports sensor data to the cloud using lightweight protocols.
   - Components: Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT gateways, MQTT brokers, edge routers.
   - Example: An MQTT broker aggregates sensor packets and forwards them to AWS IoT Core over TLS.

3. **Application Layer (Service Layer):**
   - Function: Stores, analyzes, visualizes data and triggers business actions.
   - Components: Cloud databases, ML models, dashboards (Grafana, Power BI), alert engines, ERP integrations.
   - Example: A Grafana dashboard displays live cold-chain temperature across all trucks; an alert SMS is sent if any value exceeds 8°C.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) First Violation Instant:**
The compliance band is **2°C to 8°C**. Reading the sequence: 5, 6, 7, 8, **9**, ...
- The 5th reading (at **t = 25 min**) is 9°C, which **exceeds 8°C** for the first time.
- **First violation instant = 25 minutes** (the 5th sample).

**(ii) Mean and Standard Deviation:**

Mean:
$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{5+6+7+8+9+10+11+12+11+10+9+8}{12}$$
$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{106}{12} = 8.833 \text{ °C}$$

Variance:
$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_i - \bar{T})^2$$
$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{12}\left[(-3.83)^2+(-2.83)^2+(-1.83)^2+(-0.83)^2+(0.17)^2+(1.17)^2+(2.17)^2+(3.17)^2+(2.17)^2+(1.17)^2+(0.17)^2+(-0.83)^2\right]$$
$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{12}\left[14.67+8.01+3.35+0.69+0.03+1.37+4.71+10.05+4.71+1.37+0.03+0.69\right]$$
$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{49.68}{12} = 4.14$$

Standard Deviation:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{4.14} \approx 2.03 \text{ °C}$$

**(iii) GDP Compliance Determination:**
- 5 out of 12 readings (≈ 41.7%) exceed the 8°C upper limit.
- The mean (8.83°C) is itself above the upper threshold.
- **Conclusion:** The shipment is **NON-COMPLIANT** with GDP regulations. The batch must be **quarantined** and subjected to stability testing before release.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain how IoT is used for **real-time fleet management and vehicle tracking** in modern logistics. Mention the communication technologies and cloud services typically employed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company wants to optimize its inventory using IoT data. The annual demand $D = 18{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S = 200$ per order, and holding cost $H = 10$ per unit per year. **(i)** Calculate the **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)**, **(ii)** Number of orders per year, **(iii)** Total inventory cost, and **(iv)** Comment on how IoT would **reduce** the holding cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**IoT-Enabled Real-Time Fleet Management:**

Each vehicle in the fleet is equipped with a **telematics unit** containing:
- **GPS/GNSS module** — for latitude/longitude coordinates
- **OBD-II interface** — for engine diagnostics (RPM, fuel level, DTC codes)
- **3-axis accelerometer** — for harsh-braking/acceleration events
- **Temperature sensor** — for cold-chain reefer trucks
- **Cellular modem (4G/5G)** — for data uplink

**Working Principle:**
1. The telematics unit samples vehicle data every **5–10 seconds**.
2. Data is packetized into a **JSON payload** and published via **MQTT** over **TLS** to a cloud IoT platform (e.g., **AWS IoT Core**, **Azure IoT Hub**, or **Google Cloud IoT**).
3. The cloud platform streams the data into a **time-series database** (e.g., InfluxDB, AWS Timestream).
4. A **Grafana/Mapbox dashboard** renders the live fleet position, route deviations, and KPI metrics (avg speed, idle time, fuel efficiency).
5. **ML models** predict ETAs and detect anomalies (route deviation, fuel theft, harsh driving).

**Communication Technologies:** 4G LTE, 5G, satellite (for remote regions).
**Cloud Services:** AWS IoT Core + Lambda + S3 + Timestream; Azure IoT Hub + Stream Analytics.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 18{,}000$ units/year, $S = 200$ per order, $H = 10$ per unit/year.

**(i) EOQ Calculation:**
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 18000 \times 200}{10}}$$
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7{,}200{,}000}{10}}$$
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{720{,}000} \approx 848.53$$
$$\boxed{\text{EOQ} \approx 849 \text{ units per order}}$$

**(ii) Number of Orders per Year:**
$$N = \dfrac{D}{\text{EOQ}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{849} \approx 21.2 \approx 22 \text{ orders/year}$$

**(iii) Total Inventory Cost:**
Total cost = Ordering cost + Holding cost:
$$\text{TC} = \left(\dfrac{D}{\text{EOQ}}\right) \times S + \left(\dfrac{\text{EOQ}}{2}\right) \times H$$
$$\text{TC} = \left(\dfrac{18000}{849}\right) \times 200 + \left(\dfrac{849}{2}\right) \times 10$$
$$\text{TC} = 21.2 \times 200 + 424.5 \times 10$$
$$\text{TC} = 4240 + 4245 \approx 8485$$
$$\boxed{\text{TC} \approx 8485 \text{ currency units/year}}$$

**(iv) How IoT Reduces Holding Cost:**
- **Real-time stock visibility** prevents over-ordering → lower average inventory → lower $H$.
- **Predictive demand sensing** (via ML on IoT data) tightens safety stock → fewer slow-moving items.
- **Smart shelf + RFID** reduces shrinkage (theft) → effective $D$ realized is higher, lowering $H$ per unit.
- Net effect: IoT typically reduces effective holding cost by **20–40%**, leading to an even lower EOQ-based TC.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "monitoring" with "control":** Monitoring observes; control actuates. Don't mix them up in definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting the third layer:** Many students only write Perception + Network. Always include the **Application/Service Layer**.
> 3. **Wrong Z-value:** Memorize **1.65 for 95%**, **1.28 for 90%**, **2.33 for 99%**. No approximation marks if you write 1.96 (that's for 97.5%).
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** A block diagram is **mandatory** in 7-mark sub-questions. No diagram = lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the communication protocol:** Always mention **MQTT, CoAP, or HTTP/HTTPS** explicitly. Generic terms like "Internet" are insufficient.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers:** KTU deducts **0.5 mark** per numerical answer if units are missing.
> 7. **Writing algorithms/code:** This is a theory paper — pseudocode is acceptable; full Python/C code is **not** and wastes time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Definition:** IoT-SCM = Sensors + Connectivity + Analytics for real-time visibility.
- ✅ **Three Layers:** Perception (sensors) → Network (MQTT/LoRa/5G) → Application (Cloud + Dashboards).
- ✅ **Key Sensors:** RFID, GPS, temperature, humidity, accelerometer, BLE beacon, smart-shelf weight sensor.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** ROP, EOQ, SS, FR, POR, ITR, Latency Budget — all 10 in the cheat sheet.
- ✅ **Z-values:** 1.28 (90%), **1.65 (95%)**, 2.33 (99%) — memorize!
- ✅ **Customer Monitoring Tools:** BLE beacons, mobile telemetry, smart carts, connected products.
- ✅ **Standards/Compliance:** GDP (pharma), GS1 (RFID), MQTT (ISO/IEC 20922).
- ✅ **Real-world Wins:** Walmart RFID (+30% accuracy), Pfizer cold chain, Maersk TradeLens, Tesla OTA.
- ✅ **Architecture Visualization:** Always include a block diagram in 7-mark questions.
- ✅ **Exam Buzzwords to Use:** *cyber-physical system, digital twin of supply chain, edge analytics, real-time exception management, OTIF, cold-chain integrity, sensor fusion, anomaly detection, predictive replenishment.*

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Supply Chain and Customer Monitoring**

topics.title: Identification/Addressing - Electronic Product Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Identification/Addressing - Electronic Product Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Electronic Product Code (EPC) — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Electronic Product Code (EPC)** is a globally unique identifier designed to provide an unambiguous identity for a physical object (e.g., a pallet, a carton, or an individual product) when it is represented within an Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem. It is encoded on a passive Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) tag and resolved through services such as the **EPC Information Services (EPCIS)** and the **Object Name Service (ONS)**.

The EPC was originally conceptualized by the **Auto-ID Center** at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and is currently standardized and maintained by **GS1** under the EPCglobal standards framework. Unlike the traditional Universal Product Code (UPC) barcode — which identifies only a *product class* (i.e., the manufacturer and the item type) — the EPC uniquely identifies *every single physical instance* of an object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 OEERT614 Module 2 framework, EPC is positioned as the cornerstone addressing primitive that enables "Things" to be named and looked up across the global IoT network, much like a Uniform Resource Locator (URL) addresses a webpage.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom with 500 identical-looking notebooks issued to students. A standard barcode printed on the cover could only say: *"This is a Class Notebook, Brand X."* It could **never** tell you *whose* notebook it is. If a notebook is lost, the barcode alone is useless for tracing it back to the owner.

Now suppose we glue a tiny RFID sticker on each notebook that contains a unique 96-bit electronic code — essentially a **digital fingerprint**. Now we can scan any notebook and immediately know:
- Which **brand/manufacturer** produced it (EPC Manager).
- Which **product type** it belongs to (Object Class).
- Which **specific individual copy** it is (Serial Number).

This unique digital fingerprint is the **Electronic Product Code**.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Rule of Thumb:** UPC Barcode = Product Type. EPC = Product Type + Unique Serial. In short, EPC is a *serial-numbered upgrade* of the barcode designed for the IoT age.

---

## Key Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Standard | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Tag Length** | **64 bits, 96 bits, or 170 bits** | The most prevalent deployment is **96 bits**. |
| **Identification Range** | **2^96 ≈ 7.9 × 10^28** objects | Sufficient to assign a unique ID to every grain of sand on Earth (and then some). |
| **Storage Medium** | Passive / Active RFID tag, NFC | EPC can also be encoded in QR codes or 2D barcodes. |
| **Resolving Service** | **ONS (Object Name Service)** | The DNS-like lookup mechanism for EPCs. |
| **Governing Body** | **GS1 / EPCglobal** | Standards authority. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Addressing Philosophy of EPC

The genius of the EPC lies in its **hierarchical, variable-length, and densely packed addressing structure**. It mirrors the hierarchical nature of the Internet Protocol (IP) addressing in IPv4, where bits are partitioned to convey *who* allocated the number, *what* class of object it is, and *which specific instance* it represents.

The 96-bit EPC, which is the KTU-favoured standard for academic problems, is partitioned into four logical fields:

1. **Header (8 bits):** Defines the encoding scheme (e.g., SGTIN, SSCC, GIAI, GID). Think of this as the "address family" indicator.
2. **EPC Manager Number (28 bits):** Identifies the company/entity that allocated the Object Class. Equivalent to the network prefix in IP addressing.
3. **Object Class (24 bits):** Identifies the "stock-keeping unit" (SKU) or product type assigned by the EPC Manager.
4. **Serial Number (36 bits):** The unique instance-level identifier — the actual "serial number" of the physical item.

---

## 2.2 The General Identifier (GID-96) — The Foundational Scheme

The most academically straightforward encoding scheme is **GID-96** (General Identifier, 96 bits). It uses the four fields above and is independent of any pre-existing GS1 keys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** For examination questions on EPC, the GID-96 scheme is the most frequently tested variant because it isolates the addressing logic from commercial GS1 key concepts.

---

## 2.3 EPC Encoding for Commercial Use: SGTIN

In real-world deployment, EPCs are derived from existing **GS1 keys** to maintain backwards compatibility with the existing barcode infrastructure. The most common is the **SGTIN-96** (Serialized Global Trade Item Number).

The mapping is:
- **Company Prefix** (from GTIN) → EPC Manager field.
- **Item Reference** (from GTIN) → Object Class field.
- **Serial Number** → Serial Number field.

---

## 2.4 Architecture of the EPC Network

The EPC is not just a number; it is the "glue" inside a five-component architecture:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **EPC Tag** | Stores the EPC on the physical object (RFID chip). |
| **EPC Reader** | Interrogates the tag via RF waves. |
| **EPC Middleware (Savant)** | Filters, deduplicates, and routes reader data. |
| **ONS (Object Name Service)** | DNS-like service that returns the address of the EPCIS server holding data about the tagged item. |
| **EPCIS (Information Services)** | Stores business-event data (e.g., "shipped from Warehouse A on Date X"). |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **EPC-96 Address Space** | $N_{96} = 2^{96}$ | Upper bound on uniquely identifiable objects. |
| **EPC-64 Address Space** | $N_{64} = 2^{64}$ | Used in low-cost tags (e.g., Gen 2). |
| **EPC-170 Address Space** | $N_{170} = 2^{170}$ | Reserved for advanced cryptographic IDs. |
| **Serial Number Range** | $0 \le S \le 2^{36} - 1 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735$ | Max unique instances per Object Class. |
| **Object Class Range** | $0 \le C \le 2^{24} - 1 = 16{,}777{,}215$ | Max distinct product types per EPC Manager. |
| **Manager Range** | $0 \le M \le 2^{28} - 1 = 268{,}435{,}455$ | Max manufacturers globally allocatable. |
| **Total Identifiable Items** | $2^{8} \cdot 2^{28} \cdot 2^{24} \cdot 2^{36}$ | Reflects hierarchical partitioning. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict KTU Notation Rule:** When writing mathematical expressions in the answer sheet, never use the vertical bar `|` inside LaTeX blocks — always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown table rendering conflicts.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Supply Chain Management:** Walmart, Amazon, and Tesco use EPC + RFID for pallet-level and item-level tracking.
- **Pharmaceutical Anti-Counterfeiting:** Every drug vial receives a unique EPC to thwart black-market duplication.
- **Smart Logistics:** The ONS acts as the DNS of the physical world, allowing a downstream warehouse to query upstream manufacturer data.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** EPC-tagged components feed Assembly-Line analytics platforms in Industry 4.0 environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Identifiable Items in GID-96

**Problem Statement:**
For a standard GID-96 Electronic Product Code, derive the maximum number of unique physical objects that can be addressed globally.

**Step 1 — Identify the bit-allocation.**
The GID-96 standard allocates bits as follows:
- Header: $h = 8$ bits
- EPC Manager: $m = 28$ bits
- Object Class: $c = 24$ bits
- Serial Number: $s = 36$ bits

**Step 2 — Verify the bit-sum.**
The total bit-length must equal 96:
$$h + m + c + s = 8 + 28 + 24 + 36$$

$$\begin{aligned}
8 + 28 &= 36 \\
36 + 24 &= 60 \\
60 + 36 &= 96 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Express the address space.**
The total address space $N_{total}$ is the product of the cardinality of each field:
$$N_{total} = 2^{h} \cdot 2^{m} \cdot 2^{c} \cdot 2^{s}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**
$$N_{total} = 2^{8} \cdot 2^{28} \cdot 2^{24} \cdot 2^{36}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the exponents.**
Using the product rule of exponents:
$$N_{total} = 2^{(8 + 28 + 24 + 36)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
8 + 28 + 24 + 36 &= 96 \\
N_{total} &= 2^{96}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerical magnitude.**
$$2^{96} = 2^{96} \approx 7.92 \times 10^{28}$$

This is approximately **79 octillion** uniquely identifiable items.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Items per Object Class

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturer (EPC Manager) wants to know how many uniquely serialized items of a single Object Class can be produced. Derive this bound for GID-96.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant field.**
Only the Serial Number field governs instance-level uniqueness within a single Object Class.
$$s = 36 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the cardinality.**
$$N_{serial} = 2^{36}$$

**Step 3 — Expand to find the integer bound.**
$$2^{36} = 2^{10} \cdot 2^{10} \cdot 2^{10} \cdot 2^{6}$$

$$2^{10} = 1024$$
$$2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$$
$$2^{30} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$$
$$2^{36} = 2^{30} \cdot 2^{6} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \cdot 64 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}736$$

Since the count starts at zero, the maximum value of the serial number is:
$$S_{max} = 2^{36} - 1 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of EPC Tag Types

| Parameter | EPC-64 Class I | EPC-96 Class I Gen 2 | EPC-170 Class III |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Length** | **64** | **96** | **170** |
| **Header Bits** | 8 | 8 | 8 |
| **Manager Bits** | Variable | 28 | Variable |
| **Object Class Bits** | Variable | 24 | Variable |
| **Serial Number Bits** | Variable | 36 | Variable |
| **Total Address Space** | $2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}$ | $2^{96} \approx 7.92 \times 10^{28}$ | $2^{170}$ |
| **Use Case** | Low-cost retail items | Most industrial IoT | High-security pharma / aerospace |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Question:** A factory assigns an EPC Manager number of `12345`, an Object Class of `789`, and a Serial Number of `1`. Formally, how many *additional* unique items can be produced for the same Object Class `789`?

**Step 1 — Identify the constraint.**
The Object Class `789` is fixed; therefore, only the Serial Number field can vary.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum serial value.**
$$S_{max} = 2^{36} - 1 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735$$

**Step 3 — Account for the already-issued serial `1`.**
Since serial `1` has been used, the number of *additional* unique items is:
$$N_{remaining} = S_{max} - 1$$

$$N_{remaining} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735 - 1 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}734$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer.**
$$\boxed{N_{remaining} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}734 \text{ unique items}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs are purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes. No reserved keywords or inline markdown formatting inside labels.

---

## 4.2 EPC-96 Bit-Partitioning Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bit0["Bits 0 to 7: HEADER - 8 bits - Encoding Scheme ID"]:::header
    bit1["Bits 8 to 35: EPC MANAGER - 28 bits - Company ID"]:::manager

    bit3["Bits 60 to 95: SERIAL NUMBER - 36 bits - Unique Instance ID"]:::serial

```

---

---

## 4.4 EPC Encoding Scheme Decision Flow

```mermaid

    startA --> qA
```
