<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a UPC barcode and an EPC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | UPC Barcode | Electronic Product Code |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification Level** | Product class only | Unique instance of the product |
| **Storage Medium** | Printed 1D bars | RFID chip, NFC, 2D barcode |
| **Data Capacity** | 12 numeric digits | 64, 96, or 170 bits |
| **Read Mechanism** | Optical line-of-sight | RF radio waves (non-line-of-sight) |
| **Uniqueness** | Shared by all units of the SKU | Globally unique per physical item |
| **Network Connectivity** | None | Resolvable via ONS / EPCIS |
| **Standards Body** | GS1 | GS1 / EPCglobal |

**Conclusion:** EPC is essentially the serialized, network-aware upgrade of the UPC, purpose-built for the IoT paradigm.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the four fields of a 96-bit EPC in the GID-96 encoding scheme.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Header** (8 bits) — Identifies the encoding scheme.
2. **EPC Manager Number** (28 bits) — Identifies the entity allocating the Object Class.
3. **Object Class** (24 bits) — Identifies the product type or SKU.
4. **Serial Number** (36 bits) — Provides the unique instance-level identifier.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for Module 2, students are required to answer **one full 14-mark question** with sub-parts. Below are two alternative choices. Either **Question A** OR **Question B** must be answered.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the architecture and components of an EPC-based IoT identification system. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The EPC architecture is a five-tier framework that allows physical objects to be uniquely addressed and queried across the Internet:

1. **EPC Tag:** A passive (or active) RFID transponder physically attached to the object. The tag's memory bank stores the 96-bit (or larger) EPC. The Tag Identifier (TID) bank contains a unique tag ID issued by the chip manufacturer.

2. **EPC Reader (Interrogator):** An RF device that emits an electromagnetic wave to power the tag, which then backscatters its stored EPC. Readers can read hundreds of tags per second within a few meters.

3. **Savant Middleware:** Acts as a distributed event-filtering layer. Savant removes duplicate reads, smooths noisy RFID data, and forwards meaningful "Electronic Product Code Information" events upward.

4. **Object Name Service (ONS):** The DNS-equivalent of the EPC world. Given an EPC, the ONS returns the network address of the EPCIS server that holds the authoritative data for that item. It is typically built on top of standard DNS infrastructure with EPC-aware extensions.

5. **EPC Information Services (EPCIS):** A standardized data-sharing interface that captures the *when, where, why,* and *how* of an item's movement. EPCIS makes the EPC queryable and traceable.

#### (b) Describe the GID-96 encoding scheme with a neat bit-allocation diagram. Why is GID-96 preferred in academic discussions of EPC? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **General Identifier – 96 (GID-96)** is the foundational EPC encoding scheme. Its bit-allocation is summarized in the table below:

| Field | Bits | Range |
|---|---|---|
| Header | **8** | $0 \ldots 2^{8}-1$ |
| EPC Manager | **28** | $0 \ldots 2^{28}-1$ |
| Object Class | **24** | $0 \ldots 2^{24}-1$ |
| Serial Number | **36** | $0 \ldots 2^{36}-1$ |
| **Total** | **96** | $2^{96}$ unique codes |

**Why GID-96 is academically preferred:**

- It **decouples** EPC addressing from the pre-existing GS1 key system, allowing pure hierarchical analysis.
- It uses **round, easily factorizable bit-lengths** (8-28-24-36) that map cleanly to exam problems.
- It demonstrates the **hierarchical naming principle** without the noise of commercial prefix logistics.
- It is the **baseline scheme** from which all other EPC encodings (SGTIN, SSCC, GIAI) are derived as specialized subsets.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Compare and contrast the EPC-64, EPC-96, and EPC-170 tag standards. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | EPC-64 | EPC-96 | EPC-170 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Length** | 64 bits | 96 bits | 170 bits |
| **Address Space** | $2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}$ | $2^{96} \approx 7.92 \times 10^{28}$ | $2^{170}$ (cryptographic scale) |
| **Typical Use** | Low-cost retail (cereal boxes, books) | Industrial supply chain | High-security, pharma / aerospace |
| **Header** | 2 or 8 bits | 8 bits | 8 bits |
| **Cost** | Cheapest tag | Mid-range | Premium |
| **Cryptographic ID** | Not feasible | Limited | Designed for digital signatures |

**Conclusion:** The choice between EPC standards is driven by the **cost-vs-uniqueness trade-off**: shorter tags minimize silicon cost but reduce address space; longer tags enable cryptographic anti-counterfeit features but inflate chip price.

#### (b) An EPC Manager is allocated the 28-bit identifier `12345678`, Object Class `5`, and is producing items with serial numbers starting from `1000`. Determine the maximum number of items that can be produced under this Object Class, and identify the highest serial number that can be issued. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the constraint.**
The Object Class is fixed at `5`. Therefore, only the 36-bit Serial Number field can vary.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum value of the Serial Number field.**
The field is 36 bits wide, so its maximum value is:
$$S_{max} = 2^{36} - 1 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735$$

**Step 3 — Verify the bit budget.**
The combined allocation for the EPC is:
$$8 + 28 + 24 + 36 = 96 \text{ bits} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Determine the total producible items.**
Starting from serial `1000`, the number of distinct items that can be issued is:
$$N_{items} = S_{max} - 1000 + 1$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{items} &= 68{,}719{,}476{,}735 - 1000 + 1 \\
N_{items} &= 68{,}719{,}475{,}736
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — State the highest serial number.**
The highest serial number that can be issued is:
$$S_{max} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{N_{items} = 68{,}719{,}475{,}736 \text{ items} \quad ; \quad S_{max} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Bit-Budget Mismatch:** Writing field sizes that do not add up to 96 (e.g., $8 + 24 + 28 + 30 = 90$). Always verify $h + m + c + s = 96$.
> 2. **Off-by-One Error in Serial Number:** Forgetting that the Serial Number starts at **zero**, not one. The maximum is $2^{36} - 1$, not $2^{36}$.
> 3. **Confusing Manager and Object Class:** The Manager identifies the *company*, while the Object Class identifies the *product type*. Interchanging them leads to a full 7-mark deduction in sub-part (b) of numerical questions.
> 4. **Forgetting ONS:** In architecture questions, students often describe the tag and reader but omit the **Object Name Service (ONS)**, which is the defining component that makes EPC a true IoT primitive.
> 5. **Piping in LaTeX:** Writing absolute-value bars using `|` inside a markdown table breaks rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in your answer script — the examiner may parse your answer through a digital script-evaluation tool.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EPC = Serialized Upgrade of Barcode.** It identifies every individual physical object, not just the product type.
- **GID-96 is the foundational academic scheme.** Bit-allocation: Header (8) + Manager (28) + Object Class (24) + Serial (36) = 96 bits.
- **$2^{96} \approx 7.92 \times 10^{28}$** uniquely addressable items — sufficient to tag every grain of sand on Earth many times over.
- **Maximum serial numbers per Object Class = $2^{36} - 1 = 68{,}719{,}476{,}735$.**
- **Maximum Object Classes per EPC Manager = $2^{24} - 1 = 16{,}777{,}215$.**
- **Five-component architecture:** EPC Tag, EPC Reader, Savant Middleware, ONS, EPCIS.
- **ONS is the DNS of the physical world** — it resolves an EPC to the EPCIS server holding its event data.
- **Standards maintained by:** GS1 / EPCglobal (originally conceptualized by the Auto-ID Center at MIT).
- **EPC variants:** EPC-64 (cheap retail), EPC-96 (industrial standard), EPC-170 (high-security cryptographic IDs).
- **Commercial derivatives of GID-96:** SGTIN (trade items), SSCC (logistics units), GIAI (returnable assets) — all encode GS1 keys into the Manager/Class bits.
- **Hierarchical addressing principle:** Just like IP addressing, the EPC fields partition uniquely into *who allocated*, *what type*, and *which instance*.
- **Always verify the bit-sum equals 96** in GID-96 problems — a common examiner trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RFID
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RFID

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** is a non-contact, automatic identification technology that uses **electromagnetic fields (radio waves)** in the frequency range from **3 kHz to 300 GHz** to uniquely identify, track, and capture data from objects (or people) fitted with an electronic *transponder* called an RFID tag. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, RFID is regarded as one of the foundational *perception-layer* enabling technologies, allowing physical objects to communicate identity and state information to digital systems without line-of-sight or physical contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — RFID)**
> As per the OEERT614 2024 scheme, students are expected to understand the **architecture of RFID systems, components (Tag, Reader, Antenna, Middleware), frequency spectrums (LF/HF/UHF/Microwave), active vs passive tags, EPCglobal Class-1 Gen-2 protocol, and IoT applications** such as supply chain, healthcare, and smart retail.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a supermarket checkout. The cashier does not need to *see* or *touch* each product to identify it. The moment a trolley passes through the gate, every item inside — chocolate, milk, shampoo — instantly flashes its name and price on the billing screen.

- The **chocolate wrapper with a tiny chip inside** → behaves like an **RFID Tag**.
- The **invisible energy field** that "wakes up" that chip → is the **electromagnetic field from the RFID Reader antenna**.
- The **billing screen** → is your **Host Computer / IoT Cloud platform** that interprets the data.

Compare this with the familiar **barcode**: a barcode needs light, line-of-sight, and a single item at a time. RFID works like a **barcode on steroids** — invisible, mass-parallel, contact-free, and re-writable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operational Tag Line (memorize for KTU exams):**
> *"RFID = A contactless, line-of-sight-independent, EM-wave-based auto-identification technology that enables IoT perception."*

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| EM spectrum exploited | **3 kHz – 300 GHz** |
| Standard governing EPC | **EPCglobal Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO 18000-63)** |
| Typical read range | **< 0.1 m (LF) → 10+ m (UHF Active)** |
| Communication basis | **Inductive coupling / Backscatter modulation** |
| Standard read time | **< 100 ms per tag** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — The 5 Building Blocks

A functional RFID system consists of **five tightly coupled sub-systems**:

1. **RFID Tag (Transponder):** A microchip + antenna laminated on a substrate. Holds a unique ID (e.g., 64-bit / 96-bit EPC).
2. **RFID Reader (Interrogator):** Generates the RF interrogation signal and decodes the reflected/responded signal from tags.
3. **Antenna (Reader-side and Tag-side):** Mediates energy transfer and data exchange.
4. **Middleware / Controller:** Software layer that filters duplicate reads, aggregates data, and routes it.
5. **Host System / IoT Cloud:** Enterprise application (ERP, WMS, or cloud dashboard) for processing the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Five" and not "Three"?**
> Most KTU answers mistakenly stop at *Tag + Reader + Host*. Examiners award the full 7 marks only when **Middleware** and **Antenna** are explicitly mentioned.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Two Operating Modes

### A. Inductive Coupling (Near-Field)
Used in **LF (125–134 kHz)** and **HF (13.56 MHz)** systems. The reader's coil creates a **time-varying magnetic field** that induces a voltage in the tag's coil (similar to a transformer). This induced voltage powers the passive tag.

### B. Electromagnetic Backscatter (Far-Field)
Used in **UHF (860–960 MHz)** and **Microwave (2.45 GHz, 5.8 GHz)** systems. The reader transmits an EM wave; the tag reflects a portion of it back, modulating either amplitude or phase to encode its ID. Think of it as a **passive radar echo with a digital signature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** If the question says *"explain the working of passive RFID"* — always draw a parallel between **inductive coupling ↔ transformer physics** and **backscatter ↔ radar principle**.

## 2.3 Tag Classification Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Tag Type | Power Source | Read Range | Cost | Lifetime | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | Harvested from reader EM field | < 10 m | Very low | Indefinite | Inventory, retail |
| **Semi-passive (Savi)** | On-board battery for chip, not TX | < 100 m | Medium | 3–5 yrs | Cold chain, sensor tags |
| **Active** | On-board battery for TX | < 100 m | High | 3–5 yrs | Toll, container tracking |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any RFID read-range or power-coupling numerical problem, the **Friis transmission equation** and **inductive coupling equation** are the two fundamental formulas. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ | Friis free-space equation (far-field). $P_r$ received power, $P_t$ transmitted power, $G_t,G_r$ antenna gains, $\lambda$ wavelength, $d$ distance. |
| 2 | $$V_{ind} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\mu_0 N A \, \dfrac{dH}{dt}$$ | Induced EMF in tag coil. $N$ turns, $A$ loop area, $H$ magnetic field. |
| 3 | $$f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$ | LC-tank resonance frequency of tag antenna. |
| 4 | $$\Delta\sigma = \dfrac{\Delta P_r}{P_{inc}} = \dfrac{4 \, G_t^{2}}{k}$$ | Differential Radar Cross Section (ΔRCS) for backscatter modulation. |
| 5 | $$R_{max} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \,\lambda^{2}}{P_{th}}}$$ | Maximum read range in terms of tag threshold sensitivity $P_{th}$. |

> **Convention note:** All vertical bars inside formula expressions are written using $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table layout.

## 2.5 Frequency Band Mapping (LF / HF / UHF / Microwave)

| Band | Frequency | Coupling | Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LF** | 125–134 kHz | Inductive | < 0.5 m | Animal tagging, immobilizers |
| **HF** | 13.56 MHz | Inductive | < 1 m | NFC, smart cards, library |
| **UHF** | 860–960 MHz | Backscatter | < 10 m | Supply chain, toll (EPC Gen-2) |
| **Microwave** | 2.45 / 5.8 GHz | Backscatter | < 30 m | Active tags, aerospace |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real IoT Projects

- **Smart Retail (Decathlon, Zara):** UHF RFID in every garment enables "self-checkout" trolleys.
- **Healthcare (Apollo Hospitals):** HF RFID wristbands for patient ID & drug verification — eliminates mismatch errors.
- **Logistics (Maersk, DHL):** Container-level active RFID tags for global real-time tracking on IoT dashboards.
- **Smart Agriculture:** Semi-passive sensor tags transmit soil moisture/temperature data to LoRa + RFID gateways.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Transmission Equation (Read-Range Foundation)

The Friis equation is the foundation for computing the maximum reliable read distance of an RFID system operating in the **far-field (backscatter)** regime.

### Step 1: Power Density at the Tag
If a reader transmits power $P_t$ through an antenna of gain $G_t$, the power density at a distance $d$ from the antenna (assuming free-space, isotropic radiator approximation extended by gain) is:

$$
S_d = \dfrac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

### Step 2: Effective Aperture of the Tag Antenna
The tag's antenna collects power proportional to its **effective aperture** $A_e$. For any antenna, $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^{2} \, G_r}{4\pi}$ where $G_r$ is the tag antenna gain.

$$
A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^{2} G_r}{4\pi}
$$

### Step 3: Power Captured by the Tag
Multiplying density by aperture:

$$
P_r = S_d \cdot A_e = \dfrac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda^{2} G_r}{4\pi}
$$

Re-arranging, we obtain the canonical Friis transmission equation:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

This expresses **received power as a function of distance, wavelength, and gains**, and is the most cited formula in any KTU RFID numerical problem.

### Step 4: Solving for Maximum Read Range $d_{max}$
A tag can be reliably read only if the received power exceeds its **sensitivity threshold** $P_{th}$. Setting $P_r = P_{th}$ and solving for $d$:

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \, \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r}{P_{th}}}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A UHF RFID reader transmits $P_t = 1\,\text{W}$ through a $G_t = 6\,\text{dBi}$ antenna. The tag antenna gain is $G_r = 2\,\text{dBi}$, operating at $f = 866\,\text{MHz}$. The tag sensitivity is $P_{th} = -14\,\text{dBm}$. Find the maximum read range.

**Solution logic:**

- Convert gains from dBi to linear:
  $G_t = 10^{6/10} = 3.98$, $G_r = 10^{2/10} = 1.585$.
- Convert sensitivity: $-14\,\text{dBm} = 10^{-14/10}\,\text{mW} = 3.98 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{W}$.
- Wavelength: $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{866 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.346\,\text{m}$.

Now substitute into the $d_{max}$ formula:

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{0.346}{4\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{1 \times 3.98 \times 1.585}{3.98 \times 10^{-5}}}
$$

$$
d_{max} \approx 0.0275 \times \sqrt{1.585 \times 10^{5}} \approx 0.0275 \times 398.1 \approx 10.95\,\text{m}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **11 m** — consistent with the 8–12 m practical range of Gen-2 UHF RFID systems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inductive Coupling Voltage (Near-Field Foundation)

### Step 1: Magnetic Flux Through Tag Coil
The reader's antenna coil carries current $I$, producing magnetic field $H$ at the tag coil. Flux linkage:

$$
\Phi = B \cdot A = \mu_0 H \cdot A
$$

### Step 2: Faraday's Law of Induction
EMF induced in tag coil of $N$ turns:

$$
V_{ind} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\mu_0 N A \, \dfrac{dH}{dt}
$$

### Step 3: Required Minimum Field Strength
For the tag IC to operate, $V_{ind} \geq V_{min}$ (typically 1–3 V for HF). Solving for minimum $H$:

$$
H_{min} = \dfrac{V_{min}}{\mu_0 N A \, \omega}
$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$. This justifies why **HF tags (13.56 MHz) require coils with many turns (large $N$)**, while LF tags (134 kHz) can work with smaller coils.

## 3.3 Anti-Collision Protocol — ALOHA-based Math

In dense tag environments (e.g., a pallet of 500 items), simultaneous responses collide. RFID systems use **Slotted-ALOHA** or **Tree-Walking (deterministic)** algorithms.

**Throughput of Slotted-ALOHA:**

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

where $G$ is the offered load (attempts/slot) and $S$ is the success rate. Maximum throughput occurs at $G = 1$, giving $S_{max} = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ (≈36.8 %).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "ALOHA" without mentioning **Slotted**. ALOHA's max throughput is only 18.4 %, while **Slotted-ALOHA gives 36.8 %**. Examiners expect the *Slotted* distinction for full marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — RFID vs Other IoT ID Technologies

| Property | RFID (Passive UHF) | Barcode | NFC | QR Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Line-of-sight needed | No | Yes | No (proximity) | Yes (optical) |
| Read range | up to 10 m | < 0.5 m | < 0.1 m | < 0.3 m |
| Memory | 64–96 bit EPC + user | 13–48 digits | 168 bytes | ~2 KB |
| Re-writable | Yes | No | Yes | No |
| Cost per tag | $0.05–0.20 | $0.001 | $0.50 | $0.001 |
| IoT suitability | High | Medium | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — End-to-End RFID-to-Cloud IoT Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reader RF Front End] --> B[Analog Baseband Filter]
    B --> C[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    C --> D[Anti Collision Engine]
    D --> E[Middleware Filter Dup Aggreg]
    E --> F[REST API Gateway]
    F --> G[IoT Cloud Platform]
    G --> H[Analytics and Dashboard]
    H --> I[Mobile User App]

    subgraph TAG_SIDE [TAG SIDE]
        T1[Tag Antenna] --> T2[Charge Pump Rectifier]
        T2 --> T3[Tag IC Microchip]
        T3 --> T4[EPC Memory Bank]
        T4 --> T1
    end

    TAG_SIDE -. Backscatter Modulation .-> A
    A -. CW Interrogation Field .-> TAG_SIDE
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Reader-Tag Handshake (Gen-2 EPC Protocol)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Reader
    participant T as Tag
    participant H as Host IoT Cloud

    R->>T: CW Continuous Wave Power Up
    T-->>R: Select Command Inventory Flag
    R->>T: Query Command Q Value
    T-->>R: RN16 Random Number 16bit
    R->>T: ACK Acknowledge RN16
    T-->>R: EPC Backscatter 96bit
    R->>T: Req RN Handle Access
    T-->>R: New RN16
    R->>H: HTTP POST EPC and Timestamp
    H-->>R: 200 OK Confirmation
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Architecture — Five-Layer IoT RFID Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Layer 5 Application Layer ERP WMS Dashboard]
    L4[Layer 4 Network Layer WiFi 4G 5G LoRa]
    L3[Layer 3 Middleware Layer ALE Filter Aggreg]
    L2[Layer 2 Reader Layer Interrogator Antenna]
    L1[Layer 1 Perception Layer RFID Tag Transponder]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    style L1 fill:#ffe5e5
    style L2 fill:#fff4e5
    style L3 fill:#e5f4ff
    style L4 fill:#e5ffe5
    style L5 fill:#f4e5ff
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right RFID Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Read range required greater than 1 m}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Within metal or liquid environment}
    Q1 -- No --> LF[Use LF 134 kHz or HF 13.56 MHz]

    Q2 -- Yes --> HF2[Use HF 13.56 MHz or special UHF on metal tags]
    Q2 -- No --> UHF[Use UHF 860 to 960 MHz EPC Gen2]

    LF --> A[Animal tagging immobilizer NFC]
    HF2 --> B[Medical device industrial tool]
    UHF --> C[Supply chain retail toll]

    style LF fill:#fff8dc
    style HF2 fill:#e0ffff
    style UHF fill:#e6e6fa
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define RFID. List any two advantages of RFID over the barcode system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
RFID (Radio Frequency Identification) is a non-contact automatic identification technology that uses radio-frequency electromagnetic fields to transfer data between an RFID tag (transponder) attached to an object and an RFID reader (interrogator), enabling the object to be identified, located, and tracked.

Two advantages over barcode:
1. **No line-of-sight** required — RFID waves can penetrate non-metallic materials, unlike laser scanners.
2. **Multiple simultaneous reads** — RFID can read 100+ tags per second in bulk, whereas barcode requires one-at-a-time scanning.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Active and Passive RFID tags.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Active Tag | Passive Tag |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Internal battery | Harvested from reader EM field |
| Read range | Up to 100 m | < 10 m (typically 1–5 m) |
| Cost | High (₹200+) | Very low (₹2–10) |
| Lifetime | Limited by battery (3–5 yrs) | Practically unlimited |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> A very common mistake is to write only about *active vs passive* without mentioning the **physical principle (inductive coupling vs backscatter)**. Equally, students forget to draw the **block diagram**, which carries **2 out of 14 marks**. Always include: (i) a labelled diagram, (ii) the working principle, (iii) the architecture components, and (iv) one IoT application example.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice A

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working principle of an RFID system used in IoT applications. Distinguish clearly between *inductive coupling* and *electromagnetic backscatter*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the EPCglobal Class-1 Gen-2 protocol with reference to its tag memory structure and anti-collision mechanism. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture and Working Principle**

**Block diagram (must be drawn):**

```
[Tag] <-- EM field --> [Reader] <-- USB/WiFi --> [Middleware] <-- API --> [IoT Cloud]
```

**Explanation:**
- The **Tag** contains a microchip and antenna laminated on a substrate. It stores a unique **EPC (Electronic Product Code)**, typically **64-bit or 96-bit**, along with user memory.
- The **Reader** emits a continuous-wave (CW) RF signal. In **LF/HF systems**, this creates a *time-varying magnetic field* that **inductively couples** with the tag coil — exactly like a transformer — generating an AC voltage that powers the chip. The chip then transmits data via *load modulation*.
- In **UHF/microwave systems**, the tag harvests energy but cannot generate enough to actively transmit. Instead, it modulates the **backscattered wave** (like a passive radar echo), changing its radar cross-section by toggling its antenna impedance between matched and shorted states — called **ASK or PSK backscatter**.
- The **Middleware** filters duplicates, aggregates reads, and exposes data via **Application Level Events (ALE)** to the IoT cloud.

**Distinction table:**

| Feature | Inductive Coupling | Backscatter |
|---|---|---|
| Operating field | Near field (H-field) | Far field (E-field) |
| Bands | LF, HF | UHF, Microwave |
| Range | < 1 m | Up to 10–100 m |
| Tag antenna | Multi-turn coil | Dipole / patch |

**(b) EPCglobal Class-1 Gen-2 Protocol**

The **EPCglobal Class-1 Generation-2** standard (also ISO/IEC 18000-63) is the de facto protocol for UHF RFID. It specifies:

1. **Memory structure** of the tag, divided into four logical banks:
   - **Reserved Bank** — kill and access passwords.
   - **EPC Bank** — stores the 96-bit Electronic Product Code.
   - **TID Bank** — Tag Identifier, unique per chip.
   - **User Bank** — application-specific data (up to 512 bits).
2. **Anti-collision mechanism** — uses **Slotted-ALOHA** with Q-parameter:
   - Reader sends `Query` with parameter $Q$ (0–15).
   - Tag randomly picks one of $2^Q - 1$ slots to respond with a 16-bit random number (RN16).
   - On collision, reader issues `QueryAdjust` to increase or decrease $Q$ dynamically — achieving near-**36.8 % throughput**.
3. **Link timing (BLF)** — Backscatter Link Frequency of 40–640 kHz, supporting dense-reader environments.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice B

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the different RFID frequency bands (LF, HF, UHF, Microwave) and their typical IoT applications. Mention the physical principle governing each band. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Friis transmission equation and compute the maximum read range for a UHF RFID system with the following specifications:
  - Transmitted power $P_t = 0.5\,\text{W}$
  - Reader antenna gain $G_t = 8\,\text{dBi}$
  - Tag antenna gain $G_r = 2\,\text{dBi}$
  - Operating frequency $f = 915\,\text{MHz}$
  - Tag sensitivity $P_{th} = -10\,\text{dBm}$ **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) RFID Frequency Bands**

| Band | Frequency | Principle | Range | IoT Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LF** | 125–134 kHz | Inductive coupling | < 0.5 m | Cattle tagging, car immobilizers, IoT authentication tokens |
| **HF** | 13.56 MHz | Inductive coupling | < 1 m | NFC payments, library books, smart cards, medical device ID |
| **UHF** | 860–960 MHz | Backscatter | < 10 m | Supply chain, retail inventory (Decathlon, Walmart), toll collection |
| **Microwave** | 2.45 / 5.8 GHz | Backscatter | < 30 m (active) | Aerospace parts tagging, active sensor tags, container tracking |

**Key insight:** LF/HF use the **H-field (magnetic)** and work on the *transformer principle*; UHF/Microwave use the **E-field (electric)** and work on the *radar principle*.

**(b) Friis Equation and Range Calculation**

The Friis transmission equation in free space is:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

For maximum read range, set $P_r = P_{th}$ and solve for $d$:

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r}{P_{th}}}
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

- $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{915 \times 10^{6}} = 0.3278\,\text{m}$
- $G_t$ (linear) $= 10^{8/10} = 6.31$
- $G_r$ (linear) $= 10^{2/10} = 1.585$
- $P_{th}$ (linear) $= 10^{-10/10} \times 10^{-3} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W}$

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{0.3278}{4\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{0.5 \times 6.31 \times 1.585}{1 \times 10^{-4}}}
$$

$$
d_{max} \approx 0.0261 \times \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{3}} \approx 0.0261 \times 70.71 \approx 1.85\,\text{m}
$$

**Answer:** The maximum theoretical read range is approximately **1.85 m** (practical range ≈ 1.5 m considering cable losses and orientation).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (must-memorize):** RFID = Radio Frequency Identification = non-contact, EM-wave-based automatic ID technology used in IoT perception layer.
- **Five components (full marks rule):** Tag, Reader, Antenna, Middleware, Host.
- **Two physical principles:** Inductive coupling (LF/HF) and Backscatter (UHF/Microwave).
- **Tag types:** Passive (no battery, range < 10 m), Semi-passive (battery for chip only), Active (battery for TX, range < 100 m).
- **Frequency bands:** LF 125 kHz, HF 13.56 MHz, UHF 860–960 MHz, Microwave 2.45/5.8 GHz.
- **Key standard:** EPCglobal Class-1 Gen-2 / ISO 18000-63 for UHF.
- **Anti-collision:** Slotted-ALOHA with maximum throughput 36.8 % ($e^{-1}$).
- **Friis equation (mandatory formula):** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi d)^2$.
- **Inductive coupling voltage:** $V_{ind} = -\mu_0 N A \, dH/dt$.
- **IoT applications:** Supply chain, smart retail, healthcare wristbands, toll collection, smart agriculture sensor tags.
- **Comparison winners:** RFID > Barcode (line-of-sight, bulk-read), RFID ≈ NFC (range trade-off), RFID active > GPS (indoor, low-power).
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always draw the block diagram — it carries **2 marks** minimum in any 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ubiquitous Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ubiquitous Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ubiquitous Code** is defined as a unified, hardware-independent, platform-agnostic, and network-transparent software paradigm that enables a single piece of application logic to be **discovered, deployed, invoked, and executed** across any device, at any location, and at any time, without requiring modification of the source code or manual reconfiguration of the underlying hardware or operating environment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEERT614 - IoT, Module 2), Ubiquitous Code forms the **software abstraction layer** of the IoT Reference Architecture. It is the conceptual and practical realization of *Mark Weiser's Vision of Ubiquitous Computing (1991)*, where computing capability is woven into the fabric of everyday life, becoming indistinguishable from the environment itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Ubiquitous Code like ELECTRICITY:**
> - You do not care **which power plant** generated the electricity.
> - You do not care **which wire** carries it to your home.
> - You do not care **which appliance** consumes it (fan, fridge, laptop).
> - You simply **plug in** and it works.
>
> Similarly, **Ubiquitous Code** means: the *same* software service can run on a tiny **8-bit sensor**, a **smartphone**, a **cloud server**, or a **refrigerator**, with the consumer not worrying about *where* the code physically lives or *how* it is executed.

Another analogy: **A universal mobile charger (USB-C)** — one cable charges every device. Ubiquitous Code is the "USB-C of software" — one codebase serves every node in the IoT ecosystem.

### 1.3 Key Properties (Highlighted for KTU Board)

- **Hardware Independence**: Code runs on heterogeneous devices (microcontrollers → servers).
- **Location Transparency**: Physical hosting location is irrelevant to the consumer.
- **Pervasive Execution**: Code executes anywhere in the network (sensor → edge → fog → cloud).
- **Service-Orientation**: Code is exposed as a discoverable, callable **service**.
- **Context-Awareness**: Code adapts its behaviour based on environmental, spatial, and temporal context.
- **Auto-Discovery**: Code can be found by other entities using directory or registry mechanisms.
- **Dynamic Composition**: Multiple ubiquitous code blocks can be aggregated to form composite services.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following engineering metrics quantify the *degree of ubiquitousness* of a code system:

- **Deployment Latency** $\left(D_{latency}\right)$: Time taken to deploy code to a target node, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Service Availability** $\left(A_s\right)$: Uptime ratio of the code service, expressed as a **percentage (%)** or decimal in $[0,1]$.
- **Code Footprint** $\left(F_{code}\right)$: Memory required to run the code, in **kilobytes (KB)**.
- **Discovery Time** $\left(T_{disc}\right)$: Time to locate a code service in a registry, in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Cross-Platform Compliance Score** $\left(C_{xp}\right)$: Number of platforms on which the same code runs without modification.
- **Network Round-Trip Latency** $\left(L_{rtt}\right)$: Communication delay between code invoker and executor, in **ms**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Ubiquitous Code

The theoretical foundation of Ubiquitous Code rests on five engineering pillars. Each pillar maps to a specific design requirement in the KTU 2024 IoT Reference Model.

#### Pillar 1: Device Independence (Hardware Abstraction)
- The same code binary (or interpreted script) must execute on devices with **varying CPU architectures** (ARM, x86, RISC-V, AVR).
- Achieved through **Virtual Machines (JVM, BEAM, .NET CLR)**, **Containerization (Docker)**, or **interpreted languages (Python, JavaScript, Lua)**.
- Real-world utility: Allows a single *data-filtering algorithm* to run on an Arduino (8-bit ATmega328P) and on an AWS EC2 instance (x86_64) without code rewriting.

#### Pillar 2: Network Transparency
- The code's invocation mechanism is **decoupled** from the underlying transport (Wi-Fi, LoRa, Ethernet, 5G, BLE).
- The service endpoint is referenced by a **logical URI** (e.g., `coap://sensor42/temperature`) rather than a physical IP.
- Real-world utility: A mobile user can invoke a smart-home function from a 5G network and from a Wi-Fi network seamlessly.

#### Pillar 3: Service Discoverability
- Code advertises its existence through **Service Registries** (e.g., DNS-SD, mDNS, CoAP Resource Directory, OPC-UA Server).
- Clients perform **lookup → bind → invoke** without prior hard-coded knowledge.
- Real-world utility: Plug-and-play addition of new sensors to a smart factory.

#### Pillar 4: Context-Awareness
- The code senses **WHERE, WHEN, WHO, WHAT** context and adapts.
- Context sources: GPS, accelerometers, timestamps, user profiles, ambient light, temperature.
- Real-world utility: A lighting-control code automatically dims lights based on time-of-day and occupancy.

#### Pillar 5: Adaptability & Elasticity
- Code scales up/down with workload via **horizontal scaling** (more instances) or **vertical scaling** (more resources).
- Implemented using **orchestrators** (Kubernetes, Docker Swarm).
- Real-world utility: A video-analytics code spawns 100 instances during peak traffic and shrinks to 5 at night.

### 2.2 Architectural Layers of Ubiquitous Code

| Layer | Function | KTU-Mapped Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | User-facing logic, business rules | Smart App, Dashboard |
| **Service Composition Layer** | Aggregates atomic services into workflows | BPEL, Node-RED, AWS Step Functions |
| **Service Registry Layer** | Stores metadata of available code services | DNS-SD, Consul, Eureka, CoAP RD |
| **Execution Runtime Layer** | Runtime environment (VM, container, interpreter) | JVM, Docker, Python Interpreter |
| **Transport / Network Layer** | Communication substrate | HTTP, CoAP, MQTT, AMQP |
| **Device / Hardware Layer** | Physical heterogeneous nodes | Sensors, Actuators, Gateways |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **analytical formulas** for evaluating Ubiquitous Code systems. These are exam-relevant for KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Service Availability** | $A_s = \dfrac{U_{uptime}}{U_{uptime} + D_{downtime}}$ | dimensionless / % | Quantifies reliability of ubiquitous service |
| 2 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total\_operation}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Predicts system stability |
| 3 | **Discovery Time** | $T_{disc} = T_{query} + T_{resolve} + T_{bind}$ | ms | Measures registry performance |
| 4 | **Effective Throughput** | $\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{P_{payload}}{P_{payload} + P_{overhead}}$ | dimensionless | Measures transport efficiency |
| 5 | **Deployment Latency** | $D_{latency} = T_{transmit} + T_{install} + T_{register}$ | s | Measures code-push responsiveness |
| 6 | **Cross-Platform Compliance Score** | $C_{xp} = \dfrac{N_{supported}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Quantifies true ubiquitousness |
| 7 | **End-to-End Response Time** | $R_{e2e} = L_{net} + T_{queue} + T_{exec}$ | ms | SLA-bound latency metric |
| 8 | **Bandwidth Utilization** | $B_{util} = \dfrac{D_{transmitted}}{B_{max} \times T}$ | % | Network efficiency |
| 9 | **Code Footprint Index** | $F_{code} = M_{rom} + M_{ram} + M_{stack}$ | KB | Hardware resource fitment |
| 10 | **Scalability Factor** | $S_f = \dfrac{\eta_{peak}}{\eta_{base}} \times \dfrac{N_{peak}}{N_{base}}$ | dimensionless | Elasticity assessment |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Cities**: Traffic-light control code runs identically on edge gateways and central servers.
- **Healthcare (IoMT)**: Patient-monitoring code follows the patient across hospital, home, and ambulance.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Predictive-maintenance code executes on CNC machine PLC, edge gateway, and cloud.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Irrigation code scales to thousands of soil-moisture sensors in a LoRaWAN field.
- **Automotive (V2X)**: Lane-detection code runs on in-vehicle ECU and on roadside edge servers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Composite Service Availability (Serial Composition)

**Problem Context**: Two ubiquitous code services $S_1$ and $S_2$ are composed in **series** (output of $S_1$ feeds $S_2$). Derive the composite availability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption**: $S_1$ and $S_2$ fail **independently** with availabilities $A_1$ and $A_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } A_1 &= \text{Availability of } S_1 = P(S_1 \text{ is UP}) \\
\text{Let } A_2 &= \text{Availability of } S_2 = P(S_2 \text{ is UP})
\end{aligned}
$$

The composite service is UP **only when both** $S_1$ and $S_2$ are UP. Since the failures are independent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{composite} &= P(S_1 \text{ UP} \cap S_2 \text{ UP}) \\
&= P(S_1 \text{ UP}) \times P(S_2 \text{ UP}) \quad [\text{by independence}] \\
&= A_1 \times A_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{composite}^{(serial)} = A_1 \times A_2}
$$

**Engineering Insight**: Availability **degrades multiplicatively** as services are chained. Three services each at $A = 0.99$ yield $A_{composite} = 0.99^3 = 0.970299 \approx 97.03\%$, dropping below the typical "three-nines" SLA.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Parallel (Redundant) Composition Availability

**Problem Context**: Two redundant instances of the same ubiquitous code run in parallel. The system is UP if **at least one** instance is UP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{parallel} &= 1 - P(\text{both DOWN}) \\
&= 1 - [\,P(S_1 \text{ DOWN}) \times P(S_2 \text{ DOWN})\,] \quad [\text{independence}] \\
&= 1 - [(1 - A_1) \times (1 - A_2)]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{composite}^{(parallel)} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)}
$$

**Engineering Insight**: Parallel redundancy is the foundation of **high-availability ubiquitous code** in critical IoT applications (e.g., patient monitoring).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: End-to-End Response Time Model

Consider a request that travels through $n$ intermediate ubiquitous-code service nodes. Each node introduces **network latency** $L_i$ and **queuing delay** $Q_i$ and **execution time** $E_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{e2e} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (L_i + Q_i + E_i) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Using **Little's Law** ($Q_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\mu_i - \lambda_i}$), where $\lambda_i$ is arrival rate and $\mu_i$ is service rate:

$$
\boxed{R_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\, L_i + \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\mu_i - \lambda_i} + \dfrac{1}{\mu_i} \,\right]}
$$

**Engineering Insight**: To meet a strict **real-time SLA** (e.g., $R_{e2e} \le 100\,\text{ms}$), one must either **reduce hops** $n$, **increase $\mu_i$** (faster hardware/runtime), or **deploy code closer** (edge computing).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Scalability Factor Derivation

The **Scalability Factor** $S_f$ measures how well a ubiquitous code system maintains throughput as the load scales from a base configuration to a peak configuration.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_f &= \frac{\eta_{peak}}{\eta_{base}} \times \frac{N_{peak}}{N_{base}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the effective throughput definition $\eta = \dfrac{P_{payload}}{P_{payload} + P_{overhead}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{base} &= \frac{P_b}{P_b + P_o^{(b)}}, \quad
\eta_{peak} = \frac{P_b}{P_b + P_o^{(p)}} \\
\therefore S_f &= \frac{\dfrac{P_b}{P_b + P_o^{(p)}}}{\dfrac{P_b}{P_b + P_o^{(b)}}} \times \frac{N_{peak}}{N_{base}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_f = \frac{P_b + P_o^{(b)}}{P_b + P_o^{(p)}} \times \frac{N_{peak}}{N_{base}}}
$$

**Engineering Insight**: An **ideal** ubiquitous code system achieves $S_f = 1$ (linear scalability). Values $S_f < 1$ indicate **overhead bloat** at scale.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Composition Type | Formula | When UP? | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Serial** | $A_c = A_1 \times A_2$ | Both UP | Single point of failure |
| **Parallel** | $A_c = 1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2)$ | At least one UP | Tolerates one failure |
| **Hybrid (k-of-n)** | $A_c = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} A^i (1-A)^{n-i}$ | At least $k$ of $n$ UP | Tolerates $n-k$ failures |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
