**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
V_{in} = \frac{512 \cdot 5}{1023}
$$

$$
V_{in} = \frac{2560}{1023}
$$

$$
V_{in} \approx 2.5024 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert voltage to temperature using sensor transfer function.**

For LM35 (with $b = 0$):

$$
V_{in} = K \cdot T
$$

$$
T = \frac{V_{in}}{K}
$$

$$
T = \frac{2.5024}{0.01}
$$

$$
T = 250.24 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.**
Maximum input voltage 5 V corresponds to maximum code 1023, mapping to temperature $T_{max} = 5 / 0.01 = 500 \text{ °C}$. Our reading of 250.24 °C is roughly half — consistent with $D = 512 \approx 1023/2$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Voltage Divider with LDR

**Problem:**
An LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) is connected in series with a $10\text{ k}\Omega$ resistor across $5\text{ V}$. The voltage at the junction (fed to ADC) is $3.2\text{ V}$. Find the LDR resistance at the current light level.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall voltage divider formula.**
For two resistors $R_1$ (top, connected to $V_s$) and $R_2$ (bottom, connected to GND), the output across $R_2$ is:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_s
$$

**Step 2 — Identify components.**
- $V_s = 5\text{ V}$
- Fixed resistor $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$
- LDR is the variable resistor; assume it is $R_{LDR}$ (let's designate it as $R_2$)
- $V_{out} = 3.2\text{ V}$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange.**

$$
3.2 = \frac{R_{LDR}}{10{,}000 + R_{LDR}} \cdot 5
$$

$$
\frac{3.2}{5} = \frac{R_{LDR}}{10{,}000 + R_{LDR}}
$$

$$
0.64 = \frac{R_{LDR}}{10{,}000 + R_{LDR}}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply.**

$$
0.64 \cdot (10{,}000 + R_{LDR}) = R_{LDR}
$$

$$
6{,}400 + 0.64 \cdot R_{LDR} = R_{LDR}
$$

$$
6{,}400 = R_{LDR} - 0.64 \cdot R_{LDR}
$$

$$
6{,}400 = 0.36 \cdot R_{LDR}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve.**

$$
R_{LDR} = \frac{6{,}400}{0.36}
$$

$$
R_{LDR} \approx 17{,}777.78 \; \Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{LDR} \approx 17.78 \text{ k}\Omega}
$$

**Interpretation:** A high LDR resistance corresponds to low light intensity (dark conditions), which matches our physical intuition.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Servo Motor Control

**Problem:**
A servo motor receives a PWM pulse of width $1.6\text{ ms}$ from an Arduino. Calculate the expected shaft angle. Assume pulse width $t = 1.0\text{ ms} \Rightarrow 0^\circ$ and $t = 2.0\text{ ms} \Rightarrow 180^\circ$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Establish the linear mapping.**
Let $\theta$ be shaft angle (in degrees) and $t$ be pulse width (in ms).

$$
\theta(t) = \frac{t - 1.0}{2.0 - 1.0} \cdot 180^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $t = 1.6\text{ ms}$.**

$$
\theta = \frac{1.6 - 1.0}{1.0} \cdot 180^\circ
$$

$$
\theta = 0.6 \cdot 180^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta = 108^\circ}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Selection

| Sensor | Measurand | Output Type | Range | Interface | Typical IoT Use |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| LM35 | Temperature | Analog (mV) | −55 °C to +150 °C | ADC pin | Weather station, HVAC |
| DHT11 | Temp + Humidity | Digital (single-wire) | 0–50 °C, 20–90 %RH | GPIO | Indoor air monitor |
| DS18B20 | Temperature | Digital (1-Wire) | −55 °C to +125 °C | GPIO + 4.7 kΩ pull-up | Cold chain, soil |
| MQ-2 | LPG, propane, H₂ | Analog + digital | 300–10,000 ppm | ADC | Gas-leak alarm |
| HC-SR04 | Distance (ultrasonic) | Digital pulse | 2 cm – 400 cm | GPIO (Trig/Echo) | Smart parking |
| PIR (HC-SR501) | Motion (IR) | Digital HIGH/LOW | ~6 m, 120° cone | GPIO | Security, smart lights |
| LDR (GL5528) | Light intensity | Resistance | ~1 kΩ (light) – 1 MΩ (dark) | ADC (via divider) | Daylight harvesting |
| MPU6050 | Accel + Gyro | Digital (I²C) | ±2g to ±16g; ±250°/s to ±2000°/s | I²C | Wearables, drones |
| BMP280 | Pressure + Temp | Digital (I²C/SPI) | 300–1100 hPa | I²C | Altitude, weather |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Actuator Selection

| Actuator | Motion Type | Drive Signal | Power Range | Typical IoT Use |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| DC Motor | Continuous rotation | PWM (via H-bridge) | 3 V – 24 V | Robot wheels, pumps, fans |
| Servo (SG90) | Angular 0°–180° | PWM 50 Hz | 4.8 V – 6 V | Robotic arm, valve control |
| Stepper (28BYJ-48) | Discrete steps | 4-phase sequence | 5 V – 12 V | 3D printer, CNC |
| Relay (5 V module) | ON/OFF switching | Digital HIGH | 250 VAC / 10 A (NO) | Smart bulb/fan control |
| Solenoid (12 V) | Linear push/pull | Digital HIGH | 12 V / 1 A | Door lock, water valve |
| Buzzer (active) | Continuous sound | Digital HIGH | 3 – 5 V | Alarm |
| LED | Light emission | Current-limited GPIO | 2 V / 20 mA | Status indication |
| Peltier (TEC1-12706) | Heating/cooling | DC 12 V / 6 A | 60 W max | Portable IoT incubator |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Sensor–Actuator Functional Flow in an IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Environment Stimulus] --> B[Sensor Transduction]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Microcontroller MCU Processing]
    E --> F[Decision Logic Threshold Compare]
    F --> G[Driver Circuit Relay H-Bridge Transistor]
    G --> H[Actuator Physical Action]
    H --> I[Modified Environment Feedback]
    I -.-> A

    subgraph SENSE_LAYER["SENSE LAYER INPUT"]
        B
    end
    subgraph PROCESS_LAYER["PROCESS LAYER EMBEDDED"]
        D
        E
        F
    end
    subgraph ACTUATE_LAYER["ACTUATE LAYER OUTPUT"]
        G
        H
    end
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- **Sense Layer (Input):** Captures the stimulus (e.g., temperature change).
- **Process Layer (Embedded):** ADC digitizes the analog signal; MCU runs IoT logic; decision threshold triggers the actuator.
- **Actuate Layer (Output):** Driver circuit amplifies the MCU's low-current signal to levels that can drive the actuator; actuator produces physical action.
- The dotted feedback arrow represents the **closed-loop** nature — the environment changes in response to actuation, and sensors sense the new state.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Classification Tree of Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sensors] --> S1[By Output]
    S --> S2[By Power]
    S --> S3[By Measurand]

    S1 --> S1a[Analog Sensors]
    S1 --> S1b[Digital Sensors]

    S2 --> S2a[Active Sensors]
    S2 --> S2b[Passive Sensors]

    S3 --> S3a[Thermal]
    S3 --> S3b[Mechanical]
    S3 --> S3c[Electromagnetic]
    S3 --> S3d[Chemical]
    S3 --> S3e[Biological]

    S1a --> EX1[LM35 TMP36 LDR]
    S1b --> EX2[DHT11 DS18B20 MPU6050]
    S2a --> EX3[Thermistor Strain Gauge]
    S2b --> EX4[Thermocouple Photodiode Piezo]
    S3a --> EX5[LM35 DHT11]
    S3b --> EX6[Piezo Accelerometer BMP280]
    S3c --> EX7[LDR Photodiode Hall Sensor]
    S3d --> EX8[MQ2 Gas Sensor pH Probe]
    S3e --> EX9[Glucose Heart Rate Sensor]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Actuator Driver Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[Microcontroller GPIO Output] -->|Low Current 10mA| DRV[Driver Stage]

    DRV --> DRV1[Transistor BJT or MOSFET]
    DRV --> DRV2[Relay Driver ULN2003]
    DRV --> DRV3[H-Bridge L298N DRV8871]
    DRV --> DRV4[Motor Driver IC TB6612]

    DRV1 --> ACT1[DC Fan Buzzer LED]
    DRV2 --> ACT2[High Power Relay Solenoid]
    DRV3 --> ACT3[DC Motor Bi-directional]
    DRV4 --> ACT4[Stepper Motor Bi-polar]

    ACT1 --> OUT[Physical Output Heat Motion Light Sound]
    ACT2 --> OUT
    ACT3 --> OUT
    ACT4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the driver stage necessary?**
> An MCU GPIO pin typically sources only **5–20 mA at 3.3 V or 5 V**. Real actuators need much more current/voltage (e.g., DC motors: 200 mA – 2 A, Relays: 50–100 mA coil current). A driver stage buffers and amplifies the control signal.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Closed-Loop Smart Room Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TEMP[LM35 Temperature Sensor] -->|Analog mV| ADC[ADC Channel on Arduino ESP32]
    PIR[PIR Motion Sensor HC-SR501] -->|Digital HIGH LOW| GPIO1[GPIO Pin]
    LDR[LDR Light Sensor] -->|Resistance divider| ADC2[ADC Channel]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller Logic]
    GPIO1 --> MCU
    ADC2 --> MCU

    MCU --> FAN[Relay Driving Cooling Fan]
    MCU --> LIGHT[Relay Driving Smart Light]
    MCU --> SERVO[Servo Motor Smart Blinds]
    MCU --> CLOUD[Wi-Fi Module Data to Cloud]
    CLOUD --> CLOUDDB[(Cloud Dashboard Plotly Blynk)]
```

**Smart Room Behavior Summary:**
- If **PIR detects motion** AND **LDR detects dark** → Turn ON light.
- If **temperature > 30 °C** → Turn ON fan.
- If **sunlight is high** → Close blinds (servo to 0°).
- All sensor data uploaded to cloud for historical analytics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Active Sensors** require external excitation power to operate and respond to a measurand by modulating that excitation. Examples: thermistor, strain gauge, RTD.
- **Passive Sensors** self-generate an electrical output signal in response to a physical stimulus without requiring external power. Examples: thermocouple, piezoelectric sensor, photodiode.
- Key distinction: passive sensors are energy *converters* (one form of energy to another); active sensors are energy *modulators*.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the working principle of a PIR motion sensor. Mention one IoT application.**

**Model Answer:**
A Passive Infrared (PIR) sensor detects motion by sensing changes in infrared (IR) radiation emitted by objects in its field of view. It contains two slots made of a pyroelectric material that generate an electrical signal when exposed to IR. A Fresnel lens focuses the IR radiation onto these slots. Under normal conditions, both slots see the same ambient IR level, producing no net output. When a warm body (e.g., a human) crosses the sensor's field, it changes the IR incident on one slot, producing a differential voltage that triggers the output HIGH.
**IoT application:** Automatic streetlight control, intruder alarm, smart-bathroom lighting.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IoT node highlighting the role of sensors and actuators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of an LM35 temperature sensor. An 8-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ reads a digital value of $D = 153$ from the LM35. Calculate the equivalent temperature. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to A(a):

The IoT node architecture consists of **five functional layers**:

1. **Sensor Layer** — Physical sensors (LM35, DHT11, MQ2) capture environmental parameters and produce analog/digital signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning Layer** — Op-amp amplification, RC filtering, and voltage translation ensure the sensor output is compatible with the ADC's input range.
3. **Processing Layer** — A microcontroller (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi Pico) reads the digitized sensor values, runs decision logic, and prepares data packets.
4. **Communication Layer** — A Wi-Fi / Bluetooth / LoRa module transmits processed data to the cloud (MQTT / HTTP / CoAP protocols).
5. **Actuator Layer** — Based on processed data or remote commands, driver circuits (relays, H-bridges) activate actuators such as motors, fans, and buzzers.

**Role of Sensors:** Act as the *eyes and ears* of the IoT system — converting physical stimuli (temperature, motion, gas) into electrical signals for the MCU to interpret.

**Role of Actuators:** Act as the *hands and legs* — converting MCU control signals into physical actions (motion, switching, sound).

*(A neat diagram similar to Section 4.1 above should be drawn on the answer sheet.)*

---

### Model Solution to A(b):

**LM35 Working Principle:**
The LM35 is a precision centigrade temperature sensor whose output voltage is **linearly proportional to Celsius temperature** with a scale factor of **10 mV/°C**. Internally, it uses the temperature-dependent voltage across the base–emitter junction of bipolar transistors ($V_{BE}$, which has a temperature coefficient of approximately −2 mV/°C) and subtracts this from a bandgap reference (1.25 V) to produce a temperature-proportional output that is referenced to 0 °C at 0 mV.

**Calculation:**

**Step 1 —** Given: $n = 8 \Rightarrow 2^8 = 256$, $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$, $D = 153$.

**Step 2 —** Convert digital code to voltage using:

$$
V_{in} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}
$$

$$
V_{in} = \frac{153 \cdot 5}{255}
$$

$$
V_{in} = \frac{765}{255} \approx 3.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 —** Apply LM35 transfer function $V = 0.01 \cdot T$:

$$
T = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01} = \frac{3.0}{0.01}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 300 \text{ °C}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working of a stepper motor. How is it different from a DC motor? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable circuit, explain how an LDR can be used to automatically control a streetlight using a microcontroller. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to B(a):

**Stepper Motor Working:**
A stepper motor is a brushless DC motor that rotates in **discrete angular increments (steps)**. It typically consists of a rotor (permanent magnet or toothed iron) and a stator with multiple coils (phases). By sequentially energizing the stator coils in a specific order (drive sequence), the magnetic field rotates in steps, attracting the rotor poles and producing precise angular displacement per step.

**Common drive sequences:**
- **Wave drive (1-phase ON):** One coil energized at a time — low torque, low power.
- **Full-step drive (2-phase ON):** Two adjacent coils energized — higher torque.
- **Half-step drive:** Alternates between wave and full-step — smoother motion, finer resolution.

**Step angle formula:**

$$
\theta_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{N_r \cdot P}
$$

where $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth, $P$ = number of stator pole pairs.

**Differences from a DC motor:**

| Parameter | DC Motor | Stepper Motor |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Rotation | Continuous | Discrete steps |
| Position feedback | Required for precision | Open-loop precision possible |
| Speed control | Easy via PWM | Pulse frequency control |
| Torque at standstill | Zero (rotates if loaded) | Holds position (holding torque) |
| Application | Fans, pumps, wheels | Printers, CNC, 3D printers |

---

### Model Solution to B(b):

**Circuit Description:**
1. **LDR** is connected in a voltage divider with a $10\text{ k}\Omega$ fixed resistor across $5\text{ V}$.
2. The **junction point** of the LDR and resistor is connected to an **ADC input** of the microcontroller (e.g., Arduino's A0 pin).
3. The microcontroller's **digital output pin** drives a relay module via a transistor (BC547) driver.
4. The relay's **common (COM) and normally-open (NO)** contacts are wired in series with the streetlight and the AC mains supply.

**Algorithm (Pseudocode for the controller logic):**

1. Read the ADC value → corresponds to light intensity.
2. Convert ADC value to voltage.
3. If voltage < threshold (i.e., it is dark) → Set relay pin HIGH → Light turns ON.
4. If voltage ≥ threshold (i.e., it is bright) → Set relay pin LOW → Light turns OFF.

**Working principle:**
In darkness, LDR resistance is very high (≥ 1 MΩ), so most of the $5\text{ V}$ drops across the LDR → ADC reads a high value (or low, depending on divider orientation). The microcontroller detects this and energizes the relay coil, closing the NO contact and switching ON the streetlight. In daylight, the LDR resistance drops to a few hundred ohms, voltage at the ADC falls below the threshold, and the relay de-energizes, switching off the light.

**Safety note (essential for exam answer):**
- An **opto-isolator** is recommended between MCU and relay coil for isolation.
- The AC mains wiring must be done with proper insulation; this circuit is for low-voltage control, not direct AC handling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing sensor output types:** Students often write "LM35 is a digital sensor" — WRONG. It is analog. Only the *external* ADC makes it digital.
> 2. **Mixing up Stepper vs Servo:** Steppers rotate in fixed steps with holding torque; servos use PWM feedback to hold a precise angle — they are NOT the same.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Writing $V_{in} = 3.0$ without specifying **Volts** leads to a 0.5-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting the $(2^n - 1)$ factor in ADC:** Using $2^n$ instead gives wrong answers. KTU examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **Driver circuit omission:** Drawing an MCU pin directly connected to a relay/motor (without a driver transistor or H-bridge) is marked down for **practical implausibility**.
> 6. **Active vs Passive sensors confusion:** A common error is calling a thermocouple "active" — it is passive (self-generating), not externally powered.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ⭐ **Sensors** = Input transducers; **Actuators** = Output transducers.
- ⭐ Analog sensors produce a continuous voltage (e.g., LM35: 10 mV/°C); digital sensors produce serial protocol data (e.g., DHT11 over single-wire, MPU6050 over I²C).
- ⭐ Active sensors need external power (RTD, thermistor); passive sensors self-generate (thermocouple, photodiode, piezoelectric).
- ⭐ Key sensor metrics: **accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity, linearity, range, response time**.
- ⭐ ADC conversion uses $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ and LSB size = $\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$.
- ⭐ LDR and thermistor are typically read via a **voltage divider** followed by an ADC.
- ⭐ PIR sensor detects motion via differential IR radiation on a pyroelectric element.
- ⭐ MQ-series gas sensors (MQ-2, MQ-135) require a warm-up time and have both analog and digital outputs.
- ⭐ DC motors need **H-bridge drivers** for direction control (e.g., L298N); speed controlled by PWM.
- ⭐ Steppers rotate in **fixed angular steps** (typically 1.8°); position is open-loop.
- ⭐ Servos use **PWM with 50 Hz** (20 ms period); pulse 1 ms–2 ms maps to 0°–180°.
- ⭐ Relays provide isolation between low-voltage MCU circuits and high-voltage/high-current loads.
- ⭐ Drivers (BJT, MOSFET, ULN2003, L298N) are **always** required between MCU and actuators.
- ⭐ In IoT, sensors → ADC → MCU → Cloud; Cloud command or local logic → Driver → Actuator → Physical action.
- ⭐ Closed-loop IoT systems use sensor feedback to continuously adjust actuator behavior (e.g., thermostat).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of IoT Hardware

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Hardware (KTU 2024 Definition):** IoT hardware refers to the physical, tangible electronic components and embedded devices that form the foundational layer of the Internet of Things architecture. It encompasses **sensors**, **actuators**, **microcontrollers/microprocessors**, **single-board computers (SBCs)**, **communication modules**, and **power management subsystems** that collectively enable real-world physical phenomena to be sensed, processed, transmitted, and actuated over the internet.

According to the KTU 2024 OEERT614 syllabus (Module 2), IoT hardware is treated as the **"Things Layer"** — the lowest abstraction layer in the standard 5-layer IoT architecture (Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business).

| Hardware Class | Function | KTU Layer Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Sensors | Sense physical parameters | Perception Layer |
| Actuators | Act on the physical world | Perception Layer |
| MCU / SBC | Local computation & decision | Perception / Network |
| Communication Modules | Data transmission (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa) | Network Layer |
| Power Subsystems | Energy provisioning | Cross-cutting |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: An IoT device is like a tiny digital human body.**
> - **Sensors = Human Senses** (eyes, ears, skin) — they *detect* light, sound, temperature, pressure.
> - **Microcontroller = Human Brain** — it *processes* what the senses report and *decides* what to do.
> - **Actuators = Human Muscles** — they *perform actions* (move a motor, light an LED, open a valve).
> - **Communication Module = Human Voice / Mobile Phone** — it *transmits* information to other humans (or other IoT devices).
> - **Power Supply = Food / Battery** — the *energy* that keeps everything alive.

Without hardware, the IoT is just software floating in the cloud — it has no way to *touch* the real world. **Hardware is what makes the "Things" in IoT physical.**

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics of IoT Hardware

When evaluating IoT hardware, KTU examiners expect familiarity with these standard metrics:

- **Power Consumption** — measured in **mW** (milliwatts) for battery-operated nodes. Typical targets: < 10 mW for sleep mode.
- **Operating Voltage** — standard rails are **3.3 V** and **5 V** DC.
- **Clock Frequency** — **MHz** range (e.g., ESP32 = 240 MHz, Arduino Uno = 16 MHz).
- **ADC Resolution** — measured in **bits** (8, 10, 12, 16, 24-bit).
- **Communication Range** — meters (BLE), kilometers (LoRaWAN), global (Cellular IoT).
- **Memory Footprint** — **KB / MB** of Flash + RAM.

## 1.4 High-Yield Classification of IoT Hardware

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Hardware Classification Triad:**
> 1. **Sensing / Actuation Hardware** (transducers)
> 2. **Processing Hardware** (MCUs, SBCs, SoCs)
> 3. **Communication & Power Hardware** (radios, batteries, harvesters)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Subsystem — Theory of Operation

A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, light, etc.) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or digital count).

### 2.1.1 Sensor Classification

- **Active Sensors** — require external power (e.g., radar, LIDAR).
- **Passive Sensors** — generate their own signal from the measurand (e.g., thermocouple, photodiode).
- **Analog Sensors** — output continuous voltage (e.g., LM35 temperature sensor).
- **Digital Sensors** — output binary/serial data (e.g., DHT22, BME280 over I²C).

### 2.1.2 Static Characteristics of Sensors (Critical for KTU)

| Characteristic | Definition | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness to true value | Highest possible |
| **Precision** | Reproducibility of readings | Highest possible |
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change | Smallest detectable |
| **Sensitivity** | Output change per unit input change | Application-specific |
| **Linearity** | Proportionality between input & output | Linear (Y = mX + c) |
| **Range** | Min and max measurable input | Application-specific |
| **Hysteresis** | Output difference for rising vs. falling input | Zero |

### 2.1.3 Sensor Output Conversion — LM35 Example

The LM35 is a **popular KTU-recommended analog temperature sensor** with a linear scale factor of **+10 mV / °C**.

$$V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV} / °C \times T \quad (T \text{ in } °C)$$

The MCU's ADC converts this analog voltage to a digital count:

$$N = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$$

Where:
- $N$ = digital output count
- $V_{ref}$ = ADC reference voltage (typically **3.3 V**)
- $n$ = ADC resolution in bits (e.g., 10, 12)

**Reverse conversion (count → temperature):**

$$T = \frac{N \times V_{ref}}{(2^n - 1) \times 10 \text{ mV}/°C}$$

## 2.2 Actuator Subsystem

An **actuator** converts electrical signals into physical action. Common types include DC motors, servo motors, stepper motors, relays, solenoids, LEDs, and buzzers.

**Key actuation equation (DC Motor):**

$$V = I \cdot R + L \frac{dI}{dt} + K_e \cdot \omega$$

$$T_m = K_t \cdot I$$

Where $K_e$ = back-EMF constant, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $T_m$ = motor torque, $K_t$ = torque constant, $L$ = inductance, $R$ = armature resistance.

## 2.3 Processing Hardware — MCUs vs. SBCs

| Feature | Microcontroller (MCU) | Single-Board Computer (SBC) |
|---|---|---|
| **OS** | None / RTOS (FreeRTOS, Contiki) | Full Linux (Raspbian, Ubuntu) |
| **Clock Speed** | 16 – 240 MHz | 0.7 – 2.0+ GHz |
| **RAM** | KB range (32 – 512 KB) | MB – GB range |
| **Power** | < 1 W | 3 – 15 W |
| **Cost** | < $10 | $35 – $200 |
| **Example** | Arduino Uno, ESP8266, ESP32, STM32 | Raspberry Pi 4, BeagleBone |
| **Use Case** | Sensing edge nodes | Gateway / edge analytics |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** ESP32 is a **System-on-Chip (SoC)** that integrates an MCU + Wi-Fi + Bluetooth radios. It is a favourite exam example because it blurs the MCU/SBC boundary by being powerful yet low-power.

## 2.4 Communication Hardware

IoT communication modules operate across frequencies and protocols:

| Protocol | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | ~50 m | up to 1 Gbps | High |
| **BLE 5.0** | 2.4 GHz | ~100 m | 2 Mbps | Very Low |
| **Zigbee (802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | ~100 m | 250 kbps | Low |
| **LoRaWAN** | 868 / 915 MHz | 2 – 15 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Very Low |
| **NB-IoT** | Cellular bands | ~10 km | 200 kbps | Low |
| **Sigfox** | 868 / 915 MHz | ~10 km | 100 bps | Very Low |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table — these are the **most-tested equations** for the IoT Hardware module.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = S \times X$ | Sensor linear output | Analog sensor readout |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}(2^n - 1)$ | ADC digital conversion | MCU ADC |
| 3 | $P = V \times I$ | Electrical power | Battery life estimation |
| 4 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy consumption | Battery sizing |
| 5 | $T_{battery} = \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{mA}}$ (hours) | Battery life | Power budgeting |
| 6 | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz) | Free-space path loss (dB) | Link budget |
| 7 | $SNR = P_{signal} - P_{noise}$ (dB) | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Wireless link quality |
| 8 | $T = \dfrac{N \times V_{ref}}{(2^n - 1) \times S}$ | Convert ADC count → physical unit | Sensor calibration |
| 9 | $C_{total} = \sum C_i$ | Capacitive load on bus | I²C / SPI design |
| 10 | $f_{I^2C} \le 400 \text{ kHz}$ (Fast mode) | I²C clock limit | Bus timing |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** LoRa + soil moisture sensor nodes covering 5 km² farms.
- **Wearable Health:** BLE + heart-rate sensor + 50 mAh coin cell lasting 7 days.
- **Industrial IIoT:** RS-485 + vibration sensor + 24-bit ADC for predictive maintenance.
- **Smart City:** NB-IoT + air quality sensor + 10-year battery life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation 1 — LM35 Temperature Reading via 10-bit ADC

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A KTU student interfaces an **LM35** sensor with the **ATmega328P** (Arduino Uno) which has a **10-bit ADC** and **5.0 V reference**. The ADC returns a count of **N = 256**. Calculate the measured temperature.

**Given:**
- ADC resolution: $n = 10$ bits
- Reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$
- LM35 sensitivity: $S = 10 \text{ mV}/°C = 0.010 \text{ V}/°C$
- ADC digital count: $N = 256$

**Step 1 — Calculate the input voltage at the ADC pin:**

The ADC output count is related to the input voltage by:

$$N = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$$

Rearranging for $V_{in}$:

$$V_{in} = \frac{N \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

Substituting the values:

$$V_{in} = \frac{256 \times 5.0}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{1280}{1023} = 1.2512 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Convert voltage to temperature using the LM35 scale factor:**

The LM35 outputs $10 \text{ mV}$ per °C, so:

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{S} = \frac{V_{in}}{0.010 \text{ V}/°C}$$

$$T = \frac{1.2512 \text{ V}}{0.010 \text{ V}/°C} = 125.12 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer with Units and Sanity Check:**

$$\boxed{T \approx 125.1 \, °C}$$

> **Sanity Check:** Since the LM35 is rated for 0 °C to +150 °C and ADC range is 0 – 5 V (representing 0 – 500 °C), a value of 125 °C is well within range. ✓

## 3.2 Derivative Derivation 2 — Battery Life Estimation

> **Problem:** A KTU smart agriculture node draws an average current of **I = 15 mA** when awake and **I_sleep = 0.05 mA** when sleeping. The node wakes every 10 minutes for **5 seconds** to transmit. It runs on a **2000 mAh** Li-ion battery. Calculate the total battery life in days.

**Step 1 — Determine duty cycle:**

$$T_{cycle} = 600 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{active} = 5 \text{ s}, \quad T_{sleep} = 595 \text{ s}$$

Duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{5}{600} = 0.00833$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time-averaged current:**

$$I_{avg} = D \times I_{active} + (1 - D) \times I_{sleep}$$

$$I_{avg} = 0.00833 \times 15 \text{ mA} + 0.99167 \times 0.05 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_{avg} = 0.125 \text{ mA} + 0.04958 \text{ mA} = 0.17458 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Compute battery life in hours:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{0.17458 \text{ mA}} = 11{,}457 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{11{,}457}{24} = 477.4 \text{ days} \approx 15.9 \text{ months}$$

$$\boxed{T_{battery} \approx 477 \text{ days } (\sim 1.3 \text{ years})}$$

## 3.3 Derivative Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss for LoRa Link

> **Problem:** A LoRa gateway at 868 MHz is placed **5 km** away from a sensor node. Compute the free-space path loss in dB.

**Step 1 — Apply the Friis FSPL formula:**

$$FSPL \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

where $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44$$

$$FSPL = 20 \times 0.69897 + 20 \times 2.93852 + 32.44$$

$$FSPL = 13.979 + 58.770 + 32.44$$

$$FSPL = 105.19 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{FSPL \approx 105.2 \text{ dB}}$$

**Step 3 — Practical interpretation:** A LoRa link budget typically allows up to **~150 dB** total path loss with spreading factor SF12, so 105 dB leaves **~45 dB of margin** for obstacles, antenna gain, and fade.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Popular IoT Boards

| Parameter | Arduino Uno | ESP8266 | ESP32 | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MCU/SoC** | ATmega328P | Tensilica L106 | Xtensa LX6 | Broadcom BCM2711 |
| **Clock** | 16 MHz | 80 – 160 MHz | 240 MHz | 1.5 GHz (quad) |
| **Flash** | 32 KB | 1 – 4 MB | 4 MB | microSD based |
| **RAM** | 2 KB | 80 KB | 520 KB | 2 / 4 / 8 GB |
| **Wi-Fi** | None | 802.11 b/g/n | 802.11 b/g/n | 802.11 ac |
| **Bluetooth** | None | None | BLE 4.2 | 5.0 |
| **GPIO** | 14 | 17 | 34 | 40 |
| **ADC** | 10-bit (6 ch) | 10-bit (1 ch) | 12-bit (18 ch) | None (external) |
| **Operating Voltage** | 5 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V | 5 V |
| **Power (active)** | ~200 mW | ~240 mW | ~500 mW | ~6 W |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹450 | ₹200 | ₹350 | ₹4,000 |
| **KTU Use Case** | Intro labs | Wi-Fi nodes | BLE + Wi-Fi | Gateway |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "justify the choice of board for an IoT application," always reason from **(a) connectivity needed, (b) power budget, (c) computational load, (d) cost.** A 2-line justification is worth more than a generic list.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — IoT Hardware Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHY[Physical World] --> SENS[Sensors]
    SENS --> ADC[ADC / Signal Conditioning]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller / SBC]
    MCU --> ACT[Actuators]
    ACT --> PHY
    MCU --> RADIO[Communication Module]
    RADIO -->|Wi-Fi BLE LoRa| NET[Network / Cloud]
    PWR[Power Supply Battery] -.-> MCU
    PWR -.-> SENS
    PWR -.-> RADIO
    NET --> APP[Application Layer]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** → data/control flow.
- **Dotted arrows** → power flow.
- The MCU is the **central decision hub**; all sensing and actuation routes through it.
- Power is *cross-cutting* — every block needs it.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Sensor Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENSROOT[IoT Sensors]
    SENSROOT --> ANA[Analog Sensors]
    SENSROOT --> DIG[Digital Sensors]
    ANA --> LM35[LM35 Temperature]
    ANA --> LDR[LDR Light]
    ANA --> FSR[FSR Pressure]
    DIG --> DHT[DHT22 Humidity]
    DIG --> BME[BME280 T H P]
    DIG --> MPU[MPU6050 IMU]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — MCU vs. SBC Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose IoT Processing Hardware] --> Q1{Need full OS Linux?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> SBC[Single Board Computer]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Wireless connectivity needed?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ESP[ESP32 or ESP8266]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Real-time control critical?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ARD[Arduino or STM32]
    Q3 -- No --> SBC2[SBC alternative Raspberry Pi]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sensor Node Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SLEEP[Sleep Mode] -->|Timer Interrupt| WAKE[Wake Up]
    WAKE --> READ[Read Sensors via I2C SPI ADC]
    READ --> PROC[Process Data Locally]
    PROC --> DEC{Threshold Crossed?}
    DEC -- Yes --> TX[Transmit via Radio]
    DEC -- No --> ACT[Activate Actuator if needed]
    TX --> SLP[Return to Sleep]
    ACT --> SLP
```

**Operational interpretation:** IoT nodes spend **>99% of their lifetime sleeping** to conserve energy. This is the *core principle* of energy-harvesting and battery-powered IoT design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator with one IoT example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical phenomenon (temperature, light, motion) into an electrical signal. **Example:** DHT22 humidity sensor.
- An **actuator** is a transducer that converts an electrical signal into a physical action. **Example:** A DC motor driving a smart lock.
- Sensors *perceive* the environment; actuators *modify* it.
- In IoT, they form the **"perception and action"** boundary between the digital and physical worlds. *(3 marks: definition + example + IoT context)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. List any three differences between a microcontroller (MCU) and a single-board computer (SBC).**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Microcontroller (MCU) | Single-Board Computer (SBC) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Runs bare-metal or RTOS, no full OS | Runs full Linux / OS |
| 2 | Power < 1 W | Power 3 – 15 W |
| 3 | RAM in KB, Flash in KB – MB | RAM in GB, storage via microSD |
| 4 | Examples: ESP32, Arduino | Examples: Raspberry Pi 4 |

*(3 marks: any 3 valid differences)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Choice Question, Model Paper 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an IoT hardware node**. List the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An **LM35** sensor is interfaced to an ESP32 (12-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V). The ADC reading is **N = 1500**. Determine the measured temperature in °C. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a)**

The IoT hardware node has the following functional blocks:

1. **Sensors** — Perceive physical quantities (temperature, humidity, light).
2. **Signal Conditioning / ADC** — Amplify and digitize analog signals.
3. **Microcontroller / Processing Unit** — Local computation, decision logic, scheduling.
4. **Communication Module** — Transmits data via Wi-Fi / BLE / LoRa.
5. **Actuators** — Perform physical actions (relays, motors, LEDs).
6. **Power Supply** — Battery, regulator, energy harvesting circuit.

> **Block diagram (describe):**
> Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → MCU → (Radio ↔ Network) + (Actuator ↔ Physical World), all powered by the supply rail.

**Block-level role explanation (each block ~1 mark):**

- **Sensors:** Convert physical quantities to electrical signals; act as the *eyes and ears* of the IoT system.
- **Signal Conditioning:** Filters noise, amplifies weak signals (e.g., instrumentation amp for thermocouples).
- **ADC:** Converts analog voltage to digital count readable by the MCU.
- **MCU:** Runs the application logic — sampling rate, threshold detection, encryption, radio control.
- **Communication Module:** Encodes data into RF packets (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT).
- **Actuators:** Act on the environment based on decisions (e.g., turn on a fan if temperature > 35 °C).
- **Power:** Stable, regulated voltage rails; may include sleep-mode switching for energy efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to mention **signal conditioning** and **power management** as separate blocks. Drawing only "Sensor → MCU → Cloud" will *cost you 2–3 marks*. Always show **conditioning, ADC, and power** explicitly.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b)**

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, $N = 1500$, $S = 10 \text{ mV}/°C$.

**Step 1 — Compute ADC step size (LSB voltage):**

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} = 0.000806 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute input voltage:**

$$V_{in} = N \times V_{LSB} = 1500 \times 0.000806 = 1.209 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to temperature:**

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{S} = \frac{1.209 \text{ V}}{0.010 \text{ V/°C}} = 120.9 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 120.9 \text{ °C}}$$

**Sanity check:** ESP32 ADC is rated 0 – 3.3 V → 0 – 330 °C equivalent (since LM35 outputs 10 mV/°C). 120.9 °C is well within range. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, and LoRaWAN** IoT communication protocols in terms of **range, data rate, power consumption, and typical IoT application**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A battery-powered IoT sensor node operates in a duty-cycled mode: **active for 2 s** drawing **30 mA**, then **sleeps for 598 s** drawing **0.05 mA**. Calculate the average current draw and total battery life in days for a **2500 mAh** battery. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a)**

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi** | ~50 m | up to 1 Gbps | High | Indoor cameras, home gateways |
| **BLE 5.0** | ~100 m | 2 Mbps | Very Low | Wearables, beacons, health monitors |
| **Zigbee** | ~100 m | 250 kbps | Low | Mesh home automation, smart lighting |
| **LoRaWAN** | 2 – 15 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Very Low | Smart agriculture, smart metering, asset tracking |

**Selection rule of thumb:**
- **Short range + high data:** Wi-Fi
- **Short range + very low power:** BLE / Zigbee
- **Long range + tiny data:** LoRaWAN / NB-IoT
- **Mesh required:** Zigbee / Thread

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b)**

**Step 1 — Duty cycle:**

$$D = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{2}{600} = 0.00333$$

**Step 2 — Average current:**

$$I_{avg} = D \times 30 + (1 - D) \times 0.05$$

$$I_{avg} = 0.00333 \times 30 + 0.99667 \times 0.05$$

$$I_{avg} = 0.100 + 0.0498 = 0.1498 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Battery life in hours:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{2500}{0.1498} = 16{,}688 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{16{,}688}{24} = 695 \text{ days} \approx 1.9 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{I_{avg} \approx 0.15 \text{ mA}, \quad T_{battery} \approx 695 \text{ days}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Numerical):** A common mistake is to **forget the (1 − D) term** and use the active current alone, or to compute the sleep current with the *active duty cycle*. Always verify: $D + (1 - D) = 1$. Marks lost: usually 2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IoT Hardware (Module 2)**

- **Three Hardware Pillars:** Sensors + Actuators + MCUs/SBCs.
- **LM35 Sensitivity:** Always $10 \text{ mV}/°C$ — memorize it.
- **ADC Formula:** $N = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}(2^n - 1)$; reverse using $V_{in} = N \cdot V_{LSB}$.
- **MCU vs SBC:** MCU = no OS, low power, RTOS. SBC = full Linux, high power, gateway use.
- **ESP32** is the *most-tested* SoC in KTU papers (240 MHz, Wi-Fi + BLE, 12-bit ADC, 3.3 V).
- **Communication Trade-off:** Range vs. Data Rate vs. Power (you can optimise only 2 of 3).
- **LoRa** is for *long range, low data*; **Wi-Fi** is for *short range, high data*; **BLE** is for *very low power wearables*.
- **FSPL formula** is testable: $20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz).
- **Battery life formula:** $T = C_{mAh} / I_{avg}$ — average current uses **duty cycle weighting**.
- **Block diagram must include** signal conditioning, ADC, and power — not just sensor-to-cloud.
- **Duty cycling** is the primary energy-saving technique in battery IoT nodes.
- **Resolution vs. Accuracy:** Resolution is the smallest step; accuracy is closeness to truth.
- **Active vs. Passive Sensors:** Active needs external power; passive generates its own signal.
- **RTOS examples to know:** FreeRTOS, Contiki, Zephyr — relevant for MCU-side scheduling.
- **FreeRTOS** is the *most likely* RTOS mentioned in KTU questions.
- **Actuator equation:** $V = IR + L\dfrac{dI}{dt} + K_e \omega$ — know it for motor questions.
- **Smart agriculture** is the canonical KTU example for **LoRa + low-power MCU**.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question says "design a battery-powered IoT node for X application," your answer should *always* include — (1) chosen sensor with interface, (2) chosen MCU with justification, (3) chosen radio with range justification, (4) duty-cycle calculation, and (5) estimated battery life. This structure is what KTU examiners reward with full 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Software - Overview of Operating Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Software - Overview of Operating Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Operating System (IoT OS):** A specialized, resource-constrained system software that manages hardware resources, executes application logic, handles network communication, and provides real-time scheduling for devices operating within the **Internet of Things (IoT)** ecosystem. It is designed to operate efficiently on devices with limited **CPU (often < 200 MHz)**, **RAM (typically 10 KB – 512 KB)**, **ROM/Flash (100 KB – 4 MB)**, and strict **energy budgets (battery lifetimes measured in months to years)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the IoT OS is positioned as the **"middleware-firmware hybrid"** layer that sits between the bare-metal hardware (sensors, MCUs, radios) and the high-level IoT application logic. It abstracts the hardware so that developers can write portable, modular, network-aware applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller of Tiny Devices"

Imagine a busy **international airport** with hundreds of small propeller planes (IoT devices) landing and taking off continuously. There is no giant jumbo jet (cloud server) controlling them locally — each plane must coordinate with a **small, local control tower** that:
- Decides **when** each plane can take off (scheduling)
- Decides **which runway** to use (resource allocation)
- Maintains contact with neighboring towers (networking)
- Wakes up the pilot only when needed (power management)
- Speaks a common language with passengers (standard APIs)

That local control tower is exactly what an **IoT Operating System** does for sensors, microcontrollers, and edge nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** A traditional OS (Windows, Linux) is built for *abundance* (GBs of RAM, GHz CPUs, mains power). An **IoT OS is built for scarcity** — every byte of memory, every CPU cycle, every micro-ampere of current matters.

## 1.3 Why General-Purpose OSes Fail in IoT

| Property | Desktop / Server OS | IoT OS |
|---|---|---|
| RAM footprint | 512 MB – 64 GB | 10 KB – 512 KB |
| Power profile | Mains (watts to kilowatts) | Battery (µW to mW) |
| Real-time deadlines | Soft / best-effort | Hard / firm deadlines |
| Boot time | Seconds – minutes | Milliseconds |
| Update mechanism | OS-level package manager | Firmware OTA (Over-The-Air) |
| Connectivity stack | TCP/IP, sockets | 6LoWPAN, CoAP, MQTT, BLE |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this:** IoT OSes are **not** "stripped-down Linux." They are **architecturally distinct**, often event-driven instead of thread-heavy, and frequently built on a **monolithic or microkernel** design rather than the monolithic+modules hybrid used in GNU/Linux.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Architectural Components of an IoT OS

Every IoT OS — whether **Contiki, RIOT, TinyOS, FreeRTOS, Zephyr, or Mbed OS** — exposes the following logical layers:

1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** — Register-level drivers for GPIO, ADC, UART, SPI, I²C, timers, and radio transceivers.
2. **Kernel / Microkernel** — Minimal scheduler, IPC, and synchronization primitives.
3. **Memory Manager** — Static allocation pools (no MMU in most MCUs), often **stack-based** or **region-based** allocators.
4. **Networking Stack** — IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, MQTT-SN, BLE, Zigbee, LoRaWAN.
5. **File System (optional)** — LittleFS, Coffee FS, or simple flash wear-leveled stores.
6. **Device Driver Framework** — Pluggable drivers for sensors/actuators.
7. **Application Runtime / API** — POSIX-like or component-based API exposed to user code.
8. **Power Management Module** — Sleep modes, duty cycling, tickless idle.

## 2.2 Classification of IoT Operating Systems

IoT OSes can be classified along **three orthogonal axes** for KTU examinations:

### Axis A — By Scheduling Paradigm
- **Event-driven / Reactive:** TinyOS (uses *tasks* and *commands*, no preemptive threads).
- **Multi-threaded preemptive:** FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RIOT (uses priorities and time slicing).
- **Hybrid:** ContikiNG (protothreads + events).

### Axis B — By Kernel Architecture
- **Monolithic kernel:** Contiki, TinyOS — entire OS in one binary image.
- **Microkernel:** Zephyr, RIOT — minimal core + loadable services.

### Axis C — By Deployment Target
- **Constrained-node OSes (Class 0 / Class 1):** FreeRTOS, Contiki, TinyOS (RAM < 50 KB).
- **Rich-node OSes (Class 2):** Embedded Linux (OpenWrt, Yocto), Android Things — for gateways and single-board computers (RPi, BeagleBone).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination-grade** — memorize it for the 14-mark questions.

| IoT OS | Kernel Type | Scheduling | Min RAM | License | Key Strength | Wireless Stack |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TinyOS** | Monolithic | Event-driven (tasks) | ~1 KB | Open (BSD) | Mature academic OS for motes | IEEE 802.15.4 |
| **Contiki / ContikiNG** | Monolithic | Protothreads + events | ~10 KB | Open (BSD) | IPv6 / 6LoWPAN native | 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP |
| **RIOT** | Microkernel | Multi-threaded preemptive | ~1.5 KB | Open (LGPL) | POSIX-like API, developer-friendly | 6LoWPAN, BLE, LoRa |
| **FreeRTOS** | Microkernel | Preemptive + cooperative | ~4 KB | Open (MIT) | Industry standard, huge ecosystem | Vendor-specific |
| **Zephyr** | Microkernel | Priority + tickless preemptive | ~8 KB | Open (Apache 2.0) | Linux Foundation backed, safety certifiable | BLE, Wi-Fi, 802.15.4 |
| **Mbed OS** | Monolithic (event-driven) | Single-threaded event loop | ~10 KB | Open (Apache 2.0) | ARM Cortex-M optimized, online IDE | BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa |
| **Embedded Linux (Yocto/OpenWrt)** | Monolithic | CFS, real-time patches | ~32 MB | GPL / LGPL | Full Linux, gateway class | All IP stacks |

## 2.4 Design Goals (The "Five Pillars" of IoT OS Design)

1. **Small Footprint** — Fits in tens of KB.
2. **Determinism** — Bounded worst-case execution time (WCET) for real-time tasks.
3. **Energy Efficiency** — Aggressive duty cycling, sleep states.
4. **Connectivity** — Native low-power wireless protocol support.
5. **Security-by-Design** — Secure boot, encrypted storage, lightweight TLS (DTLS, TinyDTLS).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** A smart agricultural field uses **Contiki** on Class-0 motes to forward soil-moisture readings via **RPL** mesh routing, while a **Zephyr-based** gateway aggregates data and pushes it to the cloud over MQTT. Both devices are "IoT" but demand very different OSes.

## 2.5 Quantitative Metrics You Must Know

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU-expected numerical thresholds for IoT OS classification (per **RFC 7228 / IETF Terminology for Constrained-Node Networks**):

$$
\text{Class 0:} \quad \text{RAM} < 10 \text{ KB}, \quad \text{Flash} < 100 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
\text{Class 1:} \quad \text{RAM} \approx 10 \text{ KB}, \quad \text{Flash} \approx 100 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
\text{Class 2:} \quad \text{RAM} \approx 50 \text{ KB}, \quad \text{Flash} \approx 250 \text{ KB}
$$

These are the **only** formulae you need to memorize for this topic — they frequently appear as 3-mark questions in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Logic

> Since IoT OS selection is a *decision-engineering* problem rather than a pure math derivation, this section develops the **analytical decision matrix** that examiners expect students to construct in a 14-mark answer.

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right IoT OS

The selection of an IoT OS is governed by **six primary decision variables**. Each variable contributes a weighted score; the OS with the highest total is the engineering choice.

| Decision Variable $V_i$ | Weight $w_i$ | TinyOS | ContikiNG | RIOT | FreeRTOS | Zephyr |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RAM footprint minimization | 0.20 | 9 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Real-time determinism | 0.20 | 4 | 5 | 8 | 9 | 9 |
| IPv6 / 6LoWPAN support | 0.15 | 5 | 10 | 9 | 4 | 7 |
| POSIX-like developer API | 0.10 | 2 | 3 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Safety / certification (IEC 61508) | 0.15 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 7 | 9 |
| Community & industry adoption | 0.20 | 4 | 6 | 7 | 10 | 9 |

### Step-by-Step Weighted Score Computation

For each OS, the **Engineering Suitability Index (ESI)** is computed as:

$$
\text{ESI} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

where $s_i$ is the score of the OS on variable $V_i$ (on a 1–10 scale).

**Step 1** — Compute ESI for **TinyOS**:

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{TinyOS}} = (0.20 \cdot 9) + (0.20 \cdot 4) + (0.15 \cdot 5) + (0.10 \cdot 2) + (0.15 \cdot 2) + (0.20 \cdot 4)
$$

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{TinyOS}} = 1.80 + 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.80
$$

$$
\therefore \text{ESI}_{\text{TinyOS}} = 4.65
$$

**Step 2** — Compute ESI for **ContikiNG**:

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{ContikiNG}} = (0.20 \cdot 8) + (0.20 \cdot 5) + (0.15 \cdot 10) + (0.10 \cdot 3) + (0.15 \cdot 2) + (0.20 \cdot 6)
$$

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{ContikiNG}} = 1.60 + 1.00 + 1.50 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 1.20
$$

$$
\therefore \text{ESI}_{\text{ContikiNG}} = 5.90
$$

**Step 3** — Compute ESI for **RIOT**:

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{RIOT}} = (0.20 \cdot 9) + (0.20 \cdot 8) + (0.15 \cdot 9) + (0.10 \cdot 9) + (0.15 \cdot 3) + (0.20 \cdot 7)
$$

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{RIOT}} = 1.80 + 1.60 + 1.35 + 0.90 + 0.45 + 1.40
$$

$$
\therefore \text{ESI}_{\text{RIOT}} = 7.50
$$

**Step 4** — Compute ESI for **FreeRTOS**:

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{FreeRTOS}} = (0.20 \cdot 8) + (0.20 \cdot 9) + (0.15 \cdot 4) + (0.10 \cdot 7) + (0.15 \cdot 7) + (0.20 \cdot 10)
$$

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{FreeRTOS}} = 1.60 + 1.80 + 0.60 + 0.70 + 1.05 + 2.00
$$

$$
\therefore \text{ESI}_{\text{FreeRTOS}} = 7.75
$$

**Step 5** — Compute ESI for **Zephyr**:

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{Zephyr}} = (0.20 \cdot 7) + (0.20 \cdot 9) + (0.15 \cdot 7) + (0.10 \cdot 8) + (0.15 \cdot 9) + (0.20 \cdot 9)
$$

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{Zephyr}} = 1.40 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.80 + 1.35 + 1.80
$$

$$
\therefore \text{ESI}_{\text{Zephyr}} = 8.20
$$

**Step 6** — Rank the OSes:

$$
\text{ESI}_{\text{Zephyr}} \; (8.20) \; > \; \text{ESI}_{\text{FreeRTOS}} \; (7.75) \; > \; \text{ESI}_{\text{RIOT}} \; (7.50) \; > \; \text{ESI}_{\text{ContikiNG}} \; (5.90) \; > \; \text{ESI}_{\text{TinyOS}} \; (4.65)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** For a *modern, industrial-grade, safety-conscious* IoT deployment (e.g., medical wearables, industrial sensor hubs), **Zephyr** is the optimal choice. For *legacy/ultra-low-power research motes*, **ContikiNG** wins on IPv6 features. This decision matrix is the **canonical 14-mark answer** that KTU examiners look for.

## 3.2 Analytical Note on the Decision Logic

The weights $w_i$ are **not** universal — they shift with application context:
- For **medical / automotive** applications, set $w_{\text{safety}} = 0.35$ → Zephyr dominates further.
- For **smart agriculture** with **Class 0 motes**, set $w_{\text{RAM}} = 0.40$ → TinyOS / ContikiNG re-emerge as winners.

This is the **engineering judgement** a KTU 2024 graduate is expected to demonstrate in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of an IoT Operating System

> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of a generic IoT OS, showing how application code interacts with the kernel, hardware, and the external network.

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP1[Application Layer - User Code]:::userlayer
    APP2[Middleware - CoAP, MQTT, LWM2M]:::userlayer
    OSAPI[OS API Surface - POSIX or Component API]:::apilayer
    KERN[Kernel Core - Scheduler, IPC, Sync]:::kernlayer
    HAL[Hardware Abstraction Layer HAL]:::hallayer
    DRV1[Sensor Driver - Temp, Humidity, IMU]:::drv
    DRV2[Radio Driver - 802.15.4, BLE]:::drv
    DRV3[GPIO and Timer Driver]:::drv
    NET[Networking Stack - 6LoWPAN, RPL, TCP IP]:::netlayer
    PWR[Power Management Module]:::pwrlayer
    MEM[Memory Manager - Static Pools, Flash FS]:::memlayer
    MCU[Microcontroller Hardware Cortex M, AVR, ESP]:::hw

    APP1 --> OSAPI
    APP2 --> OSAPI
    OSAPI --> KERN
    KERN --> HAL
    HAL --> DRV1
    HAL --> DRV2
    HAL --> DRV3
    KERN --> NET
    KERN --> PWR
    KERN --> MEM
    HAL --> MCU
    NET --> DRV2
    PWR --> MCU

    classDef userlayer fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef apilayer fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef kernlayer fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hallayer fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef netlayer fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pwrlayer fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef memlayer fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Classification Flow of IoT Operating Systems

> This decision tree shows how a designer classifies the appropriate OS class based on the **hardware capability** of the target IoT node, as defined by **IETF RFC 7228**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start - Identify Target Node]:::startnode
    Q1{RAM less than 10 KB}:::qnode
    Q2{RAM between 10 KB and 50 KB}:::qnode
    Q3{RAM greater than 50 KB and runs Linux}:::qnode
    OS0[Class 0 - TinyOS, Contiki]:::osnode
    OS1[Class 1 - RIOT, FreeRTOS, Zephyr]:::osnode
    OS2[Class 2 - Embedded Linux, Android Things]:::osnode
    GATE[Gateway - Bridges to Cloud]:::gateway

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> OS0
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> OS1
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> OS2
    Q3 -- No --> GATE

    classDef startnode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef qnode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef osnode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gateway fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Power-State Transition Diagram of an IoT OS

> Modern IoT OSes implement **tickless idle** with multiple sleep states (per **ARM Cortex-M power modes**). The following state machine illustrates the lifecycle of a power-managed IoT node.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Sleep1: No Event for 1 ms
    Sleep1 --> Active: Interrupt or Radio IRQ
    Sleep1 --> Sleep2: Inactivity Threshold Exceeded
    Sleep2 --> Active: RTC Wakeup or Sensor Trigger
    Sleep2 --> DeepSleep: Inactivity over 60 s
    DeepSleep --> Active: Watchdog Reset or GPIO Edge
    Active --> Transmit: Application Publishes Data
    Transmit --> Active: ACK Received
    Transmit --> Sleep1: ACK Timeout
    note right of DeepSleep: Current Draw less than 2 microampere
    note right of Active: Current Draw approx 15 mA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose 4–6 marks on this topic by (a) confusing **kernel type with scheduling type**, (b) writing only **names** of OSes without architectural reasoning, and (c) omitting the **energy / real-time** angle. Always include the **"why this OS, not that one"** justification in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an IoT Operating System and list any four design constraints that distinguish it from a general-purpose OS. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **IoT Operating System (IoT OS)** is a lightweight system software designed to run on resource-constrained devices (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers) within an IoT network. It manages hardware, networking, and application execution under strict power, memory, and real-time constraints.

**Four design constraints:**

1. **Small memory footprint** — must operate within **10 KB – 512 KB RAM** (Class 0/1 nodes).
2. **Low power consumption** — supports **deep sleep states (< 2 µA)** and aggressive duty cycling to extend battery life to years.
3. **Real-time responsiveness** — must guarantee **bounded worst-case execution time (WCET)** for time-critical tasks.
4. **Built-in wireless protocol support** — natively supports **6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, BLE, Zigbee** without bulky external libraries.

*(1 mark definition + 0.5 mark per constraint = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between TinyOS and Contiki in terms of execution model and networking support. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TinyOS | Contiki |
|---|---|---|
| Execution model | **Event-driven** — uses *tasks* and *commands*; no preemptive threads. | **Protothreads + events** — lightweight stackless threads. |
| Scheduling | Non-preemptive FIFO of tasks. | Cooperative, event-driven with protothread context switch. |
| Networking | IEEE 802.15.4 MAC; **no native IPv6**. | **Native IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / RPL / CoAP**. |
| Application language | **nesC** (C dialect). | Standard C. |
| Typical use | Academic motes, MicaZ, TelosB. | IPv6-based WSN deployments. |

*(1.5 marks execution model + 1.5 marks networking = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both have sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the layered architecture of a typical IoT Operating System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) and the Power Management Module. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The layered architecture of an IoT OS typically consists of **five layers**:

**Layer 1 — Application Layer:** Hosts user code (sensor reading, actuation logic) and middleware (CoAP/MQTT clients).

**Layer 2 — OS API / Runtime:** Exposes POSIX-like or component-based APIs (e.g., `thread_create()`, `sem_wait()`).

**Layer 3 — Kernel Core:** Contains the **scheduler** (preemptive priority-based or event-driven), **IPC mechanisms** (message queues, mailboxes, signals), and **synchronization primitives** (mutexes, semaphores).

**Layer 4 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Provides uniform register-level access to peripherals. The HAL **decouples** the kernel from the silicon vendor (e.g., STM32 vs. nRF52). Role: portability, faster driver porting, and testability via stub HALs.

**Layer 5 — Microcontroller Hardware:** The actual CPU, memory, radios, and sensors.

**Role of the Power Management Module:**
- Implements **tickless idle** — the scheduler stops the system tick when no task is runnable, putting the CPU into **WFI (Wait For Interrupt)** state.
- Manages **DCDC converter states, peripheral clock gating, and radio duty cycling**.
- Coordinates **wake-up sources** (RTC alarms, GPIO edges, radio IRQ).
- Goal: achieve **average current < 50 µA** for battery-powered Class 1 nodes.

*(2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for layer explanation, 2 marks HAL role, 1 mark power management — total 7 marks)*

---

#### (b) Compare FreeRTOS, RIOT, and Zephyr IoT operating systems across at least six engineering parameters. Justify which one is best suited for a safety-critical industrial IoT application. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | FreeRTOS | RIOT | Zephyr |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kernel | Microkernel | Microkernel | Microkernel |
| Scheduling | Preemptive priority | Multi-threaded preemptive | Preemptive + tickless |
| Min RAM | **~4 KB** | **~1.5 KB** | **~8 KB** |
| API style | Custom | POSIX-like | POSIX + custom |
| Certification | SafeRTOS variant | None | **IEC 61508 SIL 3, ISO 26262** |
| Connectivity | Vendor libs | 6LoWPAN, BLE, LoRa | BLE, Wi-Fi, 802.15.4 |
| Community | Very large (Amazon) | Academic / open | Linux Foundation backed |

**Justification for safety-critical industrial IoT:**

**Zephyr** is the optimal choice for safety-critical industrial deployments because:
1. It is the **only** OS among the three with **built-in certification evidence** for IEC 61508 SIL 3 and ISO 26262 (ASIL-D) — mandatory for industrial / automotive IoT.
2. Its **tickless idle scheduler** delivers deterministic wake-ups with bounded jitter, essential for real-time control loops.
3. The **Linux Foundation governance** ensures long-term maintenance, security patching, and vendor neutrality — critical for industrial product lifecycles (10+ years).
4. The **device tree and Kconfig** build system allows fine-grained footprint and feature control, enabling certification audits.

FreeRTOS, although popular, requires the proprietary **SafeRTOS** variant for full certification — adding cost. RIOT lacks formal certification evidence, making it unsuitable for safety-critical regulated environments.

*(2 marks comparison table, 3 marks justification, 2 marks conclusion — total 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the function of each component of an IoT Operating System: scheduler, memory manager, networking stack, and file system. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Scheduler:** Decides *which* task runs *when*.
- **Preemptive priority-based:** FreeRTOS, Zephyr.
- **Event-driven (FIFO of tasks):** TinyOS.
- **Tickless variant:** Zephyr / ContikiNG for power saving.
- Ensures **bounded worst-case latency** for real-time tasks.

**2. Memory Manager:** Allocates and deallocates RAM to tasks.
- **Static allocation pools** are preferred over `malloc()` to avoid fragmentation on MCUs without MMU.
- Stack sizes are pre-computed at compile time.
- Example: Contiki uses **memory blocks**; FreeRTOS uses `heap_1`..`heap_5` schemes.

**3. Networking Stack:** Provides IP connectivity.
- Lower layers: **IEEE 802.15.4** MAC, radio drivers.
- Adaptation layer: **6LoWPAN** (compresses IPv6 headers).
- Routing: **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks).
- Application layer: **CoAP** (constrained HTTP equivalent), **MQTT-SN** (sensor-network variant of MQTT).
- Security: **DTLS / TinyDTLS** for lightweight TLS.

**4. File System:** Persists data to internal flash.
- **LittleFS** (used in Zephyr, Mbed): power-loss resilient, wear-leveled.
- **Coffee FS** (Contiki): designed for small flash chips.
- Used for configuration storage, OTA staging, and sensor log buffering.

*(1.5 marks for diagram, 5.5 marks for component-wise explanation = 7 marks)*

---

#### (b) Discuss the design considerations for selecting an IoT OS for a Class 0 constrained device. What factors make ContikiNG a preferred choice in such scenarios? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Design considerations for a Class 0 device (RAM < 10 KB):**

1. **Footprint:** The entire OS + application must fit in < 100 KB flash and < 10 KB RAM. This rules out Linux, Android, and most monolithic RTOSes.
2. **Energy:** Battery-only operation requires **sleep currents < 5 µA** and **radio duty cycle < 1%**.
3. **Real-time behavior:** Even Class 0 devices (e.g., intrusion sensors, fire alarms) need deterministic response — typically **< 100 ms** latency from event to transmission.
4. **Network stack:** Must support **6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP** for IP-based interoperability with the wider IoT.
5. **Programming model:** The OS must support **event-driven or protothread** execution — full preemptive threading wastes precious RAM on stacks.
6. **Update mechanism:** Firmware OTA must be supported in tiny chunks (delta updates) due to bandwidth constraints.

**Why ContikiNG is preferred for Class 0:**

- **Footprint:** ContikiNG's core runs in **~10 KB RAM and ~30 KB flash** — the lowest among IPv6-capable OSes.
- **Native IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / RPL / CoAP stack** — out-of-the-box, no porting effort.
- **Protothread model** — gives the *illusion* of multi-threading with a single stack, saving memory.
- **Coffee file system** — power-loss-safe logging on tiny flash.
- **RIME stack** — provides multi-hop mesh routing with minimal code footprint, ideal for dense sensor deployments.
- **Open BSD license** — enables commercial deployment without licensing fees.

*(3 marks design considerations, 4 marks ContikiNG justification = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following bullets verbatim for the KTU 2024 ESE.**

- An **IoT OS** is a lightweight system software for **Class 0 / Class 1 / Class 2** constrained devices (RFC 7228).
- **Class 0** = RAM < 10 KB (e.g., TinyOS, Contiki); **Class 1** = RAM ≈ 10 KB (e.g., RIOT, FreeRTOS, Zephyr); **Class 2** = RAM ≈ 50 KB+ (e.g., Embedded Linux).
- **TinyOS** = event-driven, nesC, no native IPv6. **Contiki** = protothreads, native IPv6 / 6LoWPAN. **RIOT** = POSIX-like, microkernel. **FreeRTOS** = industry standard, preemptive. **Zephyr** = safety-certifiable, Linux Foundation backed.
- Five pillars of IoT OS design: **small footprint, determinism, energy efficiency, connectivity, security**.
- Key networking protocols: **6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, MQTT-SN, DTLS**.
- Key power features: **tickless idle, sleep states, RTC wakeups, radio duty cycling**.
- For **safety-critical** applications (industrial, medical, automotive) → prefer **Zephyr**.
- For **academic IPv6 mesh research** → prefer **ContikiNG**.
- For **legacy industry adoption + huge ecosystem** → prefer **FreeRTOS**.
- Always include a **justification matrix** (weight × score) in 14-mark OS selection answers.
- **Common valuation pitfall:** do not confuse *kernel architecture* (monolithic vs. microkernel) with *scheduling paradigm* (event-driven vs. preemptive) — they are orthogonal axes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firmware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Firmware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Firmware** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) is defined as the **permanent, semi-permanent, or rewritten software program** that is programmed into the read-only memory (ROM), flash memory, or EEPROM of an embedded IoT device to provide low-level control, hardware abstraction, and core functional logic for the device's specific hardware components.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEERT614)**, firmware is the lowest tier of the IoT software stack, sitting directly above the silicon/hardware layer. It is distinct from regular software because it is tightly coupled to a specific microcontroller unit (MCU) or System on Chip (SoC) and is rarely (though increasingly) updated over the device's lifetime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Firmware is positioned as the "**brain of the IoT node**" — it bridges the hardware (sensors/actuators) and the network/application layer. Without firmware, an IoT device is an inert collection of electronic components.

**Core Properties of IoT Firmware:**
1. **Hardware-Specific:** Written for a particular MCU architecture (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, ESP32, RISC-V, PIC).
2. **Resource-Constrained:** Must operate within tight memory (< 512 KB RAM typical), CPU cycles, and power budgets.
3. **Persistent or Semi-Persistent:** Stored in non-volatile memory; survives power cycles.
4. **Real-Time Oriented:** Often must respond to sensor events within deterministic time bounds (milliseconds).
5. **Updateable (Modern IoT):** Supports Over-the-Air (OTA) updates, though historically considered immutable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Manager"

Imagine a **smart building** (your IoT device):
- The **concrete structure, pipes, and electrical wires** = the **Hardware** (sensors, microcontrollers, antennas).
- The **Building Manager** who lives inside the building 24/7, knows where every switch and valve is, and decides when to turn on the elevator, dim the lobby lights, and unlock the door when the fire alarm rings = the **Firmware**.
- The **Building Manager's rulebook** (procedures he follows) = the **Firmware Code**.
- The **Owner (you)** who occasionally calls the manager to update his rulebook = the **OTA Update Mechanism**.

The Building Manager (firmware) is **always resident** in the building, **knows the building intimately** (hardware-specific), has **limited resources** (no army of staff — just him and a walkie-talkie), and must respond **immediately** to events (real-time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Firmware is NOT the same as "Software":**
> Regular software (e.g., Microsoft Word) is hardware-independent and can be uninstalled. Firmware is fused with the hardware identity; removing it renders the device non-functional (a "bricked" device).

### 1.3 Position in the IoT Protocol Stack

In the **4-layer IoT Architecture** taught in KTU Module 1, firmware operates at the **Perception/Device Layer**:

| Layer | Function | Example Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | Business logic, dashboards | Cloud Web App |
| **Network/Transport Layer** | Data routing, protocols | MQTT, CoAP, Wi-Fi |
| **Edge/Gateway Layer** | Local processing, protocol translation | Raspberry Pi Gateway |
| **Device/Perception Layer** | **Firmware + Hardware** | ESP32 + DHT22 sensor |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for Exam:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Where does firmware fit in IoT?", the precise answer is: *"Firmware is the device-level software embedded in the microcontroller that directly controls hardware peripherals and provides the Application Programming Interface (API) to upper layers."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Firmware Architecture — The 5-Layer Internal Model

Modern IoT firmware is not a monolithic blob of code. It is structured as a **layered architecture** to manage complexity and enable portability:

**Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)**
- The lowest software layer.
- Provides uniform APIs to read GPIO pins, configure timers, drive ADC/DAC, manage UART/SPI/I²C buses.
- Example: STM32 HAL, ESP-IDF `driver` library, Arduino `digitalWrite()`.
- **Why:** Allows upper layers to remain agnostic of the specific MCU vendor's register-level complexity.

**Layer 2 — Board Support Package (BSP)**
- Configures the specific development board (e.g., NodeMCU, Arduino Uno, custom PCB).
- Maps physical pins to logical names (`LED_PIN = GPIO2`).
- Defines clock tree, pin muxing, and peripheral initialization sequences.

**Layer 3 — Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Kernel (Optional but Common)**
- Examples: FreeRTOS, Zephyr, Contiki-NG, RIOT.
- Provides: **Task scheduling**, semaphores, queues, mutexes, timers, memory pools.
- **Why:** Modern IoT devices run multiple concurrent activities (read sensor, transmit via Wi-Fi, blink LED, monitor battery). A bare-metal `while(1)` loop is insufficient.

**Layer 4 — Middleware / Networking Stack**
- Implements communication protocols: **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, LwM2M, BLE, LoRaWAN**.
- Contains cryptographic libraries (mbedTLS, wolfSSL) for TLS/DTLS.
- Handles data serialization (CBOR, JSON, Protocol Buffers).

**Layer 5 — Application Logic**
- The "business logic" of the IoT node.
- Example: Read temperature every 30 seconds; if > 50°C, publish alert to MQTT broker; if battery < 20%, enter deep sleep.

### 2.2 Firmware Development Lifecycle (FDLC) — KTU High-Yield

The lifecycle of IoT firmware follows a strict 6-stage process, frequently asked in 14-mark KTU questions:

1. **Requirements Engineering** — Define sensors, sample rates, power budget, network protocol.
2. **Architecture & Design** — Choose bare-metal vs. RTOS; select HAL; design state machine.
3. **Coding** — Write in C/C++ (most common), MicroPython (prototyping), Rust (emerging).
4. **Compilation & Linking** — Cross-compilation using `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `xtensa-esp32-elf-gcc`.
5. **Testing & Debugging** — Unit tests on host; integration tests on hardware; logic analyzers, JTAG/SWD debuggers.
6. **Deployment & OTA Maintenance** — Flash via UART/USB/OTA; manage versioning; security patches.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics and equations are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions. Memorize the units!

| Metric / Equation | Formula / Value | Description | Typical IoT Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Firmware Image Size Constraint** | $F_{size} \le F_{flash} - F_{bootloader} - F_{reserved}$ | Available flash for app | KB / MB |
| **Average Current Draw** | $I_{avg} = \frac{(I_{active} \cdot t_{active}) + (I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep})}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}$ | Battery life estimation | mA |
| **Battery Life (Hours)** | $L_h = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}$ | Where $C$ in mAh | Hours |
| **Task Deadline (RTOS)** | $D_i \le T_i$ (period) | Real-time schedulability | ms |
| **CPU Utilization (Rate Monotonic)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu & Layland bound | dimensionless |
| **FOTA Delta Size Ratio** | $R_{delta} = \frac{\Delta F_{ota}}{F_{full}} \times 100\%$ | Bandwidth efficiency | % |
| **Watchdog Timeout** | $T_{wdt} = \frac{P_{prescaler} \cdot R_{reload}}{f_{clock}}$ | System recovery | ms |
| **Power Consumption (Active)** | $P_{active} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{active}$ | DC power draw | mW |

> **Note on Markdown Tables:** All absolute-value and conditional notations have been written using `\vert` or `\le` (e.g., $F_{size} \le F_{flash}$) to preserve table integrity. Do **not** substitute the pipe character `|` in your answer sheets either.

### 2.4 Why Firmware is Engineering-Critical in IoT

- **Security Surface:** Firmware bugs in IoT have caused real-world disasters (Mirai botnet exploited default firmware credentials in 2016).
- **Safety-Critical Applications:** Medical IoT (pacemakers, insulin pumps), automotive IoT (ADAS) — firmware failure = life loss.
- **Scalability:** A single firmware bug may brick **millions of deployed devices**; hence robust OTA rollback is mandatory.
- **Power Optimization:** Firmware decisions (sleep modes, peripheral gating) determine whether a node lasts 1 week or 5 years on a coin cell.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Battery Life Calculation for an IoT Node

A KTU-favourite numerical problem. Let us derive the operating lifetime of a battery-powered IoT sensor node that uses duty-cycling.

**Given:**
- Active mode current: $I_{active} = 30 \text{ mA}$
- Sleep mode current: $I_{sleep} = 10 \text{ \mu A}$
- Active duration per cycle: $t_{active} = 200 \text{ ms}$
- Sleep duration per cycle: $t_{sleep} = 4800 \text{ ms}$ (Total cycle = 5 s, 4% duty cycle)
- Battery capacity: $C_{battery} = 2400 \text{ mAh}$

**Step 1 — Compute the average current draw per duty cycle.**

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{(I_{active} \cdot t_{active}) + (I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep})}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (convert µA → mA: $10 \text{ \mu A} = 0.01 \text{ mA}$).**

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{(30 \text{ mA} \times 200 \text{ ms}) + (0.01 \text{ mA} \times 4800 \text{ ms})}{200 \text{ ms} + 4800 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
Numerator = 6000 + 48 = 6048 \text{ mA \cdot ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
Denominator = 5000 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{6048}{5000} = 1.2096 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute battery life in hours.**

$$
L_h = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{1.2096 \text{ mA}} \approx 1984.13 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to days.**

$$
L_{days} = \frac{1984.13}{24} \approx 82.67 \text{ days} \approx 2.7 \text{ months}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state units explicitly. KTU examiners award 1 mark for the final correct unit (e.g., "days", "hours"). A bare number = -1 mark.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Firmware Image Size Budget for an ESP32-Based Node

**Given:**
- Total flash: $F_{flash} = 4 \text{ MB}$ (typical ESP32)
- Bootloader: $F_{bootloader} = 32 \text{ KB}$
- Partition table: $F_{partition} = 4 \text{ KB}$
- OTA reserved partition: $F_{ota} = 1.28 \text{ MB}$ (for firmware A + firmware B)
- NVS (non-volatile storage) for credentials: $F_{nvs} = 16 \text{ KB}$
- File system (SPIFFS/LittleFS) for logs: $F_{fs} = 256 \text{ KB}$

**Step 1 — State the constraint equation.**

$$
F_{size} \le F_{flash} - F_{bootloader} - F_{partition} - F_{ota} - F_{nvs} - F_{fs}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert all to consistent unit (KB).**

$$
F_{flash} = 4 \text{ MB} = 4096 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
F_{size} \le 4096 - 32 - 4 - 1280 - 16 - 256
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the overhead subtotal.**

$$
Overhead = 32 + 4 + 1280 + 16 + 256 = 1588 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute available firmware size.**

$$
F_{size} \le 4096 - 1588 = 2508 \text{ KB} \approx 2.45 \text{ MB}
$$
