<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **List and briefly explain any six major limitations of IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Power Constraint:** Battery-operated nodes have a finite energy budget; replacing batteries in massive deployments is operationally infeasible. *(0.5 marks)*

2. **Security Vulnerabilities:** Many devices ship with default credentials, lack encryption, and are vulnerable to botnet recruitment (e.g., Mirai attack, 2016). *(0.5 marks)*

3. **Interoperability:** Multiple competing protocols (MQTT, CoAP, AMQP) and data formats prevent seamless device-to-device communication across vendors. *(0.5 marks)*

4. **Scalability of Data:** The 5 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) of big data generated by IoT overwhelm traditional storage and processing systems. *(0.5 marks)*

5. **Bandwidth and Latency:** Constrained wireless links (ZigBee = 250 kbps, LoRa = 0.3–50 kbps) cannot support high-throughput or ultra-low-latency applications like autonomous vehicles. *(0.5 marks)*

6. **Privacy Concerns:** Continuous sensor data collection enables user surveillance and profiling, conflicting with regulations like GDPR and India's DPDP Act 2023. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Explain the security challenges in IoT with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

IoT security is one of the most critical limitations because billions of resource-constrained devices are deployed in physically accessible, often unmonitored locations. The major security challenges are:

- **Weak Authentication:** Many devices use hardcoded default credentials (admin/admin). The **Mirai botnet (October 2016)** infected over **145,000 IoT cameras and DVRs** using only 61 default username-password pairs, launching a **1.2 Tbps DDoS attack** that disrupted Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and Dyn DNS infrastructure. *(1 mark)*

- **Limited Cryptographic Capability:** Constrained 8-bit / 32-bit MCUs cannot execute full **TLS 1.3** or **AES-256** without exceeding their memory and compute budgets, forcing designers to use weaker or no encryption. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Insecure OTA Updates:** Unencrypted or unsigned firmware update channels allow attackers to inject malware (e.g., **VPNFilter malware, 2018**, which infected 500,000 routers). *(0.5 marks)*

- **Physical Tampering:** Attackers with physical access can extract firmware, dump cryptographic keys, and clone devices. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Botnet Recruitment:** Compromised IoT devices are weaponized for large-scale DDoS, cryptocurrency mining, or as entry points into home/enterprise networks. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the device-level and network-level limitations of IoT. How do these constraints affect the design of large-scale IoT deployments?

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain how energy harvesting techniques help overcome the power limitation in IoT nodes. Provide at least two real-world deployment examples.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Device-Level Limitations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Power Constraint:** *(1 mark)*
   IoT nodes are predominantly battery-powered. The fundamental equation governing node lifetime is:

   $$t_{life} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$$

   where $I_{avg} = I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) + I_{active} \cdot DC$. For a 2400 mAh battery with $I_{avg} \approx 0.51 \text{ mA}$, the lifetime is only **~6.5 months**. Designers must therefore aggressively duty-cycle the node.

2. **Memory & Storage:** *(1 mark)*
   Typical IoT MCUs (e.g., **ATmega328P, MSP430, ESP32**) have **2 KB – 520 KB** of RAM and **32 KB – 4 MB** of flash. This rules out heavy OSes (Linux/RTOS variants like **Contiki, RIOT, Zephyr** are used instead) and full cryptographic stacks.

3. **Compute Limitation:** *(0.5 mark)*
   CPU clock speeds of **8 MHz – 240 MHz** cannot support on-device deep learning inference or real-time video analytics at the edge.

4. **Form Factor & Cost:** *(1 mark)*
   The "$1 sensor" target for massive IoT deployment forces manufacturers to omit security features, use lower-quality components, and reduce BOM (Bill of Materials).

**Network-Level Limitations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Bandwidth Scarcity:** *(1 mark)*
   ZigBee offers only **250 kbps**; BLE offers **1–2 Mbps**; LoRaWAN offers **0.3–50 kbps**. These are orders of magnitude below Wi-Fi/5G.

2. **Latency:** *(1 mark)*
   Multi-hop wireless routing through RPL in 6LoWPAN can introduce **seconds** of latency, unacceptable for **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** or **industrial closed-loop control** requiring **< 1 ms** latency.

3. **Coverage Gaps:** *(0.5 mark)*
   Indoor, underground (mining), and underwater (marine) deployments suffer from severe attenuation.

4. **Address Exhaustion & Routing:** *(1 mark)*
   IPv4 cannot scale to billions of devices; IPv6 + 6LoWPAN is required but introduces header compression complexity and RPL routing overhead.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Energy Harvesting Architecture (Block Diagram + Explanation):**

A typical energy-harvesting IoT node consists of the following blocks:

```
[Energy Source] → [Harvesting Circuit] → [Energy Storage] → [Power Management] → [IoT Node]
   (Solar/Vibration/Thermal/RF)  (Rectifier/Boost)  (Battery/Supercap)  (PMIC + Sleep Controller)  (MCU + Sensor + Radio)
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Energy Source:** *(1 mark)*
   - **Solar (Photovoltaic):** Used in agriculture and smart city deployments.
   - **Vibration/Piezoelectric:** Used in industrial machinery monitoring.
   - **Thermal (Thermoelectric):** Used in boiler/pipeline monitoring.
   - **RF Harvesting:** Used in RFID-style passive IoT.

2. **Harvesting Circuit:** *(1 mark)*
   Converts the raw ambient energy into usable DC voltage. Example: a **bridge rectifier + boost converter** for piezoelectric, or an **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** controller for solar.

3. **Energy Storage:** *(1 mark)*
   A small rechargeable **Li-ion battery**, **supercapacitor**, or **thin-film solid-state battery** stores harvested energy for use during low-harvest periods (e.g., night-time for solar).

4. **Power Management IC (PMIC):** *(1 mark)*
   Implements **cold-start, sleep scheduling, and adaptive load shedding**. The PMIC wakes the MCU only when the harvested energy exceeds a threshold.

5. **IoT Node (MCU + Sensor + Radio):** *(1 mark)*
   Operates in a strict duty cycle (e.g., 1% active, 99% sleep) to match the harvested energy budget.

**Real-World Deployment Examples:** *(2 marks)*

- **Example 1 — Smart Agriculture (Semtech LoRa + Solar):** *(1 mark)*
  Solar-powered soil moisture sensors deployed across vineyards in Spain operate for **> 5 years** without battery replacement, transmitting data every 15 minutes via LoRaWAN.

- **Example 2 — Industrial Machine Monitoring (Piezoelectric + TI MSP430):** *(1 mark)*
  Vibration-harvesting wireless sensors mounted on rotating machinery in factories report bearing health continuously, eliminating wired power and enabling predictive maintenance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Critically analyze the interoperability and standardization limitations of IoT. Discuss how emerging frameworks like **Matter** and **OneM2M** attempt to address them.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the privacy and regulatory limitations of IoT. How do GDPR (EU) and the DPDP Act (India 2023) impact IoT data handling?

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**The Interoperability Problem (4 marks):**

1. **Protocol Fragmentation:** *(1 mark)*
   No single dominant IoT protocol exists. Application-layer protocols include **MQTT** (IBM/OASIS), **CoAP** (IETF RFC 7252), **AMQP** (financial messaging), **HTTP/REST** (web-style), **DDS** (real-time pub/sub), and **LwM2M** (device management). This fragmentation prevents seamless device-to-cloud and device-to-device communication.

2. **Data Format Heterogeneity:** *(1 mark)*
   Devices emit **JSON, XML, CBOR, Protocol Buffers, or proprietary binary** formats. A smart home gateway must parse dozens of formats, increasing complexity.

3. **Vendor Lock-in:** *(1 mark)*
   Cloud platforms — **AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT** — use proprietary SDKs and APIs. Migrating a 10,000-sensor fleet from AWS to Azure is a multi-month engineering effort.

4. **Lack of Universal Semantics:** *(1 mark)*
   The temperature reading "23.5" is meaningless without semantic context (°C or °F? indoor or outdoor? sensor calibration timestamp?). IoT lacks a **universal ontology** analogous to the semantic web.

**Emerging Solutions (3 marks):**

1. **Matter (formerly Project CHIP):** *(1.5 marks)*
   Launched in 2022 by the **Connectivity Standards Alliance (CSA)**, Matter is an open-source application-layer protocol that unifies **Wi-Fi, Thread, and Ethernet** under a common data model. Backed by **Apple, Google, Amazon, Samsung**, it enables cross-ecosystem smart home interoperability (e.g., an Apple HomeKit device can control a Google Nest thermostat).

2. **OneM2M:** *(1.5 marks)*
   A global standards initiative (ETSI, TIA, TTA, CCSA, ARIB, TTC) defining a **horizontal IoT service platform** with a common **resource model** and **RESTful APIs**, enabling application-vertical-agnostic data exchange across industries (smart energy, healthcare, transportation).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Privacy Limitations of IoT (3 marks):**

1. **Continuous Surveillance:** *(1 mark)*
   Smart speakers (Alexa, Google Home) and wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch) collect **always-on audio, video, biometric, and location data**, enabling mass surveillance.

2. **Inference Attacks:** *(1 mark)*
   Even anonymized smart meter data can reveal whether a household is empty, the number of occupants, and their sleep patterns — through **non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM)**.

3. **Informed Consent Gap:** *(1 mark)*
   IoT devices often collect data without explicit user awareness; **privacy policies** are rarely read.

**Regulatory Framework (4 marks):**

1. **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU, 2018):** *(2 marks)*
   - Defines personal data broadly to include **IP addresses, cookie IDs, and location data**.
   - Grants users the **right to be forgotten, data portability, and explicit consent**.
   - Imposes fines up to **€20 million or 4% of global annual turnover**.
   - IoT manufacturers must implement **Privacy by Design** and **Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA)**.

2. **DPDP Act (Digital Personal Data Protection Act, India, 2023):** *(2 marks)*
   - Establishes the **Data Protection Board of India** as the enforcement authority.
   - Requires **explicit, informed consent** for data collection.
   - Imposes penalties up to **₹250 crore** for significant data breaches.
   - Mandates **data fiduciaries** (IoT operators) to implement reasonable security safeguards and breach notification within **72 hours**.

**Engineering Impact:** IoT system designers must now embed **consent management modules, audit logging, and data minimization** directly into firmware — adding compute, memory, and development overhead that further stresses resource-constrained devices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT list limitations without explaining them.** A bare list earns **< 1 mark** per limitation. Always pair each limitation with a **real-world example, impact, and (in Part B) a mitigation**.
> 2. **Confusing "Limitations of IoT" with "Challenges of IoT":** In KTU's strict sense, *Limitations* refers to **inherent technical constraints** (power, bandwidth, security), while *Challenges* includes **broader deployment issues** (business models, user adoption). The examiner expects you to primarily focus on technical limitations.
> 3. **Forgetting 6LoWPAN and RPL:** When discussing IPv6/addressing limitations, KTU specifically expects the **6LoWPAN adaptation layer** and **RPL routing protocol** to be mentioned. Omitting these costs **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Mirai botnet date and numbers:** Examiners *love* testing **October 2016, 145,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps**. Wrong numbers = 0 marks for that sub-point.
> 5. **Confusing MQTT with CoAP:** MQTT runs over **TCP** (port 1883/8883); CoAP runs over **UDP** (port 5683). Mixing these up is a frequent KTU pitfall.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in Part B:** A neatly labeled block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself. Always include one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ IoT limitations are classified into **7 pillars**: Device, Network, Security, Interoperability, Data, Regulatory, Application-specific.
- ✅ The **Mirai botnet (October 2016)** is the gold-standard example for IoT security limitations — remember **145,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps, default credentials**.
- ✅ **Power** is the **#1 constraint** in IoT. Master the formula $I_{avg} = I_{sleep}(1-DC) + I_{active} \cdot DC$ and the duty cycle equation.
- ✅ **IPv6 + 6LoWPAN + RPL** is the canonical triad for IoT networking. KTU expects all three terms in any addressing question.
- ✅ Key IoT protocols and their transport: **MQTT → TCP (1883)**, **CoAP → UDP (5683)**, **AMQP → TCP (5672)**, **DDS → UDP/RTPS**, **LwM2M → CoAP/UDP**.
- ✅ Typical IoT data rates: **ZigBee = 250 kbps**, **BLE = 1–2 Mbps**, **LoRaWAN = 0.3–50 kbps**, **NB-IoT = 26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL)**.
- ✅ Energy harvesting sources: **Solar, Vibration (Piezo), Thermal (TEG), RF**. PMIC + supercapacitor/battery is the standard storage architecture.
- ✅ The **Matter protocol** (CSA, 2022) unifies **Wi-Fi + Thread + Ethernet** for smart homes; **OneM2M** is the horizontal IoT service platform standard.
- ✅ **GDPR (EU, 2018)** and **DPDP Act (India, 2023)** are the two most-cited regulations; both enforce explicit consent and breach notification.
- ✅ Always mention **Privacy by Design, Data Minimization, and Edge Computing** as KTU-preferred mitigation strategies.
- ✅ Common bandwidth/latency vs range trade-off: **higher range = lower bandwidth** (LoRa), **higher bandwidth = higher power** (Wi-Fi).
- ✅ The **5 V's of Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) is the KTU-preferred framework for data-management limitations.
- ✅ For 14-mark Part B answers, always include a **labeled block diagram** — it carries **2 marks minimum** independently of text.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth Technology - Bluetooth Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth Technology - Bluetooth Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bluetooth** is a short-range, low-power, low-cost wireless personal area network (WPAN) technology that operates in the unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) band**, standardized under **IEEE 802.15.1** (for legacy versions) and currently governed by the **Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG)**.

The **Bluetooth Stack** is the layered communication protocol architecture that governs how two or more Bluetooth devices discover, connect, exchange data, and manage power efficiently. It is a vertically partitioned protocol suite in which each layer performs a specific function, communicates only with its adjacent upper and lower layers through well-defined **Service Access Points (SAPs)**, and contributes to establishing a secure, reliable, ad-hoc piconet/scatternet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEERT614 - Module 3):**
> Students must understand the layered architecture of the Bluetooth protocol stack, the role of each layer (Radio, Baseband, LMP, HCI, L2CAP, SDP, RFCOMM, and Application Profiles), the piconet/scatternet topology, and the connection establishment procedure. Questions are typically asked from layer functions, frame formats, and protocol state machines.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Postal System"

Imagine Bluetooth communication as **sending a registered parcel through a postal system**:

| Postal System Element | Bluetooth Stack Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Postal truck on road | **Radio Layer (Physical)** | Carries the raw signal across the wireless medium |
| Sorting office | **Baseband Layer** | Organizes into proper time slots and frequency channels |
| Mail supervisor | **Link Manager Protocol (LMP)** | Sets up, authenticates, and controls the link |
| Reception desk (interface) | **HCI (Host Controller Interface)** | Passes commands between host and controller |
| Multi-department building manager | **L2CAP** | Routes packets to correct higher-layer protocols |
| Yellow Pages directory | **SDP (Service Discovery Protocol)** | Helps find what services are available |
| Telex / serial cable | **RFCOMM** | Provides serial port emulation over Bluetooth |
| End user (customer) | **Application Layer (Profiles)** | Uses the data for specific purposes (audio, file transfer) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?**
> Just as a parcel passes through the truck → sorting office → supervisor → reception → building manager → customer, a Bluetooth packet passes vertically through its stack layers. Each layer adds its own header (encapsulation) and performs a defined function. **A KTU examiner loves asking "What does Layer X do?" — so memorize the layer functions in the exact order.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

- **Operating Frequency:** **2.4 GHz ISM Band** (2.402 – 2.480 GHz)
- **Number of RF Channels (Classic BR/EDR):** **79 channels**, each **1 MHz wide**
- **Hop Rate:** **1600 hops/second**
- **Symbol Rate:** **1 Msymbol/s** (Basic Rate), **3 Msymbol/s** (Enhanced Data Rate)
- **Range:** Class 1 ≈ 100 m, Class 2 ≈ 10 m, Class 3 ≈ 1 m
- **Modulation (BR):** Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)
- **Modulation (EDR):** π/4-DQPSK and 8-DPSK
- **Network Topologies:** **Piconet** (1 master + up to 7 active slaves) and **Scatternet** (interconnected piconets)
- **Device Address:** **48-bit BD_ADDR** (Bluetooth Device Address, similar to MAC)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bluetooth Protocol Stack — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

The Bluetooth stack is officially divided into **three major functional blocks**: the **Controller** (lower layers), the **Host** (upper layers), and the **Application Layer**. A standard interface called **HCI** separates the Controller and the Host.

#### A. THE CONTROLLER SUBSYSTEM

##### 1. Physical (Radio) Layer
- Responsible for **transmitting and receiving bits** over the 2.4 GHz air interface.
- Performs **modulation/demodulation**, frequency synthesis, and transmission power control.
- Uses **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** — a pseudo-random hopping pattern of 79 RF channels at 1600 hops/s. The pattern is derived from the master's clock and BD_ADDR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 1600 hops/second?**
> The fast hopping provides **frequency diversity** (immunity to narrowband interference) and enables **co-existence** with Wi-Fi (which also uses 2.4 GHz). Each time slot is **625 μs** long.

##### 2. Baseband Layer
- Establishes the **physical RF link** between devices.
- Defines the **piconet topology**: 1 master (whose clock and BD_ADDR define the hopping sequence) and up to **7 active slaves** (255 parked slaves possible in standby).
- Manages **synchronous** (SCO) and **asynchronous** (ACL) connections.
- Handles **error correction** (FEC — Forward Error Correction), **packet assembly**, and **channel access** (polling-based TDMA).
- Provides two packet types:
  - **SCO (Synchronous Connection-Oriented):** Used for voice; reserved time slots, no retransmission.
  - **ACL (Asynchronous Connection-Less):** Used for data; uses polling, supports retransmission.

##### 3. Link Manager Protocol (LMP)
- Manages the **logical link** between two Bluetooth devices.
- Functions include:
  - **Authentication & Pairing** (using a 128-bit link key)
  - **Encryption control** (E0/E1 stream cipher)
  - **Power management** (Hold, Sniff, Park modes)
  - **Master/Slave role switching**
  - **QoS negotiation** and link supervision.

##### 4. Host Controller Interface (HCI)
- A **standardized command interface** between the host (L2CAP and above) and the controller (Baseband, LMP, Radio).
- Allows the host to issue commands like `HCI_Inquiry`, `HCI_Create_Connection`, `HCI_Accept_Connection_Request`.
- Transport options: USB, UART, SDIO, or Three-Wire UART.

#### B. THE HOST SUBSYSTEM

##### 5. Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)
- Provides **multiplexing** of higher-layer protocols over a single ACL link.
- Performs **segmentation and reassembly (SAR)** of large packets (Baseband payload is only 2745 bits).
- Supports **QoS flows** and **group management** (one-to-many).
- Uses **Channel Identifiers (CIDs)** to route traffic.

##### 6. Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)
- Allows devices to **discover what services** other devices offer and how to connect to them.
- Uses a **client-server model**: the SDP client queries; the SDP server responds with a **Service Record** (containing Service Class ID, Protocol Descriptor List, Profile Descriptor, etc.).

##### 7. RFCOMM
- A **serial port (RS-232) emulation protocol** built on top of L2CAP.
- Provides up to **60 simultaneous emulated serial ports** between two devices.
- Used by profiles like **SPP (Serial Port Profile)**, **OBEX** (file transfer), and **DUN** (dial-up networking).

##### 8. Other Host Protocols
- **OBEX (Object Exchange):** File/object transfer protocol.
- **TCS (Telephony Control Service):** For headset and telephony profiles.
- **AVCTP / AVDTP:** For audio/video control and distribution.

#### C. THE APPLICATION LAYER (PROFILES)

- A **Profile** is a vertical slice of the protocol stack that defines a specific use case.
- Examples:
  - **GAP (Generic Access Profile):** Defines device discovery, connection, and authentication.
  - **SPP (Serial Port Profile):** Simple cable replacement.
  - **HFP (Hands-Free Profile), HSP (Headset Profile):** Voice.
  - **A2DP (Advanced Audio Distribution Profile):** Stereo music streaming.
  - **HID (Human Interface Device Profile):** Keyboards, mice.
  - **BLE Profiles:** Heart Rate, Battery Service, GATT-based profiles.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The Bluetooth stack layer model is a **conceptual / architectural** topic. The numerical formulas below are derived from the physical layer (RF channel calculation, hop duration, and capacity) and are the only quantitative problems that may appear.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RF Channel Count (Classic) | $N_{ch} = 79$ | channels | Each 1 MHz wide, from 2.402 to 2.480 GHz |
| 2 | Channel Frequency | $f_c = 2402 + k \text{ MHz}, \quad k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 78\}$ | MHz | k = channel index |
| 3 | Slot Duration | $T_{slot} = 625 \text{ μs}$ | microseconds | $T_{slot} = \frac{1}{1600}$ s |
| 4 | Hop Rate | $R_{hop} = 1600$ | hops/sec | $R_{hop} = \frac{1}{T_{slot}}$ |
| 5 | BR Bit Rate | $R_{BR} = 1$ | Mbps | GFSK, 1 bit/symbol |
| 6 | EDR Bit Rate (2 Mbps) | $R_{2M} = 2$ | Mbps | $\pi/4$-DQPSK, 2 bits/symbol |
| 7 | EDR Bit Rate (3 Mbps) | $R_{3M} = 3$ | Mbps | 8-DPSK, 3 bits/symbol |
| 8 | Frame Period | $T_{frame} = 2 \times T_{slot} = 1250$ | μs | Master→Slave + Slave→Master |
| 9 | Piconet Active Devices | $N_{active} = 1 \text{ master} + 7 \text{ slaves} = 8$ | devices | 255 more can be parked |
| 10 | BD_ADDR Length | $L_{addr} = 48$ | bits | Unique, IEEE-style address |
| 11 | Asymmetric ACL Throughput (max) | $R_{ACL} \approx 723.2$ | kbps | Forward or reverse |
| 12 | Asymmetric ACL Throughput (max) | $R_{ACL} \approx 57.6$ | kbps | Reverse in asymmetric mode |
| 13 | Maximum Data Payload | $P_{max} = 2745$ | bits | Per Baseband packet, 5-slot DH5 |
| 14 | RF Band | $f \in [2402, 2480]$ | MHz | ISM, unlicensed |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wearables & Health IoT:** BLE GATT profiles for heart-rate, body temperature monitoring.
- **Smart Home:** Bluetooth Mesh for lighting, locks, sensors.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Bluetooth gateways aggregate data from factory sensors.
- **Audio:** A2DP profile powers wireless headphones.
- **Asset Tracking:** BLE beacons (iBeacon, Eddystone) for indoor positioning.
- **Automotive:** Hands-free calling (HFP), infotainment control.
- **Healthcare:** Medical telemetry devices (e.g., glucose monitors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: RF Channel Frequency Calculation

> **Problem:** Determine the center frequency of the 40th RF channel used in Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR).

**Step 1 — Identify the formula from the cheat sheet.**
The lower edge of the ISM band used by Bluetooth is $f_0 = 2402$ MHz, and the $k$-th channel is offset by $k$ MHz from $f_0$.

$$f_c = 2402 + k \text{ MHz}, \quad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 78\}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute k = 40.**

$$f_c = 2402 + 40$$

$$f_c = 2442 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Verify that the result lies in the valid band.**

$$2402 \text{ MHz} \leq 2442 \text{ MHz} \leq 2480 \text{ MHz} \quad \text{(valid)}$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $f_c = 2442$ MHz (or equivalently 2.442 GHz).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Bluetooth Classic uses 79 channels of 1 MHz bandwidth. Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), in contrast, uses **40 channels of 2 MHz** — three advertising channels (37, 38, 39) and 37 data channels. This is a frequently tested KTU distinction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Frame and Slot Timing Analysis

> **Problem:** Calculate (a) the time duration of a single Bluetooth time slot, (b) the duration of a full duplex (Master + Slave) frame, and (c) the number of frames that can be transmitted per second.

**Step 1 — Recall the fundamental relation.**

The Bluetooth hop rate is fixed by the specification at:

$$R_{hop} = 1600 \text{ hops/second}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the slot duration.**

$$T_{slot} = \frac{1}{R_{hop}} = \frac{1}{1600} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{slot} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 625 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the frame duration.**

A Bluetooth frame consists of one Master-to-Slave slot followed by one Slave-to-Master slot:

$$T_{frame} = 2 \times T_{slot} = 2 \times 625 \text{ μs} = 1250 \text{ μs} = 1.25 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the number of frames per second.**

$$N_{frame} = \frac{1}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$N_{frame} = 800 \text{ frames/second}$$

✅ **Final Answers:**
- (a) $T_{slot} = 625$ μs
- (b) $T_{frame} = 1250$ μs = 1.25 ms
- (c) $N_{frame} = 800$ frames/second

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Asymmetric ACL Throughput

> **Problem:** A Bluetooth Classic system uses a DH5 packet (5-slot, no FEC). The maximum payload is 2745 bits. Compute the maximum forward (master→slave) throughput in an asymmetric connection.

**Step 1 — Identify the data carried in one frame.**

Each frame carries one 5-slot packet (1.25 ms × 5 / 2 = 3.125 ms) from master to slave and a 1-slot return packet from slave to master. However, in asymmetric mode, the entire bandwidth can be allocated to forward direction. The maximum baseband data rate in 5-slot packets:

$$R_{fwd} = \frac{P_{max}}{T_{packet}} = \frac{2745 \text{ bits}}{5 \times 625 \text{ μs}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$T_{packet} = 5 \times 625 \times 10^{-6} = 3125 \text{ μs} = 3.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the throughput.**

$$R_{fwd} = \frac{2745}{3.125 \times 10^{-3}} = 878400 \text{ bps} \approx 723.2 \text{ kbps}$$

(The exact value, accounting for protocol overhead, is 723.2 kbps as per the Bluetooth specification.)

✅ **Final Answer:** $R_{fwd} \approx 723.2$ kbps.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Layer-to-Responsibility Mapping (KTU High-Yield)

| Layer | Sub-Block | Key Function | Key Protocol/Entity | Common KTU Question |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical (Radio) | Bit transmission, modulation, hopping | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | "How many RF channels?" |
| 2 | Baseband | Piconet formation, packet framing, error correction | SCO, ACL links, FHSS | "Piconet capacity?" |
| 3 | LMP | Link setup, auth, encryption, power modes | Link key, E0 cipher | "What does LMP do?" |
| 4 | HCI | Host-Controller command interface | HCI Commands/Events | "Purpose of HCI?" |
| 5 | L2CAP | Multiplexing, segmentation, QoS | CIDs, SAR | "Why L2CAP?" |
| 6 | SDP | Service discovery | Service Records | "How does a device find a printer?" |
| 7 | RFCOMM | Serial port emulation | RS-232 emulation, 60 ports | "Why is RFCOMM used?" |
| 8 | Application / Profiles | Use-case-specific behavior | GAP, SPP, A2DP, HFP, HID | "Name 3 profiles." |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Piconet vs Scatternet

| Property | Piconet | Scatternet |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | 1 master + up to 7 active slaves | Two or more interconnected piconets |
| Maximum Active Devices | 8 (1 master + 7 slaves) | Dozens; bounded by interference and timing |
| Hop Sequence | One master defines it | Each piconet has its own; a device in multiple piconets time-shares |
| Topology Star | Yes | No (mesh of piconets) |
| Example | Smartphone ↔ 7 BLE sensors | Headset + smartphone + laptop, all sharing data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Bluetooth Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER (Profiles)"]
        A1["GAP - Generic Access"]
        A2["SPP - Serial Port"]
        A3["A2DP - Audio"]
        A4["HFP - Hands Free"]
        A5["HID - Input Device"]
    end

    subgraph HOST["HOST SUBSYSTEM"]
        OBEX["OBEX (Object Exchange)"]
        RFCOMM["RFCOMM (Serial Port Emulation)"]
        SDP["SDP (Service Discovery)"]
        TCS["TCS / AVCTP / AVDTP"]
        L2CAP["L2CAP (Logical Link Control)"]
    end

    subgraph HCI["HCI - Host Controller Interface"]
        HCI1["HCI Transport (USB / UART / SDIO)"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROLLER SUBSYSTEM"]
        LMP["LMP (Link Manager Protocol)"]
        BASE["Baseband (Piconet / SCO / ACL / FHSS)"]
        RADIO["Physical / Radio (2.4 GHz, 79 channels)"]
    end

    A1 --> OBEX
    A1 --> RFCOMM
    A2 --> RFCOMM
    A3 --> TCS
    A4 --> RFCOMM
    A5 --> L2CAP
    OBEX --> L2CAP
    RFCOMM --> L2CAP
    SDP --> L2CAP
    TCS --> L2CAP
    L2CAP --> HCI1
    HCI1 --> LMP
    LMP --> BASE
    BASE --> RADIO
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Information flows downward from the user-facing application (top) to the radio (bottom) on the transmit side, and upward on the receive side. Each layer's **Service Access Point (SAP)** is the boundary where it talks to the next layer.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Piconet and Scatternet Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PICONET1["PICONET 1 (Master M1)"]
        M1["M1 - Master"]
        S1["S1 - Slave"]
        S2["S2 - Slave"]
        S3["S3 - Slave"]
    end

    subgraph PICONET2["PICONET 2 (Master M2)"]
        M2["M2 - Master"]
        S4["S4 - Slave"]
        S5["S5 - Slave"]
    end

    M1 --- S1
    M1 --- S2
    M1 --- S3
    M2 --- S4
    M2 --- S5
    S2 -. "Bridge Device" .-> M2
    S4 -. "Bridge Device" .-> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A device can be a **master in one piconet** and a **slave in another**, acting as a **bridge**. This is what creates a scatternet. The scatternet, however, is rarely used in practice because of synchronization complexity — modern BLE uses **Bluetooth Mesh** for multi-hop communication.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Bluetooth Connection Establishment State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STANDBY
    STANDBY --> INQUIRY : HCI_Inquiry (device discovery)
    INQUIRY --> INQUIRY_SCAN : Listening for Inquiry
    INQUIRY_SCAN --> INQUIRY_RESPONSE : FHS packet reply
    INQUIRY_RESPONSE --> PAGE_SCAN : Device discovered
    PAGE_SCAN --> PAGE : HCI_Create_Connection
    PAGE --> MASTER_RESPONSE : Slave responds with ID
    MASTER_RESPONSE --> CONNECTION : Link established
    CONNECTION --> DATA_TRANSFER : L2CAP / SDP / RFCOMM
    DATA_TRANSFER --> STANDBY : HCI_Disconnect
    STANDBY --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** What is the difference between **Inquiry** and **Page**?
> - **Inquiry** = "Is anyone out there?" → discovers unknown devices (collects BD_ADDR + clock).
> - **Page** = "You specifically — wake up and connect" → uses the known BD_ADDR to form a piconet.
> This two-step process is essential to remember for the **Part A (3-mark)** questions.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: HCI Command/Event Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Host CPU (L2CAP)"] -->|HCI Command| B["HCI Driver / Transport (USB/UART)"]
    B -->|HCI Command Packet| C["Host Controller (Firmware)"]
    C -->|LMP Message| D["Baseband / Radio"]
    D -->|Over the air| E["Remote Device"]
    E -->|Over the air| D
    D -->|LMP Event| C
    C -->|HCI Event Packet| B
    B -->|HCI Event| A
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram: BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BLEAPP["BLE APPLICATION"]
        P1["Generic Profile (GATT-based)"]
    end
    subgraph BLEHOST["BLE HOST"]
        G["GATT (Generic Attribute Profile)"]
        ATT["ATT (Attribute Protocol)"]
        SM["SM (Security Manager)"]
        GAP["GAP (Generic Access Profile)"]
        L2C["L2CAP"]
    end
    subgraph BLEHCI["HCI"]
        H["HCI Transport"]
    end
    subgraph BLECTRL["BLE CONTROLLER"]
        LL["Link Layer (40 channels, 2 MHz)"]
        PHY["PHY (1 Mbps GFSK / 2 Mbps)"]
    end

    P1 --> G --> ATT --> L2C --> H --> LL --> PHY
    P1 --> GAP
    P1 --> SM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **BLE vs Classic Bluetooth** (high-yield comparison):
> - BLE = **40 channels × 2 MHz**; Classic = **79 channels × 1 MHz**.
> - BLE = **GATT/ATT** for data model; Classic = **SDP + RFCOMM**.
> - BLE connection interval = 7.5 ms – 4 s; Classic = continuous.
> - BLE peak current ≈ 15 mA (for IoT coin-cell use).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**
*List and briefly explain the layers of the Bluetooth protocol stack.*

**Model Answer:**

The Bluetooth protocol stack is divided into three major functional blocks:

1. **Controller Subsystem (Lower Layers)**
   - **Physical (Radio) Layer:** Handles modulation (GFSK/π/4-DQPSK/8-DPSK) and transmission of bits over 79 RF channels in the 2.4 GHz ISM band, using 1600 hops/s FHSS.
   - **Baseband Layer:** Manages the piconet (1 master + 7 slaves), packet framing, and SCO/ACL links; uses 625 μs time slots.
   - **Link Manager Protocol (LMP):** Performs authentication, encryption, and power management (Hold/Sniff/Park).
   - **HCI (Host Controller Interface):** Standard command interface between host and controller.

2. **Host Subsystem (Upper Layers)**
   - **L2CAP:** Multiplexing, segmentation/reassembly, QoS.
   - **SDP:** Service discovery between devices.
   - **RFCOMM:** Emulated serial port for cable-replacement profiles.
   - **OBEX / TCS / AVCTP:** Object exchange, telephony, audio/video transport.

3. **Application Layer**
   - **Profiles (GAP, SPP, A2DP, HFP, HID):** Define specific use cases.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for naming, 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**
*What is the difference between a piconet and a scatternet? State the maximum number of active devices in a piconet.*

**Model Answer:**

- A **piconet** is a small ad-hoc network consisting of **one master** and **up to 7 active slaves** (8 devices total). The master defines the frequency-hopping sequence using its BD_ADDR and clock.
- A **scatternet** is formed when two or more piconets are interconnected. A device acting as a slave in one piconet and a master in another serves as a **bridge**.
- Maximum active devices in a piconet = **8** (1 master + 7 active slaves); up to 255 additional slaves may be in **parked** (low-power) state.

*(3 marks — 1 for piconet, 1 for scatternet, 1 for max count.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Rule:** Module-level long-answer questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (i.e., **either–or** between two sub-questions A or B). Each part of the 14-mark question is typically split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the Bluetooth protocol stack. Discuss the functions of the HCI, L2CAP, and SDP layers in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Inquiry and Paging procedures used by Bluetooth to discover and connect to devices. How are FHSS and the 79-channel hopping sequence managed by the master? **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of the Bluetooth Protocol Stack — 7 marks**

The Bluetooth stack is partitioned into **Controller, Host, and Application** blocks, separated by the **HCI** transport.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["APPLICATION (Profiles)"] --> B["HOST (L2CAP, SDP, RFCOMM)"] --> C["HCI"] --> D["CONTROLLER (LMP, Baseband, Radio)"]
```

**Functions of HCI, L2CAP, SDP:**

- **HCI (Host Controller Interface):** Standardized command interface between the host (CPU) and the controller (Baseband chip). Allows commands such as *HCI_Inquiry*, *HCI_Create_Connection*, *HCI_Accept_Connection*, and *HCI_Disconnect*. Transport options: USB, UART, Three-Wire UART, SDIO.
- **L2CAP (Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol):** Operates above the Baseband. (1) Multiplexes multiple higher-layer protocols over a single ACL link using 16-bit **Channel Identifiers (CIDs)**. (2) Performs **segmentation and reassembly (SAR)** of large packets, since the Baseband payload is limited to 2745 bits. (3) Provides **QoS** and group (one-to-many) communication. (4) Default CID 0x0001 for SDP, 0x0003 for RFCOMM.
- **SDP (Service Discovery Protocol):** Used by a device (client) to discover what services are offered by another device (server). A *Service Record* is a list of attributes (ServiceClassIDList, ProtocolDescriptorList, ProfileDescriptorList, ServiceName). Uses a simple request/response model over L2CAP.

*(Award 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark each layer function x 3 layers, 2 marks for additional supporting detail.)*

---

**(b) Inquiry and Paging — 7 marks**

**1. Inquiry Procedure (Discovery):**
- A device that wants to discover unknown neighbors broadcasts an **ID packet** on a dedicated inquiry hopping sequence (32 of the 79 channels).
- Scanning devices enter the **Inquiry Scan state**, hopping at a slower rate on the inquiry response hopping sequence.
- On hearing an ID, the scanner replies with an **FHS (Frequency Hop Synchronization) packet** containing its **48-bit BD_ADDR**, native clock, and class-of-device.
- The inquirer collects these FHS packets and stores the discovered devices in a list.

**2. Paging Procedure (Connection):**
- Using the discovered BD_ADDR and clock, the master computes the slave's page hopping sequence.
- It transmits a slave's access code on this sequence.
- The slave, periodically listening on the **Page Scan** state, recognizes its access code and replies.
- A handshake (page → page scan response → FHS → poll) completes the link setup.

**3. FHSS Management by Master:**
- The master selects one of **32 inquiry-response hop sequences** and one of **32 page hop sequences**, all derived from its **BD_ADDR and clock**.
- For the established piconet, the master defines the **Channel Hopping Sequence** (a pseudo-random ordering of all 79 channels) used by **all** slaves. The slave's local clock is offset to align with the master.
- Hop rate is **1600 hops/s** (slot duration = 625 μs), giving 800 full-duplex frames per second.

*(Award 2 marks for inquiry, 2 marks for paging, 2 marks for FHSS management, 1 mark for diagram.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) and Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) protocol stacks. Mention at least **five points of difference** covering physical layer, channel count, modulation, connection model, and power consumption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the following Bluetooth security mechanisms: **Authentication, Pairing, and Encryption** (E0 stream cipher). Explain how the **link key** is derived and used. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Bluetooth Classic vs BLE — 7 marks**

| Parameter | Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| **RF Channels** | 79 channels × 1 MHz | 40 channels × 2 MHz |
| **Modulation** | GFSK (BR), π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK (EDR) | GFSK (LE 1M), LE 2M (2 Mbps), LE Coded |
| **Hopping** | FHSS, 1600 hops/s | Fixed channel per connection (no hopping in 4.0/4.2; CSA in 5.x) |
| **Connection Model** | Continuous, point-to-point, fast data | Event-based, connection interval 7.5 ms – 4 s |
| **Data Model** | SDP + RFCOMM + L2CAP | GATT (Services, Characteristics, Descriptors) on ATT |
| **Peak Current** | ~30 mA (BR), ~25 mA (EDR) | ~10–15 mA (TX), ~1 μA (sleep) |
| **Range** | ~10 m (Class 2), 100 m (Class 1) | ~50–100 m (LE Coded) |
| **Discovery** | Inquiry + Page | Advertising (3 channels) + Scanning + Initiating |

**Best-suited for:** Classic → audio streaming (A2DP), file transfer. BLE → IoT sensors, wearables, beacons.

*(Award 1.4 marks per valid comparison point × 5 points = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Bluetooth Security — 7 marks**

**1. Authentication:**
- A challenge-response mechanism using a **128-bit link key**.
- The verifier sends a **random AU_RAND** (128 bits) to the claimant.
- The claimant computes an **E1 algorithm** response (SRES, 32 bits) using the link key and AU_RAND.
- The verifier checks SRES; if it matches, the claimant is authenticated.

**2. Pairing (Initial Key Generation):**
- Performed when two devices meet for the first time.
- A **temporary initialization key** is generated from a **PIN** (user-entered) and a random number, using the **E22 algorithm**.
- The initialization key is then used in the **E21 algorithm** along with both BD_ADDRs to derive a **unit key** or **combination key** (128 bits). This becomes the **link key**, stored for future authentications.

**3. Encryption (E0 Stream Cipher):**
- Once authentication is complete, the master may enable encryption.
- The **E3 algorithm** generates an **encryption key** (Kc, 128 bits) using the link key, a random **EN_RAND**, and the **ciphering offset (COF)**.
- The E0 stream cipher uses Kc, the master BD_ADDR, and a real-time clock value to produce a **keystream**.
- The keystream is XORed with the plaintext to produce ciphertext (and XORed again to decrypt).
- Max encryption key length is 128 bits; typical is 64 bits.

**Link Key Derivation Summary:**

$$K_{init} = E22(\text{PIN}, \text{RAND}) \quad \text{(Initialization key)}$$

$$K_{link} = E21(K_{init}, BD\_ADDR_A, BD\_ADDR_B) \quad \text{(Unit/Combination key)}$$

$$K_c = E3(K_{link}, \text{EN\_RAND}, \text{COF}) \quad \text{(Encryption key)}$$

*(Award 2 marks for authentication, 2 marks for pairing/derivation, 2 marks for E0 cipher, 1 mark for key formulas.)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up piconet and scatternet:** A piconet has *only 1 master*; a scatternet has *multiple piconets connected*. Students often write "8 devices in a scatternet" — this is wrong.
> 2. **Confusing 79 vs 40 channels:** 79 channels × 1 MHz is **Classic Bluetooth**; 40 channels × 2 MHz is **BLE**. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Wrong slot duration:** The slot is **625 μs**, NOT 1 ms or 312.5 μs.
> 4. **HCI layer confusion:** HCI is *not a layer* in the strict sense — it is a **transport interface** between host and controller. Drawing it as a "layer" between L2CAP and LMP loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping the PIN step in pairing:** Many students jump directly to the link key, missing the **initialization key (E22 algorithm with PIN)** step. This is a 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **Writing "Bluetooth uses CSMA" — WRONG.** Bluetooth uses **polled TDMA** (master polls slaves in round-robin). Don't confuse with Wi-Fi.
> 7. **Forgetting RFCOMM and OBEX:** A common omission when listing the host layers. Always list L2CAP, SDP, RFCOMM, and OBEX in order.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Bluetooth Stack = Controller (Radio, Baseband, LMP, HCI) + Host (L2CAP, SDP, RFCOMM, OBEX) + Application (Profiles).**

✅ **Piconet = 1 master + 7 active slaves = 8 total. Scatternet = interconnected piconets.**

✅ **Classic Bluetooth: 79 channels × 1 MHz, FHSS at 1600 hops/s, 625 μs slots, GFSK/π/4-DQPSK/8-DPSK.**

✅ **BLE: 40 channels × 2 MHz, 3 advertising + 37 data channels, GFSK (1M/2M/Coded), GATT-based data model.**

✅ **HCI = standardized host-controller transport interface, NOT a logical layer.**

✅ **L2CAP = multiplexing, segmentation/reassembly, QoS; uses 16-bit CIDs.**

✅ **SDP = service discovery via Service Records; works over L2CAP CID 0x0001.**

✅ **RFCOMM = RS-232 serial port emulation, supports up to 60 ports, used by SPP, OBEX, DUN.**

✅ **Inquiry = device discovery; Page = connection establishment (both use different hopping sequences).**

✅ **Master controls FHSS hopping sequence based on its BD_ADDR + clock.**

✅ **Security triplet = Authentication (E1) → Pairing (E22 + E21 for link key) → Encryption (E3 → E0 stream cipher).**

✅ **Profiles = vertical slices — GAP, SPP, A2DP, HFP, HID, OBEX. Memorize at least 5.**

✅ **GFSK in BR; π/4-DQPSK (2 Mbps EDR); 8-DPSK (3 Mbps EDR).**

✅ **BD_ADDR is 48 bits; link key, encryption key, and PIN-related keys are 128 bits.**

✅ **Co-existence with Wi-Fi is achieved through AFH (Adaptive Frequency Hopping) in Bluetooth 1.2+.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piconet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Piconet # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Piconet** is the fundamental *ad-hoc wireless networking topology* used in **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)** technology. It is defined as a short-range, low-power Personal Area Network (PAN) formed by a collection of Bluetooth-enabled devices interconnected in a *Master–Slave* configuration for the purpose of exchanging data and synchronizing communication using frequency-hopping spread spectrum techniques. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A piconet consists of **one Master device** and up to **7 active Slave devices**, giving a maximum of **8 active nodes** per piconet. Additional slaves may exist in *parked* (inactive) or *standby* states. ### Formal Properties of a Piconet | Property | Standard Value / Behavior | |---|---| | Topology | Star (Master-centric) | | Maximum Active Nodes | 1 Master + 7 Slaves = 8 devices | | Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) | | Channel Access | Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS) | | Hop Rate | **1600 hops/second** | | Frequency Channels | **79 RF channels** (1 MHz spacing) | | Range | Typically **10 meters** (Class 2); up to 100 m (Class 1) | | Addressing Length | 3-bit Active Member Address (AM_ADDR) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Classroom Piconet":** > Imagine a teacher (the **Master**) standing in front of a classroom. The teacher assigns a unique seat number to each of the **7 students** (the **active slaves**). When the teacher wants information, he calls a specific student by their seat number. Only that student responds. The teacher also controls the entire schedule of who speaks when (this represents the master's polling of slaves). > > The "seating chart" keeps changing every **625 microseconds** (one time slot) because the teacher changes the radio frequency channel (frequency hopping), so eavesdroppers cannot easily listen in. If the classroom gets too crowded (>7 students), extras are placed in a "parked" state, like students sitting quietly at the back waiting to be called. > > Now, if a student from Classroom A is also connected to Classroom B's teacher through a window, that single student is part of **two piconets** — this forms a **Scatternet**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > The following constants are **exam-favorite numerical values** for KTU 2024: > - **Frequency Band:** 2.4 GHz to 2.4835 GHz (ISM band) > - **Hop Frequency:** 1600 hops/sec > - **Slot Duration:** 1/1600 = **625 μs** > - **Maximum Active Slaves:** **7** > - **Total Nodes in Piconet:** **8** > - **Frequency Channels:** **79** > - **3-bit AM_ADDR:** 000 (reserved for broadcast) → 111 = 7 slaves # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture Breakdown A Piconet operates on a **Master-Slave** synchronous model. The following structured logic explains the "Why" and "How" behind each operational step: ### Step 1: Master Election / Initialization - **Why:** Bluetooth devices need a *central clock* to synchronize frequency hopping. - **How:** The device that initiates the connection (page/inquiry) automatically becomes the **Master**. Its internal clock and 48-bit BD_ADDR (Bluetooth Device Address) define the piconet's identity. ### Step 2: Slave Synchronization - **Why:** Slaves must lock onto the master's hop sequence to receive packets. - **How:** Each slave adds a time offset to align with the master's clock. The master's BD_ADDR and clock determine the unique hop pattern. ### Step 3: Channel Access (TDD Polling) - **Why:** Prevent collisions among 7 slaves sharing one radio channel. - **How:** The master uses **Time Division Duplexing (TDD)** with strict polling. The channel is divided into 625 μs slots, alternating between Master→Slave (M→S) and Slave→Master (S→M) transmission. ### Step 4: Frequency Hopping (FHSS) - **Why:** Avoid interference from Wi-Fi, microwaves, and other 2.4 GHz devices. - **How:** The piconet hops among **79 RF channels** at a rate of **1600 hops/second**. The hop sequence is computed from the master's BD_ADDR. ### Step 5: Addressing and Active Members - **Why:** Identify which slave the master is communicating with. - **How:** A 3-bit **AM_ADDR (Active Member Address)** is assigned — `000` is reserved for broadcast; `001` to `111` are for the 7 active slaves. ## 2.2 States of a Bluetooth Slave Device | State | Description | Active Node Count | |---|---|---| | **Active** | Actively communicating, has AM_ADDR | Up to 7 | | **Sniff** | Listens to piconet at reduced intervals | Not counted in 7 | | **Hold** | Temporarily suspended, may do other tasks | Not counted in 7 | | **Park** | Inactive, no AM_ADDR, synchronized to beacon | Unlimited (typical max 255) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Piconet Calculations:** | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Slot Duration | $T_{slot} = \dfrac{1}{f_{hop}}$ | $f_{hop}$ = hop frequency | seconds | | Hop Frequency | $f_{hop} = 1600\ \text{hops/s}$ | Fixed by Bluetooth spec | Hz | | Time per Hop | $T_{slot} = \dfrac{1}{1600} = 625\ \mu s$ | Computed | μs | | Active Slaves | $N_{slave} \leq 2^{AM\_ADDR} - 1 = 2^{3} - 1$ | AM_ADDR bits = 3 | count | | Total Active Nodes | $N_{total} = 1 + N_{slave} \leq 8$ | Master + slaves | count | | RF Bandwidth Span | $BW = 2.4835 - 2.4 = 0.0835\ \text{GHz}$ | ISM band | GHz | | Channel Spacing | $\Delta f = 1\ \text{MHz}$ | Between adjacent channels | MHz | | Number of Channels | $N_{ch} = \dfrac{BW}{\Delta f} = 79$ | After guard bands | count | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why Piconets Matter in IoT:** > - **Wearable Health Devices:** Smartwatch (Master) coordinating with heart-rate, SpO2, and temperature sensors (Slaves). > - **Smart Home Clusters:** A central hub managing lights, locks, and thermostats within a room. > - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** A controller on a factory floor interfacing with up to 7 sensor clusters. > - **Medical Piconets:** Patient bedside monitor acting as Master for ECG, BP, pulse-oximeter slaves. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Slot Duration and Hop Frequency The Bluetooth specification mandates a fixed hopping rate. We derive the per-slot timing: $$f_{hop} = 1600\ \text{hops per second}$$ Each frequency hop occupies exactly one time slot. Therefore, the slot duration $T_{slot}$ is the reciprocal of the hop frequency: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{slot} &= \dfrac{1}{f_{hop}} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{1600}\ \text{seconds} \\ &= 0.000625\ \text{seconds} \\ &= 625\ \mu s \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** A frequency of 1600 Hz means 1600 cycles/hop-cycles per second. One complete hop cycle consumes $\frac{1}{1600}$ seconds. Converting to microseconds: $\frac{1}{1600} \times 10^{6} = 625\ \mu s$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Number of Active Slaves Bluetooth assigns each active slave a unique 3-bit address (AM_ADDR). The total number of unique non-zero 3-bit combinations gives the slave capacity: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total 3-bit combinations} &= 2^{3} = 8\ \text{addresses} \\ \text{Reserved for broadcast} &= 1\ (\text{AM\_ADDR} = 000) \\ \text{Available for slaves} &= 2^{3} - 1 = 7 \end{aligned} $$ So the maximum number of active slaves is **7**, and combined with the master, the piconet supports a maximum of **8 active nodes**. > **Conversion Logic:** Three bits give $2^{n} = 2^{3} = 8$ distinct values from 000 to 111. The Bluetooth protocol reserves one address (000) for broadcast packets (Master to all slaves simultaneously), leaving 7 unique addresses for slave assignment. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of RF Channels in the ISM Band Bluetooth operates between 2.4 GHz and 2.4835 GHz. With a 1 MHz channel spacing, the number of channels is: $$ \begin{aligned} B_{total} &= 2.4835 - 2.4000 = 0.0835\ \text{GHz} \\ B_{total} &= 83.5\ \text{MHz} \\ N_{ch} &= \dfrac{B_{total}}{\Delta f} = \dfrac{83.5\ \text{MHz}}{1\ \text{MHz}} \\ N_{ch} &= 83.5 \approx 79\ \text{channels (with guard bands)} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** The raw bandwidth gives 83.5 MHz, but Bluetooth reserves guard bands at the edges of the ISM band to avoid interference. The standard uses **79 channels** spaced 1 MHz apart. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Piconet Throughput Analysis (TDD) A piconet alternates between Master-to-Slave (M→S) and Slave-to-Master (S→M) transmissions in consecutive slots. The full duplex cycle therefore occupies 2 slots: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{cycle} &= 2 \times T_{slot} = 2 \times 625\ \mu s = 1250\ \mu s \\ \text{Cycles per second} &= \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{1250 \times 10^{-6}} = 800\ \text{cycles/s} \end{aligned} $$ If each packet occupies one full slot, the effective one-way packet rate per slave is **800 packets/second**, multiplied by the maximum packet payload gives the throughput bound. > **Conversion Logic:** Time Division Duplexing means one slot is for downstream and the next slot is for upstream. Two slots form a complete Master-Slave exchange cycle. The reciprocal gives the cycle frequency. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary | Parameter | Piconet | Scatternet | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) | |---|---|---|---| | Topology | Star (1 Master, ≤7 Slaves) | Multiple interconnected Piconets | Star/BSS or Mesh | | Max Active Nodes | 8 per piconet | Many (via piconet linking) | 2007 (theoretical) | | Range | 10 m (Class 2) | 10 m per piconet link | 50–100 m | | Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | | Hop Pattern | 1600 hops/s | Multiple per piconet | No hopping (DSSS/OFDM) | | Power | Low (~1 mW) | Low | High (~100 mW) | | Use Case in IoT | Wearables, PANs | Multi-room BLE mesh | LAN, video streaming | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Piconet Topology (Star — Master-Centric) ```mermaid graph TD Master["MASTER DEVICE
Provides Clock
Controls Hop Pattern
Polls Slaves"] S1["SLAVE 1
AM_ADDR = 001
Active State"] S2["SLAVE 2
AM_ADDR = 010
Active State"] S3["SLAVE 3
AM_ADDR = 011
Active State"] S4["SLAVE 4
AM_ADDR = 100
Active State"] S5["SLAVE 5
AM_ADDR = 101
Active State"] S6["SLAVE 6
AM_ADDR = 110

AM_ADDR = 111
