# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **List and briefly explain any six major limitations of IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Power Constraint:** Battery-operated nodes have a finite energy budget; replacing batteries in massive deployments is operationally infeasible. *(0.5 marks)*

2. **Security Vulnerabilities:** Many devices ship with default credentials, lack encryption, and are vulnerable to botnet recruitment (e.g., Mirai attack, 2016). *(0.5 marks)*

3. **Interoperability:** Multiple competing protocols (MQTT, CoAP, AMQP) and data formats prevent seamless device-to-device communication across vendors. *(0.5 marks)*

4. **Scalability of Data:** The 5 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) of big data generated by IoT overwhelm traditional storage and processing systems. *(0.5 marks)*

5. **Bandwidth and Latency:** Constrained wireless links (ZigBee = 250 kbps, LoRa = 0.3–50 kbps) cannot support high-throughput or ultra-low-latency applications like autonomous vehicles. *(0.5 marks)*

6. **Privacy Concerns:** Continuous sensor data collection enables user surveillance and profiling, conflicting with regulations like GDPR and India's DPDP Act 2023. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Explain the security challenges in IoT with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

IoT security is one of the most critical limitations because billions of resource-constrained devices are deployed in physically accessible, often unmonitored locations. The major security challenges are:

- **Weak Authentication:** Many devices use hardcoded default credentials (admin/admin). The **Mirai botnet (October 2016)** infected over **145,000 IoT cameras and DVRs** using only 61 default username-password pairs, launching a **1.2 Tbps DDoS attack** that disrupted Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and Dyn DNS infrastructure. *(1 mark)*

- **Limited Cryptographic Capability:** Constrained 8-bit / 32-bit MCUs cannot execute full **TLS 1.3** or **AES-256** without exceeding their memory and compute budgets, forcing designers to use weaker or no encryption. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Insecure OTA Updates:** Unencrypted or unsigned firmware update channels allow attackers to inject malware (e.g., **VPNFilter malware, 2018**, which infected 500,000 routers). *(0.5 marks)*

- **Physical Tampering:** Attackers with physical access can extract firmware, dump cryptographic keys, and clone devices. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Botnet Recruitment:** Compromised IoT devices are weaponized for large-scale DDoS, cryptocurrency mining, or as entry points into home/enterprise networks. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the device-level and network-level limitations of IoT. How do these constraints affect the design of large-scale IoT deployments?

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain how energy harvesting techniques help overcome the power limitation in IoT nodes. Provide at least two real-world deployment examples.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Device-Level Limitations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Power Constraint:** *(1 mark)*
   IoT nodes are predominantly battery-powered. The fundamental equation governing node lifetime is:

   $$t_{life} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$$

   where $I_{avg} = I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) + I_{active} \cdot DC$. For a 2400 mAh battery with $I_{avg} \approx 0.51 \text{ mA}$, the lifetime is only **~6.5 months**. Designers must therefore aggressively duty-cycle the node.

2. **Memory & Storage:** *(1 mark)*
   Typical IoT MCUs (e.g., **ATmega328P, MSP430, ESP32**) have **2 KB – 520 KB** of RAM and **32 KB – 4 MB** of flash. This rules out heavy OSes (Linux/RTOS variants like **Contiki, RIOT, Zephyr** are used instead) and full cryptographic stacks.

3. **Compute Limitation:** *(0.5 mark)*
   CPU clock speeds of **8 MHz – 240 MHz** cannot support on-device deep learning inference or real-time video analytics at the edge.

4. **Form Factor & Cost:** *(1 mark)*
   The "$1 sensor" target for massive IoT deployment forces manufacturers to omit security features, use lower-quality components, and reduce BOM (Bill of Materials).

**Network-Level Limitations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Bandwidth Scarcity:** *(1 mark)*
   ZigBee offers only **250 kbps**; BLE offers **1–2 Mbps**; LoRaWAN offers **0.3–50 kbps**. These are orders of magnitude below Wi-Fi/5G.

2. **Latency:** *(1 mark)*
   Multi-hop wireless routing through RPL in 6LoWPAN can introduce **seconds** of latency, unacceptable for **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** or **industrial closed-loop control** requiring **< 1 ms** latency.

3. **Coverage Gaps:** *(0.5 mark)*
   Indoor, underground (mining), and underwater (marine) deployments suffer from severe attenuation.

4. **Address Exhaustion & Routing:** *(1 mark)*
   IPv4 cannot scale to billions of devices; IPv6 + 6LoWPAN is required but introduces header compression complexity and RPL routing overhead.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Energy Harvesting Architecture (Block Diagram + Explanation):**

A typical energy-harvesting IoT node consists of the following blocks:

```
[Energy Source] → [Harvesting Circuit] → [Energy Storage] → [Power Management] → [IoT Node]
   (Solar/Vibration/Thermal/RF)  (Rectifier/Boost)  (Battery/Supercap)  (PMIC + Sleep Controller)  (MCU + Sensor + Radio)
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Energy Source:** *(1 mark)*
   - **Solar (Photovoltaic):** Used in agriculture and smart city deployments.
   - **Vibration/Piezoelectric:** Used in industrial machinery monitoring.
   - **Thermal (Thermoelectric):** Used in boiler/pipeline monitoring.
   - **RF Harvesting:** Used in RFID-style passive IoT.

2. **Harvesting Circuit:** *(1 mark)*
   Converts the raw ambient energy into usable DC voltage. Example: a **bridge rectifier + boost converter** for piezoelectric, or an **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** controller for solar.

3. **Energy Storage:** *(1 mark)*
   A small rechargeable **Li-ion battery**, **supercapacitor**, or **thin-film solid-state battery** stores harvested energy for use during low-harvest periods (e.g., night-time for solar).

4. **Power Management IC (PMIC):** *(1 mark)*
   Implements **cold-start, sleep scheduling, and adaptive load shedding**. The PMIC wakes the MCU only when the harvested energy exceeds a threshold.

5. **IoT Node (MCU + Sensor + Radio):** *(1 mark)*
   Operates in a strict duty cycle (e.g., 1% active, 99% sleep) to match the harvested energy budget.

**Real-World Deployment Examples:** *(2 marks)*

- **Example 1 — Smart Agriculture (Semtech LoRa + Solar):** *(1 mark)*
  Solar-powered soil moisture sensors deployed across vineyards in Spain operate for **> 5 years** without battery replacement, transmitting data every 15 minutes via LoRaWAN.

- **Example 2 — Industrial Machine Monitoring (Piezoelectric + TI MSP430):** *(1 mark)*
  Vibration-harvesting wireless sensors mounted on rotating machinery in factories report bearing health continuously, eliminating wired power and enabling predictive maintenance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Critically analyze the interoperability and standardization limitations of IoT. Discuss how emerging frameworks like **Matter** and **OneM2M** attempt to address them.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the privacy and regulatory limitations of IoT. How do GDPR (EU) and the DPDP Act (India 2023) impact IoT data handling?

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**The Interoperability Problem (4 marks):**

1. **Protocol Fragmentation:** *(1 mark)*
   No single dominant IoT protocol exists. Application-layer protocols include **MQTT** (IBM/OASIS), **CoAP** (IETF RFC 7252), **AMQP** (financial messaging), **HTTP/REST** (web-style), **DDS** (real-time pub/sub), and **LwM2M** (device management). This fragmentation prevents seamless device-to-cloud and device-to-device communication.

2. **Data Format Heterogeneity:** *(1 mark)*
   Devices emit **JSON, XML, CBOR, Protocol Buffers, or proprietary binary** formats. A smart home gateway must parse dozens of formats, increasing complexity.

3. **Vendor Lock-in:** *(1 mark)*
   Cloud platforms — **AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT** — use proprietary SDKs and APIs. Migrating a 10,000-sensor fleet from AWS to Azure is a multi-month engineering effort.

4. **Lack of Universal Semantics:** *(1 mark)*
   The temperature reading "23.5" is meaningless without semantic context (°C or °F? indoor or outdoor? sensor calibration timestamp?). IoT lacks a **universal ontology** analogous to the semantic web.

**Emerging Solutions (3 marks):**

1. **Matter (formerly Project CHIP):** *(1.5 marks)*
   Launched in 2022 by the **Connectivity Standards Alliance (CSA)**, Matter is an open-source application-layer protocol that unifies **Wi-Fi, Thread, and Ethernet** under a common data model. Backed by **Apple, Google, Amazon, Samsung**, it enables cross-ecosystem smart home interoperability (e.g., an Apple HomeKit device can control a Google Nest thermostat).

2. **OneM2M:** *(1.5 marks)*
   A global standards initiative (ETSI, TIA, TTA, CCSA, ARIB, TTC) defining a **horizontal IoT service platform** with a common **resource model** and **RESTful APIs**, enabling application-vertical-agnostic data exchange across industries (smart energy, healthcare, transportation).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Privacy Limitations of IoT (3 marks):**

1. **Continuous Surveillance:** *(1 mark)*
   Smart speakers (Alexa, Google Home) and wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch) collect **always-on audio, video, biometric, and location data**, enabling mass surveillance.

2. **Inference Attacks:** *(1 mark)*
   Even anonymized smart meter data can reveal whether a household is empty, the number of occupants, and their sleep patterns — through **non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM)**.

3. **Informed Consent Gap:** *(1 mark)*
   IoT devices often collect data without explicit user awareness; **privacy policies** are rarely read.

**Regulatory Framework (4 marks):**

1. **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU, 2018):** *(2 marks)*
   - Defines personal data broadly to include **IP addresses, cookie IDs, and location data**.
   - Grants users the **right to be forgotten, data portability, and explicit consent**.
   - Imposes fines up to **€20 million or 4% of global annual turnover**.
   - IoT manufacturers must implement **Privacy by Design** and **Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA)**.

2. **DPDP Act (Digital Personal Data Protection Act, India, 2023):** *(2 marks)*
   - Establishes the **Data Protection Board of India** as the enforcement authority.
   - Requires **explicit, informed consent** for data collection.
   - Imposes penalties up to **₹250 crore** for significant data breaches.
   - Mandates **data fiduciaries** (IoT operators) to implement reasonable security safeguards and breach notification within **72 hours**.

**Engineering Impact:** IoT system designers must now embed **consent management modules, audit logging, and data minimization** directly into firmware — adding compute, memory, and development overhead that further stresses resource-constrained devices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT list limitations without explaining them.** A bare list earns **< 1 mark** per limitation. Always pair each limitation with a **real-world example, impact, and (in Part B) a mitigation**.
> 2. **Confusing "Limitations of IoT" with "Challenges of IoT":** In KTU's strict sense, *Limitations* refers to **inherent technical constraints** (power, bandwidth, security), while *Challenges* includes **broader deployment issues** (business models, user adoption). The examiner expects you to primarily focus on technical limitations.
> 3. **Forgetting 6LoWPAN and RPL:** When discussing IPv6/addressing limitations, KTU specifically expects the **6LoWPAN adaptation layer** and **RPL routing protocol** to be mentioned. Omitting these costs **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Mirai botnet date and numbers:** Examiners *love* testing **October 2016, 145,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps**. Wrong numbers = 0 marks for that sub-point.
> 5. **Confusing MQTT with CoAP:** MQTT runs over **TCP** (port 1883/8883); CoAP runs over **UDP** (port 5683). Mixing these up is a frequent KTU pitfall.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in Part B:** A neatly labeled block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself. Always include one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ IoT limitations are classified into **7 pillars**: Device, Network, Security, Interoperability, Data, Regulatory, Application-specific.
- ✅ The **Mirai botnet (October 2016)** is the gold-standard example for IoT security limitations — remember **145,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps, default credentials**.
- ✅ **Power** is the **#1 constraint** in IoT. Master the formula $I_{avg} = I_{sleep}(1-DC) + I_{active} \cdot DC$ and the duty cycle equation.
- ✅ **IPv6 + 6LoWPAN + RPL** is the canonical triad for IoT networking. KTU expects all three terms in any addressing question.
- ✅ Key IoT protocols and their transport: **MQTT → TCP (1883)**, **CoAP → UDP (5683)**, **AMQP → TCP (5672)**, **DDS → UDP/RTPS**, **LwM2M → CoAP/UDP**.
- ✅ Typical IoT data rates: **ZigBee = 250 kbps**, **BLE = 1–2 Mbps**, **LoRaWAN = 0.3–50 kbps**, **NB-IoT = 26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL)**.
- ✅ Energy harvesting sources: **Solar, Vibration (Piezo), Thermal (TEG), RF**. PMIC + supercapacitor/battery is the standard storage architecture.
- ✅ The **Matter protocol** (CSA, 2022) unifies **Wi-Fi + Thread + Ethernet** for smart homes; **OneM2M** is the horizontal IoT service platform standard.
- ✅ **GDPR (EU, 2018)** and **DPDP Act (India, 2023)** are the two most-cited regulations; both enforce explicit consent and breach notification.
- ✅ Always mention **Privacy by Design, Data Minimization, and Edge Computing** as KTU-preferred mitigation strategies.
- ✅ Common bandwidth/latency vs range trade-off: **higher range = lower bandwidth** (LoRa), **higher bandwidth = higher power** (Wi-Fi).
- ✅ The **5 V's of Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) is the KTU-preferred framework for data-management limitations.
- ✅ For 14-mark Part B answers, always include a **labeled block diagram** — it carries **2 marks minimum** independently of text.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth Low Energy (Key Features)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Bluetooth Low Energy (Key Features) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**, also standardized as **Bluetooth Smart** or **IEEE 802.15.1 / Bluetooth 5.x**, is a Wireless Personal Area Network (WPAN) technology engineered and optimized for ultra-low-power, short-range communication between resource-constrained IoT devices. It operates in the unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio band** and is characterized by extremely low peak, average, and idle-mode power consumption, allowing coin-cell batteries to operate for months or even years. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **OEERT614 – Internet of Things**, BLE is treated as a cornerstone **edge connectivity protocol** that bridges physical sensors/actuators to gateways, smartphones, and cloud platforms. It is formally defined by the **Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG)** and is differentiated from "Classic Bluetooth" (BR/EDR) by its entirely re-architected physical layer, link layer, and host stack. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of BLE as a "Walkie-Talkie optimized for status updates, not long conversations."** Imagine a **smart fitness band** on your wrist. Classic Bluetooth would be like a phone call — you dial in, stay connected the whole time, and continuously exchange data, draining battery fast. BLE, on the other hand, is like **briefly tapping a walkie-talkie button**: the device wakes up for just a **few milliseconds (typically 3 ms)**, transmits a small packet (e.g., heart rate = 72 bpm), and immediately goes back to deep sleep. The receiving smartphone (acting as the "Central") only listens in short bursts. | Analogy Element | Real-World Mapping | |---|---| | Walkie-talkie short tap | BLE short connection/event | | Person speaking briefly | Peripheral (sensor node) | | Person listening | Central (smartphone/gateway) | | Push-to-talk channel | One of 40 RF channels (2.4 GHz) | | Sleeping the rest of the time | Low-power idle/sleep state | This "burst-and-sleep" paradigm is the **soul of BLE** and is the reason it dominates battery-powered IoT deployments. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis) > [!TIP] > **Memorize these values — they appear verbatim in KTU Part A questions.** - **Operating Frequency Band:** **2.4 GHz ISM band** (2.400 – 2.4835 GHz) - **Number of RF Channels:** **40 channels** (Channel 0 – Channel 39) - **Channel Spacing:** **2 MHz** - **Modulation Scheme:** **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** with a modulation index between **0.45 and 0.55** - **Raw Data Rate:** **1 Mbps (LE 1M PHY)**, **2 Mbps (LE 2M PHY)**, **500 kbps (LE Coded PHY)** - **Maximum TX Power:** **+20 dBm (100 mW)** for Class 1, **+4 dBm** for typical IoT peripherals - **Typical Range:** **10 m to 100 m** depending on environment and PHY - **Connection Establishment Time:** **< 3 ms** (vs. ~100 ms for Classic Bluetooth) - **Maximum Payload per Packet:** **251 bytes** (LE 1M/2M), **27 bytes (Advertising)** - **Maximum Concurrent Slaves per Master:** Determined by link-layer implementation (theoretically unlimited, practically 10s to 100s) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The 40 channels are partitioned into **3 advertising channels** (Channels 37, 38, 39) used for device discovery and connection initiation, and **37 data channels** (0–36) used for active communication — a deliberate design that avoids the heavily congested Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, and 11. --- ## 1.4 The "Why" Behind BLE — Motivation in IoT > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why was BLE invented if Classic Bluetooth already existed?** | Limitation of Classic BT | How BLE Solves It | |---|---| | High power consumption (continuous connection) | Sleep-dominant duty cycle, peak current ~15 mA | | Long connection setup (100s of ms) | Connection in < 3 ms | | Complex stack (footprint ~100 KB+ RAM) | Lightweight stack (~50 KB) | | Optimized for audio/streaming | Optimized for small, periodic sensor packets | | Always-connected model | Connectionless & connection-oriented modes | This makes BLE ideal for **fitness bands, smart watches, medical sensors, beacons (iBeacon/Eddystone), smart home sensors, industrial telemetry, and asset tracking**. --- ## 1.5 Section 1 Quick Recap > [!TIP] > - BLE = ultra-low-power WPAN protocol for IoT > - Operates in 2.4 GHz ISM band with 40 channels (3 advertising + 37 data) > - Uses GFSK modulation at 1/2 Mbps > - Connection setup in < 3 ms > - Designed for bursty, intermittent sensor data — not streaming # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered Architecture of the BLE Stack (Logical Breakdown) The BLE protocol stack is divided into **three logical blocks**, as illustrated in Section 4. Below is the deep "WHY and HOW" of each layer. ### 2.1.1 Controller Block (Lower Layers) #### **Physical Layer (PHY)** - **Why:** To convert digital bits into radio waves on the 2.4 GHz band. - **How:** Uses GFSK — a bit `0` shifts the carrier frequency **down** by `f_dev`, and a bit `1` shifts it **up** by `f_dev`. - **KTU Note:** The Gaussian filter smooths the abrupt frequency transitions, reducing spectral side-lobes and improving bandwidth efficiency. #### **Link Layer (LL)** - **Why:** To manage radio state, channel selection, and connection timing. - **How:** Implements **Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH)** across 37 data channels, with hop intervals of **7.5 ms to 4 s** (connection event length). - **Five Core States:** Standby, Advertising, Scanning, Initiating, Connection. - **Two Primary Roles:** **Master (Central)** and **Slave (Peripheral)**. #### **Host Controller Interface (HCI)** - **Why:** Standardizes communication between controller hardware and host software. - **How:** A command/event/transport interface (e.g., UART, USB, SPI). ### 2.1.2 Host Block (Upper Layers) #### **Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)** - **Why:** To multiplex multiple higher-layer protocols over a single link. - **How:** Provides **channel-based** framing, segmentation, and reassembly. #### **Attribute Protocol (ATT)** - **Why:** BLE is fundamentally about **exposing data as attributes** (key-value pairs) on a server. - **How:** Defines operations: `Read`, `Write`, `Notify`, `Indicate`, `Read By Type`. #### **Generic Attribute Profile (GATT)** - **Why:** To organize attributes into **Services** and **Characteristics** in a standardized way. - **How:** A profile (e.g., Heart Rate Profile) is a pre-defined collection of services (e.g., Heart Rate Service with a Heart Rate Measurement Characteristic). #### **Generic Access Profile (GAP)** - **Why:** To define device roles, discovery procedures, and connection modes. - **How:** Specifies roles: **Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central**. #### **Security Manager (SM)** - **Why:** To provide pairing, bonding, and AES-128 encryption. - **How:** Uses **LE Secure Connections** with Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) P-256. ### 2.1.3 Application Block - Contains user code, **profiles** (e.g., HID-over-GATT, Environmental Sensing), and application logic. --- ## 2.2 The Four GAP Roles — Bullet Logic Breakdown > [!NOTE] > **GAP (Generic Access Profile)** governs how a BLE device behaves at the macroscopic level. | Role | Function | Typical Example | |---|---|---| | **Broadcaster** | Sends non-connectable advertisements | iBeacon, Eddystone beacon | | **Observer** | Scans for advertisements, no connection | Smartphone scanning for beacons | | **Peripheral** | Advertises and accepts connections | Fitness band, temperature sensor | | **Central** | Initiates connections to peripherals | Smartphone, gateway, laptop | - **Why:** This role separation enables **connectionless one-way telemetry** (broadcast) AND **bidirectional command/control** (connection). - **How:** The advertisement is sent on the 3 dedicated advertising channels; once the Central receives it and requests a connection, both devices hop to one of the 37 data channels. --- ## 2.3 The Five Link Layer States 1. **Standby** – Default idle state. No TX, no RX. Lowest power. 2. **Advertising** – Transmitting advertisement packets on channels 37, 38, 39. 3. **Scanning** – Listening for advertisements. 4. **Initiating** – Responding to an advertisement with a connection request. 5. **Connection** – Active two-way communication on data channels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A device can be in **only one state at a time**, and a single device can simultaneously support multiple GAP roles (e.g., a smartphone is both Central and Observer). --- ## 2.4 Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS) in BLE > [!TIP] > **Why does BLE use frequency hopping?** Because the 2.4 GHz band is shared with Wi-Fi, microwave ovens, Zigbee, and other devices, interference is inevitable. BLE mitigates this via: - **Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH):** Channels marked "bad" (e.g., by Wi-Fi interference detection) are **excluded** from the hopping sequence. - **Hop Increment Formula:** $$ \text{Channel}_{n+1} = (\text{Channel}_n + \text{HopIncrement}) \pmod{37} $$ where the default `HopIncrement` is determined by a pseudorandom function of the connection's access address. --- ## 2.5 BLE Modulation — GFSK Mathematics > [!NOTE] > **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** is the only modulation scheme used in BLE 1M/2M PHY (LE Coded PHY uses different schemes). For a binary input bit $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$: $$ \Delta f(t) = \begin{cases} +f_d & \text{if } b_k = 1 \\ -f_d & \text{if } b_k = 0 \end{cases} $$ where $f_d$ is the frequency deviation. The instantaneous phase deviation is first shaped by a **Gaussian low-pass filter** with bandwidth-bit period product $BT = 0.5$ before modulation. - **Modulation Index (h):** $$ h = \frac{2 f_d}{R_b} $$ where $R_b$ is the bit rate. For BLE 1M PHY, $R_b = 1\,\text{Mbps}$, and the modulation index is constrained to $0.45 \le h \le 0.55$. --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Operating frequency range | $f_c$ | 2.400 – 2.4835 | GHz | ISM band | | Number of channels | $N_{ch}$ | 40 | — | 3 adv + 37 data | | Channel spacing | $\Delta f_{ch}$ | 2 | MHz | Uniform | | Bit rate (LE 1M) | $R_b$ | 1 | Mbps | Mandatory | | Bit rate (LE 2M) | $R_b$ | 2 | Mbps | Optional | | Bit rate (LE Coded) | $R_b$ | 500 / 125 | kbps | Long-range mode | | Modulation | — | GFSK | — | $h \in [0.45, 0.55]$ | | Frequency deviation | $f_d$ | $\pm (185 \text{ to } 250)$ | kHz | Symbol-dependent | | Max TX power (Class 1) | $P_{tx}$ | +20 | dBm | 100 mW | | Min TX power | $P_{tx}$ | -20 | dBm | 0.01 mW | | Max payload (data) | $L_{data}$ | 251 | bytes | Per packet | | Max payload (advertising) | $L_{adv}$ | 31 / 255 | bytes | Legacy / Extended | | Connection interval | $T_{CI}$ | 7.5 – 4000 | ms | Multiples of 1.25 ms | | Connection event time | — | < 3 | ms | Typical | | Encryption | — | AES-128 | — | LE Secure Connections | | Security key exchange | — | ECDH P-256 | — | FIPS-compliant | | Hop interval | $T_{hop}$ | 7.5 ms – 4 s | — | Per connection event | | Number of adv channels | — | 3 (idx 37, 38, 39) | — | Center frequencies | | Time to connect | $T_{conn}$ | < 3 | ms | From adv to data | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Healthcare:** Continuous glucose monitors, pulse oximeters, ECG patches (low power, short bursts). - **Retail/Proximity:** Apple iBeacon and Google Eddystone for indoor navigation and marketing. - **Smart Home:** Door sensors, leak detectors, smart locks. - **Industrial IoT:** Wireless condition monitoring on rotating machinery (no battery replacement). - **Sports & Fitness:** Heart rate straps, foot pods, smartwatches. - **Asset Tracking:** Real-time location systems (RTLS) in warehouses and hospitals. > [!TIP] > BLE's **billions of shipments per year** make it the **de-facto wireless standard** for short-range IoT — a frequent KTU exam point. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Channel Indexing and Carrier Frequency Mapping > [!IMPORTANT] > The relationship between channel index $n$ and the RF center frequency $f_c$ in BLE is a linear mapping that students are expected to derive for the KTU exam. ### 3.1.1 Statement For a given BLE channel index $n \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 39\}$, find the carrier center frequency $f_c(n)$. ### 3.1.2 Derivation The BLE specification defines the radio band from **2402 MHz** to **2480 MHz**, partitioned into 40 channels, each of width **2 MHz**. **Step 1:** Identify the starting frequency (lowest channel, $n = 0$): $$ f_c(0) = 2402 \text{ MHz} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the channel spacing: $$ \Delta f_{ch} = 2 \text{ MHz} $$ **Step 3:** Write the linear frequency mapping: $$ f_c(n) = f_c(0) + n \cdot \Delta f_{ch} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute values: $$ f_c(n) = 2402 + 2n \text{ MHz}, \quad n \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 39\} $$ **Step 5:** Verify the upper bound for $n = 39$: $$ f_c(39) = 2402 + 2(39) = 2402 + 78 = 2480 \text{ MHz} $$ This matches the upper edge of the BLE band — derivation complete and consistent. ✓ ### 3.1.3 Verification for Advertising Channels The 3 advertising channels are at indices **37, 38, 39**: $$ f_c(37) = 2402 + 2(37) = 2402 + 74 = 2476 \text{ MHz} $$ $$ f_c(38) = 2402 + 2(38) = 2402 + 76 = 2478 \text{ MHz} $$ $$ f_c(39) = 2402 + 2(39) = 2402 + 78 = 2480 \text{ MHz} $$ These are deliberately chosen to **avoid the heavily used Wi-Fi channels 1 (2412 MHz), 6 (2437 MHz), and 11 (2462 MHz)**, ensuring reliable discovery. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Modulation Index Range and Bit Rate ### 3.2.1 Statement Show that for the BLE 1M PHY, the frequency deviation $f_d$ must lie between 185 kHz and 250 kHz to satisfy the modulation index constraint $0.45 \le h \le 0.55$. ### 3.2.2 Derivation **Step 1:** State the relationship between modulation index, frequency deviation, and bit rate: $$ h = \frac{2 f_d}{R_b} $$ **Step 2:** For LE 1M PHY, $R_b = 1\,\text{Mbps} = 10^6\,\text{bits/s}$. **Step 3:** Solve for $f_d$ in terms of $h$: $$ f_d = \frac{h \cdot R_b}{2} $$ **Step 4:** Compute the lower bound for $h = 0.45$: $$ f_{d,\min} = \frac{0.45 \times 10^6}{2} = 225 \times 10^3\,\text{Hz} = 225 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 5:** Compute the upper bound for $h = 0.55$: $$ f_{d,\max} = \frac{0.55 \times 10^6}{2} = 275 \times 10^3\,\text{Hz} = 275 \text{ kHz} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Correction Note (BT SIG Spec):** The Bluetooth Core Specification actually states $f_d$ must be in the range **185 kHz to 250 kHz**, which corresponds to a slightly different convention. The exact numerical bound students should quote for the KTU exam is **185 kHz to 250 kHz**. The derivation above demonstrates the *principle* of relating $h$ to $f_d$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Feature-by-Feature Comparison > [!TIP] > **This matrix is the single most exam-useful artifact in this module. Memorize it.** | Feature | Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) | |---|---|---| | Frequency band | 2.4 GHz ISM | 2.4 GHz ISM | | Channels | 79 | 40 | | Channel spacing | 1 MHz | 2 MHz | | Modulation | GFSK, π/4 DQPSK, 8DPSK | GFSK | | Symbol rate | 1 Msps (GFSK) | 1 Msps / 2 Msps / 1 Msps (coded) | | Data rate | 1 / 2 / 3 Mbps | 1 / 2 / 0.5 / 0.125 Mbps | | Connection setup time | ~100 ms | < 3 ms | | Peak current | ~30 mA | < 15 mA | | Average current (sniff) | ~1 mA | ~1 µA | | Battery life | Days to weeks | Months to years | | Stack size | ~100 KB+ | ~50 KB | | Topology | Point-to-point, scatternet | Star, broadcast | | Max packet payload | 1021 bytes (DH5) | 251 bytes | | Use case | Audio streaming, file transfer | Sensors, beacons, control | | Power optimization | Sniff/hold/park modes | Deep sleep between events | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Connection Event Timing ### 3.4.1 Problem A BLE peripheral advertises once every 100 ms with an advertising interval of 50 ms. A Central scans with a scan window of 25 ms every 30 ms. Calculate: (a) The duty cycle of the advertiser. (b) The minimum time to establish a connection. (c) The number of advertising channels used. ### 3.4.2 Solution **(a) Advertiser Duty Cycle:** The advertiser transmits for a short duration $T_{adv\_tx}$ (typically 0.6 ms to 1.5 ms) and sleeps for the remainder of the 100 ms period. $$ \text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{adv\_tx}}{T_{adv\_interval}} \times 100\% $$ Assuming $T_{adv\_tx} = 1 \text{ ms}$: $$ \text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{1\,\text{ms}}{100\,\text{ms}} \times 100\% = 1\% $$ **(b) Minimum Connection Time:** The BLE specification guarantees connection setup in **less than 3 ms** from the moment the Central issues a `CONNECT_IND` (LL connection request) PDU. $$ T_{conn} < 3 \text{ ms} $$ **(c) Number of Advertising Channels:** The 3 advertising channels are indices **37, 38, 39**, so: $$ N_{adv} = 3 $$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Power Budget Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-style calculation.** ### 3.5.1 Problem A BLE temperature sensor reports data every 5 seconds. Each transmission takes 2 ms at a peak current of 10 mA. Between transmissions, the device sleeps at 1 µA. The battery is a CR2032 coin cell with capacity 220 mAh. Calculate the theoretical battery life. ### 3.5.2 Solution **Step 1:** Compute the average current. Average current in active mode over the cycle: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{(I_{active} \times T_{active}) + (I_{sleep} \times T_{sleep})}{T_{total}} $$ $$ I_{avg} = \frac{(10 \text{ mA} \times 2 \text{ ms}) + (0.001 \text{ mA} \times 4998 \text{ ms})}{5000 \text{ ms}} $$ $$ I_{avg} = \frac{20 + 4.998}{5000} = \frac{24.998}{5000} \approx 0.005 \text{ mA} = 5 \text{ µA} $$ **Step 2:** Compute battery life. $$ \text{Life} = \frac{\text{Capacity}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{220 \text{ mAh}}{0.005 \text{ mA}} = 44{,}000 \text{ hours} $$ **Step 3:** Convert to years. $$ \text{Life (years)} = \frac{44{,}000}{24 \times 365} \approx 5.02 \text{ years} $$ > [!TIP] > This is the **magic of BLE** — a coin cell lasting multiple years, despite periodic transmissions. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagrams are rendered using the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** paradigm — strictly alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown formatting inside labels. --- ## 4.1 BLE Protocol Stack — High-Level Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER
