# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **List and briefly explain any six major limitations of IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Power Constraint:** Battery-operated nodes have a finite energy budget; replacing batteries in massive deployments is operationally infeasible. *(0.5 marks)*

2. **Security Vulnerabilities:** Many devices ship with default credentials, lack encryption, and are vulnerable to botnet recruitment (e.g., Mirai attack, 2016). *(0.5 marks)*

3. **Interoperability:** Multiple competing protocols (MQTT, CoAP, AMQP) and data formats prevent seamless device-to-device communication across vendors. *(0.5 marks)*

4. **Scalability of Data:** The 5 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) of big data generated by IoT overwhelm traditional storage and processing systems. *(0.5 marks)*

5. **Bandwidth and Latency:** Constrained wireless links (ZigBee = 250 kbps, LoRa = 0.3–50 kbps) cannot support high-throughput or ultra-low-latency applications like autonomous vehicles. *(0.5 marks)*

6. **Privacy Concerns:** Continuous sensor data collection enables user surveillance and profiling, conflicting with regulations like GDPR and India's DPDP Act 2023. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Explain the security challenges in IoT with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

IoT security is one of the most critical limitations because billions of resource-constrained devices are deployed in physically accessible, often unmonitored locations. The major security challenges are:

- **Weak Authentication:** Many devices use hardcoded default credentials (admin/admin). The **Mirai botnet (October 2016)** infected over **145,000 IoT cameras and DVRs** using only 61 default username-password pairs, launching a **1.2 Tbps DDoS attack** that disrupted Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and Dyn DNS infrastructure. *(1 mark)*

- **Limited Cryptographic Capability:** Constrained 8-bit / 32-bit MCUs cannot execute full **TLS 1.3** or **AES-256** without exceeding their memory and compute budgets, forcing designers to use weaker or no encryption. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Insecure OTA Updates:** Unencrypted or unsigned firmware update channels allow attackers to inject malware (e.g., **VPNFilter malware, 2018**, which infected 500,000 routers). *(0.5 marks)*

- **Physical Tampering:** Attackers with physical access can extract firmware, dump cryptographic keys, and clone devices. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Botnet Recruitment:** Compromised IoT devices are weaponized for large-scale DDoS, cryptocurrency mining, or as entry points into home/enterprise networks. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the device-level and network-level limitations of IoT. How do these constraints affect the design of large-scale IoT deployments?

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain how energy harvesting techniques help overcome the power limitation in IoT nodes. Provide at least two real-world deployment examples.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Device-Level Limitations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Power Constraint:** *(1 mark)*
   IoT nodes are predominantly battery-powered. The fundamental equation governing node lifetime is:

   $$t_{life} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$$

   where $I_{avg} = I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) + I_{active} \cdot DC$. For a 2400 mAh battery with $I_{avg} \approx 0.51 \text{ mA}$, the lifetime is only **~6.5 months**. Designers must therefore aggressively duty-cycle the node.

2. **Memory & Storage:** *(1 mark)*
   Typical IoT MCUs (e.g., **ATmega328P, MSP430, ESP32**) have **2 KB – 520 KB** of RAM and **32 KB – 4 MB** of flash. This rules out heavy OSes (Linux/RTOS variants like **Contiki, RIOT, Zephyr** are used instead) and full cryptographic stacks.

3. **Compute Limitation:** *(0.5 mark)*
   CPU clock speeds of **8 MHz – 240 MHz** cannot support on-device deep learning inference or real-time video analytics at the edge.

4. **Form Factor & Cost:** *(1 mark)*
   The "$1 sensor" target for massive IoT deployment forces manufacturers to omit security features, use lower-quality components, and reduce BOM (Bill of Materials).

**Network-Level Limitations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Bandwidth Scarcity:** *(1 mark)*
   ZigBee offers only **250 kbps**; BLE offers **1–2 Mbps**; LoRaWAN offers **0.3–50 kbps**. These are orders of magnitude below Wi-Fi/5G.

2. **Latency:** *(1 mark)*
   Multi-hop wireless routing through RPL in 6LoWPAN can introduce **seconds** of latency, unacceptable for **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** or **industrial closed-loop control** requiring **< 1 ms** latency.

3. **Coverage Gaps:** *(0.5 mark)*
   Indoor, underground (mining), and underwater (marine) deployments suffer from severe attenuation.

4. **Address Exhaustion & Routing:** *(1 mark)*
   IPv4 cannot scale to billions of devices; IPv6 + 6LoWPAN is required but introduces header compression complexity and RPL routing overhead.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Energy Harvesting Architecture (Block Diagram + Explanation):**

A typical energy-harvesting IoT node consists of the following blocks:

```
[Energy Source] → [Harvesting Circuit] → [Energy Storage] → [Power Management] → [IoT Node]
   (Solar/Vibration/Thermal/RF)  (Rectifier/Boost)  (Battery/Supercap)  (PMIC + Sleep Controller)  (MCU + Sensor + Radio)
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Energy Source:** *(1 mark)*
   - **Solar (Photovoltaic):** Used in agriculture and smart city deployments.
   - **Vibration/Piezoelectric:** Used in industrial machinery monitoring.
   - **Thermal (Thermoelectric):** Used in boiler/pipeline monitoring.
   - **RF Harvesting:** Used in RFID-style passive IoT.

2. **Harvesting Circuit:** *(1 mark)*
   Converts the raw ambient energy into usable DC voltage. Example: a **bridge rectifier + boost converter** for piezoelectric, or an **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** controller for solar.

3. **Energy Storage:** *(1 mark)*
   A small rechargeable **Li-ion battery**, **supercapacitor**, or **thin-film solid-state battery** stores harvested energy for use during low-harvest periods (e.g., night-time for solar).

4. **Power Management IC (PMIC):** *(1 mark)*
   Implements **cold-start, sleep scheduling, and adaptive load shedding**. The PMIC wakes the MCU only when the harvested energy exceeds a threshold.

5. **IoT Node (MCU + Sensor + Radio):** *(1 mark)*
   Operates in a strict duty cycle (e.g., 1% active, 99% sleep) to match the harvested energy budget.

**Real-World Deployment Examples:** *(2 marks)*

- **Example 1 — Smart Agriculture (Semtech LoRa + Solar):** *(1 mark)*
  Solar-powered soil moisture sensors deployed across vineyards in Spain operate for **> 5 years** without battery replacement, transmitting data every 15 minutes via LoRaWAN.

- **Example 2 — Industrial Machine Monitoring (Piezoelectric + TI MSP430):** *(1 mark)*
  Vibration-harvesting wireless sensors mounted on rotating machinery in factories report bearing health continuously, eliminating wired power and enabling predictive maintenance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Critically analyze the interoperability and standardization limitations of IoT. Discuss how emerging frameworks like **Matter** and **OneM2M** attempt to address them.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the privacy and regulatory limitations of IoT. How do GDPR (EU) and the DPDP Act (India 2023) impact IoT data handling?

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**The Interoperability Problem (4 marks):**

1. **Protocol Fragmentation:** *(1 mark)*
   No single dominant IoT protocol exists. Application-layer protocols include **MQTT** (IBM/OASIS), **CoAP** (IETF RFC 7252), **AMQP** (financial messaging), **HTTP/REST** (web-style), **DDS** (real-time pub/sub), and **LwM2M** (device management). This fragmentation prevents seamless device-to-cloud and device-to-device communication.

2. **Data Format Heterogeneity:** *(1 mark)*
   Devices emit **JSON, XML, CBOR, Protocol Buffers, or proprietary binary** formats. A smart home gateway must parse dozens of formats, increasing complexity.

3. **Vendor Lock-in:** *(1 mark)*
   Cloud platforms — **AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT** — use proprietary SDKs and APIs. Migrating a 10,000-sensor fleet from AWS to Azure is a multi-month engineering effort.

4. **Lack of Universal Semantics:** *(1 mark)*
   The temperature reading "23.5" is meaningless without semantic context (°C or °F? indoor or outdoor? sensor calibration timestamp?). IoT lacks a **universal ontology** analogous to the semantic web.

**Emerging Solutions (3 marks):**

1. **Matter (formerly Project CHIP):** *(1.5 marks)*
   Launched in 2022 by the **Connectivity Standards Alliance (CSA)**, Matter is an open-source application-layer protocol that unifies **Wi-Fi, Thread, and Ethernet** under a common data model. Backed by **Apple, Google, Amazon, Samsung**, it enables cross-ecosystem smart home interoperability (e.g., an Apple HomeKit device can control a Google Nest thermostat).

2. **OneM2M:** *(1.5 marks)*
   A global standards initiative (ETSI, TIA, TTA, CCSA, ARIB, TTC) defining a **horizontal IoT service platform** with a common **resource model** and **RESTful APIs**, enabling application-vertical-agnostic data exchange across industries (smart energy, healthcare, transportation).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Privacy Limitations of IoT (3 marks):**

1. **Continuous Surveillance:** *(1 mark)*
   Smart speakers (Alexa, Google Home) and wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch) collect **always-on audio, video, biometric, and location data**, enabling mass surveillance.

2. **Inference Attacks:** *(1 mark)*
   Even anonymized smart meter data can reveal whether a household is empty, the number of occupants, and their sleep patterns — through **non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM)**.

3. **Informed Consent Gap:** *(1 mark)*
   IoT devices often collect data without explicit user awareness; **privacy policies** are rarely read.

**Regulatory Framework (4 marks):**

1. **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU, 2018):** *(2 marks)*
   - Defines personal data broadly to include **IP addresses, cookie IDs, and location data**.
   - Grants users the **right to be forgotten, data portability, and explicit consent**.
   - Imposes fines up to **€20 million or 4% of global annual turnover**.
   - IoT manufacturers must implement **Privacy by Design** and **Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA)**.

2. **DPDP Act (Digital Personal Data Protection Act, India, 2023):** *(2 marks)*
   - Establishes the **Data Protection Board of India** as the enforcement authority.
   - Requires **explicit, informed consent** for data collection.
   - Imposes penalties up to **₹250 crore** for significant data breaches.
   - Mandates **data fiduciaries** (IoT operators) to implement reasonable security safeguards and breach notification within **72 hours**.

**Engineering Impact:** IoT system designers must now embed **consent management modules, audit logging, and data minimization** directly into firmware — adding compute, memory, and development overhead that further stresses resource-constrained devices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT list limitations without explaining them.** A bare list earns **< 1 mark** per limitation. Always pair each limitation with a **real-world example, impact, and (in Part B) a mitigation**.
> 2. **Confusing "Limitations of IoT" with "Challenges of IoT":** In KTU's strict sense, *Limitations* refers to **inherent technical constraints** (power, bandwidth, security), while *Challenges* includes **broader deployment issues** (business models, user adoption). The examiner expects you to primarily focus on technical limitations.
> 3. **Forgetting 6LoWPAN and RPL:** When discussing IPv6/addressing limitations, KTU specifically expects the **6LoWPAN adaptation layer** and **RPL routing protocol** to be mentioned. Omitting these costs **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Mirai botnet date and numbers:** Examiners *love* testing **October 2016, 145,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps**. Wrong numbers = 0 marks for that sub-point.
> 5. **Confusing MQTT with CoAP:** MQTT runs over **TCP** (port 1883/8883); CoAP runs over **UDP** (port 5683). Mixing these up is a frequent KTU pitfall.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in Part B:** A neatly labeled block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself. Always include one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ IoT limitations are classified into **7 pillars**: Device, Network, Security, Interoperability, Data, Regulatory, Application-specific.
- ✅ The **Mirai botnet (October 2016)** is the gold-standard example for IoT security limitations — remember **145,000 devices, 1.2 Tbps, default credentials**.
- ✅ **Power** is the **#1 constraint** in IoT. Master the formula $I_{avg} = I_{sleep}(1-DC) + I_{active} \cdot DC$ and the duty cycle equation.
- ✅ **IPv6 + 6LoWPAN + RPL** is the canonical triad for IoT networking. KTU expects all three terms in any addressing question.
- ✅ Key IoT protocols and their transport: **MQTT → TCP (1883)**, **CoAP → UDP (5683)**, **AMQP → TCP (5672)**, **DDS → UDP/RTPS**, **LwM2M → CoAP/UDP**.
- ✅ Typical IoT data rates: **ZigBee = 250 kbps**, **BLE = 1–2 Mbps**, **LoRaWAN = 0.3–50 kbps**, **NB-IoT = 26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL)**.
- ✅ Energy harvesting sources: **Solar, Vibration (Piezo), Thermal (TEG), RF**. PMIC + supercapacitor/battery is the standard storage architecture.
- ✅ The **Matter protocol** (CSA, 2022) unifies **Wi-Fi + Thread + Ethernet** for smart homes; **OneM2M** is the horizontal IoT service platform standard.
- ✅ **GDPR (EU, 2018)** and **DPDP Act (India, 2023)** are the two most-cited regulations; both enforce explicit consent and breach notification.
- ✅ Always mention **Privacy by Design, Data Minimization, and Edge Computing** as KTU-preferred mitigation strategies.
- ✅ Common bandwidth/latency vs range trade-off: **higher range = lower bandwidth** (LoRa), **higher bandwidth = higher power** (Wi-Fi).
- ✅ The **5 V's of Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) is the KTU-preferred framework for data-management limitations.
- ✅ For 14-mark Part B answers, always include a **labeled block diagram** — it carries **2 marks minimum** independently of text.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cellular Technology - GSM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cellular Technology - GSM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Cellular Technology** is a radio network architecture in which the geographical service area is divided into contiguous hexagonal-shaped regions called **cells**, each served by a low-power transceiver called a **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)**. The same set of radio frequencies can be reused in non-adjacent cells, allowing a cellular system to support a virtually unlimited number of subscribers with a limited radio spectrum.

**GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)** is the **Second Generation (2G)** digital cellular standard developed by the **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)** to provide circuit-switched voice, Short Message Service (SMS), and low-rate circuit-switched data services to mobile subscribers worldwide. It is formally specified as the **"Digital Cellular Telecommunications System (Phase 2+)"** and is the foundational technology upon which modern IoT cellular modules (2G fallback) and GPRS/EDGE (2.5G) extensions are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Hot Definition:**  
> *"GSM is a TDMA/FDMA-based, 2G digital cellular standard that operates primarily in the 900 MHz and 1800 MHz bands, uses a 200 kHz carrier bandwidth subdivided into 8 time-slots, and provides a gross data rate of 270.833 kbps per RF channel before channel coding."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honeycomb Post Office"

Imagine a large city with millions of people who all want to make phone calls, but the government has given the telephone company only **124 radio channels** to work with. How do you serve millions of people with just 124 lines?

The trick is the **"honeycomb post office"** analogy:

- The city is divided into **hexagonal cells** (like a honeycomb) — each cell is a small "post office."
- Every post office (cell) has its **own set of counters (frequencies)**, but it does **not** use the same frequencies as the **six neighboring post offices**.
- However, the post office **two cells away** *can* use the *same* frequencies — because the signals are weak enough by then that they won't interfere with each other.
- As you drive across the city, a **switchboard operator (the Mobile Switching Center, MSC)** hands your call over from one post office to the next without you ever noticing. This is the **handover**.

This "spatial reuse" is the genius of cellular technology: by **dividing space**, we multiply the effective capacity of a tiny spectrum many times over.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Hexagonal Cell Geometry:**  
> A hexagon is used (not a square or circle) because it provides the largest coverage area with the least overlap between cells, the fewest number of "cells per cluster," and the cleanest mathematical description of co-channel interference. The radius of a cell is denoted **R**, the side of the hexagon is also **R**.

### 1.3 Why Cellular? — The Capacity Problem

Before cellular systems, a single high-power transmitter (a "big tower in the middle of the city") served the entire metropolitan area using one set of frequencies. As soon as the user count exceeded the number of available channels, the system **saturated**. Cellular technology solves this via three pillars:

1. **Frequency Reuse** — the same frequency is used in geographically separated cells.
2. **Cell Splitting** — busy cells are split into smaller cells to multiply capacity.
3. **Sectoring / Cell Breathing** — directional antennas reduce interference and boost capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard GSM Frequency Bands (ETSI 05.05):**
> - **GSM 900:** Uplink **890 – 915 MHz** (25 MHz), Downlink **935 – 960 MHz** (25 MHz) — **124 carriers** of **200 kHz** each.
> - **GSM 1800 (DCS):** Uplink **1710 – 1785 MHz** (75 MHz), Downlink **1805 – 1880 MHz** (75 MHz) — **374 carriers** of **200 kHz** each.
> - **GSM 1900 (PCS):** Uplink **1850 – 1910 MHz**, Downlink **1930 – 1990 MHz** — used in North America.
> - **GSM 850:** Uplink **824 – 849 MHz**, Downlink **869 – 894 MHz**.

### 1.4 GSM in the IoT Context

For KTU 2024 (Internet of Things course), GSM is critically important because:

- It is the **fallback bearer** for nearly every cellular IoT module (SIM800, SIM900, BG96, etc.).
- It is the parent technology of **GPRS** and **EDGE**, which constitute **2.5G** packet data used by early IoT deployments.
- It evolved into **LTE-M (eMTC)** and **NB-IoT** — both of which are deployed *inside* the LTE carrier bandwidth but inherit the GSM network architecture (eNodeB → EPC → S-GW → P-GW).
- Any IoT device using a SIM card (asset trackers, smart meters, vehicle telematics) is using a descendant of the GSM core network.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The GSM Radio Interface — How a Call Physically Happens

GSM uses a hybrid **FDMA + TDMA** multiple-access scheme. Here is the structured logical breakdown:

1. **Frequency Division (FDMA layer):** The **25 MHz** uplink band is sliced into **124 carriers**, each of bandwidth **200 kHz**. This is the "lane" on the highway.
2. **Time Division (TDMA layer):** Each 200 kHz carrier is then sliced in time. A GSM frame is **4.615 ms** long and contains **8 time-slots**. Each time-slot carries one user's burst of **148 bits** of payload.
3. **Burst Structure:** Each time-slot burst is **156.25 bits** long, comprising 3 tail bits, 57 training (equalizer) bits, 2 control bits, **57 information bits**, and 3 tail bits, with a guard period of 8.25 bits.
4. **Duplex:** GSM uses **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex)** — uplink and downlink use *paired* frequencies separated by **45 MHz** (in GSM 900) or **95 MHz** (in GSM 1800).
5. **Modulation:** **GMSK (Gaussian-filtered Minimum Shift Keying)** with a BT product of **0.3** and a modulation rate of **270.833 kbps**.
6. **Speech Codec:** **RPE-LTP (Regular Pulse Excitation – Long Term Prediction)** produces a **13 kbps** digital speech stream, which is then channel-coded (rate 1/2 convolutional code) to give a net user rate of **9.6 kbps** for full-rate speech.
7. **Logical Channels:** Each physical channel (frequency × time-slot) is mapped to a *logical* channel — Broadcast (BCCH), Common Control (CCCH), Dedicated Control (DCCH), Traffic (TCH).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GMSK?** GMSK was chosen because it has a **constant envelope** — the RF power amplifier can operate at high efficiency in saturation without re-introducing spectral regrowth. This is critical for the small, battery-powered handsets of the 1990s.

### 2.2 The GSM Network Architecture

The GSM network is divided into **four major subsystems**. This is one of the **highest-weight topics** in KTU Module 3 — expect a 7- or 14-mark architecture question.

| Subsystem | Abbreviation | Key Components | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mobile Station** | MS | ME (Mobile Equipment) + SIM card | User's handset; ME is the radio, SIM holds IMSI & Ki |
| **Base Station Subsystem** | BSS | BTS + BSC | BTS = radio transceiver on the tower; BSC = controls many BTS, handles handover |
| **Network & Switching Subsystem** | NSS | MSC, HLR, VLR, AuC, EIR | The "telephone exchange" of GSM; switches calls, holds subscriber data |
| **Operation Support Subsystem** | OSS | OMC, NMC, AUC admin | Billing, network monitoring, fault management |

#### 2.2.1 Detailed NSS Functions (Exam Favorites)

- **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** The heart of NSS. Performs call switching, routing, location registration, and inter-BSS handover. Interfaces with PSTN via the **Gateway MSC (GMSC)**.
- **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent database of every subscriber belonging to this operator — stores **IMSI, MSISDN, Ki, current VLR address, service profile**.
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database of subscribers currently *roaming* in the MSC's area. Created on first registration in a new MSC.
- **AuC (Authentication Center):** Stores the **128-bit secret key Ki** for every subscriber. Generates the **SRES (Signed Response)** triplet used for authentication.
- **EIR (Equipment Identity Register):** Three lists — **white** (allowed), **grey** (under observation), **black** (stolen/blocked) — keyed on the **15-digit IMEI**.

#### 2.2.2 GSM Identifiers (Must Memorize)

- **IMSI (International Mobile Subscriber Identity):** 15 digits — MCC (3) + MNC (2–3) + MSIN (9–10). Stored in SIM.
- **MSISDN:** The actual phone number — CC + NDC + SN. Up to 15 digits.
- **IMEI (International Mobile Equipment Identity):** 15 digits — TAC + SNR + CD. Identifies the *handset*, not the subscriber.
- **TMSI (Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity):** 4-byte pseudonymous ID assigned by VLR for over-the-air privacy.
- **LAI (Location Area Identity):** MCC + MNC + LAC. Used for paging.
- **CI (Cell Identity):** 16 bits. Identifies a cell within an LA.
- **MSRN (Mobile Station Roaming Number):** Temporary number used to route an incoming call to the visited MSC.

### 2.3 The GSM Protocol Stack

GSM protocols are organized into three lower layers, mapped to the OSI model:

| Layer | GSM Protocol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 1 (Physical) | Radio interface (Um) physical layer | Burst formatting, GMSK modulation, channel coding, interleaving |
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | LAPDm | Reliable signaling between MS and BTS; derived from ISDN LAPD |
| Layer 3 (Network) | CM, MM, RR | RR = Radio Resource (channel assignment, handover); MM = Mobility Management (location update, attach); CM = Connection Management (call control, SMS, SS) |

### 2.4 The Frequency Reuse Concept (Mathematical Foundation)

The cellular system must balance two opposing requirements:

1. **Maximum Capacity** → cluster size N should be **small** (frequencies reused frequently).
2. **Minimum Interference** → cluster size N should be **large** (geographic isolation between co-channel cells).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Master Equations of Frequency Reuse:**
>
> 1. **Cluster Size:** $\quad N = i^2 + ij + j^2$
> 2. **Co-channel Reuse Ratio:** $\quad q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$
>
> Where:
> - $i, j$ are non-negative integers (shift parameters for the reuse pattern)
> - $D$ = distance between the centers of two co-channel cells
> - $R$ = radius of a cell
> - $N$ = number of cells in a cluster (must be a valid value: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, 28...)

### 2.5 Co-Channel Interference Ratio (C/I)

The **Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR)** for a mobile at the cell boundary, considering only the **first tier of 6 co-channel interferers**, is given by:

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{R}\right)^{n}
$$

> Here $n$ is the **path-loss exponent** (typically **n = 4** for an urban environment, and **n = 3** for a suburban environment). The factor **6** comes from the **first tier** of exactly six co-channel cells surrounding any given cell in a hexagonal layout.

**In decibels:**

$$
\left[\dfrac{S}{I}\right]_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\sqrt{3N}\right)^{n}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Reference:** A minimum **C/I of 9 dB** is required for a voice-quality, full-rate GSM call. The minimum **C/I of 12 dB** is required for GMSK with frequency hopping.

### 2.6 Capacity of a Cellular System

$$
C = M \cdot \dfrac{k}{N}
$$

> Where:
> - $C$ = total number of simultaneous users in the system
> - $M$ = total number of channels assigned to the system
> - $k$ = number of channels per cell
> - $N$ = cluster size (number of cells in a cluster)

### 2.7 Handover (Handoff) Types in GSM

GSM is famous for its **four types of handover**, all initiated and controlled by the **BSC**, not the MS:

| Type | Old → New | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Intra-cell** | Same BTS, same cell, new channel | Quality degraded on current channel |
| **Inter-cell, Intra-BSC** | New BTS, same BSC | Mobile moves to adjacent cell |
| **Inter-BSC, Intra-MSC** | New BSC, same MSC | Mobile crosses BSC boundary |
| **Inter-MSC** | New MSC, new VLR | Mobile roams to a new MSC area (roaming handover) |

### 2.8 GSM Security Triplet — Authentication & Encryption

When a mobile registers, the **AuC** sends a **triplet** to the VLR:

| Field | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **RAND** | 128 bits | Random challenge sent over the air |
| **SRES** | 32 bits | Expected response, computed in both SIM and AuC using **A3** algorithm with Ki |
| **Kc** | 64 bits | Session ciphering key, computed using **A8** algorithm with Ki |

The MS computes its own SRES using RAND + Ki, and the network compares. **A5** is the stream cipher (export-restricted / A5/1 strong, A5/2 weak, A5/3 = Kasumi for 3GPP) used to encrypt the over-the-air traffic with Kc.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cluster size** | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | 4, 7, 12, 19 | Number of cells per frequency-reuse cluster |
| 2 | **Reuse distance ratio** | $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | 4.58 (for N=7) | Spatial separation of co-channel cells |
| 3 | **Co-channel SIR** | $S/I = \dfrac{1}{6}(D/R)^n$ | 9 dB (N=7, n=4) | Voice quality threshold |
| 4 | **System capacity** | $C = M \cdot k/N$ | — | Total simultaneous users |
| 5 | **GSM 900 carriers** | $(25 \text{ MHz}) / (200 \text{ kHz})$ | **124** | Number of RF channels in 900 band |
| 6 | **GSM 1800 carriers** | $(75 \text{ MHz}) / (200 \text{ kHz})$ | **374** | Number of RF channels in 1800 band |
| 7 | **Frame duration** | fixed | **4.615 ms** | Length of one TDMA frame |
| 8 | **Slots per frame** | fixed | **8** | Number of users per RF carrier |
| 9 | **Modulation bit rate** | $1306/48 \text{ MHz}$ | **270.833 kbps** | Gross over-the-air rate |
| 10 | **Burst length** | $156.25$ bits | — | Bits in one time-slot |
| 11 | **Speech codec rate** | RPE-LTP | **13 kbps** | Pre-channel-coding voice rate |
| 12 | **Net user rate (FR)** | rate-1/2 code | **9.6 kbps** | Full-rate traffic channel |
| 13 | **Duplex gap GSM 900** | fixed | **45 MHz** | Up/down separation |
| 14 | **Duplex gap GSM 1800** | fixed | **95 MHz** | Up/down separation |
| 15 | **GSM max speed (GSM)** | — | **250 km/h** | Mobility supported |
| 16 | **GSM max delay (TRAU)** | 4 frames × 8 slots | **60 ms** | One-way speech delay budget |
| 17 | **Reuse cluster choices** | N = 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 19, 21, 27, 28 | N = 7 (common) | Valid N values |
| 18 | **Subscriber identity IMSI** | MCC + MNC + MSIN | 15 digits | Uniquely identifies a SIM globally |
| 19 | **MSRN length** | E.164 | max 15 digits | Temporary routing number |
| 20 | **TMSI purpose** | Privacy of IMSI | 4 bytes (32 bits) | Pseudonymous ID over-the-air |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The capacity formula $C = M \cdot k/N$ is the basis of *every* cellular-network dimensioning exercise at an operator. Reducing $N$ (e.g., from 7 to 4) **doubles the capacity** — but it also halves the C/I, so the operator must add cell-site sectoring or tighter cell-planning to keep the SIR above 9 dB. The same trade-off lies at the heart of **5G NR** design: aggressive frequency reuse balanced against beamforming-driven interference mitigation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cluster Size from Shift Parameters

**Problem:** A GSM operator uses a frequency-reuse pattern with shift parameters $i = 2$ and $j = 1$. Find the cluster size $N$.

**Step 1 — Identify the cluster-size formula.**

The cluster size in a hexagonal cellular layout is given by the standard formula:

$$
N = i^2 + ij + j^2
$$

> Here $i$ and $j$ are non-negative integers describing how the reuse pattern is shifted along two axes of the hexagonal grid. $N$ must produce a *valid* cluster that can tile the plane without gaps.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

Substitute $i = 2$ and $j = 1$ into the formula:

$$
N = (2)^2 + (2)(1) + (1)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term individually.**

$$
(2)^2 = 4
$$

$$
(2)(1) = 2
$$

$$
(1)^2 = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the three terms.**

$$
N = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

> **Result: $N = 7$** — this is the most commonly deployed cluster size in classical GSM systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** $N = 7$ is a *valid* cluster size. The next few valid values, in order, are: **1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, 28, 31, 36, 37, 39, 43, 48, 49**. Notice that $N = 5, 6, 8, 10, 11, 14, 15$ are **invalid** because no integer $i, j$ combination can generate them.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Co-Channel Reuse Distance Ratio

**Problem:** For the $N = 7$ cluster derived above, compute the co-channel reuse distance ratio $D/R$ and the actual distance $D$ if each cell has a radius $R = 2$ km.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$
q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $N = 7$.**

$$
q = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the square root.**

$$
\sqrt{21} = 4.5825\ldots
$$

> **Result: $q = 4.58$** (often rounded to 4.6 in textbooks).

**Step 4 — Compute the actual reuse distance for $R = 2$ km.**

$$
D = q \cdot R = 4.5825 \times 2 \text{ km}
$$

$$
D = 9.165 \text{ km}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Two cells that use the *same* set of radio frequencies must be at least **9.165 km** apart in a $N = 7$ cluster, otherwise their co-channel interference will exceed the 9 dB threshold.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Co-Channel Interference Ratio (C/I) in dB

**Problem:** For the $N = 7$ cluster with urban path-loss exponent $n = 4$, compute the **co-channel SIR at the cell boundary** in dB.

**Step 1 — Start from the linear-domain equation.**

The received signal power at distance $R$ from the *serving* BTS is:

$$
S = K \cdot R^{-n}
$$

> where $K$ is a constant that captures transmit power and antenna gains. The interference from each of the 6 co-channel BTSs at distance $D$ is:

$$
I_{k} = K \cdot D_{k}^{-n}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 6
$$

**Step 2 — Make the standard simplifying assumption.**

We assume all six co-channel interferers are approximately at the same distance $D$ from the mobile. This is the **"first-tier worst-case"** model used in every textbook derivation:

$$
I_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{6} I_k = 6 \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the SIR ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{K \cdot R^{-n}}{6 \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}} = \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{R}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert the values $D/R = 4.5825$ and $n = 4$.**

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot (4.5825)^{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the power of 4.5825.**

$$
(4.5825)^2 = 21.000
$$

$$
(4.5825)^4 = (21.000)^2 = 441.00
$$

> Note: This is exactly $(3N)^2 = (21)^2 = 441$ — a clean number, which is why $N=7$ is the most common cluster size.

**Step 6 — Divide by 6.**

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{441}{6} = 73.5
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to dB.**

$$
\left[\dfrac{S}{I}\right]_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 10 \times 1.866 = 18.66 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Result: C/I = 18.66 dB ≈ 19 dB at the cell boundary for an N=7 cluster, n=4 urban case.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Margin Check:** The minimum C/I for a full-rate GSM voice channel is **9 dB**. The system therefore has a **9.66 dB margin** to absorb real-world effects (shadowing, multipath fading, antenna tilt errors, second-tier interference, etc.). This is why the textbook figure of **C/I ≈ 18 dB** is widely quoted in KTU study material.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capacity Calculation for a GSM System

**Problem:** An operator has a **25 MHz** spectrum allocation in the GSM 900 band, a cluster size of $N = 7$, and **8 time-slots per carrier** (TDMA). How many simultaneous voice users can the system support? *(Assume full-rate TCH with frequency hopping disabled for simplicity.)*

**Step 1 — Compute the number of carriers (FDMA channels).**

Each carrier occupies 200 kHz, and there is a 100 kHz guard band at the bottom of the band that is unusable:

$$
M = \dfrac{25 \text{ MHz} - 0.1 \text{ MHz}}{0.2 \text{ MHz}} = \dfrac{24.9}{0.2} = 124.5
$$

> In practice, this is rounded to **124 carriers** (the lower 100 kHz is a guard band; the upper 100 kHz is absorbed into the last carrier or into the GSM-R / EGSM extension).

$$
M \approx 124
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of channels per cell.**

Since the cluster has $N = 7$ cells, each cell gets $M/N$ RF carriers, and each RF carrier has $k = 8$ time-slots:

$$
\text{channels per cell} = \dfrac{M}{N} \times k = \dfrac{124}{7} \times 8
$$

> Note: $124/7 = 17.71$, which is not an integer. In real deployments, this is *uneven* — some cells get 17 carriers, some get 18. For the textbook calculation, we either average or we *redesign* the cluster to $N = 4$ so that $124/4 = 31$ is an integer.

**Step 3 — Compute the total system capacity $C$.**

$$
C = M \times k = 124 \times 8 = 992 \text{ traffic channels per cluster}
$$

$$
C_{\text{per cell}} = \dfrac{992}{7} = 141.7 \text{ simultaneous users per cell (average)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with $N = 4$ (high-capacity configuration).**

If the operator instead uses $N = 4$ (i=2, j=0):

$$
C = 124 \times 8 = 992 \text{ channels per cluster}
$$

$$
C_{\text{per cell}} = \dfrac{992}{4} = 248 \text{ users per cell (average)}
$$

> **Insight:** Switching from $N = 7$ to $N = 4$ **doubles the per-cell capacity** (from 142 to 248), but the C/I drops from **18.66 dB to approximately 14 dB** — still above the 9 dB minimum, so it is acceptable with proper cell planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off summary table:**

| Cluster Size N | Reuse ratio D/R | C/I (n=4) | Channels/cell (GSM 900) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 3.46 | 14.0 dB | 248 | High-capacity urban |
| 7 | 4.58 | 18.7 dB | 142 | Standard urban / suburban |
| 12 | 6.00 | 22.6 dB | 83 | Low-density rural / interference-limited |
| 19 | 7.55 | 25.6 dB | 52 | Very low interference (e.g., near borders) |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — GSM Logical Channels

The KTU paper frequently asks to **map a logical channel name to its purpose**. Memorize the following 3×3 grid:

| Direction | Channel | Acronym | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Downlink (BTS → MS)** | Broadcast Control | **BCCH** | Carries cell identity, LAI, frequency list, control-channel description |
| Downlink | Frequency Correction | **FCCH** | Bursts of pure sine wave; mobile uses to correct its local oscillator |
| Downlink | Synchronization | **SCH** | Carries frame number and BSIC for time alignment |
| Downlink | Paging | **PCH** | Notifies a specific MS of an incoming call/SMS |
| Downlink | Access Grant | **AGCH** | Carries the SDCCH or TCH assignment in response to a RACH |
| **Uplink (MS → BTS)** | Random Access | **RACH** | Slotted Aloha; the MS's first message to request a channel |
| Uplink | (No uplink counterpart for BCCH/FCCH/SCH/PCH/AGCH) | — | — |
| **Bidirectional (both)** | Stand-alone Dedicated Control | **SDCCH** | Used for signaling: location update, SMS, authentication |
| Bidirectional | Slow Associated Control | **SACCH** | Continuous in-band signaling: TX power, timing advance, measurement reports |
| Bidirectional | Fast Associated Control | **FACCH** | Steals traffic-channel bursts for urgent messages (e.g., handover command) |
| Bidirectional | Traffic Channel | **TCH/F** or **TCH/H** | Full-rate (FR) 22.8 kbps or half-rate (HR) 11.4 kbps user payload |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mobility Management Procedures

| Event | Procedure | Network Element Involved |
|---|---|---|
| MS powered ON in new area | **IMSI Attach** + **Location Update** | MS → BSC → MSC → HLR/VLR |
| MS moves to new LA | **Normal Location Update** | Old VLR → New VLR → HLR |
| MS moves to new MSC area | **Inter-VLR Location Update** | Old VLR → New VLR → Old HLR → New HLR |
| Incoming call to MS | **Paging** on PCH | GMSC → HLR → VLR → all BTS in LA |
| MS on call crosses cells | **Handover** | Old BSC → New BSC (or same BSC) → MSC |
| Mobile-detected radio link failure | **Radio Link Failure (RLF)** → MS camps on new cell | MS |
| Periodic registration | **Periodic Location Update** (timer T3212) | MS → VLR |
| Power OFF | **IMSI Detach** | MS → VLR (deletes record) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GSM Network Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS["MS - Mobile Station<br/>ME plus SIM card<br/>IMSI plus IMEI"]:::user

    subgraph BSS["BSS - Base Station Subsystem"]
        BTS["BTS - Base Transceiver Station<br/>Radio TX plus RX on the tower<br/>200 kHz carriers GMSK"]
        BSC["BSC - Base Station Controller<br/>Controls many BTS<br/>Handover decisions Radio Resource"]
    end

    subgraph NSS["NSS - Network and Switching Subsystem"]
        MSC["MSC - Mobile Switching Center<br/>Call switching Handover control"]
        GMSC["GMSC - Gateway MSC<br/>Interface to PSTN"]
        HLR["HLR - Home Location Register<br/>Permanent subscriber data IMSI MSISDN"]
        VLR["VLR - Visitor Location Register<br/>Temporary roaming data"]
        AuC["AuC - Authentication Center<br/>Stores Ki generates triplets"]
        EIR["EIR - Equipment Identity Register<br/>IMEI white grey black list"]
    end

    subgraph OSS["OSS - Operation Support Subsystem"]
        OMC["OMC - Operations and Maintenance Center<br/>Fault configuration performance billing"]
    end

    PSTN["PSTN - Public Switched Telephone Network<br/>Fixed line network"]

    MS -. "Um radio interface<br/>900 or 1800 MHz GMSK" .-> BTS
    BTS -- "Abis interface" --> BSC
    BSC -- "A interface signaling BSSAP" --> MSC
    MSC -- "ISUP signaling" --> GMSC
    GMSC <--> PSTN
    MSC <--> HLR
    MSC <--> VLR
    HLR <--> AuC
    MSC <--> EIR
    BSC <--> OMC

    classDef user fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,color:#000
    classDef btsNode fill:#7EC8E3,stroke:#1F5F8B,color:#000
    classDef mscNode fill:#A4D4AE,stroke:#2E5E3F,color:#000
    classDef storeNode fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#5C2A6E,color:#000
    classDef extNode fill:#CCCCCC,stroke:#333,color:#000

    class MS user
    class BTS,BSC btsNode
    class MSC,GMSC mscNode
    class HLR,VLR,AuC,EIR storeNode
    class OMC,PSTN extNode
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this diagram as follows:** A voice call from the MS travels over the **Um** radio interface to the **BTS**, which is one of many towers controlled by a single **BSC** over the **Abis** interface. The BSC hands the call to the **MSC** over the **A** interface, which is the brains of the network — it queries the **HLR** to find the called subscriber's location, consults the **AuC** for security keys, checks the **VLR** for the subscriber's current MSC, and uses the **EIR** to verify that the handset is not blacklisted. The **GMSC** is the gateway to the **PSTN** (landline) world.

### 4.2 GSM Protocol Stack (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APP["Layer 7 - Applications"]
        A1["Voice call SMS USSD"]
    end

    subgraph CM["Layer 3 - Connection Management CM"]
        A2["Call Control CC<br/>SMS<br/>Supplementary Services SS"]
    end

    subgraph MM["Layer 3 - Mobility Management MM"]
        A3["Location update<br/>Attach Detach<br/>Authentication TMSI reallocation"]
    end

    subgraph RR["Layer 3 - Radio Resource RR"]
        A4["Cell selection reselection<br/>Channel assignment<br/>Handover decision"]
    end

    subgraph LAPD["Layer 2 - Data Link LAPDm"]
        A5["Framing error correction<br/>Acknowledged info transfer"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["Layer 1 - Physical"]
        A6["GMSK modulation<br/>Burst formatting<br/>Channel coding interleaving<br/>200 kHz carrier 8 TDMA slots"]
    end

    APP --> CM --> MM --> RR --> LAPD --> PHY
    PHY -. "Um radio air interface" .-> PHY
```

### 4.3 Frequency-Reuse Pattern (N = 7 Cluster)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLUSTER["One N equal 7 Frequency Reuse Cluster"]
        direction TB
        C1["Cell 1<br/>Freq group A"]:::ga
        C2["Cell 2<br/>Freq group B"]:::gb
        C3["Cell 3<br/>Freq group C"]:::gc
        C4["Cell 4<br/>Freq group D"]:::gd
        C5["Cell 5<br/>Freq group E"]:::ge
        C6["Cell 6<br/>Freq group F"]:::gf
        C7["Cell 7<br/>Freq group G"]:::gg
    end

    C2 --- C1
    C3 --- C1
    C4 --- C1
    C5 --- C1
    C6 --- C1
    C7 --- C1

    classDef ga fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef gb fill:#FFA94D,stroke:#A05000,color:#000
    classDef gc fill:#FFD43B,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef gd fill:#69DB7C,stroke:#1F5F2F,color:#000
    classDef ge fill:#4DABF7,stroke:#1F4F8B,color:#fff
    classDef gf fill:#9775FA,stroke:#3B1F8B,color:#fff
    classDef gg fill:#F783AC,stroke:#8B1F4F,color:#000

    class C1 ga
    class C2 gb
    class C3 gc
    class C4 gd
    class C5 ge
    class C6 gf
    class C7 gg
```

> **Reading the reuse diagram:** All cells of the *same* color (e.g., all red "Freq group A" cells across the city) are **co-channel cells** — they use the **same** set of RF frequencies. In a fully-deployed city, you would see Cell 1 of cluster 1, then a few km away, Cell 1 of cluster 2, then cluster 3, etc. — each red cell spaced **D = 4.58 R** apart.

### 4.4 GSM Call-Setup Signaling Flow (MOC — Mobile Originated Call)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant MS as MS - User
    participant BTS as BTS - Tower
    participant BSC as BSC - Controller
    participant MSC as MSC - Exchange
    participant VLR as VLR - Visitor DB
    participant HLR as HLR - Home DB
    participant AuC as AuC - Auth
    participant PSTN as Called Party

    MS->>BTS: RACH (Channel Request)
    BTS->>MS: AGCH (SDCCH assignment)
    MS->>BSC: CM Service Request (on SDCCH)
    BSC->>MSC: Forward CM Service Request
    MSC->>VLR: Query subscriber
    VLR->>AuC: Request authentication triplet (RAND, SRES, Kc)
    AuC-->>VLR: Send triplet
    VLR->>MS: AUTHENTICATION REQUEST (RAND)
    MS-->>VLR: AUTHENTICATION RESPONSE (SRES)
    VLR->>MS: CIPHER MODE COMMAND
    MS-->>VLR: CIPHER MODE COMPLETE
    MSC->>HLR: Check called subscriber
    HLR-->>MSC: Routing info (MSRN)
    MSC->>PSTN: Setup outgoing leg
    MSC->>MS: ASSIGNMENT COMMAND (TCH)
    MS-->>MSC: ASSIGNMENT COMPLETE
    MS->>PSTN: ALERTING and CONNECT
    Note over MS,PSTN: Voice path established
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** This is the *minimum* message exchange. In real life, the SGSN may also be involved if GPRS is in use, and the message set is enriched with TI (Transaction Identifier), bearer capability, and facility information elements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
**Explain the three logical channels used in GSM for call setup, and identify which are uplink and which are downlink.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **PCH (Paging Channel) — Downlink:** Used by the network to *page* (notify) a specific mobile station that there is an incoming call or SMS. The MS listens on this channel while in idle mode.
- **RACH (Random Access Channel) — Uplink:** A *slotted-Aloha* contention-based channel used by the mobile to *request* a dedicated channel from the network when it originates a call, sends an SMS, or performs a location update.
- **AGCH (Access Grant Channel) — Downlink:** The network's *response* to a successful RACH request; it carries the assignment of a dedicated SDCCH or TCH to the requesting mobile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 1 mark per channel (name + direction + purpose).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Sample Paper]**  
**List any three identifiers used in GSM and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **IMSI (International Mobile Subscriber Identity)** — 15-digit identifier (MCC + MNC + MSIN) stored in the SIM card. It is the global, permanent identity of the *subscriber*.
2. **IMEI (International Mobile Equipment Identity)** — 15-digit identifier burnt into the *handset* hardware (TAC + SNR + check digit). It uniquely identifies the device and is checked against the **EIR** black-list to block stolen phones.
3. **TMSI (Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity)** — A 4-byte pseudonymous ID assigned by the **VLR** and used over the air instead of the IMSI. It prevents an eavesdropper from tracking a subscriber by IMSI as the user moves between cells.
4. *(optional third alternative)* **MSISDN** — the actual phone number dialed; up to 15 digits. The **HLR** uses it as the lookup key to find the subscriber's current VLR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 1 mark per identifier (name + size + purpose).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> In any 14-mark GSM architecture question, students **consistently lose 2–3 marks** by: (a) writing component *names* without describing the *interfaces* (Um, Abis, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H); (b) forgetting to mention the **AuC** and the **EIR**; (c) failing to differentiate HLR (permanent) from VLR (temporary); and (d) writing a *flowery* intro about "GSM is a 2G technology…" instead of a *crisp* component-wise answer that matches the question sub-parts. **Always sub-bullet the interfaces — that alone is worth 2 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture and Signaling**  
*In a 14-mark question, this is the higher-frequency KTU pick.*

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the block diagram of the GSM network architecture and explain the functions of any **four** major subsystems. **With** a clear description of the interfaces **Um, Abis,** and **A**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat sequence-flow diagram, describe the call-setup procedure for a **Mobile-Originated Call (MOC)** in GSM, listing at least **six** signaling messages exchanged.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a) — GSM Architecture [7 marks]**

The GSM network is partitioned into four subsystems. The block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes (please reproduce in exam). The four subsystems are:

1. **Mobile Station (MS) — 1 mark**
   - Comprises the **Mobile Equipment (ME)** (the physical handset, identified by the 15-digit **IMEI**) and the **SIM card** (Subscriber Identity Module, holding the **IMSI** and the secret authentication key **Ki**).
   - The MS is the user-side endpoint; it terminates the **Um** radio interface.

2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS) — 1 mark**
   - Contains the **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)** — the radio tower that transmits and receives over the air using GMSK-modulated, 200 kHz carriers — and the **Base Station Controller (BSC)** that manages several BTSs, allocates radio channels, and executes intra-BSC handovers.
   - The interface between MS and BTS is called the **Um** (or "air") interface — the 900/1800 MHz radio path with 8 TDMA slots per carrier.
   - The interface between BTS and BSC is the **Abis** interface — a 16/64 kbit/s proprietary link carrying traffic and signaling.

3. **Network & Switching Subsystem (NSS) — 2 marks**
   - The **MSC (Mobile Switching Center)** is the central exchange; it performs call switching, location-update handling, inter-BSC handover coordination, and inter-system signaling.
   - The **GMSC (Gateway MSC)** is the trunking interface to the external **PSTN** and to other PLMNs.
   - The **HLR (Home Location Register)** is the permanent database; it stores the IMSI, MSISDN, current serving VLR address, and the service profile of each subscriber.
   - The **VLR (Visitor Location Register)** is a temporary database of all subscribers currently *roaming* in the MSC's service area; it is dynamically created and updated as subscribers move.
   - The **AuC (Authentication Center)** stores the 128-bit **Ki** for every subscriber and computes the authentication triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc).
   - The **EIR (Equipment Identity Register)** maintains the white/grey/black IMEI lists.
   - The interface between BSC and MSC is the **A** interface, carrying **BSSAP** signaling.

4. **Operation Support Subsystem (OSS) — 1 mark**
   - Includes the **OMC (Operations and Maintenance Center)**, which provides fault management, configuration management, performance monitoring, and billing data export.
   - Interfaces with the BSC, MSC, and HLR via the **O** interface (Q3 / Corba in modern OSS).

5. **Interfaces summary — 1 mark**
   - **Um**: MS ↔ BTS (radio, 900/1800 MHz)
   - **Abis**: BTS ↔ BSC (terrestrial, E1/T1)
   - **A**: BSC ↔ MSC (SS7-based BSSAP)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b) — Mobile-Originated Call Setup [7 marks]**

Please reproduce the **Mermaid sequence diagram in Section 4.4** as the labeled signaling diagram (1 mark for the diagram itself). The textual narrative is as follows:

1. The user dials the called number and presses the **green SEND** key. (½ mark)
2. The MS transmits a **CHANNEL REQUEST** on the **RACH** (slotted Aloha, 1 mark).
3. The BTS forwards this to the BSC, which assigns a **SDCCH** and signals back on the **AGCH** (**Immediate Assignment** message). The MS now tunes to the assigned SDCCH (1 mark).
4. The MS sends a **CM SERVICE REQUEST** indicating the service type (MOC). The BSC tunnels this to the MSC inside a **Complete Layer 3 Information** message (1 mark).
5. The MSC consults the **VLR**; if the subscriber is unknown, the VLR fetches an authentication triplet from the **HLR/AuC** (1 mark).
6. The network challenges the MS with **AUTHENTICATION REQUEST (RAND)**. The SIM computes SRES = A3(Ki, RAND) and returns **AUTHENTICATION RESPONSE**. (½ mark)
7. The network sends **CIPHER MODE COMMAND**, the MS activates ciphering with **A5(Kc)**, and acknowledges with **CIPHER MODE COMPLETE**. (½ mark)
8. The MSC routes the call. For a landline called party, the **GMSC** queries the **HLR** for an **MSRN** and sets up the outgoing leg. (½ mark)
9. The MSC sends **ASSIGNMENT COMMAND** to switch the MS from SDCCH to a **TCH**; the MS acknowledges with **ASSIGNMENT COMPLETE**. (½ mark)
10. The MS sends **SETUP**, the called party rings (**ALERTING**), the called party answers (**CONNECT** / **CONNECT ACKNOWLEDGE**), and the voice path is established. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split recap:** Diagram (1) + 9 narrative steps (~0.5–1 mark each). Keep your handwriting neat; examiners reward well-spaced sub-parts.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Frequency Reuse & Capacity (Internal Choice to Q-A)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **frequency-reuse** concept in GSM. Derive the expressions for (i) **cluster size $N$** and (ii) **co-channel reuse ratio $D/R$**. Calculate the cluster size for $i = 3$ and $j = 2$, and the resulting C/I at the cell boundary assuming a path-loss exponent $n = 4$.

**(b) [7 marks]** An operator has a 25 MHz allocation in the GSM 900 band, cluster size $N = 7$, and 8 time-slots per carrier. Compute the total system capacity and the average number of users per cell. Repeat the calculation for $N = 4$ and discuss the **C/I vs capacity trade-off**.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a) — Frequency Reuse [7 marks]**

**Concept (2 marks):** In a cellular system, the total available spectrum is finite. To serve more users than the spectrum alone would allow, the *same* set of RF frequencies is *reused* in cells that are geographically far enough apart that their mutual co-channel interference (CCI) is below an acceptable threshold. The service area is divided into hexagonal cells grouped into **clusters**, each cluster using a *unique* set of frequencies; the pattern of clusters is then tiled across the service area.

**(i) Cluster size derivation (1.5 marks):**

In a hexagonal grid, let the cluster be defined by two non-negative integer shift parameters $i$ and $j$. The cluster size is:

$$
N = i^2 + ij + j^2
$$

**Numerical substitution (1 mark):** For $i = 3$ and $j = 2$:

$$
N = 3^2 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2^2 = 9 + 6 + 4 = 19
$$

**(ii) Reuse ratio derivation (1.5 marks):** The geometry of the hexagonal grid places the co-channel cells at a distance:

$$
D = R \cdot \sqrt{3N} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$

For $N = 19$:

$$
\dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3 \times 19} = \sqrt{57} \approx 7.55
$$

**C/I calculation (1 mark):** Using the standard first-tier interference model with $n = 4$:

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{R}\right)^n = \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot (7.55)^4
$$

Stepwise:

$$
(7.55)^2 = 57.0
$$

$$
(7.55)^4 = (57.0)^2 = 3249
$$

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{3249}{6} = 541.5
$$

In dB:

$$
\left[\dfrac{S}{I}\right]_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(541.5) = 10 \times 2.733 = 27.3 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Result:** Cluster size = **19**, reuse ratio = **7.55**, C/I = **27.3 dB** — a *very* high margin, suitable for low-density / high-interference scenarios.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b) — Capacity [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Number of carriers (1 mark):**

$$
M = \dfrac{25 \text{ MHz}}{0.2 \text{ MHz}} = 125 \text{ (theoretical)}, \quad \text{or 124 in practice}
$$

**Step 2 — Channels per cluster (1 mark):**

$$
\text{channels per cluster} = M \times 8 = 124 \times 8 = 992
$$

**Step 3 — For $N = 7$ (1 mark):**

$$
\text{users per cell (avg.)} = \dfrac{992}{7} \approx 142
$$

**Step 4 — For $N = 4$ (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{12} = 3.46
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{D}{R}\right)^4 = 12^2 = 144
$$

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{144}{6} = 24
$$

$$
\left[\dfrac{S}{I}\right]_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(24) = 13.8 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{users per cell (avg.)} = \dfrac{992}{4} = 248
$$

**Step 5 — Trade-off discussion (3 marks):**

| Parameter | $N = 7$ | $N = 4$ |
|---|---|---|
| Channels per cell | 142 | 248 |
| Reuse ratio $D/R$ | 4.58 | 3.46 |
| C/I (n=4) | 18.7 dB | 13.8 dB |
| Margin above 9 dB threshold | 9.7 dB | 4.8 dB |
| Best use case | Standard urban / suburban | High-capacity dense urban, sectored cells |

> **Conclusion:** Reducing $N$ from 7 to 4 gives a **75% increase in per-cell capacity** (142 → 248) but reduces the C/I margin from 9.7 dB to 4.8 dB. Operators using $N = 4$ must therefore deploy **sectorization** (typically 3 sectors of 120° each, which *reduces* the number of first-tier interferers from 6 to 2) and **frequency hopping** to recover the lost C/I. This is the classical engineering trade-off in cellular dimensioning and the conceptual foundation of every modern cellular technology (UMTS, LTE, 5G NR).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU student pitfalls in this question:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert MHz → kHz consistently. Always write units on every number.
> 2. Writing "C/I = 18.7 dB" without showing the intermediate $(D/R)^4$ step.
> 3. Forgetting that $N$ must be a **valid** cluster size — e.g., writing "cluster size N = 5" is impossible.
> 4. Confusing **C/I** with **S/N (signal-to-noise ratio)**. They are different concepts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 "Must Memorize" List for GSM:**

- [ ] GSM is a **2G** digital cellular standard, TDMA + FDMA, defined by ETSI.
- [ ] Frequency bands: **900 / 1800 / 1900 / 850 MHz** — uplink is always *below* downlink.
- [ ] Carrier bandwidth: **200 kHz**; time-slots per carrier: **8**; modulation: **GMSK**; gross bit rate: **270.833 kbps**.
- [ ] Frame duration: **4.615 ms**; burst length: **156.25 bits**; speech codec: **RPE-LTP at 13 kbps**, channel-coded to **9.6 kbps**.
- [ ] Subsystems: **MS, BSS (BTS+BSC), NSS (MSC, HLR, VLR, AuC, EIR, GMSC), OSS (OMC)**.
- [ ] Interfaces: **Um** (radio), **Abis** (BTS↔BSC), **A** (BSC↔MSC), **B/C/D/E/F/G** (between MSC, HLR, VLR, EIR), **O** (OSS).
- [ ] Identifiers: **IMSI** (15 digits, in SIM), **MSISDN** (the phone number), **IMEI** (15 digits, in handset), **TMSI** (32 bits, VLR-assigned pseudonymous), **MSRN**, **LAI**, **CI**, **BSIC**.
- [ ] Security: **A3** (authentication), **A8** (key generation), **A5** (ciphering); triplet = **RAND, SRES, Kc**.
- [ ] Logical channels: **RACH (UL)**, **AGCH, PCH, BCCH, FCCH, SCH (all DL)**, **SDCCH, SACCH, FACCH, TCH (bi)**.
- [ ] Cluster size formula: $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$. Valid $N$ values: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 19, 21, 25, 27...
- [ ] Reuse ratio: $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$. For $N = 7$: $D/R = 4.58$.
- [ ] C/I at cell boundary: $S/I = \frac{1}{6} (D/R)^n$. With $n = 4$ and $N = 7$: 18.7 dB (margin 9.7 dB above the 9 dB threshold).
- [ ] Capacity: $C = M \times k / N$, where $M$ = carriers, $k$ = slots/carrier, $N$ = cluster size.
- [ ] Handover types: **Intra-cell, Inter-cell Intra-BSC, Inter-BSC Intra-MSC, Inter-MSC**.
- [ ] GSM **GPRS** is 2.5G (packet-switched, 171.2 kbps theoretical per slot), **EDGE** is 2.75G (8-PSK, 384 kbps theoretical).
- [ ] GSM max mobility support: **250 km/h**; max cell radius: **35 km** (theoretical).
- [ ] TRAU (Transcoder and Rate Adaptation Unit) sits between BSC and MSC, performing 64 kbps PCM ↔ 16 kbps GSM codec conversion.

> **End of Module 3 — Cellular Technology / GSM Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 4G LTE (Overview)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**4G LTE (Fourth Generation Long Term Evolution)** is a standard for wireless broadband communication for mobile devices and data terminals, defined by the **3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)** in Release 8 (frozen in 2008). It is fundamentally a packet-switched, IP-based network architecture that delivers significantly higher peak data rates, lower latency, and improved spectral efficiency compared to its 3G predecessors (UMTS/HSPA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** 4G LTE is the OFDMA-based, all-IP, flat-radio-access network that succeeded 3G UMTS. It serves as a **critical cellular connectivity backbone for IoT** (alongside NB-IoT and LTE-M), enabling high-bandwidth, low-latency machine-type communications (e.g., vehicular telemetry, smart-city CCTV, industrial gateways).

### 1.2 The "Long Term Evolution" Terminology

- **LTE** is the *technology* (the radio interface and core network redesign).
- **E-UTRA / E-UTRAN** = Evolved UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access / Network.
- **EPC** = Evolved Packet Core (the all-IP core).
- **EPS** = Evolved Packet System = E-UTRAN + EPC.
- **LTE-Advanced (LTE-A)**, frozen in **3GPP Release 10 (2011)**, is the true "4G" per ITU-R IMT-Advanced requirements.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Highway" Metaphor

Imagine a busy old two-lane road (3G) where cars (data) move slowly because traffic lights and toll booths (circuit-switched nodes) keep stopping them. **4G LTE is like rebuilding that road as a 10-lane, fully automated expressway**:
- No toll booths — everything is a single **electronic toll** (all-IP packet switching).
- The lanes can be **dynamically reallocated** between fast cars and heavy trucks (OFDMA flexible subcarrier allocation).
- Multiple cars can ride in the same lane at different angles without colliding (MIMO spatial multiplexing).
- The time from entrance to exit drops drastically (latency < 10 ms).

### 1.4 High-Level Position in the Wireless Generations

| Generation | Radio Technology | Core Network | Peak Downlink (theoretical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2G (GSM)** | TDMA/FDMA | Circuit + Packet | ~ **0.27 Mbps** |
| **3G (UMTS)** | WCDMA | Circuit + Packet | ~ **2 Mbps** |
| **3.5G (HSPA+)** | WCDMA enhancements | Packet-dominant | ~ **42 Mbps** |
| **4G (LTE)** | **OFDMA / SC-FDMA** | **All-IP (EPC)** | ~ **100 Mbps** |
| **4G+ (LTE-A)** | OFDMA + CA + MIMO | All-IP (EPC) | ~ **1 Gbps** |
| **5G NR** | OFDMA / new waveforms | 5GC (SBA) | ~ **10 Gbps** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spectral Efficiency** is the single most-cited performance metric. LTE targets **5 bit/s/Hz** in the downlink and **2.5 bit/s/Hz** in the uplink — roughly **3–4× better** than HSPA Release 6.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of LTE Design Goals (3GPP TR 25.913)

The 3GPP defined five high-level requirements for LTE, of which three are foundational for the **Overview** topic:

1. **Higher Data Rates:** Peak downlink ≥ **100 Mbps** (20 MHz, 2×2 MIMO), uplink ≥ **50 Mbps**.
2. **Reduced Latency:** User-plane (one-way) < **5 ms**; control-plane (camped → active) < **100 ms**; later refined to < **50 ms** in LTE-A.
3. **Improved Spectral Efficiency & Capacity:** Cell-edge user throughput must improve by 2× over Release 6 HSPA.
4. **Flexibility of Spectrum:** Operate in **1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, and 20 MHz** bandwidths (six scalable options).
5. **All-IP Network (AIPN):** No circuit switching — voice handled via **VoLTE** (IMS-based) or fallback to 2G/3G (CSFB).

### 2.2 Architecture in One Page: The "Flat" Two-Node Design

Unlike 3G's Node-B → RNC → SGSN/MSC chain, **LTE collapses the access network into a single node**:

```
[UE]  ⇄  [eNodeB (E-UTRAN)]  ⇄  [EPC: MME + S-GW + P-GW]
                                    │
                                    └─→ IP Services / Internet
```

| Node | Full Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **UE** | User Equipment | Mobile terminal + USIM |
| **eNodeB (eNB)** | Evolved Node B | All radio functions: scheduling, handover, encryption, RRM |
| **MME** | Mobility Management Entity | Signaling, authentication, bearer setup |
| **S-GW** | Serving Gateway | Local mobility anchor, packet routing |
| **P-GW** | Packet Data Network Gateway | External IP allocation, QoS enforcement, DPI |
| **HSS** | Home Subscriber Server | Subscriber database (replaces HLR) |
| **PCRF** | Policy & Charging Rules Function | QoS/charging policy decisions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "flat"?** Removing the RNC eliminates one hop in the user-plane path, reducing latency and CAPEX/OPEX for operators. All handover decisions now happen **directly between eNBs via the X2 interface**.

### 2.3 LTE Radio Interface — The Two Hero Technologies

#### 2.3.1 OFDMA in the Downlink (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access)

- Bandwidth is split into narrow subcarriers (15 kHz spacing) — typically **600 subcarriers** in 10 MHz.
- Resources are allocated in **Resource Blocks (RBs)** of **12 subcarriers × 1 slot (0.5 ms) = 180 kHz × 1 slot**.
- Multi-user diversity: scheduler assigns best RBs to users with best channel conditions (proportional-fair, max-C/I).

#### 2.3.2 SC-FDMA in the Uplink (Single-Carrier FDMA)

- Lower PAPR (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio) → critical for **battery-powered UEs**.
- Implemented as **DFT-precoded OFDM**: data symbols → N-point DFT → subcarrier mapping → IFFT.
- This is why LTE uplinks look like single-carrier signals at RF despite using OFDM machinery.

#### 2.3.3 MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output)

- Downlink modes: **Transmit Diversity (TxD), Spatial Multiplexing (SM), Beamforming**.
- LTE Release 8: up to **2×2 (UE must support)**; up to **4×4 in eNB**.
- LTE-A: up to **8×8 DL, 4×4 UL**.

### 2.4 LTE Frame Structure (FDD — Type 1)

```
| 1 Radio Frame = 10 ms |
|  Subframe 0 | Subframe 1 | ... | Subframe 9 |
|  (1 ms each, 2 slots) |
|  Slot = 0.5 ms, 7 OFDM symbols (normal CP) |
|  1 Resource Element (RE) = 1 subcarrier × 1 symbol |
|  1 Resource Block (RB) = 12 subcarriers × 7 symbols = 84 REs |
```

- **TDD (Type 2):** Special subframes S, U, DwPTS, GP, UpPTS for DL→UL switching.
- **CP (Cyclic Prefix):** Normal = 4.69 µs, Extended = 16.67 µs (used in high-Doppler/deployments like NB-IoT).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Subcarrier spacing | $\Delta f$ | **15 kHz** |
| 2 | OFDM symbol duration (useful) | $T_{\text{sym}} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ | **66.67 µs** |
| 3 | Normal CP duration | $T_{\text{CP,norm}}$ | $\approx \mathbf{4.69 \; \mu s}$ |
| 4 | Resource Block bandwidth | $12 \cdot \Delta f$ | **180 kHz** |
| 5 | Number of RBs in 20 MHz | $N_{\text{RB}}$ | **100 RBs** |
| 6 | Peak DL bit rate (theoretical) | $R_{\text{peak,DL}} = N_{\text{RB}} \cdot N_{\text{sc/RB}} \cdot N_{\text{sym}} \cdot N_{\text{bits/sym}} \cdot \nu$ | 100 · 12 · 7 · 6 · 2 ≈ **100.8 Mbps** (with 2×2 MIMO, 64-QAM, 20 MHz) |
| 7 | Peak UL bit rate (theoretical) | $R_{\text{peak,UL}} = N_{\text{RB}} \cdot N_{\text{sc/RB}} \cdot N_{\text{sym}} \cdot N_{\text{bits/sym}}$ | 100 · 12 · 7 · 6 ≈ **50.4 Mbps** (single antenna, 64-QAM) |
| 8 | Shannon capacity (channel limit) | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bit/s; $B$ in Hz |
| 9 | Spectral efficiency target (DL) | $\eta_{\text{DL}}$ | **5 bit/s/Hz/3-sector site** |
| 10 | Spectral efficiency target (UL) | $\eta_{\text{UL}}$ | **2.5 bit/s/Hz** |
| 11 | Control-plane latency (camped → active) | $T_{\text{CP}}$ | **< 50 ms** (was 100 ms in R8) |
| 12 | User-plane one-way latency | $T_{\text{UP}}$ | **< 5 ms** (idle state, small IP pkt) |
| 13 | Cyclic Prefix overhead (normal) | $\dfrac{T_{\text{CP}}}{T_{\text{sym}} + T_{\text{CP}}} \cdot 100\%$ | $\approx \mathbf{7\%}$ |
| 14 | PAPR advantage (SC-FDMA vs OFDMA) | $\Delta_{\text{PAPR}}$ | **~ 2–6 dB lower** for SC-FDMA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE (anti-break):** In the table above, I deliberately used `\dfrac{...}{...}` and `\left( ... \right)` instead of raw vertical bars `\vert x \vert` so the markdown table syntax does not break. Always do this in KTU notes.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility — Why 4G LTE Matters for IoT

- **LTE-M (Cat-M1, 3GPP R13):** Reduced device complexity, 1.4 MHz bandwidth, extended DRX → 10+ year battery life, in-band deployment. Used in **smart meters, asset trackers, wearables**.
- **NB-IoT (Cat-NB1, R13):** 180 kHz bandwidth, ultra-cheap modems, **+20 dB MCL (Max Coupling Loss)** over GSM. Used in **LPWA applications, smart agriculture, parking sensors**.
- **High-bandwidth IoT:** Vehicle-to-everything (V2X) over LTE (C-V2X PC5 interface), telemedicine, industrial gateways, smart-city surveillance (where LTE's capacity replaces Wi-Fi for outdoor backhaul).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cross-Module Hook:** In Module 2 you studied short-range IoT (BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi). LTE is the **wide-area complement** — completing the coverage pyramid: PAN/LAN (short range) → LAN/MAN (Wi-Fi) → **WAN cellular (LTE/5G)**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Peak Downlink Bit Rate of LTE (20 MHz, 2×2 MIMO)

**Goal:** Verify the canonical figure **100.8 Mbps** for a fully loaded 20 MHz FDD LTE downlink with **2 transmit × 2 receive MIMO** and **64-QAM**.

**Step 1 — Set the parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{ch}} &= 20 \;\text{MHz (channel bandwidth)} \\
\Delta f &= 15 \;\text{kHz (subcarrier spacing)} \\
N_{\text{RB}} &= 100 \;\text{Resource Blocks (for 20 MHz)} \\
N_{\text{sc/RB}} &= 12 \;\text{subcarriers per RB} \\
N_{\text{sym/slot}} &= 7 \;\text{OFDM symbols (normal CP)} \\
N_{\text{slot/subf}} &= 2 \;\text{slots per subframe} \\
N_{\text{subf/frame}} &= 10 \;\text{subframes per radio frame} \\
N_{\text{bits/sym}} &= 6 \;\text{bits (64-QAM, 2^6 = 64)} \\
\nu &= 2 \;\text{MIMO spatial streams (2×2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate resource elements per subframe per antenna stream.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RE per subframe} &= N_{\text{RB}} \times N_{\text{sc/RB}} \times N_{\text{sym/slot}} \times N_{\text{slot/subf}} \\
&= 100 \times 12 \times 7 \times 2 \\
&= 16{,}800 \;\text{REs per subframe per stream}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by bits per symbol and spatial streams.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REs per second per stream} &= 16{,}800 \times 1000 \quad (\text{since 1 subframe = 1 ms}) \\
&= 16.8 \times 10^{6} \;\text{REs/s} \\
R_{\text{peak,DL}} &= 16.8 \times 10^{6} \times 6 \times 2 \\
&= 201.6 \times 10^{6} \;\text{bps} \\
&= 201.6 \;\text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Refine: the *standard* 100.8 Mbps figure accounts for overhead.**

In practice, not all 100 RBs are usable for data (control channels like PDCCH, PHICH, PCFICH, and reference signals CRS consume ~25–30%). Using the textbook convention of **1 stream** (SISO equivalent) gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{peak,DL,1 stream}} &= 16.8 \times 10^{6} \times 6 \\
&= 100.8 \;\text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

The "100 Mbps peak" quoted by 3GPP is **per spatial stream at full allocation**, with MIMO multiplicative gains applied separately. ✅ Result **matches the 3GPP TR 25.913 requirement**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Why SC-FDMA has lower PAPR (the *intuitive* math)

For an OFDM signal with $N$ subcarriers, the time-domain peak occurs when all subcarriers **add coherently** at one instant. The theoretical maximum PAPR (in dB) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PAPR}_{\text{OFDM, max}} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}(N) \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

For LTE uplink with **N = 1200 subcarriers** (≈ 20 MHz):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PAPR}_{\text{OFDM, max}} &\approx 10 \cdot \log_{10}(1200) \\
&\approx 30.79 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

For SC-FDMA, the DFT-spreading precoding distributes the energy more uniformly, and the equivalent PAPR behaves like a **single-carrier signal**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PAPR}_{\text{SC-FDMA}} &\approx 10 \cdot \log_{10}(N_{\text{FFT}}) - K \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K \approx 3$–$6$ dB is the empirical SC-FDMA gain (depending on cubic-metric). Hence the practical reduction of **2–6 dB** quoted in 3GPP studies. This reduction directly translates to:
- **Lower PA power back-off** → more efficient UE transmitter.
- **Higher efficiency of battery-driven modems** — critical for IoT.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LTE Release-by-Release Feature Map (Overview)

| Feature | R8 (LTE) | R9 | R10 (LTE-A) | R11 | R12 | R13 (IoT focus) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak DL | 100 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1 Gbps (CA) | 1 Gbps | 1 Gbps | 1 Gbps |
| DL MIMO | 2×2 | 2×2 | **8×8** | 8×8 | 8×8 | 8×8 |
| **Carrier Aggregation (CA)** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (up to 5×20 MHz) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **CoMP (Coordinated MP)** | ❌ | ❌ | (study) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **eIMTA / TDD enhancements** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **D2D (Sidelink, ProSe)** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | (study) | ✅ | ✅ |
| **NB-IoT (Cat-NB1)** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | **✅** |
| **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | **✅** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 IoT Module 3:** The rightmost column is **the single most important slide**. NB-IoT and LTE-M are how LTE directly serves low-power IoT — a guaranteed short-answer or 14-mark question topic.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Example — Spectral Efficiency Calculation

A 20 MHz cell with 3 sectors serves **120 users per sector** at average sector throughput of **60 Mbps**. Calculate the **average spectral efficiency** and the **spectral efficiency per user**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{sector}} &= \frac{\text{Throughput}}{B} = \frac{60 \times 10^{6}}{20 \times 10^{6}} = 3 \;\text{bit/s/Hz} \\
\eta_{\text{user}} &= \frac{\eta_{\text{sector}}}{N_{\text{users}}} = \frac{3}{120} = 0.025 \;\text{bit/s/Hz/user}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare to 3GPP target $\eta_{\text{DL}} = 5$ bit/s/Hz. This cell is operating at **60% of target** — typical for a loaded urban cell.
