# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define LoRaWAN. Explain its star-of-stars topology. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network)** is a Low Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN) protocol specification, standardized by the **LoRa Alliance**, that operates in the **unlicensed ISM bands** (e.g., 868 MHz in Europe, 915 MHz in the US, 923 MHz in India) using the proprietary **LoRa chirp spread spectrum (CSS)** physical layer modulation.

**Star-of-Stars Topology:** LoRaWAN uses a **two-tier star architecture**:

- The **first star** is formed between **End Devices** (sensors) and one or more **Gateways**, where each end device transmits directly to any gateway within radio range (no multi-hop).
- The **second star** is formed between **Gateways** and the **central Network Server**, connected via standard IP backhaul.
- This star-of-stars decouples the radio hop (long-range, low-power) from the backhaul hop (high-throughput, mains-powered), enabling simple, cheap, battery-powered end devices. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between LoRa and LoRaWAN. Why is the distinction important? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LoRa | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** | **MAC Layer (Layer 2)** |
| **Function** | Defines the **radio modulation** (CSS chirp) | Defines the **network protocol, addressing, security** |
| **Owner** | Semtech proprietary chip (SX1276, SX1262) | LoRa Alliance open specification |
| **Scope** | Point-to-point radio link between two modems | Multi-device network with gateways, NS, AS |
| **Key Concepts** | SF, BW, CR, symbol time | DevAddr, Join procedure, ADR, device classes |

**Importance of Distinction:** A student must understand that **LoRa alone cannot build a multi-node IoT network** — it is only the physical modulation. **LoRaWAN** provides the **MAC protocol**, addressing, and architecture that allow thousands of devices to coexist on a single network. Confusing them is a common conceptual error. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option I) — Full Marks 14 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **LoRaWAN architecture** in detail. Discuss the role of the Network Server. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the **three device classes** in LoRaWAN with their receive window behavior, power consumption profiles, and suitable applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**LoRaWAN Architecture** consists of four logical layers (as shown in the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **End Devices (Motes):** Battery-powered sensor/actuator nodes. They run a lightweight MAC stack, transmit on an ALOHA basis (no scheduling), and implement Class A behavior. They use the LoRa physical layer (e.g., Semtech SX1276) for radio transmission.

2. **Gateways:** Mains-powered base stations with multi-channel LoRa concentrators (typically 8 channels). They are **transparent bridges** — they demodulate LoRa frames and forward them via IP (using protocols like **Semtech UDP Packet Forwarder** or **LoRa Basics Station**) to the Network Server. They do not interpret the LoRaWAN MAC layer.

3. **Network Server (NS):** The **centralized brain** of the network. Its roles include:
   - **Deduplication** of frames received by multiple gateways.
   - **MAC command processing** (link check requests, ADR, duty cycle, channel mask).
   - **Frame authentication** via the Message Integrity Code (MIC) using NwkSKey.
   - **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** — per-device optimization of SF, BW, Tx power.
   - **Downlink scheduling** — queuing server-to-device traffic for the next RX1/RX2 window.
   - **Roaming** support and Join Server interface.

4. **Application Server (AS):** The end-customer system. It **decrypts the application payload using AppSKey** and runs business logic. The AS and NS are kept cryptographically isolated.

**Why a centralized NS?** Concentrating intelligence in the NS keeps end devices cheap and energy-efficient, allows global network optimizations (ADR, scheduling), and provides a single point for security audits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Class A — Bi-directional (Lowest Power):**
- Downlink receive windows open **only after an uplink** transmission.
- **RX1 window** opens 1 second (default, configurable) after the uplink ends, on the same frequency and data rate as the uplink.
- **RX2 window** opens 2 seconds after the uplink ends, on a fixed frequency and data rate (e.g., 869.525 MHz / SF12 in EU868).
- If neither window receives a downlink, the device returns to deep sleep.
- **Use case:** Battery-powered sensors (smart metering, environmental monitoring). **(2.5 marks)**

**Class B — Bi-directional with Scheduled Downlink (Medium Power):**
- End devices additionally open **scheduled ping slots** synchronized to **gateway beacons**.
- Beacons are broadcast by gateways every 128 seconds, providing a time reference.
- Network server can predict when a Class B device will be listening and send downlinks accordingly.
- **Use case:** Actuators that need periodic control (e.g., valve control in irrigation). **(2.5 marks)**

**Class C — Continuously Listening (Highest Power):**
- The receive window is **continuously open** except during uplink transmission.
- Server can send downlinks at any time with minimal latency.
- Energy budget: typically mains-powered (cannot run on coin cell).
- **Use case:** Smart street lighting, real-time actuator control. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (Option II — Alternative Choice) — Full Marks 14 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **LoRaWAN physical layer** parameters: Spreading Factor (SF), Bandwidth (BW), and Coding Rate (CR). Derive the formula for **bit rate** and **symbol duration**, and compute the bit rate for SF=11, BW=250 kHz, CR=4/5. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** mechanism. Why is ADR important for scaling LoRaWAN networks? How does the Network Server decide to change SF? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Physical Layer Parameters:**

- **Spreading Factor (SF):** Number of bits encoded per LoRa symbol. Range: **SF7 (fastest, shortest range)** to **SF12 (slowest, longest range)**. Each unit increase in SF doubles the symbol duration and increases receiver sensitivity by ~2.5 dB.

- **Bandwidth (BW):** Width of the channel. Standard values: **125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz**. Wider BW → higher data rate but lower sensitivity.

- **Coding Rate (CR):** Forward error correction overhead. Values: **4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8** (i.e., $CR = 4/(4+n)$ where n=1,2,3,4). Higher CR → more redundancy → better robustness, but slower throughput.

**Derivation of Symbol Rate:**

Each LoRa symbol is a chirp that sweeps the entire bandwidth once. The number of distinct frequency positions in the chirp is $2^{SF}$, so the chirp duration (symbol time) must be:

$$
T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}
$$

Symbol rate is the reciprocal:

$$
R_s = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}}
$$

**Derivation of Bit Rate:**

Each symbol carries $SF$ bits. With coding rate $CR$:

$$
R_b = SF \times R_s \times CR = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times \frac{4}{4+n}
$$

**Numerical Computation for SF=11, BW=250 kHz, CR=4/5 (n=1):**

$$
R_s = \frac{250{,}000}{2^{11}} = \frac{250{,}000}{2048} \approx 122.07 \text{ symbols/sec}
$$

$$
R_b = 11 \times 122.07 \times \frac{4}{5} = 11 \times 122.07 \times 0.8 \approx 1074.22 \text{ bps}
$$

**Symbol Duration:**

$$
T_s = \frac{2048}{250{,}000} \approx 8.192 \text{ ms}
$$

**(7 marks — 2 for parameter explanation, 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical answer)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Adaptive Data Rate (ADR):** ADR is a **network-side optimization algorithm** that dynamically tunes each end device's **SF, BW, and Tx power** to maintain a reliable link while **minimizing Time-on-Air (ToA) and energy consumption**.

**Why ADR is Critical for Scaling:**

1. **Spectrum efficiency:** Shorter ToA means more devices can coexist in the same channel under duty cycle regulations.
2. **Battery life:** A device close to the gateway can use SF7 (ToA ~50 ms) instead of SF12 (ToA ~1500 ms), reducing energy per transmission by **30×**.
3. **Network capacity:** More packets per unit time = more devices supported.

**How the Network Server Decides SF Change:**

The NS evaluates the **SNR margin** from the most recent uplinks:

$$
SNR_{margin} = SNR_{measured} - SNR_{required}(SF) - \text{safety margin}
$$

- $SNR_{required}$ is a function of SF (e.g., SF7 needs -7.5 dB, SF12 needs -20 dB).
- A high SNR margin (e.g., >10 dB) means the device has "headroom" — NS commands it to **reduce SF** (faster, less ToA).
- A low or negative margin means the link is marginal — NS commands it to **increase SF** or **lower BW** (more sensitivity).

The NS sends an **ADR MAC command** in the FOpts field of a downlink, which the device applies to subsequent uplinks. **(7 marks — 2 for ADR definition, 3 for importance, 2 for decision logic)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LoRa (Physical) with LoRaWAN (MAC).** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if a student uses these terms interchangeably. Always specify the OSI layer.
> 2. **Forgetting the AppSKey vs NwkSKey distinction.** The **NwkSKey** protects the **integrity** (MIC verification) between device and NS, while the **AppSKey** provides **end-to-end encryption** between device and AS. The NS **cannot decrypt application data** — this is a frequent 1-mark trap.
> 3. **Wrong formula for SF vs Range trade-off.** A common error: writing "higher SF = higher data rate." The **opposite** is true. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** Every numerical result in KTU papers must carry units (bps, kHz, ms, dB). A correct number without units loses **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Not mentioning duty cycle regulations** when discussing ToA — EU868 has 1% duty cycle sub-bands; this is a board-favorite 1-mark differentiator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision for KTU Module 3 — LoRaWAN Architecture**

- **Definition:** LoRaWAN is a **MAC layer LPWAN protocol** (NOT a physical layer). LoRa = physical modulation (CSS); LoRaWAN = network protocol.
- **Topology:** **Star-of-stars** — End Devices → Gateways → Network Server → Application Server.
- **Gateways are transparent** — they do not interpret, decrypt, or process LoRaWAN frames. They only forward.
- **Network Server is the brain** — handles deduplication, ADR, MAC commands, downlink scheduling, security verification.
- **Application Server is the payload destination** — uses **AppSKey** to decrypt application data.
- **Three Device Classes:** A (battery, RX after TX), B (scheduled ping slots), C (always-on, mains powered). **Class A is mandatory**.
- **Spreading Factor Range:** **SF7 (fast, short range) → SF12 (slow, long range)**. Higher SF = ~2.5 dB more sensitivity per step.
- **Bandwidth:** 125, 250, or 500 kHz. Wider BW = higher data rate.
- **Coding Rate:** $CR = 4/(4+n)$, where $n \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.
- **Bit Rate Formula:** $R_b = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR$ — **must memorize**.
- **Symbol Duration:** $T_s = 2^{SF}/BW$ — **must memorize**.
- **Activation:** **OTAA** (dynamic, secure, recommended) vs **ABP** (static, less secure).
- **Security Keys (all 128-bit AES):** **AppKey** (root, used in Join), **NwkSKey** (MIC & network encryption), **AppSKey** (end-to-end app payload encryption).
- **ISM Bands:** **EU 868 MHz, US 915 MHz, India/AS 923 MHz** — know at least the Indian band for KTU context.
- **Duty Cycle (EU868):** Sub-bands enforce **0.1%, 1%, or 10%** duty cycle.
- **ADR:** Network Server adjusts SF/Tx power per device to minimize ToA while keeping reliable link.
- **Max Payload:** **51 bytes** (at SF12, BW125) — very small, LoRaWAN is **not for high-throughput** applications.
- **Range:** Up to **15 km rural LoS**, **2–5 km urban** — better than Wi-Fi/BLE/ZigBee for outdoor IoT.
- **Battery Life Target:** **Up to 10 years** on a single battery (Class A, low-duty cycle).
- **Comparison Quick Hits:** LoRaWAN uses **unlicensed** spectrum (vs NB-IoT's licensed). LoRaWAN uses **CSS** (vs Sigfox's UNB). LoRaWAN is **open** (vs Sigfox's proprietary).
- **KTU High-Frequency Keywords:** "Star-of-stars," "Class A mandatory," "AppSKey end-to-end," "Adaptive Data Rate," "Network Server is the brain," "transparent gateway."

**End of Module 3 Notes — LoRaWAN Architecture**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 6LoWPAN - Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 6LoWPAN - Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**6LoWPAN** (IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks) is an **adaptation layer protocol**, defined under **RFC 4944** (updated by **RFC 6282** and **RFC 6775**), that enables the transmission of **IPv6 packets** over **IEEE 802.15.4** based low-power, low-rate wireless networks. It acts as a thin convergence layer sandwiched between the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer and the IPv6 network layer, solving the incompatibility between the large IPv6 packet size (**1280 bytes** minimum MTU) and the tiny IEEE 802.15.4 frame size (**127 bytes** total).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEERT614 - Module 3):**
> 6LoWPAN is one of the foundational protocols of the IoT application layer stack. Examiners frequently test two aspects: (1) the **motivation/need** for 6LoWPAN, and (2) its **features** (header compression, fragmentation, mesh addressing, stateless autoconfiguration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardization Bodies:**
> - **IETF 6LoWPAN Working Group** — protocol specification
> - **RFC 4944** — Transmission of IPv6 Packets over IEEE 802.15.4 Networks (original)
> - **RFC 6282** — Compression Format for IPv6 Datagrams in 6LoWPAN Networks (IPHC)
> - **RFC 6775** — Neighbor Discovery Optimization for 6LoWPAN

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are trying to send a **large A4-size letter** through a **tiny mailbox slot** designed for postcards. The letter simply does not fit.

**6LoWPAN is the "magic envelope adapter"** that:
1. **Folds** the letter (IPv6 header) into a smaller, compact form (**header compression**).
2. If still too big, **tears** the letter into multiple smaller pieces that fit through the slot (**fragmentation**).
3. On the receiver side, **unfolds** and **reassembles** the original letter perfectly.

In a 6LoWPAN network, the small, battery-operated sensor (called a **LoWPAN node** or **6LoWPAN device**) speaks "tiny-frame" language (IEEE 802.15.4), and the wider Internet speaks "big-packet" language (IPv6). The 6LoWPAN adaptation layer is the **universal translator** between the two.

## 1.3 Why 6LoWPAN is Needed (The "Why")

| Constraint | IPv6 Requirement | IEEE 802.15.4 Reality | The Gap |
|------------|------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Minimum MTU | **1280 bytes** | **127 bytes** total frame | ~10x mismatch |
| IPv6 Header | 40 bytes fixed | ~81–102 bytes usable payload | Header fills frame |
| Address length | 128 bits | 64-bit MAC, short frames | Address overhead |
| Power | Mains-powered | Battery, months/years | Energy budget tight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without 6LoWPAN, an IPv6 packet simply **cannot** be transmitted over an IEEE 802.15.4 link. The adaptation layer is what makes **"Internet-of-Things devices"** truly *on* the Internet.

## 1.4 Key Standards & Physical Metrics

- **Operating Frequency:** 2.4 GHz (ISM band), 868/915 MHz
- **Raw Data Rate:** 250 kbps (2.4 GHz), 20/40 kbps (sub-GHz)
- **Range:** 10–100 m typical
- **Frame Size:** **127 bytes** (max), **MAC payload: 81–102 bytes** (depending on addressing/security)
- **Typical Devices:** 8-bit MCUs, 32–256 KB flash, 4–16 KB RAM
- **Power Source:** Coin cell / AA batteries (target lifetime: **years**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6LoWPAN Protocol Stack

6LoWPAN sits as a **shim/adapter layer** between the data-link and network layers. It is **not** a replacement for IPv6 — IPv6 still runs end-to-end. 6LoWPAN only modifies the *on-the-wire* representation **inside** the LoWPAN.

```
+-----------------------+
|    Application (CoAP, HTTP, MQTT)
+-----------------------+
|   Transport (UDP / TCP)
+-----------------------+
|   Network (IPv6, ICMPv6, NDP)
+-----------------------+
|  6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer   <-- (Header Compression, Fragmentation,
|                                  Mesh Addressing)
+-----------------------+
|   IEEE 802.15.4 MAC
+-----------------------+
|   IEEE 802.15.4 PHY (Radio)
+-----------------------+
```

## 2.2 The Four Core Features of 6LoWPAN

### Feature 1 — Header Compression
- IPv6 has a **40-byte** fixed header + extension headers. 6LoWPAN uses **IPHC (IP Header Compression)** to compress this to as little as **2–4 bytes** over the air.
- Common contexts (link-local prefix, link-layer address) are **elided** (omitted) because they are inferable from the IEEE 802.15.4 frame.
- Defined in **RFC 6282**; supersedes older LOWPAN_IPHC dispatch (0x60–0x7F).
- **Two compression profiles:** IPHC (for IPv6) and NALP (Next Header Compression / LOWPAN_NHC) for UDP, TCP, etc.

### Feature 2 — Fragmentation & Reassembly
- IPv6 mandates a **minimum MTU of 1280 bytes**; 802.15.4 frames are only 127 bytes. The LoWPAN layer must **slice** oversized datagrams and **reassemble** them.
- **First fragment** carries: dispatch (1 B) + datagram tag (2 B) + datagram size (1 B) → 4-byte fragmentation header.
- **Subsequent fragments** carry: dispatch (1 B) + datagram tag (2 B) + datagram offset (1 B) → 4-byte fragmentation header.
- Maximum number of fragments: limited by the 8-bit datagram size field → up to **255 fragments** per datagram.

### Feature 3 — Mesh Addressing (Mesh-Under vs. Route-Over)
- 6LoWPAN supports **mesh topologies** where packets hop between 802.15.4 nodes.
- **Mesh-Under:** routing happens **inside** the LoWPAN using 16-bit short addresses; the IP layer sees one hop. 6LoWPAN uses the **dispatch type `01 00` (LOWPAN_IPHC not used; instead `01000001` / mesh header)**.
- **Route-Over:** each hop is an IP hop; routing decisions made at the IP layer using **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks).

### Feature 4 — Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) & Neighbor Discovery Optimization
- IPv6 SLAAC uses the 64-bit interface identifier derived from the EUI-64 (MAC address).
- 6LoWPAN introduces **6LoWPAN-ND** (RFC 6775) to optimize Neighbor Discovery by:
  - Replacing multicast **Router Solicitation/Advertisement** with **shorter unicast/6LoWPAN-specific messages** (e.g., Duplicate Address Request / Confirmation).
  - Using a **6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR)** as the central authority.
  - Registering nodes via **Address Registration (ARO)** to the 6LBR.

## 2.3 6LoWPAN Dispatch Byte (Header Type Identification)

The first byte(s) of a 6LoWPAN frame identify its encoding:

| Dispatch Value | Pattern | Meaning |
|----------------|---------|---------|
| `01 000001` (`0x41`) | NALP, not 6LoWPAN | IPv6 uncompressed |
| `01000001` to `0x7F` | 11 prefix | Reserved |
| `011 xxxxx` | IPHC | IPv6 header compression (RFC 6282) |
| `10 00 xxxx` | First fragment | Fragment header (first) |
| `11 00 xxxx` | Subsequent fragment | Fragment header (next) |
| `00 xxxxxx` | Mesh addressing | Mesh delivery header |
| `01 000000` | Broadcast | 6LoWPAN broadcast header |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|------|-------|
| 1 | IEEE 802.15.4 max frame | $F_{max} = 127$ | bytes | Hard physical limit |
| 2 | Max MAC payload (no security) | $P_{max} = 127 - 25 = 102$ | bytes | With 2-byte FCS, 21-byte MAC hdr |
| 3 | Max MAC payload (with security) | $P_{sec} \approx 81$ | bytes | AES-CCM-128 consumes 21 bytes |
| 4 | Minimum IPv6 MTU | $MTU_{IPv6} \ge 1280$ | bytes | IPv6 standard (RFC 2460) |
| 5 | IPv6 header (fixed) | $H_{v6} = 40$ | bytes | Uncompressed |
| 6 | 6LoWPAN IPHC compressed header | $H_{iphc} \in [2, 14]$ | bytes | Typical: 4–6 bytes |
| 7 | Number of fragments | $N = \left\lceil \frac{P_{IPv6}}{P_{payload}} \right\rceil$ | integer | Where $P_{IPv6}$ is datagram size, $P_{payload}$ = MAC payload − 4 |
| 8 | Fragmentation overhead | $H_{frag} = 4$ | bytes | Per fragment |
| 9 | Compression ratio | $R_c = \left(1 - \frac{H_{iphc}}{H_{v6}}\right) \times 100\%$ | % | Typical: 80–90% |
| 10 | Air time factor | $T_{air} = \frac{N \times (F_{max} \times 8)}{R_{data}}$ | seconds | $R_{data} = 250$ kbps for 2.4 GHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** 6LoWPAN does *not* invent a new network protocol. It only **encodes** IPv6 into smaller pieces. From end-to-end, the network is still **pure IPv6** — this is its greatest strength and a frequent exam point.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Metering** (electricity, gas, water): millions of meters using IPv6 addressing
- **Precision Agriculture:** soil sensors with 6LoWPAN over 802.15.4g
- **Building Automation:** BACnet-over-IPv6 via 6LoWPAN
- **Industrial WSN:** wireless sensor networks inside factories
- **Smart Cities:** connected streetlights, parking sensors
- **Thread Protocol:** built on top of 6LoWPAN for home IoT
- **Zigbee IP / Zigbee PRO 2017:** uses 6LoWPAN as the network layer
- **Matter / Project CHIP** (parts of the stack): influenced by 6LoWPAN design
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Fragmentation Calculation

> **Given:** An IPv6 datagram of size **$P_{IPv6} = 1280$ bytes** is to be transmitted over IEEE 802.15.4 with **AES-CCM-128 security enabled** (MAC header = 25 bytes, security overhead = 21 bytes, FCS = 2 bytes).
>
> **Find:** (a) Available MAC payload, (b) 6LoWPAN fragmentation overhead, (c) Number of fragments required, (d) Total on-air bytes.

### Step 1 — Compute Maximum MAC Payload

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{max} &= 127 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{MAC header} &= 25 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Security} &= 21 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{FCS} &= 2 \text{ bytes} \\
P_{max} &= 127 - 25 - 21 - 2 \\
P_{max} &= 79 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(raw MAC payload)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** With security enabled, only **79 bytes** are left for everything above the MAC. This is the "postcard slot" through which the entire IPv6 datagram must squeeze.

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Fragment Payload After Fragmentation Header

The 6LoWPAN fragmentation header (first or subsequent) consumes **4 bytes**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{payload} &= P_{max} - H_{frag} \\
P_{payload} &= 79 - 4 \\
P_{payload} &= 75 \text{ bytes per fragment (data only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Number of Fragments

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lceil \frac{P_{IPv6}}{P_{payload}} \right\rceil \\
N &= \left\lceil \frac{1280}{75} \right\rceil \\
N &= \left\lceil 17.0\overline{6} \right\rceil \\
N &= 18 \text{ fragments}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Total On-Air Bytes

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= N \times F_{max} \\
T_{total} &= 18 \times 127 \\
T_{total} &= 2286 \text{ bytes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead factor} &= \frac{T_{total}}{P_{IPv6}} = \frac{2286}{1280} \approx 1.79
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** A 1280-byte IPv6 datagram costs **~79% extra bytes on the air** when fragmented — a strong motivation to use **header compression first** to *reduce* $P_{IPv6}$ before fragmenting.

### Step 5 — Air-Time at 250 kbps

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{air} &= \frac{T_{total} \times 8}{R_{data}} \\
T_{air} &= \frac{2286 \times 8}{250{,}000} \\
T_{air} &= \frac{18{,}288}{250{,}000} \\
T_{air} &\approx 0.0732 \text{ s} = 73.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Header Compression Efficiency

> **Given:** A UDP/IPv6 packet with full headers (IPv6 = 40 B, UDP = 8 B, total = 48 B). Compressed by 6LoWPAN IPHC + NHC.
>
> **Compressed sizes:** IPHC dispatch + encoding = **6 bytes**, NHC-UDP = **2 bytes**.
>
> **Find:** (a) Compressed header size, (b) Compression ratio, (c) Air-time savings over 250 kbps for 100-byte payload.

### Step 1 — Total Compressed Header Size

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{comp} &= H_{iphc} + H_{NHC} \\
H_{comp} &= 6 + 2 \\
H_{comp} &= 8 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compression Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{orig} &= 40 \text{ (IPv6)} + 8 \text{ (UDP)} = 48 \text{ bytes} \\
R_c &= \left(1 - \frac{H_{comp}}{H_{orig}}\right) \times 100\% \\
R_c &= \left(1 - \frac{8}{48}\right) \times 100\% \\
R_c &= \left(1 - 0.16\overline{6}\right) \times 100\% \\
R_c &= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** 6LoWPAN compresses the headers by **~83%** — this is the *killer feature* of IPHC.

### Step 3 — Air-Time Comparison for 100-byte payload

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{uncomp} &= \frac{(48 + 100) \times 8}{250{,}000} = 4.736 \text{ ms} \\
T_{comp} &= \frac{(8 + 100) \times 8}{250{,}000} = 3.456 \text{ ms} \\
\Delta T &= T_{uncomp} - T_{comp} = 1.280 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Savings} &= \frac{\Delta T}{T_{uncomp}} \times 100\% \approx 27.03\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** At 1 transmission/second, a node saves **~27% battery** per packet — over 1 year, this means a **~2.5x longer battery life** if the radio is the dominant energy consumer.

---

## 3.3 Feature-to-Mechanism Mapping (Analytical Matrix)

| 6LoWPAN Feature | Mechanism / Standard | Field in Frame | Bytes Saved / Used |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|--------------------|
| Header compression (IPHC) | RFC 6282 | IPHC dispatch + TF + NH + HLIM bits | Up to 38 of 40 bytes saved |
| UDP header compression (NHC) | RFC 6282 §4.3 | NHC byte + inline fields | Up to 7 of 8 bytes saved |
| Fragmentation | RFC 4944 §5.3 | 1st/subsequent header | 4 bytes per fragment |
| Mesh addressing | RFC 4944 §5.2 | Mesh header | 4–16 bytes |
| Broadcast | RFC 4944 §5.1 | 1-byte dispatch (`0x50`) | Marginal |
| Stateless autoconfig | RFC 4862 + 6775 | EUI-64 from 802.15.4 MAC | 0 (out of band) |
| Neighbor Discovery opt. | RFC 6775 | DAR/DAO/NSARO messages | Replaces multicast RS/RA |
| Security | IEEE 802.15.4 + IPsec | AES-CCM-128 | 0/21 bytes cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 6LoWPAN Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps how an IPv6 datagram from the Internet is processed step-by-step by the 6LoWPAN adaptation layer before transmission over an IEEE 802.15.4 radio, and reconstructed on the receiver side.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Internet IPv6 Cloud] --> B[6LBR 6LoWPAN Border Router]
    B --> C{Needs Compression?}
    C -- Yes --> D[IPHC Header Compression RFC 6282]
    C -- No --> E[Uncompressed IPv6 Dispatch 41]
    D --> F{Fits in 127 bytes?}
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G[Add IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Header 25 bytes]
    F -- No --> H[Fragmentation Header 4 bytes per slice]
    H --> G
    G --> I[AES CCM 128 Encryption optional 21 bytes]
    I --> J[Add PHY Frame Check Sequence FCS 2 bytes]
    J --> K[2.4 GHz Radio Transmission 250 kbps]
    K --> L[Receiver 6LoWPAN Node]
    L --> M[Strip PHY and MAC Headers]
    M --> N[Verify AES and FCS]
    N --> O{Fragmented?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Reassemble using datagram tag and offset]
    O -- No --> Q[Decompress IPHC and NHC Headers]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Forward IPv6 to Upper Layers UDP CoAP MQTT]
    R --> S[Application Sensor Reading]

    style B fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 IPHC Header Bit Layout (Functional Block Map)

The IPHC 2-byte header (followed by inline fields) shows exactly which parts of the IPv6 header are elided and which are sent.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Byte 0: 011 TF NH HLIM CID SAC SAM M DAC] --> B[TF: Traffic Class and Flow Label]
    A --> C[NH: Next Header Compressed or Inline]
    A --> D[HLIM: Hop Limit Compressed or 1 or 64 or 255]
    A --> E[CID: Context Identifier Extension]
    A --> F[SAC and SAM: Source Address Compression Mode]
    A --> G[M and DAC: Multicast and Destination Address Compression]
    H[Byte 1: 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 if CID eq 0] --> A

    style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 6LoWPAN Network Topology (Mesh-Under vs Route-Over)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IPV6_INTERNET[IPv6 Internet Domain]
        S[Server Cloud]
    end
    S --> BR[6LBR Border Router]

    subgraph LOWPAN_DOMAIN[6LoWPAN Mesh Domain]
        BR --> N1[Node 1 Router]
        BR --> N2[Node 2 Host]
        N1 --> N3[Node 3 Router]
        N1 --> N4[Node 4 Sleepy Host]
        N3 --> N5[Node 5 Router]
        N3 --> N6[Node 6 Host]
        N2 --> N7[Node 7 Host]
    end

    BR -.Route-Over IP hops.-> N1
    BR -.Mesh-Under.-> N3
    N3 -.Mesh-Under.-> N6

    style BR fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N1 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N3 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N4 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N6 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**List any four features of 6LoWPAN. Briefly explain header compression as a feature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four primary features of 6LoWPAN are:
1. **Header Compression** — Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header (plus UDP/extension headers) down to as little as 2–4 bytes using the IPHC encoding (RFC 6282). This is done by **eliding** fields whose values are **inferable** from the link layer (e.g., the link-local IPv6 prefix `fe80::/64`).
2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — Splits large IPv6 datagrams to fit 802.15.4 frames of only 127 bytes, and reassembles them at the destination.
3. **Mesh Addressing** — Supports multi-hop delivery inside the LoWPAN, either as transparent mesh-under or as visible route-over using RPL.
4. **Stateless Address Autoconfiguration & Optimized Neighbor Discovery** — Each node derives a unique IPv6 address from its 64-bit IEEE 802.15.4 MAC address, and 6LoWPAN-ND (RFC 6775) reduces expensive multicast solicitations.

*(Examiner's tip: Any 4 valid features with one explained in 1–2 lines fetch full 3 marks. Header compression is the most-asked feature.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Why can't IPv6 packets be transmitted directly over IEEE 802.15.4 networks? How does 6LoWPAN solve this?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
IPv6 mandates a **minimum MTU of 1280 bytes**, but IEEE 802.15.4 frames have a **maximum size of only 127 bytes** (with a usable MAC payload of ~81–102 bytes after headers and security). This **10× size mismatch** prevents direct transmission of an IPv6 packet. 6LoWPAN solves this by introducing an **adaptation layer** that performs:
- **Header compression** (IPHC) to reduce the 40-byte IPv6 header to 2–4 bytes.
- **Fragmentation** to slice oversized datagrams into multiple 802.15.4 frames, reassembling them at the destination.
- **Mesh addressing** to route packets over multi-hop LoWPANs.

The end result: IPv6 runs **end-to-end unmodified**, but is efficiently carried over tiny radio frames.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (1 × 14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **6LoWPAN protocol stack** and clearly state the role of the **6LoWPAN adaptation layer**. Mention the relevant RFCs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **header compression** mechanism in 6LoWPAN. Show with an example how the IPv6 + UDP header (40 + 8 = 48 bytes) can be compressed to **8 bytes** using IPHC and NHC. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer A(a) — Protocol Stack & Adaptation Layer (7 marks)

```
+-----------------------------+
|   Application (CoAP, MQTT)  |
+-----------------------------+
|      Transport (UDP/TCP)    |
+-----------------------------+
|  Network: IPv6, ICMPv6, NDP |
+-----------------------------+
|  6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer   |  <--  RFC 4944, RFC 6282, RFC 6775
+-----------------------------+
|   IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Layer   |
+-----------------------------+
|    IEEE 802.15.4 PHY        |
+-----------------------------+
```

**Role of the 6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer (5 points, 1 mark each logical unit):**

1. **Header Compression (RFC 6282):** Encodes the 40-byte IPv6 header in 2–4 bytes using IPHC. Common link-local context fields are elided because they are derived from the IEEE 802.15.4 frame's MAC addresses.

2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly (RFC 4944 §5.3):** Splits IPv6 datagrams larger than the 802.15.4 payload (81–102 bytes) into multiple link-layer frames using a 4-byte fragmentation header. The receiver reassembles them using a 16-bit datagram tag and 8-bit offset.

3. **Mesh Addressing (RFC 4944 §5.2):** Provides 16-bit short-address-based hop-by-hop delivery inside the LoWPAN, so the IP layer sees only a single hop (mesh-under) or full multi-hop (route-over with RPL).

4. **Stateless Address Autoconfiguration:** A node's IPv6 interface identifier is constructed by inserting `FFFE` in the middle of the 64-bit EUI-64 (derived from 802.15.4 MAC), with the U/L bit toggled.

5. **Optimized Neighbor Discovery (RFC 6775):** Replaces multicast RS/RA with lighter 6LoWPAN-ND messages (DAR/DAC, NSARO) and uses a 6LBR as the central registrar. Reduces control-plane overhead for sleepy/battery nodes.

**RFCs (1 mark):**
- **RFC 4944** — Original 6LoWPAN specification.
- **RFC 6282** — IPHC header compression.
- **RFC 6775** — Neighbor Discovery Optimization for 6LoWPAN.
- **RFC 8138** — 6LoWPAN-GHC (Generic Header Compression, updated IPHC).

---

#### Model Answer A(b) — Header Compression Worked Example (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Original Headers (2 marks)**

| Header | Size | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| IPv6 fixed | **40 bytes** | Version, TC, FL, Payload Length, Next Header, Hop Limit, Src (16B), Dst (16B) |
| UDP | **8 bytes** | Src port, Dst port, Length, Checksum |
| **Total** | **48 bytes** | |

**Step 2 — IPHC Header (3 marks)**

The IPHC dispatch (`011` prefix) is **2 bytes**, encoding:
- **TF (Traffic Class / Flow Label):** 11 → both elided
- **NH (Next Header):** 1 → compressed using NHC
- **HLIM (Hop Limit):** 10 → hop limit = 64 (common value elided)
- **CID:** 0 → no additional context
- **SAC:** 0, **SAM:** 11 → source address fully elided (link-local, derived from MAC)
- **M:** 0, **DAC:** 0, **DAM:** 11 → destination address fully elided

Compressed IPHC = **2 bytes**

**Step 3 — NHC-UDP Header (2 marks)**

The NHC byte (`11110` prefix + next-header ID) is **1 byte**, plus a compressed checksum **1 byte** (or elided if integrity is provided by link layer).

Compressed NHC-UDP = **2 bytes**

**Step 4 — Total Compressed Header (1 mark)**

$$
H_{comp} = H_{IPHC} + H_{NHC} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(or 8 with inline fields)}
$$

> If inline context IDs / partial addresses are needed, the compressed header grows to ~8 bytes — still an **~83% reduction** from the original 48 bytes. This efficiency is what makes IPv6 viable on 8-bit MCUs with kilobytes of RAM.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **fragmentation and reassembly** mechanism in 6LoWPAN. Derive the number of fragments required to transmit an **IPv6 datagram of 1280 bytes** over an IEEE 802.15.4 link with **AES-CCM-128 security enabled**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Mesh-Under** and **Route-Over** routing in 6LoWPAN. With a diagram, show how a packet travels from a sensor node to the 6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR) in both approaches. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer B(a) — Fragmentation Worked Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Explain Fragmentation (3 marks)**

6LoWPAN fragmentation (RFC 4944 §5.3) is required because:
- IEEE 802.15.4 frames = **127 bytes** max.
- IPv6 minimum MTU = **1280 bytes**.

A **first-fragment header** (4 bytes) carries: dispatch (`11000XXX`), 16-bit **datagram tag**, and 8-bit **datagram size**.

A **subsequent-fragment header** (4 bytes) carries: dispatch (`11100XXX`), 16-bit datagram tag, and 8-byte **fragment offset**.

The receiver buffers fragments, sorts by offset, and reassembles when the total matches the original size.

**Step 2 — Compute MAC Payload with Security (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= 127 - 25 \text{ (MAC)} - 21 \text{ (AES)} - 2 \text{ (FCS)} \\
P_{max} &= 79 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-Fragment Payload (1 mark)**

$$
P_{payload} = 79 - 4 \text{ (frag header)} = 75 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Fragments (1 mark)**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{1280}{75} \right\rceil = \lceil 17.07 \rceil = 18 \text{ fragments}
$$

**Result:** **18 fragments** are required; total on-air bytes = $18 \times 127 = 2286$ bytes → overhead ≈ **78.6%**.

---

#### Model Answer B(b) — Mesh-Under vs Route-Over (7 marks)

| Aspect | Mesh-Under | Route-Over |
|--------|------------|------------|
| Where routing happens | **Inside LoWPAN** (link layer) | **At IP layer** |
| IP topology seen | **One hop** to 6LBR | **Multi-hop** visible to IP |
| Address used | **16-bit short** address | **128-bit IPv6** |
| Routing protocol | Custom mesh (e.g., LOAD) | **RPL** (RFC 6550) |
| Used by | Zigbee IP, Thread | 6LoWPAN-IP, Smart Object networks |
| Efficiency | Less overhead, no IP routing table | Standard IPv6 tools, more overhead |
| KTU tip | LoWPAN looks like "one IP hop" | Each hop is a real IPv6 router |

**Diagram (in words, 2 marks):**

```
[Sensor] --(16-bit addr)--> [Hop 1] --(16-bit addr)--> [6LBR] --(IPv6)--> [Internet]
   (Mesh-Under: One IP hop, three link hops)

[Sensor] --(IPv6)--> [R1 IPv6 Router] --(IPv6)--> [R2] --(IPv6)--> [6LBR] --(IPv6)--> [Internet]
   (Route-Over: Four IP hops)
```

In **Mesh-Under**, the IP stack sees a single hop; in **Route-Over**, every intermediate node is a full IPv6 router running RPL.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in 6LoWPAN questions — and how to avoid losing marks:**
> 1. **Confusing 6LoWPAN with a new network protocol** — 6LoWPAN is *only* an adaptation layer. IPv6 still runs end-to-end. Writing "6LoWPAN replaces IPv6" costs 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Wrong frame-size numbers** — IEEE 802.15.4 frame is **127 bytes**, not 1024 or 256. The MAC payload (not the frame) is what determines fragmentation.
> 3. **Forgetting the 4-byte fragmentation overhead per fragment** — students often divide 1280 by 127 or 81, missing the header cost.
> 4. **Confusing IPHC with LOWPAN_HC1** — HC1 was the original compression (RFC 4944); IPHC (RFC 6282) is the current standard. Using obsolete terminology loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up Mesh-Under and Route-Over** — remember: Mesh-Under = routing at link layer, IP sees 1 hop. Route-Over = routing at IP, every hop is an IP router.
> 6. **Missing the role of the 6LBR (Border Router)** — it is the **gateway** between the IPv6 world and the LoWPAN, and also the central ND authority. Always mention it in 6LoWPAN-ND answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Full form:** IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks.
- **Defined by:** RFC 4944, RFC 6282, RFC 6775, RFC 8138.
- **Layer:** Adaptation layer **between** IPv6 and IEEE 802.15.4 MAC.
- **Primary purpose:** Enable IPv6 over tiny 127-byte 802.15.4 frames.
- **Key Features (the 4 pillars):**
  - **Header Compression (IPHC)** — 40-byte IPv6 header → 2–4 bytes; ~83% reduction.
  - **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — 4-byte frag header, datagram tag, offset.
  - **Mesh Addressing** — Mesh-Under (link-layer routing) vs. Route-Over (RPL).
  - **Stateless Autoconfig + 6LoWPAN-ND** — RFC 6775, 6LBR, ARO registration.
- **Standards compliance:** End-to-end IPv6, interoperable with the global Internet.
- **Border Router (6LBR):** Gateway + ND authority for the LoWPAN.
- **Typical applications:** Smart meters, home automation (Thread/Zigbee IP), industrial WSN, smart cities.
- **Power advantage:** Compressed headers + mesh routing → years of battery life.
- **Dispatch byte:** Identifies the 6LoWPAN encoding (NALP, IPHC, fragment, mesh, broadcast).
- **Trade-off:** Fragmentation increases on-air bytes by ~78% — but **compression first** keeps the datagram small enough to avoid fragmenting most packets.
- **Security:** Inherited from IEEE 802.15.4 (AES-CCM-128) and optionally IPsec above.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocol Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protocol Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Protocol Stack (KTU Definition):** The *IoT Protocol Stack* is a **layered architectural framework** that defines the complete set of communication protocols, standards, and data formats used to enable seamless connectivity, data exchange, and interoperability between heterogeneous physical devices (sensors/actuators), edge gateways, cloud platforms, and end-user applications across the Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem.

The IoT Protocol Stack is **not a single protocol** but a **structured hierarchy of protocols** organized into functional layers — analogous to the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)** or **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** model — but customized to address the unique constraints of IoT: **low power, low bandwidth, lossy networks, constrained devices, and intermittent connectivity.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the **four-layer IoT protocol stack** (Perception/Physical → Network → Transport → Application) along with the protocols operating at each layer and their comparative analysis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Mail System"

Think of the IoT Protocol Stack as a **highly specialized international postal system** — but instead of letters between humans, it routes tiny data packets between smart devices.

| Postal System Element | IoT Protocol Stack Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Postal Address Format (PIN/ZIP)** | **Addressing & Naming Conventions** (IPv6, URI, MAC addresses) |
| **Sorting Office** | **Network Layer** (6LoWPAN, RPL, IPv6) |
| **Delivery Truck / Logistics** | **Transport Layer** (TCP, UDP, CoAP) |
| **Envelope Format & Language** | **Application Layer Protocols** (MQTT, HTTP, CoAP, AMQP) |
| **Postman (Sensor) picking up the letter** | **Perception/Sensing Layer** (Sensors, RFID, NFC, Actuators) |
| **Truck breaking down on a bad road** | **Packet Loss in Lossy IoT Networks** |
| **Express delivery (urgent)** | **QoS-aware routing** |

Just as a postal system needs rules at *every stage* (from pickup to delivery to sorting), the IoT Protocol Stack needs distinct protocols at *each layer* to ensure the data travels reliably from a tiny temperature sensor in a Kerala paddy field to a cloud server in Bengaluru.

---

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Key Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Metrics Governing IoT Protocol Stack Design:**
> - **Bandwidth:** Typically **10 kbps – 1 Mbps** (vs. >100 Mbps in traditional internet)
> - **Power Budget:** Devices must operate on **< 1 mW average** (battery life: 1–10 years)
> - **Packet Size:** Constrained to **~127 bytes** (vs. 1500 bytes for Ethernet)
> - **Latency Tolerance:** Varies from **<10 ms** (industrial control) to **seconds** (monitoring)
> - **Duty Cycle:** Often limited to **<1%** (e.g., 802.15.4 radio regulations in sub-GHz ISM bands)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** A traditional TCP/IP stack (designed for desktops with abundant power and bandwidth) would drain a coin-cell IoT sensor in **hours**, not years. Hence, the IoT Protocol Stack uses **compressed, lightweight, optimized protocols** at every layer.

---

## 1.4 Why a Separate "Stack" for IoT?

The traditional internet protocol stack (TCP/IP) was designed in the 1970s for **large, mains-powered computers**. IoT introduces **three fundamental shifts** that demand a new stack:

1. **Device Constraints:** IoT devices have CPU/memory measured in **MHz/MB**, not GHz/GB.
2. **Network Constraints:** Many IoT networks are wireless, lossy, and low-bandwidth (e.g., **LoRaWAN, Sigfox, Zigbee, BLE**).
3. **Scale:** IoT deployments scale to **billions** of devices, exceeding IPv4 address space — hence the shift to **IPv6** and **6LoWPAN** adaptation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"Why can't we use the standard TCP/IP stack directly in IoT?"* The three reasons above (constrained devices, lossy networks, massive scale) are the expected answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Layer IoT Protocol Stack — Architecture

The IoT Protocol Stack is conventionally divided into **four functional layers** (some models use five, splitting Application into Application + Service/Management). The KTU 2024 syllabus follows the **four-layer model**.

---

### Layer 1: Perception / Physical / Sensing Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *Perception Layer* is the physical foundation of the IoT stack. It is responsible for **identifying, sensing, and collecting information** from the physical world, and optionally actuating changes in response.

**Key Components & Protocols/Technologies:**

| Sub-Component | Function | Examples / Standards |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Convert physical phenomena → electrical signals | Temperature (DHT22), Humidity, Accelerometer (MPU6050) |
| **Actuators** | Convert electrical signals → physical action | Relays, Motors, Solenoids, LED Drivers |
| **RFID Tags** | Identity & tracking | Passive (EPC Class-1 Gen-2), Active |
| **NFC** | Short-range (<10 cm) communication | ISO/IEC 14443, ISO/IEC 15693 |
| **WSN Protocols** | Wireless sensor networking | IEEE 802.15.4 (basis for Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, Thread) |
| **Barcode/QR** | Optical identification | UPC, Code-128, QR Code |
| **Edge / Embedded Hardware** | Local computation | Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico, STM32 |

**Physical Layer Characteristics:**
- **Frequency Bands:** 2.4 GHz (global ISM), 868 MHz (Europe), 915 MHz (US), 433 MHz (Asia)
- **Modulation:** O-QPSK, BPSK, GFSK, CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum for LoRa)
- **Data Rate:** 20 kbps (Zigbee) to 250 kbps (Wi-Fi) to **50 kbps (LoRaWAN)** and **100 bps (Sigfox)**

---

### Layer 2: Network / Communication Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *Network Layer* handles **packet forwarding, addressing, and routing** of data from perception-layer devices to gateways, edge routers, or the wider internet.

**Key Protocols & Their Roles:**

| Protocol | Full Form | Primary Use Case | Layer Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| **6LoWPAN** | IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks | Encapsulating IPv6 packets over IEEE 802.15.4 | Adaptation |
| **RPL** | Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (LLNs) | Multi-hop routing in constrained networks | Routing |
| **IPv6** | Internet Protocol version 6 | Addressing (128-bit address space) | Network |
| **Zigbee** | Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4-based mesh) | Home automation, industrial control | Network + MAC |
| **LoRaWAN** | Long Range Wide Area Network | Long-range, low-power, low-data applications | MAC + Network |
| **Sigfox** | Sigfox (proprietary LPWAN) | Ultra-narrowband, ultra-low-power | MAC + Network |
| **NB-IoT** | Narrowband IoT (3GPP Release 13) | Cellular IoT, licensed spectrum | Network + MAC |
| **Wi-Fi** | Wireless Fidelity (IEEE 802.11) | Local high-throughput IoT (cameras, hubs) | Network + MAC |
| **Bluetooth LE** | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.0+) | Wearables, beacons, proximity | Network + MAC |
| **Thread** | Thread Group protocol | Mesh networking for smart home | Network + MAC |
| **Z-Wave** | Z-Wave (ITU-T G.9959) | Smart home mesh | Network + MAC |

**RPL (Routing Protocol for LLNs) — Key Concept:**
- Builds a **Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph (DODAG)** rooted at a gateway/border router.
- Uses **Objective Functions (OFs)** like OF0 and MRHOF to compute the best path.
- Trickle algorithm controls control message overhead — critical for energy efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** *6LoWPAN* is a frequent exam topic. It performs **header compression** and **fragmentation** of IPv6 packets (originally 40-byte header) to fit into IEEE 802.15.4 frames (max 127 bytes). Through compression, the effective IPv6 header can be reduced to a few bytes.

---

### Layer 3: Transport Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *Transport Layer* provides **end-to-end message delivery**, flow control, and reliability semantics between communicating endpoints.

**Key Protocols:**

| Protocol | Nature | Header Size | Use Case | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP** | Connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream | 20–60 bytes | Non-constrained devices (gateways, cloud) | Reference for comparison |
| **UDP** | Connectionless, best-effort, datagram | 8 bytes | Lightweight, multicast, real-time | Base for CoAP, DDS |
| **DTLS** | Datagram Transport Layer Security | ~13–25 bytes (compressed) | Security over UDP (CoAPs) | TLS for constrained IoT |
| **SCTP** | Stream Control Transmission Protocol | 12 bytes | Multi-homing, telecom signaling | Less common in IoT |

**Why UDP dominates IoT at the edge:**
- **8-byte header** vs. TCP's **20-byte minimum** → critical for constrained devices.
- **No handshake overhead** → suitable for sleepy/sleeping devices.
- **Multicast/broadcast support** → efficient for firmware updates to many devices.

---

### Layer 4: Application Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *Application Layer* defines the **message formats, semantics, and data models** that IoT applications use to communicate with devices and with each other.

**Key Protocols:**

| Protocol | Full Form | Transport | Pattern | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MQTT** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport | TCP (port 1883/8883) | Publish/Subscribe | **Most important** |
| **CoAP** | Constrained Application Protocol | UDP (port 5683) | Request/Response (REST-like) | **Most important** |
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | HyperText Transfer Protocol | TCP (port 80/443) | Request/Response | Reference baseline |
| **AMQP** | Advanced Message Queuing Protocol | TCP (port 5672/5671) | Publish/Subscribe + Queues | Enterprise/messaging |
| **DDS** | Data Distribution Service | UDP/TCP | Publish/Subscribe (real-time) | Industrial IoT |
| **XMPP** | Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol | TCP | Publish/Subscribe + Presence | Legacy / presence-based |
| **LwM2M** | Lightweight M2M | CoAP/UDP/DTLS | Device management | IoT device management |
| **WebSocket** | WebSocket | TCP | Full-duplex persistent | Browser-based IoT |
| **SSDP/UPnP** | Simple Service Discovery Protocol | UDP | Discovery | Local network discovery |
| **DNS-SD/mDNS** | Multicast DNS | UDP | Service discovery | Local discovery |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *MQTT* and *CoAP* interchangeability. Remember: **MQTT uses TCP (reliable, heavier), CoAP uses UDP (lightweight, unreliable by default, with optional confirmable mode).**

---

## 2.2 Cross-Layer Functionality — Security, Identity & Management

Beyond the four layers, the IoT stack includes **cross-cutting concerns**:

| Concern | Examples | Where Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | X.509 certificates, EUI-64, EPC | Perception + Network + App |
| **Authentication & Authorization** | OAuth 2.0, DTLS handshake, EAP | Transport + App |
| **Encryption** | AES-128/256, ECC, TLS/DTLS | Transport + App |
| **Device Management** | LwM2M, OMA-DM, TR-069 | Application |
| **Firmware Update (OTA)** | SUIT, FOTA, A/B partitions | App + Network |
| **Data Serialization** | CBOR, Protocol Buffers, MessagePack, JSON | Application |
| **Semantic Interoperability** | WoT (Web of Things), oneM2M, FIWARE NGSI | Application |
| **Time Synchronization** | NTP, PTP, IEEE 1588 | Cross-layer |
| **Network Management** | SNMP, NETCONF/YANG | Application |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The IoT Protocol Stack has very few numerical formulas (it is primarily architectural). However, the following are the key quantitative relationships that appear in KTU numerical or short-answer questions:

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IPv6 Address Space | $2^{128}$ | addresses | Total possible IPv6 addresses |
| 2 | IPv4 Address Space | $2^{32} = 4.29 \times 10^9$ | addresses | Why IPv4 is exhausted |
| 3 | 6LoWPAN MTU | $127 \text{ bytes max frame}$ | bytes | IEEE 802.15.4 limit |
| 4 | IPv6 Header Size | $40 \text{ bytes (uncompressed)}$ | bytes | Reduced to ~2–3 bytes via 6LoWPAN IPHC |
| 5 | UDP Header Size | $8 \text{ bytes}$ | bytes | 4× smaller than TCP |
| 6 | TCP Header Size | $20 \text{ bytes minimum}$ | bytes | Up to 60 bytes with options |
| 7 | Effective CoAP Message | $4 \text{ bytes header} + \text{payload}$ | bytes | vs. HTTP which adds many KBs of overhead |
| 8 | LoRaWAN Data Rate | $DR \in [0, 6]$ | tier | Higher DR = higher bitrate, lower range |
| 9 | LoRaWAN SF Range | $SF \in [7, 12]$ | dimensionless | Spreading factor: trade-off bitrate vs. sensitivity |
| 10 | LoRa Bitrate | $R_b = \dfrac{SF \times BW}{2^{SF} \times \text{symbol rate factor}}$ | bps | Approximate; symbol rate $\approx BW / 2^{SF}$ |
| 11 | Link Budget | $LB = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - P_{RX_{min}}$ | dB | Determines maximum range |
| 12 | Battery Life (estimate) | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}$ | hours | $I_{avg}$ dominated by sleep current |
| 13 | Duty Cycle | $DC = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | % | Often regulated to $\leq 1\%$ in EU sub-GHz |
| 14 | Packet Delivery Ratio | $PDR = \dfrac{P_{received}}{P_{sent}}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Quality metric for LLNs |
| 15 | Energy per Bit | $E_b = \dfrac{V \times I \times t}{N_{bits}}$ | Joules/bit | Energy efficiency metric |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IoT Protocol Stack is the **backbone of every modern smart system**:

- **Smart Agriculture (Kerala context):** DHT22 sensors → **Zigbee/6LoWPAN** mesh → **MQTT** broker → AWS IoT → Mobile dashboard.
- **Smart Healthcare:** BLE wearable → **CoAP over DTLS** → Hospital gateway → HL7 FHIR server.
- **Industrial IoT (Industry 4.0):** **OPC UA over TCP** + **DDS over UDP** for real-time machine control.
- **Smart Cities:** **LoRaWAN** (street lighting, parking) + **NB-IoT** (water meters) + **MQTT** (central aggregation).
- **Smart Home:** **Zigbee + Thread + Matter** for interoperability (Matter is the new KTU-relevant convergence layer).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** When asked *"Explain the role of MQTT in IoT"*, structure your answer around the **publish/subscribe model**, the role of the **broker**, the three QoS levels, and why MQTT (over TCP) is preferred for reliable cloud telemetry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MQTT vs. CoAP vs. HTTP vs. AMQP vs. DDS

This is the **most important analytical table for KTU exams**. Examiners frequently ask for a comparative study.

| Parameter | **MQTT** | **CoAP** | **HTTP** | **AMQP** | **DDS** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport | Constrained Application Protocol | HyperText Transfer Protocol | Advanced Message Queuing Protocol | Data Distribution Service |
| **Underlying Transport** | TCP (port 1883/8883) | UDP (port 5683/5684) | TCP (port 80/443) | TCP (port 5672/5671) | UDP or TCP (multicast) |
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (Client-Broker) | Request/Response (Client-Server, REST-like) | Request/Response (Client-Server) | Publish/Subscribe + Queues (Broker) | Publish/Subscribe (no broker, peer-to-peer) |
| **Message Pattern** | Topic-based Pub/Sub | Resource-based Req/Rep | URI-based Req/Rep | Topic/Queue-based | Topic-based with QoS |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes (fixed) | 4 bytes (fixed) | Variable (often >200 bytes) | 8 bytes (frame header) | Variable |
| **Typical Message Overhead** | Very low | Very low | High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Reliability** | 3 QoS levels (0,1,2) | Confirmable (CON) / Non-confirmable (NON) | Built on TCP (reliable) | Transactional ACKs | Multiple QoS levels |
| **Security** | TLS (MQTTS), username/password | DTLS (CoAPs) | TLS (HTTPS) | TLS (AMQPS) | DTLS/TLS |
| **Discovery** | Manual (broker) | Resource directory (RFC 7252) | DNS, well-known ports | None native | Built-in discovery |
| **Standards Body** | OASIS | IETF (RFC 7252) | IETF (RFC 9110+) | OASIS | OMG |
| **Best Suited For** | Cloud telemetry, mobile, reliable push | Constrained nodes, REST integration | Web apps, browser APIs | Enterprise messaging, financial | Real-time industrial control |
| **Power Efficiency** | Medium (TCP keep-alive) | High (UDP, sleepy) | Low (heavy) | Medium | High (UDP option) |
| **Latency** | Medium | Low | Medium–High | Medium | Very Low (real-time) |
| **Scalability** | High (millions via broker) | High (stateless) | Medium (connection-per-client) | High | Very High (peer-to-peer) |
| **Example Use** | AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub | Smart home sensors, Thread | Web dashboards, REST APIs | Banking messaging, RabbitMQ | Robotics, autonomous vehicles |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often write *"MQTT is better than CoAP"* or vice-versa. The correct answer is **context-dependent**. MQTT is preferred for **reliable cloud telemetry over stable TCP links**; CoAP is preferred for **constrained devices on lossy, low-power networks** where UDP is mandatory.

---

## 3.2 MQTT — Deep Architectural Analysis (Step-by-Step)

### 3.2.1 MQTT Architecture Components

\begin{aligned}
\text{MQTT System} &= \{\text{Broker}\} \cup \{\text{Client}_1, \text{Client}_2, \ldots, \text{Client}_n\} \\
\text{Topic Tree} &= \text{Hierarchical string-based namespace} \\
\text{Topic Example} &= \text{"kerala/ktu/iot/sensor/temperature"}\ \ (\text{wildcards: } + \ \text{and } \#)
\end{aligned}

**Three Principal Roles:**

1. **Publisher:** Generates data → sends to broker with a topic label.
2. **Subscriber:** Expresses interest in topics → receives matching messages.
3. **Broker:** Central server that filters and dispatches messages (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX).

### 3.2.2 MQTT Control Packet Format

A fixed header of just **2 bytes minimum**:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed Header} &= 1 \text{ byte (Control Packet Type + Flags)} + 1\text{–}4 \text{ bytes (Remaining Length)} \\
\text{Control Packet Types} &= \{ \text{CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, PINGREQ, DISCONNECT, etc.} \}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Total of 15 control packet types** in MQTT v5.0. The compact binary header is why MQTT is so lightweight.

### 3.2.3 The Three Quality of Service (QoS) Levels

| QoS | Name | Guarantee | Acknowledgment Flow | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Best-effort (fire-and-forget) | PUBLISH → (nothing) | Sensor telemetry, loss-tolerant |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Guaranteed delivery, possible duplicates | PUBLISH → PUBACK (with DUP flag for retries) | General telemetry |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Guaranteed, no duplicates | PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP (4-way handshake) | Billing, critical control commands |

**QoS 2 Four-Way Handshake (exhaustive):**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH (Packet ID = P)}} \text{Broker} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBREC (Packet ID = P)}} \text{Publisher} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBREL (Packet ID = P)}} \text{Broker} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBCOMP (Packet ID = P)}} \text{Publisher}
\end{aligned}

---

## 3.3 CoAP — Deep Architectural Analysis (Step-by-Step)

### 3.3.1 CoAP Message Format

A CoAP message is **4 bytes fixed header + options + payload**:

\begin{aligned}
\text{CoAP Header (4 bytes)} &= \underbrace{\text{Ver (2b) + Type (2b) + TKL (4b)}}_{\text{Byte 1}} + \underbrace{\text{Code (8b)}}_{\text{Byte 2}} + \underbrace{\text{Message ID (16b)}}_{\text{Bytes 3-4}} \\
\text{Type field} &\in \{ \text{CON, NON, ACK, RST} \}
\end{aligned}

**Message Type Semantics:**

- **CON (Confirmable):** Requires ACK. Retransmission up to MAX_RETRANSMIT (default 4).
- **NON (Non-confirmable):** No ACK, no retransmission. Fire-and-forget.
- **ACK (Acknowledgement):** Confirms a CON.
- **RST (Reset):** Indicates context loss / abort.

### 3.3.2 CoAP Methods (Verbs)

CoAP mirrors HTTP verbs but uses compact codes:

\begin{aligned}
\text{GET} &= 0x01, \quad \text{POST} = 0x02, \quad \text{PUT} = 0x03, \quad \text{DELETE} = 0x04 \\
\text{Content-Format Option} &= \text{application/senml+json}, \text{application/cbor}, \text{application/xml}, \ldots
\end{aligned}

### 3.3.3 CoAP vs. HTTP Request Mapping

| HTTP | CoAP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| `GET /sensor/temp` | `CON GET coap://[addr]/sensor/temp` |
| `POST /actuator {"on":true}` | `CON POST coap://[addr]/actuator Payload={"on":true}` |
| Response Code 200 OK | CoAP Code 2.04 Changed |
| Response Code 404 | CoAP Code 4.04 Not Found |

---

## 3.4 6LoWPAN — Header Compression Derivation (Step-by-Step)

The KTU board often asks: *"How does 6LoWPAN enable IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4?"*

**Problem:**
- IEEE 802.15.4 maximum frame payload = **81 bytes** (after MAC header).
- Standard IPv6 header = **40 bytes** + UDP header = 8 bytes → already 48 bytes consumed, leaving only 33 bytes for actual application data.

**Solution (6LoWPAN IPHC header compression):**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compress IPv6 header} \rightarrow 2 \text{ bytes (IPHC dispatch) } + \text{inline fields} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compress UDP header} \rightarrow 4 \text{ bytes (NHC dispatch) } + \text{inline fields} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Inline fields used only when addresses/ports differ from link-local assumptions} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Compressed overhead} \approx 6 \text{–} 7 \text{ bytes vs. original 48 bytes} \\
\text{Gain:} \quad & \text{Savings} = 48 - 7 = 41 \text{ bytes} \rightarrow \text{available payload increases from 33 to } \approx 74 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** 6LoWPAN compression recovers nearly the **entire IPv6+UDP header**, making IPv6 practical on 802.15.4.

---

## 3.5 RPL — DODAG Construction (Analytical Walkthrough)

**RPL builds a tree-like routing graph rooted at the border router (sink).**

\begin{aligned}
\text{DODAG Root} &= \text{Border Router} \\
\text{Objective Function (OF)} &\in \{ \text{OF0 (hop count)}, \text{MRHOF (ETX-based)} \} \\
\text{Rank} &= \text{Position metric of a node relative to root} \\
\text{Path Selection} &: \text{Node chooses parent minimizing rank + link cost}
\end{aligned}

**Control Messages:**
- **DIO (DODAG Information Object):** Downward-propagated; announces rank and config.
- **DIS (DODAG Information Solicitation):** Request DIOs.
- **DAO (Destination Advertisement Object):** Upward-propagated; populates downward routes.

---

## 3.6 IoT Communication Models (Architectural Derivation)

| Model | Flow | Example Protocol | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Device-to-Device (D2D)** | Direct, no intermediary | BLE, Zigbee, NFC | Low latency |
| **Device-to-Cloud (D2C)** | Device → cloud directly | MQTT, HTTPS, CoAP | Simplicity |
| **Device-to-Gateway (D2G)** | Device → gateway → cloud | Zigbee gateway + MQTT | Protocol translation |
| **Cloud-to-Cloud (C2C)** | Cloud ↔ cloud federation | REST APIs, WebHooks | Interoperability |
| **Back-End Data Sharing** | Cloud → application via API | REST, GraphQL, gRPC | Application integration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 mapping:** The protocol stack supports *all five* communication models. The choice of protocol is dictated by which model the system implements.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — MQTT vs. CoAP Energy Estimation

> **Scenario:** A battery-powered temperature sensor sends 1 packet of 50 bytes every 10 minutes. Compare approximate energy consumption when using MQTT-over-TCP vs. CoAP-over-UDP over a LoRaWAN-like link.
> **Assumptions:**
> - TX current: $I_{TX} = 120\ \text{mA}$ @ $V = 3.3\ \text{V}$
> - RX current: $I_{RX} = 10\ \text{mA}$
> - Sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 5\ \mu\text{A}$
> - Air time: $T_{air} = 2\ \text{s}$ per transmission
> - MQTT/TCP adds 4–6 extra round trips for keep-alive/handshakes per session; CoAP uses 1 CON/ACK exchange

**MQTT path (over TCP via gateway):**

\begin{aligned}
T_{on}^{MQTT} &= T_{air} + 6 \times T_{round-trip} \approx 2 + 6 \times 0.5 = 5\ \text{s} \\
Q_{MQTT} &= V \times I_{TX} \times T_{air} + V \times I_{RX} \times (T_{on}^{MQTT} - T_{air}) \\
&= 3.3 \times 0.120 \times 2 + 3.3 \times 0.010 \times 3 \\
&= 0.792 + 0.099 = 0.891\ \text{J per packet}
\end{aligned}

**CoAP path (over UDP, confirmable):**

\begin{aligned}
T_{on}^{CoAP} &= T_{air} + 1 \times T_{ACK} \approx 2 + 0.1 = 2.1\ \text{s} \\
Q_{CoAP} &= V \times I_{TX} \times T_{air} + V \times I_{RX} \times (T_{on}^{CoAP} - T_{air}) \\
&= 3.3 \times 0.120 \times 2 + 3.3 \times 0.010 \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.792 + 0.0033 = 0.7953\ \text{J per packet}
\end{aligned}

**Energy savings:**

\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q &= Q_{MQTT} - Q_{CoAP} = 0.891 - 0.7953 = 0.0957\ \text{J per packet} \\
\text{Savings} &= \dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q_{MQTT}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.0957}{0.891} \times 100\% \approx 10.7\%
\end{aligned}

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** CoAP saves energy primarily by avoiding TCP's keep-alive overhead. In real deployments with TLS/DTLS added, the gap widens because **DTLS handshake is one round-trip** while **TLS handshake is 2–4 round-trips**.

---

## 3.8 LPWAN Numerical Comparison (LoRaWAN vs. NB-IoT vs. Sigfox)

| Parameter | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | Sigfox |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spectrum** | Unlicensed ISM (sub-GHz) | Licensed LTE (in-band, guard-band, standalone) | Unlicensed ISM (sub-GHz) |
| **Bandwidth** | 125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz | 200 kHz (1 PRB of LTE) | 100 Hz (UNB) |
| **Modulation** | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | QPSK (LTE-based) | UNB BPSK |
| **Max Data Rate** | ~50 kbps | ~200 kbps (DL), ~20–60 kbps (UL) | ~100 bps (UL), 600 bps (max) |
| **Range** | 2–15 km (rural/urban) | 1–10 km (with LTE infra) | 3–50 km (very long range) |
| **Battery Life** | 10+ years | 10+ years | 10+ years |
| **Mobility** | Poor (no handover) | Excellent (LTE handover) | Limited |
| **Deployment Cost** | Low (deploy own gateway) | Medium (cellular operator) | Medium (Sigfox operator) |
| **Daily Message Limit** | None (Duty Cycle 1%) | None strict (network dependent) | 140 UL / 4 DL per day |
| **KTU Importance** | Very High | High | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
