# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
**"List the four layers of the IoT protocol stack and state two key protocols used at each layer."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four layers of the IoT protocol stack are:

1. **Perception/Physical Layer:** Sensors, Actuators, RFID, NFC, IEEE 802.15.4 (radio), BLE.
2. **Network/Communication Layer:** 6LoWPAN, RPL, IPv6, Zigbee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT.
3. **Transport Layer:** TCP, UDP, DTLS.
4. **Application Layer:** MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP, DDS, LwM2M.

*(Full marks for listing all four layers with two protocols each.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**"Compare MQTT and CoAP with respect to transport protocol, message pattern, and one application scenario each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (port 1883/8883) | UDP (port 5683) |
| **Message Pattern** | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Response (REST-like) |
| **Application** | Cloud telemetry (e.g., AWS IoT) | Constrained devices on lossy LLNs (e.g., smart-home sensors) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model, June 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of the **IoT protocol stack** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **MQTT protocol** in detail. Discuss its three Quality of Service (QoS) levels with message flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) Model Answer — IoT Protocol Stack Architecture (7 marks)**

The IoT protocol stack is organized as a **four-layer architecture**, with each layer providing specific functions to the layer above while using the services of the layer below.

**(1) Perception/Physical Layer (1.5 marks):**
This is the lowest layer and is responsible for **identifying physical objects and gathering data** from the environment. It includes:
- **Sensors** (temperature, pressure, motion, gas, light)
- **Actuators** (relays, motors, valves)
- **Identification devices** (RFID tags, NFC, QR codes)
- **Communication hardware** (IEEE 802.15.4 radios, BLE modules, LoRa transceivers)

Key standards: **IEEE 802.15.4**, ISO/IEC 14443 (NFC), EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (RFID).

**(2) Network/Communication Layer (1.5 marks):**
Responsible for **routing and transmitting** the collected data over various networks. Key protocols:
- **6LoWPAN** — enables IPv6 over low-power 802.15.4 networks via header compression.
- **RPL** — distance-vector routing protocol for LLNs using DODAG.
- **LoRaWAN, Zigbee, NB-IoT, Thread** — alternative link-layer network protocols.

**(3) Transport Layer (1 mark):**
Provides **end-to-end message delivery** with appropriate reliability:
- **TCP** — connection-oriented, reliable (used in MQTT, HTTP).
- **UDP** — connectionless, lightweight (used in CoAP, DDS).
- **DTLS** — security for UDP-based protocols.

**(4) Application Layer (1.5 marks):**
Defines **data formats, semantics, and application logic**:
- **MQTT** — Pub/Sub for cloud telemetry.
- **CoAP** — RESTful for constrained devices.
- **HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP, DDS, LwM2M** — additional app-layer protocols.

**Cross-Layer Concerns (1.5 marks):**
- Security (TLS/DTLS, AES, ECC), Device Management (LwM2M, OMA-DM), Data Serialization (CBOR, JSON, Protobuf), Semantic Interoperability (WoT, oneM2M).

*(Reference diagram from Section 4.1 should be drawn here for full marks.)*

---

#### **Part (b) Model Answer — MQTT Protocol in Detail (7 marks)**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a **lightweight publish/subscribe messaging protocol** designed by IBM (1999) and standardized by OASIS, now widely used for IoT cloud communication.

**Architecture (2 marks):**
- **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) — central server.
- **Publishers** — IoT devices/edge nodes that publish data to **topics**.
- **Subscribers** — applications/clients that subscribe to topics of interest.
- Topics use a hierarchical string format (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`).
- Wildcards: `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level).

**MQTT Message Format (1 mark):**
- **Fixed Header:** 2 bytes minimum (1-byte control packet type + flags, 1–4 byte remaining length).
- **Variable Header:** Depends on packet type (e.g., Packet ID for QoS > 0).
- **Payload:** Up to 256 MB, but typically small in IoT.

**Three QoS Levels (3 marks):**

| QoS | Guarantee | Handshake | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | PUBLISH only | Fire-and-forget telemetry |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | PUBLISH → PUBACK (with DUP flag for retransmit) | General telemetry |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP | Billing, critical control |

**Why MQTT for IoT (1 mark):**
- Tiny header (2 bytes) → efficient over low bandwidth.
- Asynchronous → decouples publishers and subscribers.
- Three QoS levels → flexible reliability.
- TCP-based → reliable delivery, ideal for stable cloud links.

*(Reference diagram from Section 4.2 should be drawn here for full marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model, Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** in detail. List its salient features. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP, and DDS** protocols across any **five parameters** in a tabular format. Which one is best suited for real-time industrial IoT and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) Model Answer — CoAP Protocol in Detail (7 marks)**

**CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** is an **IETF-standardized (RFC 7252) RESTful application protocol** designed for resource-constrained IoT devices and lossy networks. It is the **UDP counterpart of HTTP** in the IoT world.

**Key Features (1.5 marks):**
- **Lightweight:** 4-byte fixed header vs. HTTP's variable multi-KB headers.
- **RESTful architecture:** Uses GET, POST, PUT, DELETE methods on URI-identified resources.
- **Confirmable (CON) and Non-confirmable (NON) messages:** Built-in reliability with retransmission.
- **Built-in discovery:** Resource discovery via `.well-known/core`.
- **Asynchronous** — supports multicast for group communication.
- **Security:** CoAPs (CoAP over DTLS) using port 5684.
- **Observable resources:** Supports the observe pattern (RFC 7641) for push-based updates.

**CoAP Message Format (1.5 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Header (4 bytes)} &= \text{Ver (2b)} \mid \text{Type (2b)} \mid \text{TKL (4b)} \mid \text{Code (8b)} \mid \text{MessageID (16b)} \\
\text{Type} &\in \{ \text{CON, NON, ACK, RST} \}
\end{aligned}

- **Ver:** Version (currently `01`).
- **TKL:** Token Length (0–8 bytes).
- **Code:** Class.Detail (e.g., `0.01` = GET, `2.04` = Changed/Success, `4.04` = Not Found).
- **MessageID:** Used for matching CON with ACK and deduplication.

**CoAP Transaction Flow (2 marks):**
- **CON GET** → Server responds with **ACK + payload** (retransmitted on timeout, default 4 attempts).
- **NON POST** → No acknowledgment required (used for lossy but efficient updates).
- Supports **piggybacked response** (immediate ACK + response) and **separate response** (delayed).

**CoAP vs. HTTP (1 mark):**
- HTTP is heavy (TCP, large headers, ASCII-based). CoAP is lean (UDP, binary, 4-byte header).
- CoAP supports asynchronous push (observe), multicast — HTTP does not natively.

**CoAP Use Cases (1 mark):**
Smart home sensors, environmental monitoring, smart agriculture, smart lighting.

*(Reference diagram from Section 4.3 should be drawn here for full marks.)*

---

#### **Part (b) Model Answer — Comparative Analysis of IoT Application Protocols (7 marks)**

| Parameter | **MQTT** | **CoAP** | **HTTP** | **AMQP** | **DDS** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP | TCP | TCP | UDP/TCP (RTPS) |
| **Pattern** | Pub/Sub | Req/Rep | Req/Rep | Pub/Sub + Queues | Pub/Sub (P2P, brokerless) |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes | 4 bytes | >200 bytes | 8 bytes | Variable |
| **Reliability** | 3 QoS | CON/ACK | TCP-based | Transactional | Multi-level QoS |
| **Real-time Support** | No (latency) | Limited | No | Limited | **Yes (excellent)** |
| **Best For** | Cloud telemetry | Constrained nodes | Web APIs | Enterprise messaging | **Real-time industrial IoT** |

**Best Protocol for Real-time Industrial IoT (2 marks):**

**DDS (Data Distribution Service)** is the best-suited protocol for **real-time industrial IoT (IIoT)** because:

1. **Brokerless architecture:** Peer-to-peer with no single point of failure — critical for industrial control loops.
2. **Ultra-low latency:** Built on UDP/RTPS for sub-millisecond messaging.
3. **Fine-grained QoS:** 22 QoS policies (DEADLINE, LATENCY_BUDGET, RELIABILITY, DURABILITY) allow precise control.
4. **Real-time guarantees:** Strict timing semantics meet industrial hard-real-time requirements (e.g., IEC 61508 SIL compliance).
5. **Scalability:** Supports thousands of nodes via multicast and discovery.
6. **Standards:** OMG standard; used in autonomous vehicles, robotics, aerospace, smart grid (e.g., Tesla, NASA, ABB robots).

> **Examples:** ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2) uses DDS as its default middleware; autonomous vehicles use DDS for sensor fusion; smart grid uses DDS for substation automation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Protocol Stack Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing MQTT and CoAP layer placement:** Both are *application-layer* protocols. Do NOT classify them as transport-layer protocols.
> 2. **Forgetting the cross-cutting concerns:** Security and management are NOT a fifth layer but cut across all four layers.
> 3. **Confusing 6LoWPAN with 6Lo:** 6LoWPAN specifically refers to IPv6 adaptation; 6Lo is a broader family (e.g., 6LoWPAN, 6Lo-GAN, 6Lo-BLE).
> 4. **Drawing HTTP as IoT-native:** HTTP is fine for cloud APIs but is NOT recommended for constrained devices. Examiners dock marks if students claim "HTTP is best for IoT."
> 5. **Mixing up QoS 1 vs. QoS 2:** QoS 1 can have duplicates; QoS 2 is the *only* level that guarantees exactly-once via 4-way handshake.
> 6. **Missing units in numerical questions:** If asked to compute data rate or duty cycle, ALWAYS include units (bps, %, seconds).
> 7. **Forgetting broker role:** In MQTT questions, the broker is NOT optional. Always show it in the diagram and explain topic filtering.
> 8. **Wrong transport for CoAP:** CoAP runs over UDP by default — never TCP. CoAPs uses DTLS, not TLS.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ IoT Protocol Stack is a **layered framework** for heterogeneous IoT device communication, not a single protocol.
- ✅ **Four layers:** Perception → Network → Transport → Application (+ cross-cutting: Security, Mgmt, Semantics).
- ✅ **Perception Layer:** Sensors, actuators, RFID, NFC, IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, LoRa radios.
- ✅ **Network Layer:** 6LoWPAN (header compression), RPL (DODAG routing), IPv6, Zigbee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Thread.
- ✅ **6LoWPAN** compresses 40-byte IPv6 header to ~2–3 bytes, enabling IPv6 over 802.15.4.
- ✅ **RPL** uses Objective Functions (OF0, MRHOF) and Trickle algorithm for efficient LLN routing.
- ✅ **Transport Layer:** TCP (reliable, heavy), UDP (lightweight, connectionless), DTLS (security for UDP).
- ✅ **MQTT:** TCP-based, **Pub/Sub**, 2-byte header, **3 QoS levels** (0, 1, 2), 4-way handshake for QoS 2.
- ✅ **MQTT Topics:** Hierarchical strings with `+` and `#` wildcards. Broker = Mosquitto/HiveMQ/EMQX.
- ✅ **CoAP:** UDP-based, **RESTful**, 4-byte header, CON/NON/ACK/RST message types, observe pattern.
- ✅ **HTTP:** TCP-based, request/response, NOT ideal for constrained devices (heavy).
- ✅ **AMQP:** TCP-based, Pub/Sub + Queues, enterprise messaging (RabbitMQ).
- ✅ **DDS:** UDP/TCP, brokerless P2P, **best for real-time IIoT**, 22 QoS policies.
- ✅ **LwM2M:** Built on CoAP for IoT device management.
- ✅ **Communication Models:** D2D, D2C, D2G, C2C, Back-End Data Sharing.
- ✅ **Security stack:** TLS (TCP), DTLS (UDP), IPsec (Network), AES/ECC (Application).
- ✅ **LPWAN comparison:** LoRaWAN (unlicensed, long range, low data), NB-IoT (licensed, LTE-based), Sigfox (UNB, ultra-low data).
- ✅ **Key memory number:** IPv6 address space = $2^{128}$; 802.15.4 max payload = 81 bytes; CoAP header = 4 bytes; MQTT header = 2 bytes.
- ✅ **Board-favorite line:** *"MQTT is preferred for cloud telemetry over stable TCP links; CoAP is preferred for constrained devices on lossy UDP networks."*

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Narrowband Internet of Things (NB-IoT)** is a Low Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN) radio technology standardized by the **3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP)** in **Release 13 (June 2016)** as a cellular communication standard designed specifically for Machine-Type Communication (MTC) and massive IoT deployments. It operates within a narrow bandwidth of exactly **180 kHz** (one Physical Resource Block of LTE) and is engineered to support ultra-low complexity, low-throughput, delay-tolerant IoT devices with **battery life expectancy exceeding 10 years**, deep indoor penetration, and massive device density (up to **50,000+ devices per cell**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *NB-IoT is a 3GPP Release 13 standardized LPWAN technology that enables efficient wide-area IoT connectivity by utilizing a narrow 180 kHz carrier bandwidth, deep coverage (MCL of 164 dB), massive device support, and ultra-low power consumption.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whispering Postal Service"

Imagine the cellular network as a busy national postal service. **Regular mobile broadband (4G/5G)** is like sending a courier on a high-speed express motorcycle — fast, powerful, but extremely fuel-hungry and expensive per parcel. **NB-IoT**, on the other hand, is like the **government postal service**: slow, cheap, incredibly energy-efficient, and capable of reaching the most remote villages (deep indoor basements, underground meters) by repeating the message many times if needed. Each letter (data packet) is tiny — just a few bytes — but the service guarantees delivery even in the worst conditions, and the postman (NB-IoT device) can sleep for years between deliveries on a single battery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** NB-IoT is **not** for high-speed video or real-time gaming. It is engineered exclusively for *small, infrequent, delay-tolerant telemetry* — such as smart water meters, gas leak detectors, agricultural soil sensors, and asset trackers.

### 1.3 Standardized Performance Metrics (3GPP Targets)

| Metric | Target Value | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bandwidth** | **180 kHz** | Matches one LTE PRB for spectrum efficiency |
| **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL)** | **164 dB** | 20 dB deeper than standard LTE (144 dB) |
| **Battery Life** | **> 10 years** | On a single 5 Wh battery (2,200 mAh) |
| **Device Density** | **> 50,000 / cell** | Massive Machine-Type Communication (mMTC) |
| **Latency** | **1.6 s to 10 s** | Acceptable for non-real-time telemetry |
| **Peak Data Rate (DL)** | **~ 26 kbps** | Sufficient for firmware updates |
| **Peak Data Rate (UL)** | **~ 62.5 kbps (single-tone)** | Multi-tone up to **~ 250 kbps** |
| **Module Cost** | **< 5 USD** | Approaching the **5G USD module goal** |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Six Pillars" of NB-IoT Features (3GPP Design Goals)

The 3GPP Release 13 specification rigidly defined NB-IoT around **six non-negotiable design pillars**. Understanding each pillar is essential for KTU Module 3 answers.

#### Pillar 1: **Ultra-Low Power Consumption**
- Achieved through two principal power-saving mechanisms:
  - **Power Saving Mode (PSM)**: The device remains *registered* with the network but is unreachable, with current draw dropping to a few microamperes. The device only "wakes up" for a brief uplink (e.g., 1 transmission/day).
  - **Extended Discontinuous Reception (eDRX)**: Extends the paging cycle from LTE's 2.56 s up to **~ 2.91 hours** (10,485 s), enabling the radio to remain powered down for long intervals.
- A device waking once per day can theoretically achieve a battery life of **> 10 years** on a **5 Wh** (2,200 mAh @ 3.6 V) primary cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Budget Equation:** A simple model for battery life is $T_{battery} = \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{nom}}{I_{avg}}$, where $I_{avg}$ is the time-averaged current. For NB-IoT, $I_{avg}$ is dominated by *sleep current* due to the ultra-low duty cycle.

#### Pillar 2: **Extended Coverage (164 dB MCL)**
- Standard LTE supports up to **144 dB MCL**; NB-IoT extends this by **20 dB**, equivalent to a **7× deeper penetration** in link budget terms.
- This is realized by **repeated transmissions** (up to **128 repetitions** in the uplink) and **lower coding rates** (e.g., 1/48 in the lowest data rate configuration).
- Three **Coverage Enhancement (CE) Levels** are defined:
  - **CE Level 0**: MCL < 144 dB (normal coverage, no repetitions)
  - **CE Level 1**: 144 dB ≤ MCL < 154 dB (moderate repetitions)
  - **CE Level 2**: MCL ≥ 154 dB (maximum repetitions, up to 128 UL / 2048 DL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** A water meter installed inside a reinforced concrete basement (typical path loss ~ 150–160 dB) can connect reliably — something ordinary LTE cannot achieve.

#### Pillar 3: **Massive Device Capacity**
- A single NB-IoT cell can support **> 50,000 devices**, with theoretical simulations demonstrating capacity for up to **525,000 devices per cell** under specific traffic assumptions (1 uplink message of 20 bytes per device per 2 hours).
- Capacity is achieved by:
  - **Aggressive repetition** allowing low-SNR decoding, which increases spectral efficiency per device at low data rates.
  - **Single-tone uplink** transmission (3.75 kHz or 15 kHz subcarrier spacing), simplifying the radio frontend and reducing adjacent-channel interference.

#### Pillar 4: **Ultra-Low Device Cost (< 5 USD)**
- Cost reduction techniques:
  - **Single antenna, half-duplex FDD** operation (eliminates the duplexer — a major BOM cost).
  - **Reduced peak data rate** allows use of a low-cost baseband processor (~ 50 MHz ARM Cortex-M class).
  - **Optional reduced receiver bandwidth** (down to 180 kHz) and simplified RF chain.
  - 3GPP Release 15 further introduced a **half-duplex FDD, single-receiver "NB2"** category reducing cost further.

#### Pillar 5: **Optimized Data Rate**
- Downlink peak rate: **~ 26.15 kbps** (Multi-tone) and **~ 16.9 kbps** (Single-tone).
- Uplink peak rate: **~ 62.5 kbps** (Multi-tone) and **~ 15.625 kbps / 3.75 kbps** (Single-tone).
- Modulation: **QPSK** only — deliberately simple to minimize PA cost and power consumption.

#### Pillar 6: **Flexible Spectrum Deployment (Three Modes)**
1. **In-band operation**: NB-IoT carrier placed inside an LTE carrier's resource blocks.
2. **Guard-band operation**: NB-IoT placed in the guard band of an LTE carrier (unused spectrum).
3. **Standalone operation**: NB-IoT uses dedicated spectrum (e.g., refarmed GSM channels).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Link Budget Equation** | $L_{budget} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX} - M_{fade} - I_{margin}$ | dB | Coverage dimensioning |
| **Maximum Coupling Loss** | $MCL_{NB\text{-}IoT} = 164\ dB$ | dB | 3GPP Release 13 target |
| **MCL Gain over LTE** | $\Delta MCL = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(10) = 20\ dB$ | dB | Equivalent penetration gain |
| **Spectral Efficiency** | $\eta = \frac{R_{b}}{B_{w}}$ | bps/Hz | Per-user efficiency check |
| **Battery Life (Ideal Model)** | $T_{b} = \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{nom}}{I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) + I_{active} \cdot D}$ | Years | $D$ = duty cycle |
| **Maximum Repetitions (UL)** | $N_{rep,UL} \le 128$ | Repetitions | Coverage Level 2 ceiling |
| **Maximum Repetitions (DL)** | $N_{rep,DL} \le 2048$ | Repetitions | NPDSCH max for CE 2 |
| **Single-tone Bandwidth (UL)** | $B_{st} = 3.75\ kHz \ \text{or} \ 15\ kHz$ | kHz | Subcarrier spacing |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | $B_{ch} = 180\ kHz$ | kHz | One LTE PRB |
| **Extended DRX Cycle** | $T_{eDRX} \le 10{,}485.76\ s$ (2.91 h) | Seconds | Power-saving upper bound |
| **PSM Active Timer Range** | $T_{PSM} \le 12.1\ days$ (theoretical) | Days | Extended sleep interval |
| **Coding Rate (lowest)** | $R_{c,min} = \frac{1}{48}$ | Ratio | Used for CE Level 2 |
| **Device Capacity (3GPP target)** | $N_{dev} \ge 50{,}000$ | Devices / cell | mMTC benchmark |
| **Path Loss (Hata Urban Model)** | $PL_{dB} = 69.55 + 26.16 \log_{10}(f) - 13.82 \log_{10}(h_b) - a(h_m) + (44.9 - 6.55 \log_{10}(h_b)) \log_{10}(d)$ | dB | Macro-cell dimensioning |

> [!NOTE]

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Metering (Water, Gas, Electricity)**: Once-daily telemetry from basements, 10-year battery.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil moisture, pH sensors across hundreds of hectares.
- **Asset Tracking**: Containers, pallets, livestock — low-rate GPS reports.
- **Smart Cities**: Street lighting, parking sensors, waste-bin level monitors.
- **Industrial Monitoring**: Pressure/temperature sensors in remote pipelines and oil rigs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> NB-IoT is the **3GPP cellular answer to Sigfox and LoRaWAN**, offering carrier-grade reliability, licensed-spectrum security, and seamless integration with the existing 4G/5G core network (EPC / 5GC).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The 20 dB Coverage Gain of NB-IoT over LTE

The defining feature of NB-IoT's coverage is the **+20 dB MCL** compared to standard LTE Cat-1. The following derivation expresses this gain in linear and physical terms.

**Step 1 — Statement of base LTE and NB-IoT MCL targets.**

The 3GPP TR 45.820 specification study defines:
$$MCL_{LTE} = 144\ dB \quad \text{(Category 1, Reference Sensitivity} = -100\ dBm\text{)}$$

$$MCL_{NB\text{-}IoT} = 164\ dB \quad \text{(Reference Sensitivity} = -141\ dBm\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the difference in decibels.**

$$\Delta MCL = MCL_{NB\text{-}IoT} - MCL_{LTE}$$

$$\Delta MCL = 164\ dB - 144\ dB = 20\ dB$$

**Step 3 — Convert the decibel gain to a linear power ratio.**

By definition, a power ratio $G$ in dB satisfies $G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{ratio})$. Solving for $P_{ratio}$:

$$\log_{10}(P_{ratio}) = \frac{G_{dB}}{10} = \frac{20}{10} = 2$$

$$P_{ratio} = 10^{2} = 100$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the linear gain physically.**

The NB-IoT link budget tolerates **100× more path loss** than standard LTE. In free-space propagation where received power falls as $P_{r} \propto \frac{1}{d^{2}}$, a 100× improvement in tolerable loss corresponds to a $\sqrt{100} = 10\times$ linear increase in cell radius, or a **100× increase in coverage area** for isotropic propagation.

$$R_{NB\text{-}IoT} = 10 \cdot R_{LTE} \quad \text{(free-space approximation)}$$

**Step 5 — Adjust for urban non-line-of-sight (NLOS) propagation.**

In urban NLOS (Hata / 3GPP model), path loss exponent $n \approx 3.5$ to 4.0. With $n = 3.76$:

$$\frac{R_{NB\text{-}IoT}}{R_{LTE}} = (100)^{\frac{1}{n}} = 100^{0.266} \approx 4.64$$

$$\boxed{\frac{R_{NB\text{-}IoT}}{R_{LTE}} \approx 4.6 \times \text{ in urban NLOS} \Rightarrow \text{coverage area} \approx 21\times}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** NB-IoT's 20 dB MCL improvement translates to **~ 4.6× the cell radius** and **~ 21× the coverage area** compared to standard LTE in realistic urban deployment — this is why NB-IoT is classified as a *deep-coverage* technology.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coverage Enhancement (CE) Level and Repetition Factor

The CE level is selected based on the estimated Reference Signal Received Power (RSRP) and the device's path loss. 3GPP defines the mapping:

**Step 1 — Read the RSRP threshold for each CE level.**

| CE Level | RSRP Range | Max UL Repetitions | Coding Rate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | RSRP ≥ -105 dBm | 1 | 1/3 (Turbo) |
| 1 | -115 dBm ≤ RSRP < -105 dBm | 8 | 1/3 |
| 2 | RSRP < -115 dBm | 128 | 1/48 |

**Step 2 — Compute the energy gain from repetition.**

For BPSK/QPSK under AWGN, each repetition adds the signal energies coherently while noise adds incoherently. After $N_{rep}$ repetitions, the effective SNR improves by:

$$SNR_{eff} = SNR_{0} + 10 \log_{10}(N_{rep})$$

where $SNR_{0}$ is the SNR per repetition.

**Step 3 — Apply the formula for CE Level 2.**

For the worst case $N_{rep,UL} = 128$:

$$\Delta SNR_{CE2} = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(2^{7}) = 10 \cdot 7 \cdot 0.301 = 21.07\ dB$$

For the downlink maximum $N_{rep,DL} = 2048$:

$$\Delta SNR_{DL,CE2} = 10 \log_{10}(2048) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(2^{11}) = 10 \cdot 11 \cdot 0.301 = 33.11\ dB$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the reduced coding rate.**

A coding rate drop from $R_{c,ref} = 1/3$ to $R_{c,min} = 1/48$ provides an additional coding gain. Approximating Shannon capacity $C = B \log_{2}(1 + SNR)$ operating at the same $E_b/N_0$:

$$G_{coding} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{R_{c,ref}}{R_{c,min}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1/3}{1/48}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(16) = 12.04\ dB$$

**Step 5 — Total CE Level 2 link budget gain.**

$$G_{total,CE2} = \Delta SNR_{CE2} + G_{coding} = 21.07 + 12.04 = 33.11\ dB$$

This composite gain is the engineering basis for the **164 dB MCL** target when combined with the base LTE reference sensitivity of -100 dBm plus receiver sensitivity improvements of the NB-IoT front end.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Data-Rate Reduction due to Repetition

The transport block size $TBS$ is reduced when repetitions are applied because the effective coding rate combines the channel coding rate and the repetition factor.

**Step 1 — Define the relationship.**

$$R_{eff} = \frac{R_{c}}{N_{rep}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the user-plane data rate.**

$$R_{user} = TBS \cdot \frac{N_{sub}}{T_{sub} \cdot N_{rep}}$$

where $N_{sub}$ is the number of subframes carrying the data and $T_{sub}$ is the subframe duration (1 ms in LTE/NB-IoT).

**Step 3 — Substitute the Cat-NB1 downlink worst case (CE Level 2).**

For the smallest transport block of $TBS = 16$ bits over $N_{sub} = 1$ subframe, $N_{rep} = 2048$:

$$R_{DL,min} = 16 \cdot \frac{1}{1 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 2048} = \frac{16}{2.048} \approx 7.81\ bps$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the best case (no repetitions, multi-tone, 12 subcarriers).**

$$R_{DL,max} = TBS_{max} \cdot \frac{N_{sub}}{T_{sub}} = 2536 \cdot \frac{1}{10^{-3}} \approx 26.15\ kbps$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** NB-IoT's data rate can drop below 10 bps in CE Level 2 — slow, but the **reliability and coverage** are the design priorities, not throughput.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Summary

| Feature | Achieved By | Trade-off / Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **+20 dB MCL** | Repetitions + lower coding rate | Data rate drops 100× |
| **10-year battery** | PSM + eDRX | Latency increases (reachable only periodically) |
| **Low cost (< 5 USD)** | Half-duplex, single antenna, QPSK | No simultaneous TX/RX, no MIMO |
| **Massive capacity (50k/cell)** | Single-tone UL, narrow BW | Per-device throughput is low |
| **180 kHz channel** | Reuses one LTE PRB | Cannot support high bandwidth apps |
| **Licensed spectrum** | Reuses operator LTE spectrum | Operator-controlled, not free like LoRa ISM |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **List and briefly explain the six key design features of NB-IoT as standardized by 3GPP Release 13.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ~ 0.5 marks per feature):**

The six key design features of NB-IoT (3GPP Release 13) are:
1. **Ultra-low power consumption** — supported by Power Saving Mode (PSM) and extended DRX (eDRX) cycles, enabling a 10-year battery life on a 5 Wh cell.
2. **Extended coverage (MCL of 164 dB)** — 20 dB deeper than standard LTE, achieved through up to 128 UL repetitions and a coding rate as low as 1/48.
3. **Massive device capacity** — supports more than 50,000 NB-IoT devices per cell using single-tone uplink and aggressive repetition.
4. **Low device cost (< 5 USD)** — half-duplex FDD, single antenna, simplified RF chain, and a 180 kHz narrow receiver bandwidth.
5. **Optimized data rate** — peak downlink ~ 26 kbps and uplink ~ 62.5 kbps, sufficient for small telemetry payloads.
6. **Flexible spectrum deployment** — supports three modes: in-band, guard-band, and standalone in licensed LTE/GSM spectrum.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
> **What are the three Coverage Enhancement (CE) levels in NB-IoT? State the maximum number of repetitions supported at each level.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three CE levels in NB-IoT (3GPP TS 36.213) are:

| CE Level | RSRP Range | Max UL Repetitions | Max DL Repetitions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CE 0** | $\ge -105\ dBm$ | 1 | 1 |
| **CE 1** | $-115\ dBm \le RSRP < -105\ dBm$ | 8 | 8 |
| **CE 2** | $< -115\ dBm$ | 128 | 2048 |

The CE level is selected based on the measured RSRP. CE Level 2 is the most aggressive, used in the deepest indoor / underground deployments, with a coding rate of 1/48, supporting the 164 dB MCL target.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Question Choice 1]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the three deployment modes of NB-IoT, illustrating how the 180 kHz carrier can be placed in the LTE spectrum. Why is guard-band deployment attractive for operators? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the 20 dB coverage gain of NB-IoT over standard LTE in terms of linear power ratio, free-space cell-radius multiplication, and urban NLOS area multiplication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part A(a) — Deployment Modes (7 marks):**

The three deployment modes of NB-IoT, defined in 3GPP Release 13, are:

**1. Standalone Mode (2 marks)**
- The 180 kHz NB-IoT carrier is allocated to dedicated spectrum, e.g., a refarmed GSM channel (typically 200 kHz GSM carrier) or unused IMT spectrum.
- Used by operators transitioning from 2G/3G to NB-IoT, allowing full reuse of legacy sites.
- Advantage: zero interference from LTE traffic; full control of capacity.

**2. In-Band Mode (2 marks)**
- The 180 kHz NB-IoT carrier is placed *inside* an existing LTE carrier, occupying one LTE Physical Resource Block (PRB).
- The PRB is reserved and not used for LTE traffic; the eNodeB must schedule LTE around the NB-IoT PRB.
- Advantage: efficient spectrum utilization when the LTE network is lightly loaded.

**3. Guard-Band Mode (2 marks)**
- The 180 kHz NB-IoT carrier is positioned in the unused guard band of an LTE carrier (between two adjacent LTE carriers).
- Leverages previously wasted spectrum.
- **Why attractive to operators (1 mark):** It does not consume any usable LTE PRBs (unlike in-band), has minimal interference from LTE (unlike in-band), and does not require a separate spectrum license. It provides a "free" capacity boost for IoT.

---

**Model Answer — Part A(b) — Coverage Gain Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the MCL targets:**
$$MCL_{LTE} = 144\ dB, \quad MCL_{NB\text{-}IoT} = 164\ dB$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute dB difference:**
$$\Delta MCL = 164 - 144 = 20\ dB$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Convert to linear power ratio:**
$$P_{ratio} = 10^{\frac{20}{10}} = 10^{2} = 100$$

So NB-IoT tolerates **100× more path loss** than LTE.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Free-space cell-radius gain:**
For free-space propagation $P_{r} \propto \frac{1}{d^{2}}$, so 100× tolerable loss implies:
$$\frac{R_{NB\text{-}IoT}}{R_{LTE}} = \sqrt{100} = 10\times$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Urban NLOS area gain (path-loss exponent n = 3.76):**
$$R_{ratio} = (100)^{\frac{1}{3.76}} \approx 4.64 \Rightarrow A_{ratio} = 4.64^{2} \approx 21.5\times$$

> **Conclusion:** NB-IoT covers **~ 21× the area** of a standard LTE cell in urban environments, justifying the 164 dB MCL design goal.

---

> **Question B (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Power Saving Mode (PSM) and extended DRX (eDRX) mechanisms of NB-IoT. Show how each contributes to achieving the 10-year battery life target. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An NB-IoT device in Coverage Enhancement Level 2 transmits using 128 repetitions with coding rate 1/48. Compute: (i) the SNR gain from repetitions, (ii) the coding gain over the reference rate of 1/3, and (iii) the total composite gain in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part B(a) — PSM and eDRX (7 marks):**

**Power Saving Mode — PSM (3 marks)**
- Introduced in 3GPP Release 12 and reused in NB-IoT.
- After the last data exchange, the device enters PSM by negotiating an **Active Timer (T3324)** and a **Periodic TAU Timer (T3412)** with the network.
- During PSM the device is **not pageable**; the radio is fully off, drawing only a few microamperes for the internal timer.
- The device becomes reachable only after the PSM timer expires (typically next uplink event, e.g., once per day for a smart meter).
- T3412 can be set up to **~ 12.1 days** theoretically, but in practice 1 day or 1 week for metering.
- **Energy contribution:** Sleep current dominates the energy budget; a 5 Wh battery can last > 10 years at a 1-frame/day duty cycle.

**Extended DRX — eDRX (2 marks)**
- Extends the LTE paging cycle from 2.56 s up to **~ 2.91 hours (10,485.76 s)**.
- The device sleeps for the entire eDRX cycle and wakes only at the next paging window.
- The device is *still reachable* (unlike PSM), but reachable only intermittently.
- Used when downlink traffic is occasional but cannot tolerate the full PSM unavailability.

**Combined 10-year battery (2 marks):**
- Total energy $E = P_{active} \cdot T_{active} + P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep}$.
- For a 1 report/day device, $T_{active} \approx 1\ s/day$ and $T_{sleep} \approx 86,399\ s/day$.
- With $P_{active} \approx 0.3\ W$ and $P_{sleep} \approx 5\ \mu W$:
$$E_{day} = 0.3 \cdot 1 + 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 86399 \approx 0.30\ J + 0.43\ J \approx 0.73\ J/day$$
$$T_{battery} = \frac{5 \cdot 3600\ J}{0.73\ J/day} \approx 24,650\ days \approx 67\ years \text{ (theoretical; practical derating} \approx 10\text{ years)}$$

---

**Model Answer — Part B(b) — CE Level 2 Gain Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) SNR gain from 128 repetitions (3 marks):**

$$\Delta SNR_{rep} = 10 \log_{10}(N_{rep}) = 10 \log_{10}(128)$$

$$128 = 2^{7} \Rightarrow 10 \log_{10}(2^{7}) = 10 \cdot 7 \cdot \log_{10}(2) = 10 \cdot 7 \cdot 0.30103 = 21.07\ dB$$

**(ii) Coding gain from 1/3 to 1/48 (2 marks):**

$$G_{coding} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{R_{c,ref}}{R_{c,min}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1/3}{1/48}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(16) = 12.04\ dB$$

**(iii) Total composite gain (2 marks):**

$$G_{total} = \Delta SNR_{rep} + G_{coding} = 21.07 + 12.04 = 33.11\ dB$$

> **Result:** A total of **33.11 dB** link-budget gain is realized at CE Level 2, which is the engineering basis for the 164 dB MCL target of NB-IoT.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MCL with path loss.** MCL is the *maximum tolerable* loss, not the actual propagation loss. Many answers incorrectly compute MCL as $PL_{actual}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 12.04 dB coding gain.** Repetitions alone give only ~ 21 dB; the full 33 dB gain requires including the lowered coding rate.
> 3. **Mixing up PSM and eDRX.** PSM = *unreachable*; eDRX = *reachable only at paging window*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction for 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Quoting "180 kHz" without "PRB" context.** Always mention *"one LTE Physical Resource Block"* to earn full syllabus-specific marks.
> 5. **Incorrect log conversions.** Use $10 \log_{10}$ for power quantities and $20 \log_{10}$ for amplitude — both apply in different contexts.
> 6. **Omitting the half-duplex FDD cost rationale.** Examiners expect the link between *half-duplex* and *< 5 USD module cost* in any 7-mark question on NB-IoT cost.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] NB-IoT is a **3GPP Release 13** LPWAN technology for **mMTC** scenarios.
- [ ] Channel bandwidth: **180 kHz** = one LTE PRB.
- [ ] **MCL target = 164 dB**; **20 dB deeper than LTE (144 dB)**.
- [ ] **Battery life > 10 years** on a 5 Wh cell.
- [ ] **Device cost < 5 USD** via half-duplex FDD + single antenna + QPSK.
- [ ] **Capacity > 50,000 devices / cell**.
- [ ] Peak DL ≈ **26.15 kbps**; Peak UL ≈ **62.5 kbps** (multi-tone).
- [ ] Three deployment modes: **in-band, guard-band, standalone**.
- [ ] Three **CE Levels**: 0, 1, 2 — with up to **128 UL** and **2048 DL repetitions**.
- [ ] Lowest coding rate = **1/48**.
- [ ] **PSM** = unreachable sleep (sleep current dominates).
- [ ] **eDRX** = reachable only at paging window, up to **2.91 hours** cycle.
- [ ] Modulation: **QPSK only** (cost and PA-efficiency driven).
- [ ] Half-duplex FDD operation — eliminates the duplexer.
- [ ] Uplink supports **single-tone (3.75 kHz / 15 kHz)** and **multi-tone (12 × 15 kHz)**.
- [ ] 164 dB MCL corresponds to **~ 4.6× cell radius** and **~ 21× area** in urban NLOS.
- [ ] **CE Level 2 total gain ≈ 33.11 dB** = 21.07 dB (repetition) + 12.04 dB (coding).
- [ ] NB-IoT complements (not replaces) LTE-M (Cat-M1) — both are mMTC standards in 3GPP.
- [ ] **3GPP Releases 14, 15, 16** progressively enhanced NB-IoT (NB-IoT2, multicast, mobility, TDD support, higher data rates up to ~ 250 kbps).
- [ ] **SCEF (Service Capability Exposure Function)** in the EPC enables non-IP data delivery to the application server.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of the **KTU OEERT614 — Internet of Things (2024 Scheme)** syllabus, **IoT Applications** refers to the domain-specific deployment of networked sensor-actuator systems, communication protocols, and edge/cloud analytics that transform raw physical phenomena into actionable digital services. These applications leverage the standard **感知层–网络层–平台层–应用层** (Perception–Network–Platform–Application) IoT reference architecture to deliver intelligent monitoring, control, and decision-making across civilian, industrial, agricultural, healthcare, and infrastructural verticals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner-Verbatim):**
> *IoT Applications are context-aware, service-oriented systems that integrate heterogeneous sensing devices, communication networks, and data analytics to automate, optimize, or enhance a specific vertical-domain process.*

## 1.2 The Four-Layer IoT Application Reference Model

Every IoT application, regardless of vertical, maps to the canonical ITU-T Y.2060 four-layer model:

| Layer | Function | Typical Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Physical sensing & actuation | Sensors, RFID tags, actuators, MEMS |
| **Network Layer** | Data transport | Wi-Fi, 6LoWPAN, LoRa, NB-IoT, LTE-M, BLE |
| **Support / Platform Layer** | Storage & processing | Edge gateways, fog nodes, cloud brokers (MQTT/HTTP/CoAP) |
| **Application Layer** | Domain intelligence | Dashboards, ML models, control loops, APIs |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Body of an IoT Application"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Beginner-Friendly):**
> Think of an IoT Application as a **human body**:
> - **Sensors** are the *senses* (eyes, ears, skin) → Perception Layer.
> - **Network/Communication** is the *nervous system* → Network Layer.
> - **Cloud/Edge Platform** is the *brain* (memory & thought) → Platform Layer.
> - **App/Dashboard/Control** is the *action* (speech, movement) → Application Layer.
> 
> Just as the body cannot *see* without eyes, an IoT application cannot perceive the physical world without the Perception Layer. The vertical (agriculture, health, transport) is essentially the *job* the body performs.

## 1.4 Classification of IoT Applications (KTU High-Yield)

KTU examiners frequently ask students to **classify IoT applications by domain and by criticality**. Memorize the following taxonomy:

**A. By Vertical Domain:**
- **Consumer IoT (CIoT):** Smart Home, Wearables, Smart Retail
- **Commercial IoT:** Smart Buildings, Smart Logistics, Asset Tracking
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Smart Manufacturing, Predictive Maintenance, SCADA
- **Infrastructure IoT:** Smart City, Smart Grid, Smart Water, Smart Transport
- **Agricultural IoT (AIoT):** Precision Farming, Livestock Monitoring, Smart Greenhouses
- **Healthcare IoT (IoMT):** Remote Patient Monitoring, Telemedicine, Smart Pills
- **Environmental IoT:** Air Quality, Wildfire Detection, Flood Warning, Glacier Monitoring

**B. By Mission Criticality:**
- **Mission-Critical:** Autonomous vehicles, industrial safety, medical life-support
- **Business-Critical:** Smart metering, supply chain visibility
- **Convenience-Oriented:** Smart lighting, voice assistants

## 1.5 Common Cross-Vertical Building Blocks

Regardless of the application, every IoT deployment shares these **five universal components**:

1. **Thing / Sensor Node** — Generates data (e.g., DHT22, soil moisture, ECG electrode).
2. **Connectivity** — Carries data (Wi-Fi 802.11, BLE 5.0, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT).
3. **Gateway / Edge** — Aggregates, filters, and pre-processes.
4. **Cloud / Server** — Stores, analyzes, and runs ML models.
5. **Application / UI** — Renders actionable insights (mobile app, dashboard, actuator command).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Operational Pipeline of an IoT Application

The operational lifecycle of **any** IoT application can be broken into six deterministic stages. This pipeline is the single most-asked conceptual block in KTU Module-3 viva and ESE questions.

**Stage 1 — Sensing / Data Acquisition**
- Physical transducers convert real-world phenomena (temperature, pressure, motion) into electrical signals.
- Sampling rate must obey the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**.

**Stage 2 — Local Pre-Processing (Edge)**
- Filtering, aggregation, and feature extraction occur at the sensor or gateway.
- Reduces bandwidth and cloud cost.

**Stage 3 — Secure Transmission**
- Data is encapsulated (MQTT publish, CoAP POST, HTTP PUT) and sent via the chosen MAC/PHY.
- Encryption (TLS/DTLS), integrity (HMAC), and authentication (X.509, PSK) are enforced.

**Stage 4 — Ingestion & Storage**
- Cloud brokers (Mosquitto, EMQX, HiveMQ) or serverless queues (AWS Kinesis, Azure IoT Hub) accept streams.
- Time-series DBs (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) and data lakes (S3, HDFS) persist raw + processed data.

**Stage 5 — Analytics & Intelligence**
- Descriptive: dashboards, KPIs.
- Diagnostic: anomaly detection (Z-score, Isolation Forest).
- **Predictive**: regression, LSTM.
- **Prescriptive**: reinforcement learning, optimization.

**Stage 6 — Actuation & Feedback**
- Control signals are pushed back to actuators (relays, valves, motors, alarms).
- The loop is closed, completing a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this pipeline matter for KTU?**
> Examiners in the 2024 Scheme have repeatedly asked: *"With a neat block diagram, explain the data flow in an IoT application."* The 6-stage pipeline above is your board-exam-ready answer.

## 2.2 Key Engineering Equations (KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mathematically verifiable** and frequently tested in KTU derivations and numericals. All vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve table rendering.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Representation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling freq. (Hz), $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq. (Hz) | Determines minimum sensor sampling rate. |
| 2 | Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \tfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ = capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR (unitless) | Maximum data rate over IoT link. |
| 3 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ = distance (km), $f$ = freq. (MHz) | Link budget for LoRa/NB-IoT planning. |
| 4 | Link Budget | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX} - FSPL + G_{RX} - L_{RX}$ | All powers/gains in dB | Determines range feasibility. |
| 5 | Received Power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\tfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength (m), $d$ = distance (m) | Wireless range estimation. |
| 6 | Energy of a Sensor Node | $E_{node} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | $V$ = V, $I$ = A, $t$ = s | Battery life calculation. |
| 7 | Battery Lifetime | $T_{life} = \tfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$ | $C_{bat}$ = mAh, $I_{avg}$ = mA | Determines deployment cycle. |
| 8 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $MTTF = \tfrac{1}{\lambda_f}$ | $\lambda_f$ = failure rate (failures/hr) | Reliability of IIoT nodes. |
| 9 | Data Generation Rate | $R_{gen} = N_s \cdot f_s \cdot B_s$ | $N_s$ = sensors, $f_s$ = Hz, $B_s$ = bits/sample | Bandwidth sizing. |
| 10 | Total Storage per Day | $S_{day} = R_{gen} \cdot 86400 / 8$ | Result in bytes | Cloud storage planning. |
| 11 | Probability of False Alarm | $P_{fa} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\tfrac{\eta}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | $\eta$ = detection threshold | Energy detection in cognitive IoT. |
| 12 | Coverage Probability | $P_{cov} = \mathbb{P}\!\left[ SINR > \gamma_{th} \right]$ | $\gamma_{th}$ = SINR threshold | Massive IoT planning. |

## 2.3 Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Connectivity per Application

> [!TIP]
> KTU Module-3 often asks: *"Justify the choice of communication technology for a given IoT application."* The matrix below is the examiner's gold-standard answer.

| Application | Data Rate | Range | Power | Battery Life | Recommended Tech |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Smart Home (interior)** | Medium (Mbps) | $\leq 50$ m | Mains powered | N/A | Wi-Fi 6, Zigbee 3.0, BLE Mesh |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Low (kbps) | $\leq 15$ km | Battery | $\geq 5$ years | LoRaWAN, NB-IoT |
| **Smart Metering** | Very Low | $\leq 10$ km | Battery | $\geq 10$ years | NB-IoT, LTE-M |
| **Wearable Health** | Low | $\leq 10$ m | Battery | $\geq 7$ days | BLE 5.0 |
| **Industrial Control** | High (real-time) | $\leq 100$ m | Mains | N/A | TSN over Ethernet, 5G URLLC |
| **Vehicle V2X** | High | $\leq 1$ km | Vehicle battery | Continuous | C-V2X, DSRC |
| **Environmental Sensing** | Very Low | Wide | Solar/Battery | $\geq 2$ years | Sigfox, LoRaWAN |
| **Smart Grid / DERS** | Medium | Wide | Mains + Backup | N/A | IEC 61850, DNP3, LTE |
| **Smart City Lighting** | Low | $\leq 5$ km | Mains | N/A | PLC, NB-IoT |
| **Asset Tracking (Indoor)** | Medium | $\leq 50$ m | Battery | $\geq 1$ year | BLE 5.1 AoA, UWB |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bandwidth Sizing** uses $R_{gen}$ and $S_{day}$ to size cellular data plans.
- **Battery Sizing** uses $E_{node}$ and $T_{life}$ to choose coin-cell vs. Li-SoCl₂.
- **Link Budget** uses $FSPL$ and $P_{RX}$ to decide on tower count.
- **Reliability Engineering** uses $MTTF$ to plan IIoT preventive maintenance.
- **Cybersecurity Posture** uses $P_{fa}$ and $P_{cov}$ to harden massive-IoT detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 End-to-End Worked Example: Smart Agriculture Irrigation System

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A precision agriculture deployment uses **200 soil-moisture sensors** spread across a **5 km × 5 km** farm. Each sensor samples at **1 Hz**, with **12-bit resolution**, and reports data via **LoRaWAN** every **15 minutes**. The gateway has a **+14 dBi antenna** at **868 MHz** and the sensor transmits at **+14 dBm** with a **2 dBi antenna** at a height ensuring **160 dB maximum path loss** including all margins.
> 
> Calculate:
> 1. The total data generated per day.
> 2. The battery life if each node uses a **2400 mAh** battery with an **average current draw of 2 mA**.
> 3. The maximum usable range from the FSPL equation.

---

### Step 1 — Raw Data Generated per Sensor per Sample

The bit-depth of each sample is the resolution $B_s = 12$ bits.

$$
B_{sample} = 12 \text{ bits}
$$

**Explanation:** Each moisture reading is encoded as 12 bits of digital information (one of 4096 distinct levels). This is fixed by the ADC of the sensor.

---

### Step 2 — Data Rate per Sensor

The sensor samples at $f_s = 1$ Hz.

$$
R_{single} = f_s \cdot B_s = 1 \cdot 12 = 12 \text{ bps}
$$

**Explanation:** Every second the sensor produces 12 bits, so the *raw* data rate is 12 bits per second.

---

### Step 3 — Data Generated per Sensor per Day

Since the sensor runs continuously for 86400 seconds:

$$
S_{sensor/day} = R_{single} \cdot 86400 = 12 \cdot 86400
$$

$$
S_{sensor/day} = 1{,}036{,}800 \text{ bits/day}
$$

Converting to bytes:

$$
S_{sensor/day} = \tfrac{1{,}036{,}800}{8} = 129{,}600 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 126.6 \text{ KB/day}
$$

**Explanation:** Continuous raw sampling is wasteful; in practice, the node buffers locally and transmits once every 15 min (a 2.4 KB packet per transmission). This is why **edge pre-processing** is essential in IoT.

---

### Step 4 — Total Data for the Entire Farm (N = 200 sensors)

$$
S_{farm/day} = N_s \cdot S_{sensor/day} = 200 \cdot 129{,}600
$$

$$
S_{farm/day} = 25{,}920{,}000 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 24.73 \text{ MB/day}
$$

**Explanation:** At farm scale, daily data stays in the **tens of MB** regime — a stark contrast to video-centric IoT (which produces GBs/hour). This justifies low-bandwidth LPWAN like LoRaWAN.

---

### Step 5 — Battery Lifetime Calculation

Using the linear battery-discharge model:

$$
T_{life} = \tfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \tfrac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{2 \text{ mA}}
$$

$$
T_{life} = 1200 \text{ hours} = \tfrac{1200}{24} = 50 \text{ days}
$$

**Explanation:** Under *continuous* sampling at 1 Hz, the node lasts only 50 days — unacceptable for agriculture. **Mitigation:** Duty-cycle the sensor to 1 sample/15 min, reducing $I_{avg}$ to $\sim 30 \mu A$, which extends life to $\sim 8.9$ years.

---

### Step 6 — Maximum Range via Free-Space Path Loss

Starting with the FSPL formula at $f = 868$ MHz, given total maximum allowable path loss $L_{max} = 160$ dB (already includes all margins):

$$
L_{max} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

Substituting $L_{max} = 160$ dB and $f_{MHz} = 868$:

$$
160 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(868)
$$

$$
160 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 58.77
$$

$$
160 = 91.21 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 68.79
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 3.4395
$$

$$
d_{km} = 10^{3.4395} \approx 2756 \text{ km}
$$

**Explanation:** The 160 dB link budget in free space yields an unrealistically large range; in **real deployments** with vegetation, soil absorption, and Fresnel obstruction, achievable range drops to **5–15 km**, perfectly covering the 5 km × 5 km farm with a single gateway tower at the center.

---

### Step 7 — Final Result Summary

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Data rate per sensor | 12 | bps |
| Data per sensor per day | $\approx 126.6$ | KB |
| Data per farm per day | $\approx 24.73$ | MB |
| Battery life (continuous) | 50 | days |
| Battery life (duty-cycled) | $\approx 8.9$ | years |
| Theoretical FSPL range | 2756 | km |
| Practical LoRaWAN range | 5–15 | km |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting an Application Stack

| Decision Dimension | Option A (Cloud-Centric) | Option B (Edge-Centric) | Decision Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | 100–500 ms (round-trip to cloud) | 1–10 ms (local) | Real-time control ⇒ Edge |
| **Bandwidth** | High uplink required | Only events transmitted | Cellular cost ⇒ Edge |
| **Privacy** | Raw data leaves site | Data stays on-prem | GDPR / Health ⇒ Edge |
| **ML Model Hosting** | Cloud-trained, cloud-deployed | TinyML on MCU | Compute availability |
| **Power** | Higher (radio always on) | Lower (event-driven) | Battery life |
| **Cost** | Pay-as-you-go cloud | Capex on edge box | Capex vs. Opex |
| **Reliability** | Dependent on WAN uptime | Survives WAN outage | Mission-critical ⇒ Edge |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume *all* IoT applications are cloud-centric. In reality, **60–70% of enterprise IoT data is processed at the edge** by 2026 (industry trends). Always state the **latency, privacy, and bandwidth** constraints *before* naming the architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
