
## 4.4 SRAM Read/Write Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START(["Start Operation"]) --> PRE["Precharge BL and BLbar to VDD/2"]
    PRE --> ACTWL["Activate Word Line WL goes HIGH"]
    ACTWL --> CHK{"Read or Write?"}
    CHK -->|READ| SENSE["Sense Amplifier Detects ~50 mV Differential"]
    SENSE --> AMP["Amplify to Full Rail VDD or GND"]
    AMP --> OUT_R["Output Data on Bus"]
    OUT_R --> DEACT["Deactivate WL"]
    CHK -->|WRITE| DRIVE["Drive BL and BLbar to New Values"]
    DRIVE --> FLIP["Cross-Coupled Inverters Flip State"]
    FLIP --> DEACT
    DEACT --> END(["Operation Complete"])
```

## 4.5 DRAM Refresh Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RSTART(["Refresh Timer 64 ms Expiry"]) --> R1["Counter Points to Row N"]
    R1 --> R2["Activate Word Line of Row N"]
    R2 --> R3["Sense Amplifiers Read All Cells in Row N"]
    R3 --> R4["Charge Restored on Capacitors Write-Back"]
    R4 --> R5["Deactivate Word Line"]
    R5 --> R6["Increment Counter to Row N+1"]
    R6 --> RCHECK{"All Rows Refreshed?"}
    RCHECK -->|No| R1
    RCHECK -->|Yes| REND(["Wait 64 ms Restart"])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory. Give two examples of each used in IoT devices.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Volatile Memory | Non-Volatile Memory |
|---|---|---|
| Power dependency | Loses data when power OFF | Retains data without power |
| Examples in IoT | SRAM, DRAM | Flash, EEPROM, FRAM |
| Speed | Faster (ns access) | Slower (μs–ms access) |
| Write endurance | Unlimited (~10¹⁶) | Limited (~10⁵ for Flash) |
| Use case | Working memory, buffers | Firmware storage, logs |
| Cost per bit | Higher | Lower |

Volatile memory is used in IoT for real-time processing and buffering; non-volatile memory is used for persistent firmware and data logging.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Why does DRAM require periodic refresh while SRAM does not? Explain in 3–4 lines.**

**Model Answer:**
DRAM stores each bit as charge on a tiny capacitor (1T1C cell). This capacitor leaks charge through sub-threshold and junction leakage paths, so the stored voltage decays exponentially. To prevent data loss, every row must be read and rewritten (refreshed) approximately every **64 ms**. SRAM, in contrast, uses a 6-transistor cross-coupled latch that actively reinforces its state as long as $V_{DD}$ is supplied — no refresh is needed because the inverters continuously drive each other.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Explain the architecture and working of a 6T SRAM cell with a neat diagram. Discuss its read and write operations. **(7 Marks)**
> *(b)* Derive the expression for the sense-amplifier differential voltage in a DRAM read operation. A DRAM cell has $C_s = 30$ fF and $C_{BL} = 240$ fF with $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V. Calculate the sense voltage. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A **6T SRAM cell** consists of:
- Two cross-coupled CMOS inverters (M1–M4) forming a bistable latch
- Two NMOS access transistors (M5, M6) gated by the Word Line (WL)

**Stable States:**
- **State 0:** $Q = 0$, $\overline{Q} = 1$ → M1 ON, M2 OFF
- **State 1:** $Q = 1$, $\overline{Q} = 0$ → M1 OFF, M2 ON

**Read Operation:**
1. Pre-charge both bit lines BL and $\overline{BL}$ to $V_{DD}/2$
2. Activate WL HIGH → M5, M6 turn ON
3. The cell pulls one bit-line slightly down based on stored value
4. Sense amplifier amplifies the small differential (≈ 50 mV) to full rail

**Write Operation:**
1. Drive BL and $\overline{BL}$ to the new complementary logic values
2. Activate WL HIGH
3. Access transistors overpower the latch, flipping the inverters
4. Deactivate WL → new state is latched statically (no refresh needed)

*(Diagram: 6T cell with cross-coupled inverters and access transistors as shown in Section 4.1)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Using the charge-sharing derivation from Section 3.1:

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{C_s}{2(C_s + C_{BL})} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-15}}{2 \times (30 + 240) \times 10^{-15}} \times 1.2
$$

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{30}{540} \times 1.2 = 0.0556 \times 1.2 \approx 0.0667 \text{ V} = 66.7 \text{ mV}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write the formula as $\Delta V = \dfrac{C_s}{C_s + C_{BL}} \cdot V_{DD}$ (missing the factor of 2 in the denominator) or use $V_{DD}$ instead of $V_{DD}/2$ as the pre-charge level. The pre-charge is ALWAYS $V_{DD}/2$ in standard DRAM — this is the most common deduction point worth **2 marks**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
> *(a)* Compare SRAM and DRAM in terms of cell structure, density, speed, power, refresh requirement, and typical IoT applications. **(7 Marks)**
> *(b)* A DRAM array has 8192 rows, $V_{DD} = 1.5$ V, $C_{BL} = 150$ fF, and refresh interval 64 ms. Calculate the total refresh power consumption. Comment on its suitability for a battery-powered IoT gateway. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | SRAM (6T) | DRAM (1T1C) | Typical IoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell structure | 6 transistors, latch | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor | — |
| Density | ~$6F^2$, low | ~$8F^2$, but smaller F → high | Flash+DRAM combo in gateways |
| Speed | 1–10 ns access | 50–100 ns access | SRAM for caches, TCMs |
| Static power | High leakage per cell | Low (refresh is the dominant cost) | Trade-off based on capacity |
| Refresh | **None required** | Every ~64 ms | SRAM in sleep-mode IoT nodes |
| Applications | MCU internal SRAM, L1 cache | Linux gateway main RAM | — |

**Inference for IoT:**
- Low-power MCUs (Cortex-M0+) use **SRAM** for predictability and zero refresh overhead.
- Edge gateways running Linux use **DRAM** for high-density main memory.
- Always-on IoT nodes prefer **SRAM** to eliminate refresh energy.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$
P_{refresh} = \dfrac{N_{rows} \cdot C_{BL} \cdot V_{DD}^2}{2 \cdot t_{ref}}
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$
P_{refresh} = \dfrac{8192 \times 150 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.5)^2}{2 \times 64 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 1: Numerator**

$$
8192 \times 150 \times 10^{-15} \times 2.25 = 2.765 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J per cycle}
$$

**Step 2: Divide by refresh period**

$$
P_{refresh} = \dfrac{2.765 \times 10^{-9}}{0.128} \approx 2.16 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W} \approx 21.6 \text{ nW}
$$

**Comment on IoT Suitability:**
- At **~22 nW**, the refresh power itself is negligible compared to active MCU + radio power (mW range).
- However, in **deep-sleep IoT nodes drawing only a few μA**, this 22 nW is non-trivial (~0.2% of sleep budget).
- For a solar-powered gateway, DRAM refresh is perfectly acceptable. For a coin-cell sensor node, **SRAM-only architectures** are strongly preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is computing **energy per refresh** and forgetting to divide by the refresh interval. The question asks for **POWER** in Watts, so the division by $t_{ref} = 64$ ms is mandatory. Students who omit this lose 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Global Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the DRAM charge-sharing formula → 2 mark loss
> 2. **Confusing SRAM cell area** ($6F^2$) with DRAM cell area ($8F^2$) — DRAM has fewer elements but more overhead → 1 mark
> 3. **Writing "SRAM is faster" without numbers** — always quote access times (1–10 ns vs 50–100 ns) → 1 mark
> 4. **Skipping the pre-charge step** in DRAM/SRAM operation explanation → 1 mark
> 5. **Not mentioning destructive read** in DRAM — this is THE key reason DRAM is slower → 1 mark
> 6. **Using wrong unit prefixes** in numerical problems (fF vs pF vs nF) — silent unit error → 2 marks
> 7. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** — at least a labeled block diagram is mandatory for full marks

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Volatile memory = requires power to retain data.** SRAM and DRAM are the two pillars.
- **SRAM cell = 6 transistors (6T)**, **DRAM cell = 1 transistor + 1 capacitor (1T1C)**.
- **SRAM is static** (bistable latch reinforces itself) → **no refresh**.
- **DRAM is dynamic** (capacitor leaks) → **refresh every ~64 ms** (JEDEC).
- **DRAM read is destructive** → requires immediate write-back.
- **Sense-amp differential:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{C_s}{2(C_s + C_{BL})} \cdot V_{DD}$ — typically only **~50 mV** in modern cells.
- **Refresh power:** $P_{refresh} = \dfrac{N_{rows} \cdot C_{BL} \cdot V_{DD}^2}{2 \cdot t_{ref}}$
- **Retention time:** $t_{ref} = R_{leak} \cdot C_s \cdot \ln(V_0 / V_{min})$
- **SRAM is ~10× faster** (1–10 ns vs 50–100 ns) but **~6× less dense** and more expensive per bit.
- **SRAM dominates low-power IoT MCUs** (STM32, ESP32, nRF52 have on-chip SRAM only).
- **DRAM dominates edge gateways** running Linux (Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson).
- **Write endurance:** SRAM/DRAM ≈ unlimited; Flash ≈ 10⁵ cycles (no contest).
- **Pre-charge to $V_{DD}/2$** is standard for both BL and $\overline{BL}$ in differential sensing.
- **Access transistors (M5, M6 in SRAM) must be stronger than pull-down NMOS** (the "pull-up ratio" and "cell ratio" stability criteria) for reliable read — this is called the **read stability / write-ability trade-off**.
- **In IoT duty-cycled systems**, SRAM's zero refresh overhead saves energy → preferred for sleep-mode architectures.
- **Pseudo-SRAM (PSRAM)** combines DRAM density with SRAM-like interface — common in ESP32 external memory.
- **Cache hierarchy in IoT SoCs:** Register file → L1 SRAM → TCM SRAM → optional external DRAM.
- **Standard access times to memorize:** SRAM = **1–10 ns**, DRAM = **50–100 ns**, Flash = **25–50 μs (read) / 200 μs–1 ms (write)**.
- **Word Line (WL)** selects the row; **Bit Line (BL/$\overline{BL}$)** carries the column data; **Sense Amplifier** converts the tiny analog differential into a full digital level.
- **DRAM charge decays exponentially** with time constant $\tau = R_{leak} \cdot C_s$.
- **Refresh consumes ~1–3% of total DRAM power** in typical IoT gateways — small but always present.
- **Emerging alternatives:** MRAM, ReRAM, and FeRAM are being researched as **universal memory** that combines SRAM speed, DRAM density, and Flash non-volatility — but are not yet mainstream in 2024 IoT hardware.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Power Move:** When asked to "compare," always use a **labeled table with at least 6 parameters** and add **one concluding sentence** explaining the IoT-specific design choice. Examiners reward structured answers and IoT context awareness.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)** is a class of computer memory that retains its stored information even when the power supply is removed, disconnected, or interrupted. In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, NVM serves as the persistent storage backbone for embedded edge devices, enabling firmware persistence, sensor data logging, configuration retention, secure key storage, and **Over-The-Air (OTA)** update staging.

Mathematically, the persistence property of an NVM cell can be expressed through its **retention time** $T_{ret}$ and **endurance** $E_{cyc}$:

$$T_{ret} \gg T_{power\text{-}off}, \quad E_{cyc} \geq 10^3 \text{ to } 10^{10} \text{ cycles}$$

where $T_{ret}$ is the duration for which the memory cell reliably holds its data without power, and $E_{cyc}$ is the number of program/erase (P/E) cycles the cell can tolerate before failure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stone Tablet vs. The Whiteboard"

Imagine two types of writing surfaces in a classroom:

| Analogy Component | Real-World Mapping | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Whiteboard (volatile)** | Writing disappears when lights are switched off | **SRAM / DRAM** — data vanishes on power loss |
| **Stone tablet (non-volatile)** | Inscription remains for thousands of years | **Flash / FRAM / EEPROM** — data persists without power |
| **Chalk that wears out** | Tablet degrades after many carvings | **Endurance limit** — finite P/E cycles |
| **Engraving speed** | Time taken to carve vs. time to write | **Write latency trade-off** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The 2024 scheme explicitly lists *Non-Volatile Memory technologies and their role in IoT edge devices* as a high-weightage topic. Examiners frequently test the differentiation between **NOR** and **NAND** Flash, and the engineering trade-offs between **speed, endurance, power, and cost**.

## 1.3 Why Non-Volatile Memory is Indispensable in IoT

IoT devices — from smart thermostats and wearables to industrial sensor nodes — operate in environments where:

- **Power is intermittent** (battery-operated, energy-harvested)
- **Firmware must persist** through reboots and brown-outs
- **Critical telemetry** must survive sudden power loss
- **Security keys** must never be erased accidentally
- **OTA updates** must be staged safely before atomic commit

> [!TIP]
> A practical KTU-style question framing: *"Justify why an IoT sensor node deployed in a remote agricultural field cannot rely solely on SRAM for storing its calibration parameters."* — The answer hinges on the **non-volatility** requirement and **retention** specification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Non-Volatile Memory Technologies

NVM technologies are classified based on their **physical storage mechanism**, **erasability**, and **cell density**:

### 2.1.1 Classical (Charge-Based) NVM

- **Mask ROM (MROM):** Hard-wired during IC fabrication. Lowest cost per bit at scale, but **zero reprogrammability**.
- **PROM (Programmable ROM):** User-programmable **once** using a fuse/antifuse structure.
- **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** Erased via **ultraviolet (UV) light** through a quartz window; reprogrammable, but cumbersome.
- **EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM):** **Byte-level** electrical erase and rewrite. Used for storing small configuration data in microcontrollers.
- **Flash Memory:** **Block-level** erase, electrically programmable. The dominant NVM technology in modern IoT. Two sub-families: **NOR** and **NAND**.

### 2.1.2 Emerging (Resistance/Spin/Phase-Based) NVM

- **FRAM (Ferroelectric RAM):** Uses a **ferroelectric capacitor** to store data via polarization state. Combines **SRAM-like speed** with **non-volatility**.
- **MRAM (Magnetoresistive RAM):** Stores bits as **magnetic tunnel junction (MTJ)** orientations. Near-infinite endurance ($\geq 10^{15}$ cycles).
- **PCM / PRAM (Phase-Change Memory):** Uses **chalcogenide glass** that switches between amorphous (high-resistance) and crystalline (low-resistance) states via Joule heating.
- **RRAM / ReRAM (Resistive RAM):** Forms/dissolves **conductive filaments** in a dielectric (e.g., HfO₂). Excellent for **in-memory computing** and neuromorphic IoT.

## 2.2 The Two Pillars of Flash Memory: NOR vs. NAND

This is the **single most important comparison** for KTU Module 4 questions.

### 2.2.1 NOR Flash

- **Cell connection:** Each cell's drain is connected to a **bit-line**, source to ground, control gate to word-line — the parallel structure resembles a **NOR logic gate**.
- **Random access:** Each byte can be read independently.
- **XIP (Execute-In-Place):** Code can run directly from NOR Flash without copying to RAM.
- **Endurance:** Typically **10,000 to 100,000 P/E cycles**.
- **Use in IoT:** **Bootloader, firmware, BIOS** storage in edge MCUs (e.g., STM32 external Flash, ESP32 co-resident SPI-NOR).

### 2.2.2 NAND Flash

- **Cell connection:** Cells are connected in **series** like a NAND gate; multiple transistors share a bit-line.
- **Page/Block architecture:** Reads and writes occur in **pages** (typically 2 KB–16 KB); erases occur in **blocks** (typically 128 KB–4 MB).
- **Sequential access:** Faster bulk throughput but **no random byte access**.
- **Higher density & lower cost per bit.**
- **Endurance:** **SLC: 100,000 cycles, MLC: 10,000, TLC: 1,000–3,000, QLC: ~1,000**.
- **Use in IoT:** Mass data storage — SD cards, eMMC, raw NAND in gateways, surveillance cameras, smart speakers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination-critical**. KTU 2024 scheme papers often include numerical problems based on these formulas.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Memory Capacity (raw)** | $C = N \cdot W$ | bits | $N$ = number of cells, $W$ = bits per cell |
| 2 | **Bits per cell by type** | $W_{SLC}=1,\ W_{MLC}=2,\ W_{TLC}=3,\ W_{QLC}=4$ | bits/cell | Higher $W$ = higher density, lower endurance |
| 3 | **Density scaling** | $C_{total} = N \cdot W \cdot \text{efficiency factor}$ | bits | Accounts for ECC, spare area, bad blocks |
| 4 | **Effective write throughput** | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{P_{size}}{t_{prog} + t_{erase}/B_{pgb}}$ | bytes/s | $P_{size}$ = page size, $B_{pgb}$ = pages per block |
| 5 | **Endurance lifetime** | $L_{years} = \dfrac{E_{cyc} \cdot C}{W_{daily} \cdot 365}$ | years | $W_{daily}$ = daily write volume, $C$ = capacity |
| 6 | **Wear-leveling efficiency** | $\eta_{wl} = 1 - \dfrac{\sigma_{PE}}{E_{cyc}}$ | dimensionless | $\sigma_{PE}$ = std. deviation of P/E cycles across blocks |
| 7 | **Bit Error Rate target** | $BER \leq 10^{-15}$ (enterprise), $10^{-12}$ (IoT) | errors/bit | Determines ECC strength (e.g., BCH, LDPC) |
| 8 | **Retention time (Arrhenius)** | $t_{ret}(T) = t_0 \cdot e^{\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | seconds | Higher temperature $\Rightarrow$ shorter retention |
| 9 | **Energy per bit write** | $E_{bit} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t_{prog}}{W \cdot N}$ | joules/bit | Critical for energy-harvested IoT nodes |
| 10 | **Over-provisioning factor** | $OP = \dfrac{C_{raw} - C_{user}}{C_{user}}$ | ratio | Typical SSD: $OP \approx 0.07$ to $0.28$ |

**Boundary Conditions & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-expected values):**

- Operating temperature for industrial IoT: $-40^{\circ}\text{C} \leq T \leq 85^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Typical **NOR Flash** page program time: $t_{prog} \approx 10$–$100\ \mu s$ per word
- Typical **NAND Flash** page program time: $t_{prog} \approx 200$–$500\ \mu s$ per page
- Block erase time (NAND): $t_{erase} \approx 1$–$2$ ms
- Standard NAND page size: $2\text{ KB},\ 4\text{ KB},\ 8\text{ KB},\ 16\text{ KB}$
- Standard NAND block size: typically **128 or 256 pages**

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering

| IoT Application | Preferred NVM | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart card / RFID tag** | MROM or OTP EEPROM | Security, low cost |
| **Wearable fitness band** | NOR Flash + FRAM | Quick writes, low power |
| **Automotive ECU (CAN node)** | NOR + EEPROM | Reliable firmware, calibration storage |
| **Smart agricultural sensor** | SPI-NOR Flash | Energy-efficient, XIP support |
| **AI edge inference (TinyML)** | MRAM or FRAM | High endurance for model updates |
| **Surveillance camera** | NAND (eMMC/UFS) | High-density video buffer |
| **Industrial gateway** | SSD (NAND-based) | Mass telemetry archival |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Principle (the "Memory Trilemma"):** No single NVM technology simultaneously optimizes for **speed**, **endurance**, and **density**. Flash maximizes density; FRAM maximizes endurance; MRAM maximizes speed. IoT system architects must select based on the application's **write-amplification profile**.

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Lifetime Estimation for an IoT Sensor Node

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark analytical problem**. The model solution is provided below in full.

### **Problem Statement**

An IoT environmental sensor node uses a **NOR Flash** of capacity $C = 4$ MB with an endurance rating of $E_{cyc} = 100{,}000$ P/E cycles per block. Each block is $B = 64$ KB. The sensor writes $W_{daily} = 512$ KB of telemetry data per day, distributed uniformly across all blocks. The retention requirement is $T_{ret} = 10$ years. Determine:

**(a)** The total number of blocks $N_{blocks}$.
**(b)** The average number of P/E cycles per block per day.
**(c)** The expected lifetime in years.
**(d)** Whether the lifetime meets the 10-year retention requirement.

---

### **Step 1: Compute the total number of blocks**

$$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{C}{B} = \dfrac{4 \text{ MB}}{64 \text{ KB}} = \dfrac{4 \times 1024 \text{ KB}}{64 \text{ KB}} = 64 \text{ blocks}$$

**Explanation:** Each block of 64 KB is the atomic erase unit. The total capacity divided by block size gives the number of independently erasable units.

---

### **Step 2: Compute the daily P/E cycles per block (uniform distribution)**

Total daily data written $= 512$ KB, distributed uniformly over 64 blocks:

$$\Delta_{daily} = \dfrac{W_{daily}}{N_{blocks}} = \dfrac{512 \text{ KB}}{64} = 8 \text{ KB per block per day}$$

Converting KB to block-fractions (each block = 64 KB):

$$c_{daily} = \dfrac{8 \text{ KB}}{64 \text{ KB/block}} = 0.125 \text{ block-equivalent writes per day}$$

**Explanation:** Each block experiences 0.125 of a full P/E cycle per day on average.

---

### **Step 3: Compute the expected lifetime in years**

Each block can endure $E_{cyc} = 100{,}000$ cycles. The number of days until a block fails:

$$D_{fail} = \dfrac{E_{cyc}}{c_{daily}} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{0.125} = 800{,}000 \text{ days}$$

Converting to years:

$$L_{years} = \dfrac{800{,}000}{365} \approx 2191.78 \text{ years}$$

**Explanation:** Under ideal uniform wear, the lifetime is exceptionally long because the daily write volume is small compared to total capacity.

---

### **Step 4: Verify against retention requirement**

$$L_{years} \approx 2192 \text{ years} \gg 10 \text{ years (required)}$$

**Conclusion:** The system **comfortably meets** the 10-year retention requirement with a **safety margin of $\approx 219\times$**.

---

### **Step 5: Worst-case scenario (single hot block)**

In practice, wear-leveling is imperfect. If a **single block** is rewritten every day:

$$L_{worst} = \dfrac{E_{cyc}}{365} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{365} \approx 273.97 \text{ years}$$

Even in this pathological case, the lifetime still exceeds 10 years for this workload.

---

### **Step 6: Worst-case (1 KB per second hot block)**

If a firmware-update hot block receives $W_{hot} = 1$ KB/s continuously:

$$c_{hot} = \dfrac{1 \text{ KB/s} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s/day}}{64 \text{ KB}} = 1350 \text{ cycles/day}$$

$$L_{hot} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{1350 \times 365} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{492{,}750} \approx 0.203 \text{ years} \approx 74 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** Without wear-leveling, hot blocks can fail in **months**. This is why **dynamic + static wear-leveling** algorithms are mandatory in IoT firmware.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark analytical problem**. Always show units, write the conclusion in a sentence, and **explicitly mention the wear-leveling requirement** in step 6 to earn the full marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NOR vs. NAND vs. FRAM vs. MRAM

| Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash (SLC) | FRAM | MRAM (STT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bits/cell** | 1 | 1 (SLC) | 1 | 1 |
| **Cell size** | Large | Small (~40% of NOR) | Medium | Small |
| **Read speed** | **Fast (random)** | Moderate (sequential) | **Very fast** | **Fastest** |
| **Write speed** | Slow ($\sim 10\ \mu s$/word) | Fast (page-level) | **Fast ($\sim 100$ ns)** | Fast ($\sim 10$ ns) |
| **Endurance (cycles)** | $10^4$–$10^5$ | $10^5$ (SLC) | **$10^{10}$–$10^{14}$** | **$10^{15}$** |
| **Retention** | 10–20 yrs | 10 yrs | 10+ yrs | 10+ yrs |
| **Power (write)** | High | Medium | **Low** | **Very low** |
| **Cost/bit** | High | **Lowest** | High | Medium |
| **XIP support** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Best IoT use** | Firmware/boot | Mass storage | Fast config logs | AI edge models |

## 4.3 Wear-Leveling Algorithm Flow (Dynamic Wear Leveling)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Host issues WRITE request"] --> B{"Free block available?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Write data to free block"]
    B -->|No| D["Select block with LOWEST erase count"]
    D --> E["Erase target block"]
    E --> F["Program new data into erased block"]
    C --> G["Update erase count table in RAM"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Any block erase count exceeds threshold?"}
    H -->|Yes| I["Trigger STATIC wear leveling: swap hot block with cold block"]
    H -->|No| J["Return to idle"]
    I --> J

    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef alert fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px

    class B,H decision
    class A,C,D,E,F,G,J process
    class I alert
```

> **Figure 4.3:** Dynamic + Static wear-leveling flow. This is the algorithm that extends NVM lifetime in production IoT firmware. The static-wear-leveling branch is critical for preventing hot-block failures.

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Volatile and Non-Volatile Memory. Give two examples of each and state one IoT application that mandates the use of NVM.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Volatile Memory | Non-Volatile Memory |
|---|---|---|
| **Power dependency** | Loses data on power-off | Retains data without power |
| **Speed** | Faster (SRAM: ns) | Slower (Flash: μs) |
| **Examples** | SRAM, DRAM | NOR Flash, NAND Flash, FRAM, EEPROM |
| **Typical use in IoT** | Runtime stack, buffers | Firmware, logs, config, keys |

**IoT application mandating NVM:** A **smart energy meter** must retain cumulative energy readings during a power outage — NVM is mandatory.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is wear-leveling? Why is it essential in NAND Flash-based IoT storage?**

**Model Answer:**

**Wear-leveling** is a firmware technique that **distributes program/erase cycles uniformly** across all Flash memory blocks to prevent premature failure of any single block.

It is essential because:
1. NAND Flash has **finite endurance** (SLC: ~10⁵, TLC: ~10³ cycles).
2. IoT applications often have **hot data** (e.g., system logs, OTA staging) that over-writes a small set of blocks.
3. Without wear-leveling, hot blocks fail first, leading to **silent data corruption** and device bricking.

Wear-leveling is of two types: **Dynamic** (re-maps new writes to least-used free blocks) and **Static** (rotates cold/rarely-written blocks to spread wear).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of **NOR** and **NAND** Flash memory with neat diagrams. Compare them across **seven** distinct parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wearable health-monitoring IoT device uses a **TLC NAND Flash** of capacity **16 GB** with endurance of **1,000 P/E cycles per block**. Each block is **4 MB**. The device writes **2 MB of sensor data per day**, uniformly distributed. The retention requirement is **5 years**. Calculate:
   - (i) The total number of blocks.
   - (ii) The P/E cycles per block per day.
   - (iii) The expected lifetime in years.
   - (iv) Whether the design meets the 5-year requirement. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **(a) NOR vs. NAND Architecture & Comparison (7 marks)**

**NOR Flash Architecture:**
In NOR Flash, each memory cell (a floating-gate transistor) has its **drain connected to a dedicated bit-line**, the **source to ground**, and the **control gate to a word-line**. This parallel topology (analogous to a NOR logic gate) allows **random byte-level read access** and **Execute-In-Place (XIP)** — the CPU can fetch instructions directly from Flash without first copying to RAM.

**NAND Flash Architecture:**
In NAND Flash, multiple cells (typically 32 or 64) are **series-chained** between the bit-line and the source-line, resembling a NAND logic gate. This reduces per-bit area significantly. However, reads and writes occur in **pages** (2–16 KB) and erases in **blocks** (128 KB–4 MB). Random byte access is not possible.

**Seven-Parameter Comparison:**

| # | Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cell connection | Parallel to bit-line | Series chain |
| 2 | Random access | ✅ Yes (byte-level) | ❌ No (page/block) |
| 3 | Read speed | Fast (~50 ns) | Moderate (serial ~25 μs initial) |
| 4 | Write speed | Slow (~10 μs/word) | Fast (page-level ~200 μs) |
| 5 | Erase speed | Slow (~1 s/block) | Fast (~1–2 ms/block) |
| 6 | Density & cost/bit | Low density, high cost | High density, low cost |
| 7 | Endurance | 10⁴–10⁵ cycles | 10⁵ (SLC) to 10³ (TLC) |

**Conclusion:** NOR is preferred for **firmware/XIP**; NAND is preferred for **mass data storage** in IoT devices.

---

#### **(b) Lifetime Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $C = 16$ GB, $B = 4$ MB per block, $E_{cyc} = 1{,}000$ cycles/block
- $W_{daily} = 2$ MB/day (uniform distribution)
- Required $T_{req} = 5$ years

**Step (i): Total number of blocks**

$$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{16 \text{ GB}}{4 \text{ MB}} = \dfrac{16 \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{4 \text{ MB}} = 4096 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step (ii): P/E cycles per block per day**

$$\Delta_{daily} = \dfrac{2 \text{ MB/day}}{4096 \text{ blocks}} = \dfrac{2}{4096} \text{ MB/block/day} = 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ MB/block/day}$$

$$\text{In block-equivalents: } c_{daily} = \dfrac{4.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ MB}}{4 \text{ MB/block}} = 1.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cycles/block/day}$$

**Step (iii): Expected lifetime**

$$D_{fail} = \dfrac{E_{cyc}}{c_{daily}} = \dfrac{1000}{1.22 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 8.197 \times 10^{6} \text{ days}$$

$$L_{years} = \dfrac{8.197 \times 10^{6}}{365} \approx 22{,}460 \text{ years}$$

**Step (iv): Comparison with requirement**

$$L_{years} \approx 22{,}460 \text{ years} \gg 5 \text{ years (required)}$$

**Conclusion:** The design **exceeds** the 5-year requirement by a margin of ~4,492×. However, the engineer must still implement **wear-leveling** to handle hot blocks (e.g., filesystem metadata, OTA staging area), which can reduce effective lifetime by orders of magnitude.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Non-Volatile Memory** in IoT edge devices. List **any five** specific use cases and explain the engineering rationale for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following NVM technologies in 4–5 lines each: **(i) FRAM, (ii) MRAM (STT), (iii) PCM/PRAM, (iv) RRAM/ReRAM**. Then, in a single comparative table, evaluate them on **speed, endurance, density, and IoT suitability**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **(a) Role of NVM in IoT Edge Devices (7 marks)**

IoT edge devices operate under constraints of **intermittent power, limited energy budgets, harsh environments, and security threats**. NVM is the enabling technology that makes persistent operation feasible.

**Five specific use cases:**

1. **Firmware and Bootloader Storage:** The bootloader must survive power-cycles to bring the device up. **NOR Flash** is preferred because it supports **Execute-In-Place (XIP)**.

2. **Sensor Data Black-Box Logging:** Industrial vibration sensors store the last N seconds of data even after sudden power loss (e.g., motor trip events). **FRAM** is preferred for its high write endurance.

3. **Secure Key and Certificate Storage:** TLS certificates and AES keys must never be erased on power loss. **OTP/EEPROM** or **secure NVM enclaves** (e.g., STM32 Option Bytes) are used.

4. **OTA Update Staging:** Firmware updates are downloaded to NVM, verified via signature, then **atomically committed** to the active slot — requiring dual-bank NVM architecture.

5. **Configuration and Calibration Storage:** Device-specific calibration values (e.g., RTD sensor linearization coefficients) are stored in **EEPROM or Flash** and read at every boot.

**Engineering rationale:** Without NVM, every IoT device would require continuous power to retain its identity, configuration, and state — which is infeasible for battery and energy-harvested deployments.

---

#### **(b) Emerging NVM Technologies (7 marks)**

**(i) FRAM (Ferroelectric RAM):**
Stores data as the **polarization state of a ferroelectric capacitor** (typically PZT). The polarization persists without power, giving non-volatility. Read is destructive but fast; write is low-energy.

**(ii) MRAM — Spin-Transfer-Torque (STT-MRAM):**
Uses a **magnetic tunnel junction (MTJ)** with a fixed layer and a free layer. Data is stored as the relative magnetic orientation (parallel = 0, anti-parallel = 1). Switching is achieved by passing a spin-polarized current. Near-SRAM speed with non-volatility.

**(iii) PCM / PRAM (Phase-Change Memory):**
Uses a **chalcogenide alloy** (e.g., GST — Ge₂Sb₂Te₅) that switches between **amorphous** (high-resistance, logic 0) and **crystalline** (low-resistance, logic 1) phases via Joule heating from a current pulse.

**(iv) RRAM / ReRAM (Resistive RAM):**
Stores data as the **resistance state of a metal-oxide dielectric** (e.g., HfO₂, Ta₂O₅) sandwiched between two electrodes. Conducting filaments are formed or ruptured under applied voltage, yielding two or more resistance levels.

**Comparative Evaluation Table:**

| Technology | Read Speed | Write Speed | Endurance (cycles) | Density | IoT Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FRAM** | ~100 ns | ~100 ns | $10^{10}$–$10^{14}$ | Medium | Excellent for low-power, high-write loggers |
| **STT-MRAM** | ~10 ns | ~10 ns | $10^{15}$ | Medium-high | Excellent for AI edge / instant-on IoT |
| **PCM/PRAM** | ~50 ns | ~100 ns | $10^{8}$–$10^{9}$ | High | Good for archival + neuromorphic |
| **RRAM/ReRAM** | ~10 ns | ~100 ns | $10^{6}$–$10^{10}$ | **Very high** (3D stackable) | Excellent for in-memory compute |

**Conclusion:** Emerging NVMs do not replace Flash in IoT **today**; they are deployed in **niche** roles where Flash's endurance or speed is the bottleneck — particularly in **AI at the edge** and **energy-harvested** sensor nodes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing endurance and retention:** Endurance = P/E cycles; Retention = time data is held. Mixing these in definitions costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to write units in numerical problems:** A lifetime answer of "22460" without "years" is incomplete.
> 3. **Claiming NOR Flash has higher density than NAND:** This is the **opposite** of reality. NOR is for code; NAND is for bulk data.
> 4. **Ignoring wear-leveling in long-term lifetime estimates:** The examiner expects a sentence on **dynamic + static wear-leveling** for full marks on lifetime problems.
> 5. **Mixing up SLC/MLC/TLC endurance values:** Memorize: **SLC = 10⁵, MLC = 10⁴, TLC = 10³, QLC = 10³ (lower)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before KTU ESE):**

- ✅ **NVM** retains data without power; **VBM (SRAM/DRAM)** does not.
- ✅ **ROM family order (oldest → newest, more flexible):** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → **Flash**.
- ✅ **Flash has two architectures:** **NOR** (random access, XIP, low density, code storage) and **NAND** (sequential/page access, high density, data storage).
- ✅ **Cell density ranking (lowest → highest bits/cell):** SLC (1) → MLC (2) → TLC (3) → QLC (4).
- ✅ **Endurance ranking (highest → lowest cycles):** MRAM ($10^{15}$) > FRAM ($10^{14}$) > SLC NAND ($10^5$) > MLC ($10^4$) > TLC ($10^3$).
- ✅ **Retention time formula** follows the **Arrhenius equation** — temperature accelerates charge loss.
- ✅ **Wear-leveling** has two flavors: **Dynamic** (new writes) and **Static** (cold-block rotation) — both are required for production firmware.
- ✅ **IoT use cases for NVM:** firmware, OTA staging, sensor logs, security keys, configuration, calibration, device identity.
- ✅ **Energy per bit write** ($E_{bit}$) is the most important metric for **energy-harvested** IoT nodes.
- ✅ **ECC (BCH, LDPC)** is mandatory for NAND to meet the BER $\leq 10^{-12}$ target.
- ✅ **Memory hierarchy** in IoT: Registers → Cache → SRAM → NOR Flash → NAND/eMMC → Cloud.
- ✅ **NOR Flash** is used for **bootloaders and XIP**; **NAND** for **bulk telemetry and media**.
- ✅ **Emerging NVMs** (FRAM, MRAM, PCM, RRAM) trade cost for endurance/speed — used in **AI edge** and **ultra-low-power** nodes.
- ✅ **Killer formula for numericals:** $L_{years} = \dfrac{E_{cyc} \cdot N_{blocks}}{W_{daily} \cdot 365}$ (uniform wear case).
- ✅ **Standard NOR/NAND parameters** to remember: page = 2–16 KB, block = 128 KB–4 MB, erase time = 1–2 ms, program time = 200–500 μs.
- ✅ **Industrial temperature range** for IoT NVM: **$-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+85^{\circ}\text{C}$**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded (MTP/OTP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Embedded (MTP/OTP) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** and embedded system design, **Embedded Memory** refers to non-volatile memory (NVM) technologies that are integrated directly onto the same silicon die as the microcontroller/processor or packaged within the same module. The two principal classifications relevant to KTU Module 4 are: > [!NOTE] > **OTP (One-Time Programmable) Memory**: A non-volatile memory that can be written **exactly once** during the manufacturing or provisioning stage. Once a bit is programmed, it is permanently fixed and cannot be altered by any electrical means. Examples include **PROM (Programmable Read-Only Memory)**, fuse/anti-fuse based cells, and mask-ROM variants. > [!NOTE] > **MTP (Multi-Time Programmable) Memory**: A non-volatile memory that supports a **finite but repeated number** of program/erase (P/E) cycles, typically ranging from **1,000 to 1,00,000 cycles**. The dominant MTP technologies are **EPROM, EEPROM, and Flash memory**. These memories are the bedrock of **firmware storage, secure key provisioning, calibration data retention, and over-the-air (OTA) update capability** in IoT endpoints. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are filling out a form for a government office: | Memory Type | Real-World Analogy | Implication | |-------------|-------------------|-------------| | **OTP** | A form written with a **permanent marker pen** – once you sign, you cannot erase or change it. | Used for **immutable data** like device ID, factory-set calibration. | | **MTP (Flash/EEPROM)** | A form written with a **pencil on carbon paper** – you can erase and rewrite, but only a **limited number of times** before the paper wears out. | Used for **firmware updates** and **user-configurable data**. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it matters in IoT**: IoT devices often operate unattended for **10–15 years** in remote locations. Choosing OTP vs MTP directly impacts the **device's updateability, security posture, BOM cost, and long-term reliability**. A smart meter deployed in the field cannot be recalled for firmware bugs – it must be OTP-grade reliable or MTP-updatable over the network. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard parameters are used to characterise MTP/OTP memories in the KTU syllabus context: - **Endurance (N_end)**: Number of program/erase cycles — typically **10³ to 10⁵** for MTP, **1** for OTP. - **Data Retention (T_ret)**: Duration for which data remains valid without power — typically **10 to 20 years** at **85 °C**. - **Cell Size (A_cell)**: Silicon area per bit — OTP ≈ **1T (one transistor)**, EEPROM ≈ **2T (two transistors)**. - **Programming Voltage (V_pp)**: Typically **5 V, 12 V, or 13.5 V** depending on technology. - **Access Time (t_acc)**: Typically **25 ns to 250 ns** for embedded NVM. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 OTP Memory – Operational Analysis OTP memories are built on two primary physical mechanisms: ### 2.1.1 Fuse-Based OTP - A polysilicon or metal link is **blown** (open-circuited) by passing a high current pulse (~**10–20 mA**). - Cell area is small, but programming requires **high current** transistors. - **Logic state**: Blown fuse = `1` (or `0`, depending on convention). ### 2.1.2 Anti-Fuse-Based OTP - A thin dielectric (oxide/nitride) is **ruptured** by applying a high programming voltage (V_pp ≈ **6–13.5 V**), creating a permanent conductive path. - Lower ON-resistance, more reliable, used in **secure elements and PLDs (e.g., Actel/Microsemi antifuse FPGAs)**. - **Logic state**: Programmed anti-fuse = `0` (conducting). ### 2.1.3 Operational Logic Steps 1. **Step 1 — Manufacturing**: All cells are in their default virgin state (typically logic `1`). 2. **Step 2 — Provisioning**: A high-voltage pulse is applied through on-chip charge pumps to selected cells. 3. **Step 3 — Verification**: A read-back operation confirms successful programming (margin check). 4. **Step 4 — Lockout**: A fuse or lock bit disables the high-voltage circuitry, making the memory permanently read-only. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point**: Anti-fuse OTP is **radiation-hardened** and hence used in **aerospace and military IoT** applications. It is also intrinsically **secure** because the programmed state cannot be reverse-engineered easily. --- ## 2.2 MTP Memory – Operational Analysis MTP memories retain their bit through **charge storage on a floating gate** (the foundational principle of all modern NVM). ### 2.2.1 Sub-classification | Technology | Erase Mechanism | Granularity | Endurance | Typical Use | |------------|-----------------|-------------|-----------|-------------| | **EPROM** | UV light (≈ 20 min, 254 nm) | Whole chip | ~100 cycles | Legacy MCUs | | **EEPROM** | Electrical (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling) | Byte-level | ~10⁶ cycles | Parameter storage | | **Flash (NOR)** | Electrical (FN tunneling / hot-carrier injection) | Block/Sector | ~10⁴–10⁵ cycles | Firmware storage | | **Flash (NAND)** | Electrical (FN tunneling) | Page/Block | ~10³–10⁴ cycles | Data storage | ### 2.2.2 Operational Logic – Flash Memory (NOR type, dominant in IoT MCUs) 1. **Program (Write)**: Hot-carrier injection (HCI) or FN tunneling forces electrons onto the **floating gate**, raising the cell's threshold voltage (V_th). 2. **Erase**: FN tunneling pulls electrons off the floating gate (reverse bias on the control gate), lowering V_th. 3. **Read**: A reference voltage V_ref is applied to the control gate. If V_th > V_ref, the cell reads as `0`; otherwise, `1`. 4. **Wear-out**: Each P/E cycle degrades the tunnel oxide; cumulative damage limits **endurance**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **KTU-board-relevant** for embedded memory design problems. | # | Parameter / Formula | Description | Units | |---|--------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $C_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{words}} \times W_{\text{word}}$ | Total memory capacity (N_words = number of addressable words, W_word = bits per word) | bits | | 2 | $A_{\text{die}} = C_{\text{total}} \times A_{\text{cell}}$ | Silicon area occupied by memory array (A_cell = cell area) | $\mu m^2$ | | 3 | $N_{\text{cycles,life}} = \min(N_{\text{end}}, T_{\text{life}} \times f_{\text{writes}})$ | Effective write budget over device lifetime (f_writes = writes/year) | cycles | | 4 | $t_{\text{prog,total}} = N_{\text{bits}} \times t_{\text{prog,bit}}$ | Total programming time for full chip (t_prog,bit ≈ 1–100 $\mu s$ for flash) | seconds | | 5 | $P_{\text{write}} = V_{\text{pp}} \times I_{\text{prog}} \times t_{\text{prog,bit}}$ | Energy per bit write (E_bit = P × t) | Joules | | 6 | $T_{\text{ret,eff}} = T_{\text{ret,spec}} \times 2^{\left( \frac{T_{\text{spec}} - T_{\text{op}}}{k_T} \right)}$ | Arrhenius-style retention derating with temperature (k_T ≈ 10–15 °C/doubling) | years | | 7 | $MTTF_{\text{mem}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{NVM}}}$ | Mean-time-to-failure for NVM (used in IoT reliability calculations) | hours | | 8 | $\text{Code-density ratio} = \frac{A_{\text{OTP,cell}}}{A_{\text{MTP,cell}}} \approx \frac{1T}{2T} = 0.5$ | Cost/area advantage of OTP over EEPROM-style MTP | dimensionless | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Card / SIM / ePassport**: Use **anti-fuse OTP** for storing immutable cryptographic keys (AES-256 keys, IMSI). - **ESP32 / STM32 IoT MCUs**: Use **NOR Flash (MTP)** for storing both bootloader and user application firmware. - **BLE Beacons / RFID tags**: Use **OTP PROM** for the unique TX-power calibration value and device serial number. - **OTA-Enabled IoT Gateways**: Combine **OTP (for bootloader + root-of-trust keys)** with **MTP Flash (for OS + application)**, following the **A/B partition scheme**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix – OTP vs MTP Trade-off The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **engineering trade-off analysis** between OTP and MTP. The following analytical matrix formalises the decision logic: $$ \text{Score}_{\text{memory}} = w_1 \cdot \frac{1}{C_{\text{bit}}} + w_2 \cdot N_{\text{end}} + w_3 \cdot T_{\text{ret}} - w_4 \cdot A_{\text{cell}} - w_5 \cdot P_{\text{write}} $$ Where $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5$ are application-specific weighting factors chosen by the designer. | Decision Driver | Favours OTP | Favours MTP | |-----------------|-------------|-------------| | **Cost-sensitive, no firmware updates** | ✅ | ❌ | | **OTA / Over-the-air updates required** | ❌ | ✅ | | **Cryptographic key immutability** | ✅ | ❌ | | **Field-recoverable from bugs** | ❌ | ✅ | | **Lowest silicon area per bit** | ✅ | ❌ | | **High write-cycle logging** | ❌ | ✅ | | **Radition-hard / aerospace** | ✅ (anti-fuse) | ❌ | --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivations ### Derivation 1: Memory Endurance Budget over IoT Device Lifetime > **Problem Statement**: An IoT sensor logs data to an internal EEPROM every 10 minutes. If the EEPROM has an endurance of $N_{\text{end}} = 10^6$ P/E cycles, and the device must operate for $T_{\text{life}} = 10$ years, determine whether the write budget is sufficient. **Step 1 — Calculate the number of write events per year**: $$ f_{\text{writes}} = \frac{365 \times 24 \times 60 \text{ min}}{10 \text{ min/cycle}} = 525{,}960 \text{ writes/year} $$ **Step 2 — Total writes over device lifetime**: $$ N_{\text{writes,total}} = f_{\text{writes}} \times T_{\text{life}} = 525{,}960 \times 10 = 5.26 \times 10^{6} \text{ writes} $$ **Step 3 — Compare with endurance**: $$ N_{\text{cycles,life}} = \min(N_{\text{end}}, N_{\text{writes,total}}) = \min(10^6, 5.26 \times 10^6) = 10^6 $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion**: The EEPROM is **undersized** by a factor of 5.26×. The designer must either (a) move logging to a **wear-levelled Flash partition**, (b) use a **larger EEPROM**, or (c) **buffer writes in RAM** and flush periodically. --- ### Derivation 2: Retention Derating with Temperature > **Problem Statement**: A NOR Flash memory is rated for $T_{\text{ret,spec}} = 20$ years at $T_{\text{spec}} = 25 \degree C$. Compute the effective retention at $T_{\text{op}} = 85 \degree C$, given thermal acceleration $k_T = 10 \degree C$ per decade of retention. **Step 1 — Compute the temperature differential**: $$ \Delta T = T_{\text{op}} - T_{\text{spec}} = 85 - 25 = 60 \degree C $$ **Step 2 — Number of decades of acceleration**: $$ n_{\text{decades}} = \frac{\Delta T}{k_T} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the exponential derating**: $$ T_{\text{ret,eff}} = T_{\text{ret,spec}} \times 2^{-n_{\text{decades}}} = 20 \times 2^{-6} = \frac{20}{64} \approx 0.3125 \text{ years} $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion**: At 85 °C, the data is reliably retained for only **~3.7 months**! This is why **industrial-grade IoT devices** deployed in hot environments must use **high-temperature-rated NVM** or implement **periodic refresh algorithms**. --- ### Derivation 3: Programming Time and Energy Budget for OTP Blowing > **Problem Statement**: An OTP PROM has 32 KBytes of storage. Each bit requires $t_{\text{prog,bit}} = 50 \mu s$ at $V_{\text{pp}} = 12 V$ and $I_{\text{prog}} = 10 mA$. Compute the total programming time and the energy consumed per chip. **Step 1 — Total number of bits**: $$ N_{\text{bits}} = 32 \text{ KB} \times 8 = 32 \times 1024 \times 8 = 262{,}144 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 — Total programming time**: $$ t_{\text{prog,total}} = N_{\text{bits}} \times t_{\text{prog,bit}} = 262{,}144 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \approx 13.1 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — Energy per bit**: $$ E_{\text{bit}} = V_{\text{pp}} \times I_{\text{prog}} \times t_{\text{prog,bit}} = 12 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} = 6 \mu J $$ **Step 4 — Total energy**: $$ E_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{bits}} \times E_{\text{bit}} = 262{,}144 \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.57 \text{ J} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion**: A 32 KB OTP PROM blow takes roughly **13 seconds** and consumes **~1.6 Joules** — a significant overhead during mass production. Manufacturers use **gang programmers** that blow **hundreds of chips in parallel** to amortise this time. --- ## 3.3 Comparative Numerical Summary $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Property} \quad & : \quad \text{OTP (Anti-fuse)} \quad : \quad \text{MTP (NOR Flash)} \\ \text{Endurance} \quad & : \quad 1 \text{ cycle} \quad : \quad 10^4 - 10^5 \text{ cycles} \\ \text{Retention} \quad & : \quad > 20 \text{ years} \quad : \quad 10 \text{ years (typ)} \\ \text{Cell Area} \quad & : \quad \approx 1T \quad : \quad \approx 1.5T - 2T \\ \text{Programming} \quad & : \quad 6-13.5 \text{ V} \quad : \quad 1.8-3.3 \text{ V} \text{ (modern)} \\ \text{Cost/bit} \quad & : \quad \text{Low} \quad : \quad \text{Medium-High} \end{aligned} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy in a Typical IoT Edge Node ```mermaid graph TD A[IoT Edge Node MCU] --> B[CPU Core] A --> C[Volatile Memory - SRAM] A --> D[Non-Volatile Memory - NVM] A --> E[Peripherals - GPIO - ADC - I2C - SPI] D --> D1[OTP Region - Bootloader - Root Keys - Factory Trim] D --> D2[MTP Region - Flash - Application Firmware - User Data - OTA Slots] D1 --> D1a[Anti-fuse PROM] D1 --> D1b[Fuse PROM] D2 --> D2a[NOR Flash] D2 --> D2b[EEPROM Emulation] D2 --> D2c[NAND Flash Optional] D2a --> D2a1[Boot Partition - A] D2a --> D2a2[Application Partition - B] D2a --> D2a3[Data Partition - Wear Levelled] ``` --- ## 4.2 OTP vs MTP Selection Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[New IoT Product Design] --> Q1{Does device need
field firmware updates?} Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is data cryptographically
sensitive and immutable?} Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Choose MTP - Flash/EEPROM] Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Choose OTP - Anti-fuse] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is BOM cost the
primary constraint?} Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Choose OTP - Fuse PROM] Q3 -- No --> P4[Choose MTP - for flexibility] P1 --> END[Document memory map and
OTA strategy] P2 --> END P3 --> END P4 --> END ``` --- ## 4.3 Floating-Gate MOSFET – The Heart of MTP Memory ```mermaid graph LR CG[Control Gate] --> FG{Floating Gate
Charge Storage} FG --> OX1[Tunnel Oxide - SiO2] OX1 --> SUB[Substrate - P-type Si] SD[Source / Drain] --> SUB VPP[High V_pp 10-13V] --> CG VPP -. FN Tunneling .-> FG FG -. Stored Charge .-> VTH[Modifies V_th
Read = 0 if V_th HIGH] ``` --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Embedded NVM Subsystem ```mermaid graph TD X[Host CPU via AHB/APB Bus] --> CTRL[Memory Controller] CTRL --> ADDR[Address Decoder] CTRL --> CMD[Command Register] CTRL --> STAT[Status Register] ADDR --> ROWDEC[Row Decoder] CMD --> HV[High-Voltage Charge Pump] HV --> ARRAY[Memory Cell Array - 2D Matrix] ARRAY --> SENS[Sense Amplifiers] ARRAY --> COLDEC[Column Decoder / MUX] SENS --> DOUT[Data Output Buffer - 8/16/32 bit] COLDEC --> DOUT STAT --> X DOUT --> X CMD <-. Write/Erase Sequence .-> X ARRAY -. OTP Partition .-> A1[Anti-fuse Cells] ARRAY -. MTP Partition .-> A2[Floating-gate Cells] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Differentiate between OTP and MTP memory in embedded IoT systems. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **OTP (One-Time Programmable)**: A non-volatile memory that can be programmed only **once** by the user/manufacturer, after which its contents become permanent and unalterable. Example: **Anti-fuse PROM** used in secure elements and PLDs. > > **MTP (Multi-Time Programmable)**: A non-volatile memory that allows **multiple program/erase cycles** (typically 10⁴–10⁵ cycles) using electrical means. Example: **NOR Flash memory** in STM32/ESP32 microcontrollers. > > **Key Distinction**: OTP is **immutable** post-programming; MTP supports **in-field firmware updates** through electrical erase/reprogram. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model QP]` **What is anti-fuse OTP memory? Why is it preferred for secure IoT applications?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **anti-fuse OTP** is an OTP cell where a thin dielectric (gate oxide) is **permanently ruptured** by applying a high programming voltage (≈ 6–13.5 V), thereby creating a conductive link between two metal layers. It is preferred for secure IoT applications because: > > 1. The programmed state is **physically irreversible** – it cannot be tampered with by software. > 2. It has **excellent radiation hardness**, suitable for aerospace IoT. > 3. The ON-state resistance is **low and stable**, making bit reading reliable. > 4. It is intrinsically **non-volatile without needing refresh**. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A — `Choose EITHER A OR B` > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Adapted]`** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of **floating-gate-based MTP (NOR Flash) memory** with a neat diagram. Discuss the mechanisms of **programming (hot-carrier injection)** and **erase (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling)**. **(a) Model Answer:** > **Floating-Gate Structure (2 marks)**: A floating-gate MOSFET has two gates — a **control gate** (CG) and an electrically isolated **floating gate** (FG) sandwiched between the CG and the substrate. The FG is surrounded by SiO₂ and is **electrically floating** (no DC connection). The presence of charge on the FG shifts the transistor's threshold voltage $V_{\text{th}}$. > > **Program Operation via Hot-Channel Electron Injection (HCI) (2 marks)**: > - A high voltage (≈ 10–12 V) is applied to the control gate, and the drain is biased to ≈ 5 V. > - Electrons flowing from source to drain gain **kinetic energy** ("hot electrons") near the drain. > - These hot electrons overcome the Si–SiO₂ barrier (≈ 3.2 eV) and get **injected onto the floating gate**. > - The stored negative charge raises $V_{\text{th}}$, and the cell reads as `0`. > > **Erase Operation via Fowler-Nordheim (FN) Tunneling (2 marks)**: > - A high **negative** voltage (≈ -10 V) is applied to the control gate; the source is biased positive. > - A high electric field (≈ 10 MV/cm) across the thin tunnel oxide causes electrons on the FG to **quantum-mechanically tunnel back** to the source. > - $V_{\text{th}}$ decreases, and the cell reads as `1`. > - **Block Erase**: All cells in a sector share the same erase voltage. > > **Diagrammatic Representation (1 mark)**: A labelled cross-section showing CG, FG, tunnel oxide, source/drain, and the two charge-flow directions for program vs erase. > > *(See SECTION 4.3 for the floating-gate schematic.)* --- > **(b) [7 Marks]** A smart-meter IoT device uses an **EEPROM** to log energy consumption readings every 5 minutes. The EEPROM has $N_{\text{end}} = 5 \times 10^5$ write cycles. The device is designed for a **15-year** operational life. Determine: > (i) Total writes over device lifetime. > (ii) Whether the EEPROM endurance is sufficient. If not, suggest two design-level mitigations. **(b) Model Answer:** > **Step 1 — Writes per year (2 marks)**: > > Writes per day = (24 × 60) / 5 = 288 writes/day > Writes per year = 288 × 365 = 105,120 writes/year > > **Step 2 — Total writes over 15 years (2 marks)**: > > $N_{\text{writes,total}} = 105{,}120 \times 15 = 1{,}576{,}800 \approx 1.58 \times 10^6$ writes > > **Step 3 — Compare with endurance (1 mark)**: > > $N_{\text{cycles,life}} = \min(5 \times 10^5, 1.58 \times 10^6) = 5 \times 10^5$ > > Since $1.58 \times 10^6 > 5 \times 10^5$, the EEPROM is **insufficient** by a factor of ~3.16. > > **Step 4 — Mitigations (2 marks)**: > > 1. **Wear-levelling in software**: Distribute writes across multiple EEPROM pages using a **round-robin or LRU (Least Recently Used)** algorithm to ensure no single cell exceeds its endurance. > 2. **Write coalescing / buffering**: Aggregate multiple readings in **SRAM** and flush to EEPROM in batches (e.g., once per hour or once per day), reducing the effective write rate by 12×–288×. > 3. *(Bonus)* Use a dedicated **external Flash with hardware wear-levelling controller** instead of internal EEPROM. --- ### QUESTION B — `Choose EITHER A OR B` > **`[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 – Adapted]`** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **OTP (anti-fuse)** and **MTP (Flash)** memory technologies across the following parameters: (i) cell structure, (ii) endurance, (iii) data retention, (iv) programming voltage, (v) cost per bit, (vi) typical IoT use case. Which would you choose for storing the **root cryptographic key** in an IoT device, and why? **(a) Model Answer:** | Parameter | OTP (Anti-fuse) | MTP (NOR Flash) | |-----------|-----------------|-----------------| | (i) Cell structure | Dielectric rupture between metal layers | Floating-gate MOSFET | | (ii) Endurance | **1 cycle** (one-time only) | 10⁴–10⁵ cycles | | (iii) Data retention | **> 20 years**, often 100+ years | 10–20 years (derated by temperature) | | (iv) Programming voltage | 6–13.5 V (charge pump) | 1.8–3.3 V (modern low-voltage Flash) | | (v) Cost per bit | **Low** (1T-equivalent cell) | Higher (1.5T–2T cell + charge pump) | | (vi) IoT use case | Secure boot keys, factory trim, immutable device ID | Firmware, OTA update storage, user data | > **Selection for root cryptographic key (1 mark)**: > **OTP (Anti-fuse)** is the correct choice because the **root key** must be **immutable**, **tamper-proof**, and **non-replaceable** for the entire device lifetime. Anti-fuse OTP guarantees: > 1. **Software cannot read the key in plaintext** if the anti-fuse layer is hidden beneath metal. > 2. **Physical attackers** cannot use electrical probing to extract the key (low ON-resistance, no floating-gate charge that could leak). > 3. **No wear-out** mechanism means the key is bit-stable for the device's full 15–20 year life. --- > **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **embedded NVM subsystem** of a typical IoT microcontroller. List the functions of the **memory controller, address decoder, sense amplifier, and high-voltage charge pump**. **(b) Model Answer:** > **Block Diagram (2 marks)**: See **SECTION 4.4** for the equivalent block architecture (Host CPU → Memory Controller → Address Decoder → Memory Array → Sense Amps → Data Out). > > **Function of Each Block (5 marks — 1.25 each)**: > > 1. **Memory Controller**: Acts as the **interface between the CPU (AHB/APB bus) and the NVM array**. It decodes read/write/erase commands, generates the correct sequence of control signals, enforces timing, and implements **write protection** and **lock bits**. > > 2. **Address Decoder**: Translates the **logical address** from the CPU into a **physical word-line (row)** and **bit-line (column)** selection in the 2D memory array, using pre-decoders and final decoders. > > 3. **Sense Amplifier**: Detects the **small voltage/current difference** between a memory cell and a reference cell, amplifying it to a clean CMOS logic level for the data bus. Critical for **read-speed** and **low-power operation**. > > 4. **High-Voltage Charge Pump**: Generates the **elevated programming voltage (V_pp ≈ 10–13.5 V)** required for FN tunneling and HCI, from the low-voltage MCU supply (1.8 V or 3.3 V). Uses **Dickson charge-pump topology** with multiple clocked capacitor stages. > > **Working Sequence**: Read command → Address decoded → Sense amp reads cell → Data returned. Write command → Charge pump enabled → High V_pp applied → FN tunneling/HCI programs cell → Status register polled for completion. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Conflating OTP and PROM**: Many students write "OTP is the same as PROM" without explaining that OTP is a **broader class** that includes fuse, anti-fuse, and PROM variants. > 2. **Skipping the diagram**: KTU 2024 scheme mandates a "**neat diagram**" worth **1–2 marks**. A floating-gate cross-section is **mandatory** for MTP questions. > 3. **Wrong unit in numericals**: Writing $t_{\text{prog}}$ in seconds when the question is in $\mu s$, or reporting endurance as "10⁵" without specifying "**cycles**". > 4. **Not mentioning Fowler-Nordheim tunneling explicitly** in erase — the KTU 2024 syllabus gives full credit only when the physical mechanism is named. > 5. **Ignoring OTA considerations**: For IoT context, examiners award bonus marks if the student connects the memory choice to **OTA firmware update feasibility** in the final conclusion. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **OTP = One-Time Programmable**; **MTP = Multi-Time Programmable** — these are the two fundamental embedded NVM categories in IoT. - ✅ **Anti-fuse OTP** is the **secure, radiation-hard** choice for storing **root keys and immutable IDs**. - ✅ **NOR Flash (MTP)** is the **de-facto** firmware storage in **ARM-based IoT MCUs** (STM32, ESP32, nRF52). - ✅ **Floating-gate MOSFET** is the foundational device for MTP — its **threshold voltage $V_{\text{th}}$** is modulated by charge stored on the floating gate. - ✅ **Programming (HCI)** uses hot electrons; **Erasing (FN tunneling)** uses quantum-mechanical tunneling — **name both explicitly** in KTU answers. - ✅ Key equations: $C_{\text{total}} = N \times W$, $E_{\text{bit}} = V_{\text{pp}} \times I \times t_{\text{prog}}$, $T_{\text{ret,eff}} = T_{\text{spec}} \times 2^{-\Delta T / k_T}$. - ✅ **Endurance budget rule**: Always check $N_{\text{writes,total}} \le N_{\text{end}}$ for the device's operational lifetime. - ✅ **OTA updates require MTP**; **non-updateable, cost-optimised devices use OTP**. - ✅ **Wear-levelling** is the standard software technique to extend EEPROM/Flash lifetime. - ✅ **A/B partition scheme** in MTP Flash enables **fail-safe OTA** in IoT. - ✅ **Cell area**: OTP ≈ 1T; EEPROM ≈ 2T — this is why OTP is **cheaper per bit** but **less flexible**. - ✅ For KTU answers, always end with the **IoT application context** (e.g., smart meter, BLE beacon, smart card) to fetch full marks.

topics.title: External Flash (NAND/NOR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** External Flash (NAND/NOR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**External Flash Memory** refers to non-volatile, electrically erasable semiconductor memory chips that reside *outside* the main MCU/MPU SoC and communicate via standardized serial (SPI, QSPI, I²C) or parallel bus protocols. In IoT firmware architecture, external flash serves as the secondary storage tier responsible for retaining firmware images, OTA update staging buffers, sensor data logs, configuration profiles, and resource files (web pages, certificates) when the system is powered down.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, two dominant **floating-gate MOSFET** technologies define this tier:

- **NOR Flash** — *random-access*, byte-erasable, Execute-In-Place (XIP) capable architecture based on the **NOR logic gate cell topology**.
- **NAND Flash** — *sequential/page-access*, block-erasable, high-density architecture based on the **NAND logic gate cell topology**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEERT614 – Module 4):** External NAND/NOR flash is classified under *IoT Hardware & Memory Subsystems*. Students must be able to differentiate cell topology, justify interface selection (SPI vs. QSPI vs. parallel), and compute memory organisation parameters (pages, blocks, sectors, capacity).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine your IoT device as a **small office**:

- The **internal SRAM** of the MCU is the **desk** of an employee — extremely fast to access, but the moment the office closes (power off), everything on the desk disappears.
- The **internal Flash** (embedded in the MCU) is the **locked drawer of that desk** — keeps some important files safe across the night, but limited in size.
- The **External Flash (NAND/NOR)** is the **filing cabinet in the corner of the office** — much larger capacity, takes a few extra seconds to walk over and open a drawer, but safely stores *all* the records overnight.

Now, within that filing cabinet, there are two styles:
- A **NOR cabinet** has every single file labelled and immediately reachable (random access) — perfect when you need *one specific receipt fast*, but the cabinet is bulky and expensive per file.
- A **NAND cabinet** crams files tightly in long bundles (sequential pages) — hugely denser and cheaper per file, but you must pull out the whole bundle to find one page (slower random access), and if the power cuts while you're writing, a bundle can get corrupted.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard endurance and timing values are **bold-highlighted** as they are heavily tested in KTU boards:

- **NOR Flash endurance:** typically **10,000 – 100,000 program/erase (P/E) cycles per sector**.
- **SLC NAND Flash endurance:** typically **60,000 – 100,000 P/E cycles per block**.
- **MLC NAND Flash endurance:** typically **3,000 – 10,000 P/E cycles per block**.
- **TLC NAND Flash endurance:** typically **500 – 3,000 P/E cycles per block**.
- **Data retention:** typically **10 – 20 years** at room temperature.
- **Program time per page (NAND):** typically **200 – 700 µs**.
- **Block erase time (NAND):** typically **1.5 – 3 ms**.
- **NOR byte program time:** typically **10 – 100 µs**.
- **NOR sector erase time (4 KB):** typically **30 – 200 ms** (significantly slower than NAND).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** Endurance directly limits the *lifetime* of an IoT data-logger node. A vibration sensor writing 1 KB every minute into a sector rated for 10,000 cycles will exhaust that sector in **~694 days (~1.9 years)** without wear levelling — a critical design constraint for industrial IoT.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Cell Structure — Floating Gate MOSFET

Both NAND and NOR technologies rely on the **floating-gate MOSFET** (FGMOS). The floating gate is electrically *isolated* by a thin tunnel oxide, trapping electrons whose presence modulates the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ of the cell transistor:

- **Programmed (Logic 0):** Electrons are injected onto the floating gate via **hot-electron injection** or **Fowler-Nordheim (FN) tunnelling**, raising $V_{TH}$.
- **Erased (Logic 1):** Electrons are pulled off via reverse-biased FN tunnelling, lowering $V_{TH}$.

The cell is read by applying a reference voltage $V_{REF}$ between $V_{TH,erased}$ and $V_{TH,programmed}$ and sensing drain current.

## 2.2 NOR Flash — Operational Logic

| Step | Operation | Mechanism |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Read | Each cell has its own drain contact → true random access, byte-addressable |
| 2 | Program | Hot-electron injection — electrons forced onto floating gate |
| 3 | Erase | FN tunnelling — entire sector (typically 64 KB or 128 KB) erased to 1 |
| 4 | XIP | MCU can fetch instructions directly from NOR via address bus |

**Why NOR for Boot/XIP:** Because every byte has a dedicated word-line + bit-line path, latency is deterministic and addressable — the CPU's program counter can directly map onto NOR addresses. This is why bootloaders are NOR-resident.

## 2.3 NAND Flash — Operational Logic

| Step | Operation | Mechanism |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Read | Cells are wired in series (NAND string of 32/64 cells) — page-level access only |
| 2 | Program | FN tunnelling — electrons placed on floating gate; only 0→1 transition prohibited without erase |
| 3 | Erase | Block-level erase (typically 128 KB or 256 KB blocks) |
| 4 | Sequential | Pages (2 KB / 4 KB / 8 KB) must be read in serial order within a block |

**Why NAND for Mass Storage:** Series-wired cells dramatically shrink the cell area (≈ **40–50% smaller than NOR**), enabling multi-Gbit densities at low cost per bit.

## 2.4 Bus Interface Protocols in IoT

- **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface):** Single-bit serial, 4-wire (SCK, MOSI, MISO, CS), up to ~104 MHz in standard mode. Common low-cost external flash like **Winbond W25Q** series.
- **QSPI (Quad SPI):** 4 data lines, throughput up to **4× SPI** — enables XIP from external flash.
- **OSPI (Octal SPI):** 8 data lines for high-performance IoT gateways.
- **Parallel NAND/NOR:** Multi-bit bus used in high-throughput applications (not common in low-power IoT).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for External Flash calculations. Memorise every row — at least one formula appears in nearly every KTU board paper.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Total Capacity | $C = N_{blocks} \times B_{size}$ | bits / bytes | $B_{size}$ = block size in bytes |
| 2 | Pages per Block | $P_{blk} = \dfrac{B_{size}}{P_{size}}$ | dimensionless | e.g., $128\text{ KB} / 2\text{ KB} = 64$ |
| 3 | Address (Row–Column) | $A = (R \times P_{blk}) + C$ | page index | $R$ = row (block), $C$ = column |
| 4 | Spare/OOB Area | $S_{OOB} = P_{size} \times r_{OOB}$ | bytes | $r_{OOB}$ typically $0.03125$ (1/32) |
| 5 | Endurance Lifetime | $T_{life} = \dfrac{E_{cycles} \times C}{W_{daily}}$ | days | $W_{daily}$ = bytes written per day |
| 6 | Effective Write Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{size}}{t_{prog} + t_{erase}}$ | B/s | Lower for NAND due to block erase |
| 7 | Bit Error Rate scaling | $BER_{NAND} \propto e^{-\alpha n}$ | — | $n$ = number of program cycles |
| 8 | Worn-out block fraction | $f_{worn} = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N_{blocks}}\right)^{E}$ | — | Used in wear-levelling analysis |

> **Units & Conventions used above:** $N_{blocks}$ = total blocks, $B_{size}$ = block size, $P_{size}$ = page size, $R$ = row address, $C$ = column address, $E_{cycles}$ = endurance cycles per block, $W_{daily}$ = daily write volume in bytes, $t_{prog}$ = program time per page, $t_{erase}$ = block erase time, $n$ = P/E cycles experienced, $E$ = total erase operations.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

External flash is the *silent workhorse* behind nearly every connected device: smart thermostats (logging hourly temperature curves), wearable fitness bands (storing 7 days of accelerometer raw data), automotive telematics (black-box event recorders), and agricultural sensors (months of soil-moisture history). The choice between NOR and NAND is fundamentally a **trade-off axis**:

$$\text{NOR: } \uparrow \text{Read Speed, } \uparrow \text{Reliability, } \downarrow \text{Density, } \uparrow \text{Cost/bit}$$

$$\text{NAND: } \uparrow \text{Density, } \downarrow \text{Cost/bit, } \downarrow \text{Write Granularity, } \uparrow \text{ECC Need}$$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Address Decoding for a NAND Flash

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A NAND flash device has the following organisation: each block = 128 pages, each page = 2048 + 64 bytes (data + spare). Total capacity = 4 Gbit. Determine (a) number of blocks, (b) total spare area, and (c) the 32-bit logical address for page index 15,432 inside block 27.

**Given:**
- Page data area $P_{data} = 2048$ bytes
- Page spare area $P_{spare} = 64$ bytes
- Pages per block $P_{blk} = 128$
- Total capacity $C_{total} = 4 \text{ Gbit} = 4 \times 2^{30}$ bits $= 536{,}870{,}912$ bytes

### Step (a) — Number of Blocks

We first convert total capacity into data bytes (OOB is excluded from user-addressable capacity by convention):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total,bytes} &= \dfrac{4 \times 2^{30} \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} \\
&= 2^{29} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 536{,}870{,}912 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the block size in bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{size} &= P_{blk} \times P_{data} \\
&= 128 \times 2048 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 262{,}144 \text{ bytes per block} \\
&= 256 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the number of blocks is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks} &= \dfrac{C_{total,bytes}}{B_{size}} \\
&= \dfrac{536{,}870{,}912}{262{,}144} \\
&= 2048 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** We divide total addressable bytes by per-block bytes because each block contributes equally to total capacity in homogeneous NAND arrays.

### Step (b) — Total Spare Area

The spare (Out-Of-Band / OOB) area is reserved for ECC, bad-block markers, and filesystem metadata. Total spare area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{OOB,total} &= N_{blocks} \times P_{blk} \times P_{spare} \\
&= 2048 \times 128 \times 64 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each block contains 128 pages, each page has 64 bytes of spare. Multiplying these three counts gives the total reserve region.

### Step (c) — 32-bit Logical Address for Block 27, Page 15,432

Recall the address formula: physical page index = (block number × pages per block) + column page offset. Here the question states the page index *within* block 27 is 15,432, but we must verify feasibility since $P_{blk} = 128$ — the largest page index inside a block is 127.

**Validation check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max page index in a block} &= P_{blk} - 1 \\
&= 128 - 1 \\
&= 127
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $15{,}432 \gg 127$, the index "15,432" must be interpreted as an **absolute page index across the entire chip**, not relative to block 27. The corrected interpretation:

**Global page index = 15,432** and **block number = 27**.

Convert global page index to (block, page-in-block):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block from page} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{15432}{128} \right\rfloor \\
&= 120.5625 \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Block} = 120 \text{ (not 27)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This contradicts the problem setup — therefore the KTU-style problem likely intended **"column page 15 inside block 27, with column 432"** i.e., split the absolute index. Adopting the canonical interpretation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 27 \quad \text{(block row)} \\
C &= 432 \quad \text{(column page offset within block)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 32-bit logical byte address is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{32} &= R \times P_{blk} \times P_{data} \;+\; C \times P_{data} \\
&= (27 \times 128 \times 2048) \;+\; (432 \times 2048) \\
&= 7{,}077{,}888 \;+\; 884{,}736 \\
&= 7{,}962{,}624 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

In hexadecimal (board examiners love hex):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{32} &= 7{,}962{,}624_{10} \\
&= 0x00798C00_{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** We compute the byte offset by stacking blocks first (each block contributes $P_{blk} \times P_{data}$ bytes), then add the intra-block page offset.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Flash Endurance Lifetime

**Problem Statement:** An IoT environmental sensor writes 2 KB of data every 10 minutes to a 16 Mbit external NOR flash with 4 KB sectors, each rated for **100,000 P/E cycles**. (a) How many bytes are written per day? (b) If wear-levelling is **NOT** applied, how many days until one sector fails?

### Step (a) — Daily Write Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{daily} &= 2 \text{ KB} \times \dfrac{24 \times 60 \text{ min}}{10 \text{ min}} \\
&= 2 \text{ KB} \times 144 \\
&= 288 \text{ KB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (b) — Time to Sector Exhaustion (without wear-levelling)

Number of writes per sector before failure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{sector} &= E_{cycles} \times S_{size} \\
&= 100{,}000 \times 4 \text{ KB} \\
&= 400{,}000 \text{ KB} \;\; \text{(write volume per sector lifetime)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Daily writes per sector (worst case, all 288 KB hits the *same* sector):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{exhaust} &= \dfrac{W_{sector}}{W_{daily}} \\
&= \dfrac{400{,}000 \text{ KB}}{288 \text{ KB/day}} \\
&\approx 1388.9 \text{ days} \\
&\approx 3.8 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Without wear levelling, the device fails in under 4 years.** This is why production IoT firmware *always* uses a Flash Translation Layer (FTL) or journaling filesystem like **LittleFS** or **Wear-Leveling module in ESP-IDF**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Wear Levelling Lifetime Extension

Suppose we now apply **dynamic wear levelling** across all sectors of a 16 Mbit NOR (= 2 MB = **512 sectors** of 4 KB). Each write still hits one sector, but distributed evenly.

Total device write capacity before any single sector wears out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{device} &= N_{sectors} \times E_{cycles} \times S_{size} \\
&= 512 \times 100{,}000 \times 4 \text{ KB} \\
&= 204{,}800{,}000 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Lifetime with even distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{WL} &= \dfrac{W_{device}}{W_{daily}} \\
&= \dfrac{204{,}800{,}000}{288} \text{ days} \\
&\approx 711{,}111 \text{ days} \\
&\approx 1948 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Insight:** Wear levelling extends the lifetime by a factor equal to $N_{sectors}$ (in the ideal dynamic-levelling case). Hence a 512-sector device that would otherwise fail in 3.8 years now lasts centuries — the *only* limit becomes **data-retention ageing**, not endurance.

## 4.3 Memory Operation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ReadCmd: 0x03 issued
    ReadCmd --> DataOut: t_read latency
    DataOut --> Idle
    Idle --> WriteEnable: 0x06
    WriteEnable --> PageProgram: 0x02 + payload
    PageProgram --> BusyProg: t_prog ~200us
    BusyProg --> Idle
    Idle --> SectorErase: 0x20 / 0xD8
    SectorErase --> BusyErase: t_erase ~30ms NOR / 2ms NAND
    BusyErase --> Idle
    Idle --> ReadStatus: 0x05 / 0x0F
    ReadStatus --> Idle
    BusyProg --> PowerLoss: brown-out
    PowerLoss --> CorruptPage: data integrity risk
    CorruptPage --> [*]: ECC recovery / discard
```
