# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between NOR and NAND flash memory in terms of cell architecture, access method, and typical IoT application.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

1. **Cell Architecture:** NOR uses parallel-connected floating-gate transistors (each cell tied independently to bit-line and source-line); NAND uses series-connected cells forming a string of 32 or 64 transistors.
2. **Access Method:** NOR supports **random byte-level access** with Execute-In-Place (XIP); NAND supports only **page-level sequential access**.
3. **Typical IoT Application:** NOR is used for **bootloader and firmware storage**; NAND is used for **mass data logging, OTA staging buffers, and embedded filesystems** (e.g., LittleFS, FAT on SPI-NAND).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**What is wear levelling? Why is it mandatory in NAND-based IoT storage subsystems?**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

1. **Definition:** Wear levelling is a firmware technique (implemented in the Flash Translation Layer, FTL) that **distributes program/erase cycles uniformly across all blocks** of the flash, preventing any single block from being over-written.
2. **Two variants:** *Dynamic wear levelling* (rotates hot pages among free blocks) and *Static wear levelling* (also migrates rarely-changed cold data).
3. **Why mandatory for NAND:** NAND endurance (especially MLC/TLC = **500–10,000 cycles**) is much lower than NOR. Without wear levelling, a frequently-updated log file would exhaust a single block within weeks, causing permanent data loss in the IoT node.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Part B Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the internal cell structure of a floating-gate MOSFET-based NOR flash memory. With a neat diagram, describe the read, program (write), and erase operations, including the role of Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A NOR flash chip has the following specifications: 16 Mbit total capacity, sector size = 4 KB, page size = 256 bytes, endurance = 100,000 P/E cycles per sector. An IoT temperature node writes a 256-byte log every 5 minutes. Calculate (i) the number of sectors, (ii) total write volume per day, and (iii) the lifetime in years assuming no wear levelling.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **floating-gate MOSFET (FGMOS)** is the basic cell of NOR flash. It contains two gates stacked above the channel: a **control gate** (CG) connected to the word-line, and an electrically isolated **floating gate (FG)** that traps electrons within an oxide-nitride-oxide (ONO) dielectric. Charge stored on FG alters the transistor's threshold voltage $V_{TH}$:

- **Programmed state (Logic 0):** Electrons on FG → $V_{TH}$ rises above read voltage → cell is OFF.
- **Erased state (Logic 1):** No electrons on FG → $V_{TH}$ low → cell is ON during read.

```
        Control Gate (WL)
        ┌──────────┐
        │          │
   ─────┤          ├─────  (Channel)
        │ FG (Trap)│
        └──────────┘
         Oxide    Substrate
```

**Read Operation:** Word-line is raised to a reference voltage $V_{REF}$ between $V_{TH,erased}$ and $V_{TH,programmed}$. The bit-line is precharged; if the cell conducts (erased), the bit-line discharges → Logic 1 is sensed; otherwise Logic 0.

**Program Operation (Hot-Electron Injection / CHE):** A high drain-to-source voltage ($V_{DS} \approx 5\text{ V}$) and elevated $V_{CG}$ ($\approx 9\text{ V}$) accelerate channel electrons; some gain enough kinetic energy to surmount the oxide barrier and lodge onto the FG, raising $V_{TH}$ to Logic 0.

**Erase Operation (Fowler-Nordheim Tunnelling):** Source is floated, bulk is biased high (~12 V), control gate is grounded. The strong vertical field causes electrons to quantum-mechanically tunnel from the FG back into the channel through the thin tunnel oxide, lowering $V_{TH}$ to erased state. Erase is performed on an entire **sector** because per-byte erase is impossible in NOR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** NOR flash is *asymmetric* — byte program is fast but sector erase is slow (~30–200 ms). This asymmetry shapes every IoT filesystem design.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $C_{total} = 16 \text{ Mbit} = 2 \text{ MB}$, $S_{size} = 4 \text{ KB}$, $P_{size} = 256 \text{ B}$, $E_{cycles} = 100{,}000$, write rate = 256 B every 5 min.

**(i) Number of sectors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{sectors} &= \dfrac{C_{total}}{S_{size}} \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}097{,}152 \text{ B}}{4096 \text{ B}} \\
&= 512 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total write volume per day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{daily} &= 256 \text{ B} \times \dfrac{24 \times 60}{5} \\
&= 256 \times 288 \\
&= 73{,}728 \text{ B/day} \\
&= 72 \text{ KB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Lifetime without wear levelling (worst case = all writes hit the same sector):**

Writes a single sector can absorb:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{sector} &= E_{cycles} \times S_{size} \\
&= 100{,}000 \times 4 \text{ KB} \\
&= 400{,}000 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time to exhaust:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{W_{sector}}{W_{daily}} \\
&= \dfrac{400{,}000 \text{ KB}}{72 \text{ KB/day}} \\
&\approx 5555.6 \text{ days} \\
&\approx 15.2 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Even without wear levelling, this particular IoT workload is sustainable for ~15 years — within the typical 10–20 year data-retention window of NOR flash.

---

### Question B — Part B Option 2 (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of an external SPI-based flash subsystem in an IoT node. Discuss the role of CS, SCK, MOSI, MISO signals and the significance of the Write Enable (WREN) command before any program/erase operation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare SPI, Quad-SPI (QSPI), and Octal-SPI (OSPI) flash interfaces in terms of data-line count, throughput multiplier, typical clock frequency, and use-case in IoT firmware boot. Hence justify which interface you would select for (i) a battery-powered soil-moisture sensor node and (ii) an AI-enabled edge-camera gateway.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
         IoT MCU                 External SPI Flash
   ┌───────────────┐            ┌──────────────────┐
   │  SPI Master   │            │  SPI Slave        │
   │               │            │                   │
   │  SCK ─────────┼───────────►│ SCK               │
   │  MOSI ────────┼───────────►│ MOSI / IO0        │
   │  MISO ◄───────┼────────────│ MISO / IO1        │
   │  CS / SS ─────┼───────────►│ CS# (active low)  │
   │               │            │                   │
   └───────────────┘            │  Status Register  │
                                │  Page Buffer      │
                                │  Memory Array     │
                                └──────────────────┘
```

**Signal Roles:**

- **CS (Chip Select, active low):** Selects the slave flash among multiple SPI peripherals. When CS = HIGH, MISO goes high-impedance.
- **SCK (Serial Clock):** Generated by the master; data is sampled on rising or falling edge per SPI mode (CPOL/CPHA).
- **MOSI (Master Out Slave In):** Carries command + address + payload *into* the flash.
- **MISO (Master In Slave Out):** Carries read data *out* of the flash.

**Command Sequence for a Page Program:**

1. Assert CS low.
2. Send **Write Enable (0x06)** — sets the WEL bit in the status register. Without this, the flash *ignores* all subsequent program/erase commands (a safety feature against accidental writes due to noise on the SPI bus).
3. Send **Page Program command (0x02)** + 24-bit address + payload (≤ 256 B).
4. De-assert CS.
5. Poll the **BUSY bit (SR1[0])** via Read Status (0x05) until program completes.
6. Send **Write Disable (0x04)** to clear WEL.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget that **WREN must be issued *before* every program/erase command**, not just once at boot. Forgetting this earns a **2-mark penalty** in the 7-mark sub-question.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | SPI | QSPI | OSPI |
|-----------|-----|------|------|
| Data Lines | 2 (MOSI + MISO) | 6 (4 IO + SCK + CS) | 10 (8 IO + SCK + CS) |
| Throughput Multiplier | 1× | 4× | 8× |
| Typical Clock | up to 104 MHz | up to 133 MHz SDR / 200 MHz DDR | up to 200 MHz DDR |
| Effective Bandwidth | ~12.5 MB/s | ~50 MB/s | ~100 MB/s |
| XIP Capability | Limited (needs cache fill) | Native XIP supported | Native XIP supported |
| Pin Count on MCU | 4 | 6 | 10 |
| Power Consumption | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

**Justification for (i) battery-powered soil-moisture sensor:**

Use **standard SPI**. Rationale: the node transmits tiny packets every few minutes and does not require XIP; minimal pin count preserves GPIO for sensors; lowest power draw extends battery life; 1× SPI bandwidth is more than sufficient.

**Justification for (ii) AI-enabled edge-camera gateway:**

Use **Octal-SPI (OSPI)**. Rationale: the gateway boots a large Linux kernel + AI model weights from external flash; OSPI's 8× bandwidth enables fast boot times and on-the-fly model loading. The extra power and pin budget are acceptable in a mains-powered edge device.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part B):** Students confuse QSPI's "Quad" with 4 *data* lines only — forgetting that QSPI uses **4 bidirectional IO lines plus SCK and CS = 6 pins total**. Drawing only 4 pins costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on External Flash questions:**
> 1. **Confusing MB and Mib:** $1 \text{ Mbit} = 2^{20}$ bits, but $1 \text{ MB} = 10^6$ bytes in SI. Always specify the base.
> 2. **Forgetting Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling** is the erase mechanism, not hot-electron injection (which is for program).
> 3. **Omitting the WREN command** before program/erase in SPI flash — a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Ignoring OOB/spare area** in NAND address calculations.
> 5. **Not justifying** the choice between NOR/NAND or SPI/OSPI — boards require *reasoned engineering justification*, not just naming.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — External Flash (NAND/NOR):**

- **NOR Flash** → random access, byte-programmable, sector erase, XIP-capable, **slower erase (~30–200 ms)**, used for bootloader/firmware.
- **NAND Flash** → page-programmable, block-erase, sequential access, **higher density, lower cost/bit**, mandatory ECC, used for mass storage / logs / OTA buffers.
- **Cell mechanism:** Floating-gate MOSFET; electrons trapped on FG alter $V_{TH}$.
- **Program:** Hot-electron injection (NOR) or FN tunnelling (NAND).
- **Erase:** Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling — sector (NOR) or block (NAND).
- **Interfaces:** SPI (1×) → QSPI (4×) → OSPI (8×); parallel for high-throughput embedded systems.
- **Endurance:** NOR = 10k–100k, SLC NAND = 60k–100k, MLC = 3k–10k, TLC = 500–3k cycles.
- **Data retention:** 10–20 years at room temperature.
- **Wear levelling** is essential for NAND; extends device lifetime by a factor of $N_{blocks}$.
- **Key formulas:** $C = N_{blocks} \times B_{size}$, $T_{life} = \dfrac{E_{cycles} \times C}{W_{daily}}$, $A = R \times P_{blk} \times P_{data} + C \times P_{data}$.
- **SPI safety:** Always send **WREN (0x06)** before program/erase; poll BUSY bit afterwards.
- **ECC** mandatory for NAND; BCH or LDPC codes commonly used to correct bit-flips accumulating over P/E cycles.
- **Typical IoT chips to remember:** Winbond **W25Q** (NOR), Macronix **MX25**, GigaDevice **GD25**, Toshiba/Kioxia **TC58** (NAND).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DRAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DRAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DRAM (Dynamic Random-Access Memory)** is a type of volatile semiconductor memory that stores each bit of data in a separate tiny **capacitor** within an integrated circuit. The term "**Dynamic**" refers to the fact that the stored charge on the capacitor **leaks away** with time, even when the memory is powered, which mandates a periodic **refresh** operation to retain the data. The term "**Random-Access**" implies that any memory location can be accessed directly in **O(1)** time without sequentially traversing preceding cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEERT614 – Module 4):** DRAM is studied under the broader umbrella of *IoT hardware components, memory architectures, and edge-device resources*. Understanding DRAM is critical for evaluating storage hierarchies, latency budgets, and energy profiles of IoT gateways, edge nodes, and single-board computers (e.g., Raspberry Pi, Arduino Due, ESP32 companion chips).

The canonical DRAM cell is the **1T1C cell** — *one access transistor + one storage capacitor* — which is the densest and most economical semiconductor memory cell ever commercialized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a row of **buckets (capacitors)** connected to a long water pipe (the bit-line). Each bucket has a **faucet (transistor)** that can be opened or closed by a handle (the word-line).

- **Writing '1'** → Open the faucet, pour a cup of water into the bucket, close the faucet. The bucket now holds water.
- **Writing '0'** → Open the faucet, drain the bucket completely, close the faucet. The bucket is empty.
- **Reading** → Open the faucet briefly. If water flows out, the bucket was full ('1'); if nothing flows, it was empty ('0'). The act of opening the faucet, however, **disturbs** the water level — the bucket may now be partially filled, no matter what the original state was. This is the **destructive read** property of DRAM.
- **Refreshing** → Because the bucket has tiny holes (leakage current), water slowly evaporates. To keep the data valid, an external timer must **periodically refill** every bucket. Without this, the '1' bit would gradually turn into a '0'.

This leaky-bucket metaphor captures the three defining traits of DRAM: **(i) capacitor-based storage, (ii) destructive read, and (iii) mandatory periodic refresh**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Metric Glossary (must memorize for KTU viva):**
> - **Access Time (t<sub>AA</sub>):** Time from row-address valid to data valid on the bus.
> - **Cycle Time (t<sub>RC</sub>):** Minimum time between two successive accesses to the **same row** of the same bank.
> - **Refresh Interval (t<sub>REF</sub>):** Typically **64 ms** for standard DRAM, **32 ms** for LPDDR.
> - **Density:** Measured in **Gb (gigabits) per die** — modern DDR5 reaches **32 Gb/die**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Dielectric constant (κ) of the storage capacitor insulator:** typically **~7–12** for high-κ materials like HfO₂ or Al₂O₃.
- **Storage capacitance (C<sub>S</sub>):** typically **20–30 fF** per cell in modern nodes.
- **Leakage current per cell:** on the order of **fA to pA** at room temperature.
- **Supply voltage (V<sub>DD</sub>):** DDR3 = **1.5 V**, DDR4 = **1.2 V**, DDR5 = **1.1 V**, LPDDR5X = **0.5–0.6 V**.
- **Refresh interval:** **64 ms** (JEDEC standard) divided across all rows, yielding a **tRFC** (refresh cycle time) of a few hundred nanoseconds per row.
- **Page size (burst length × data width):** typically **1 KB–2 KB** per opened row.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a DRAM Chip

A DRAM die is organized in a **hierarchical structure**:

1. **Bank** → independent address-decoding and sense-amplifier unit (modern chips have **8–16 banks**).
2. **Sub-array / Mat** → rectangular grid of cells sharing local word-lines and bit-lines.
3. **Cell Array (M × N)** → the actual 1T1C cells. Rows = word-lines (M), Columns = bit-lines (N).
4. **Row Buffer (Sense Amplifiers)** → latches that hold a full row (typically **1 Kb–8 Kb**) after activation.
5. **I/O Gating & Global Datapath** → multiplexes column reads/writes to the external DQ pins.

When a **Row Address (RAS)** is issued:
1. The corresponding **word-line rises** to V<sub>DD</sub> + V<sub>TH</sub> (boosted voltage to compensate for threshold drop).
2. All access transistors in that row turn ON, sharing their charge with the **bit-line capacitance (C<sub>BL</sub>)**.
3. The **sense amplifier** detects the small voltage differential (ΔV ≈ tens of mV) between the reference bit-line and the accessed bit-line, then amplifies it to a full-swing rail-to-rail signal.
4. The entire row is now in the **row buffer** and can be accessed rapidly by column commands (CAS) until the row is **precharged** (closed).

## 2.2 The Three DRAM Operations Explained

### (a) WRITE Operation
- The word-line is raised.
- The sense amplifier drives the bit-line to **V<sub>DD</sub>** (for '1') or **GND** (for '0').
- Charge is transferred into the storage capacitor through the access transistor.
- The write is **non-destructive** (the capacitor ends in the correct state).

### (b) READ Operation
- The word-line is raised; the capacitor charge **shares** with the bit-line.
- The small ΔV (~100–200 mV) is sensed and amplified.
- Because charge was shared, the capacitor's voltage is **disturbed** to the bit-line midpoint — this is the **destructive read**.
- The sense amplifier then immediately **writes the sensed value back** to the cell, restoring it. This restore is sometimes called **"refresh-on-read"**.

### (c) REFRESH Operation
- An external memory controller (or internal logic in LPDDR) periodically issues a **REFRESH** command.
- Each refresh cycle activates one row (or group of rows) and runs the sense amplifier to restore charge.
- **All rows must be refreshed within t<sub>REF</sub>**, typically every **64 ms**.
- The number of refresh commands issued = **R / t<sub>RFC</sub>**, where R = number of rows per bank.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it is necessary |
|---|---|
| **Boosted word-line voltage** | The NMOS access transistor drops V<sub>TH</sub> when passing a '1', so to fully write V<sub>DD</sub> onto C<sub>S</sub>, the gate must be V<sub>DD</sub> + V<sub>TH</sub>. |
| **Sense amplifier** | The charge shared between C<sub>S</sub> (~20 fF) and C<sub>BL</sub> (~200 fF) is only ~10% of full-swing — far too small for digital logic. Amplification is essential. |
| **Destructive read auto-restore** | Reading inherently leaks charge, so the sense amplifier must rewrite the value back during every read. |
| **Periodic refresh** | Sub-threshold and junction leakage (~fA/cell) drains C<sub>S</sub> in milliseconds. Without refresh, '1' bits slowly decay to '0'. |
| **Banking & row buffers** | Opens an entire row once, then serves many column accesses at high speed — exploits **spatial/temporal locality** of typical memory access patterns. |

## 2.4 DRAM Hierarchy of Performance

Latency (clock cycles) increases as we move away from the cell:

| Hierarchy Level | Typical Access Latency |
|---|---|
| Register | < 1 ns |
| SRAM (L1/L2 cache) | 1–5 ns |
| **DRAM (main memory)** | **50–100 ns** |
| SSD (NAND Flash) | 50–150 µs |
| HDD | 5–10 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world IoT relevance:** In an IoT gateway running an MQTT broker (e.g., Mosquitto) on a Raspberry Pi 4 (which uses **LPDDR4**), the DRAM latency determines the round-trip time of publish/subscribe operations. For battery-powered edge sensors using **PSRAM** (a DRAM-based pseudo-SRAM with built-in refresh), low standby current is more important than raw bandwidth.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage developed on bit-line after access | $$\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{V_{S}}{1 + \frac{C_{BL}}{C_{S}}}$$ | 100–200 | mV |
| Sense-amplifier offset margin | $$V_{SA,\min} \approx V_{OS} + \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\cdot\frac{C_{BL}}{C_{S}+C_{BL}}$$ | tens of mV | mV |
| Charge stored on capacitor | $$Q = C_{S} \cdot V_{S}$$ | ~30 fC | fC |
| Energy per access (read) | $$E_{read} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,C_{BL}\,V_{DD}^{\,2}$$ | ~100 pJ | pJ |
| Refresh power fraction | $$P_{refresh}\%\;\approx\;\frac{N_{rows}\,\cdot\,I_{leak}\,\cdot\,V_{DD}}{P_{total}}\times 100$$ | 10–25 | % |
| Total row-refresh commands per 64 ms | $$N_{REF} = \left\lceil \frac{t_{REF}}{t_{RFC}} \right\rceil$$ | 8192 | commands |
| Bandwidth (one channel) | $$BW = f_{clk} \times \frac{Width}{8} \times 2^{(DDR-1)} \times 2^{(Channels-1)}$$ | 25.6–51.2 | GB/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva — "C-B-D-R":** **C**apacitor stores bit, **B**it-line senses, **D**estructive read, **R**efresh every 64 ms.

## 2.6 Variants of DRAM (Evolution Timeline)

| Generation | Year | Voltage | Prefetch | Peak Transfer Rate (per pin) | IoT / Edge Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SDR SDRAM** | 1993 | 3.3 V | 1n | 100–200 MT/s | Legacy industrial controllers |
| **DDR** | 2000 | 2.5 V | 2n | 200–400 MT/s | Older single-board computers |
| **DDR2** | 2003 | 1.8 V | 4n | 400–1066 MT/s | Early IoT gateways |
| **DDR3** | 2007 | 1.5 V | 8n | 800–2133 MT/s | Raspberry Pi 2/3 |
| **DDR4** | 2014 | 1.2 V | 8n | 1600–3200 MT/s | Raspberry Pi 4, modern SBCs |
| **LPDDR4/4X** | 2016+ | 1.1 / 0.6 V | 16n | 3200–4266 MT/s | Smartphones, low-power IoT |
| **LPDDR5/5X** | 2019+ | 0.5–0.6 V | 16n | 6400–8533 MT/s | AI edge, AR/VR |
| **HBM2/HBM3** | 2018+ | 1.2 V | — | 450–900 GB/s stack | AI accelerators in edge servers |
| **PSRAM** | — | 1.8 V | — | ~200 MT/s | Battery-powered IoT MCUs (ESP32) |

## 2.7 Why DRAM Dominates Main Memory — Engineering Trade-offs

| Property | DRAM | SRAM | Flash (NAND) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell size | **6 F²** (1T1C) | 120 F² (6T) | 4 F² (3D stack) |
| Density | **Highest per area** | Lowest | Highest (3D) |
| Volatility | Volatile | Volatile | Non-volatile |
| Refresh needed? | **Yes (mandatory)** | No | No |
| Read speed | Moderate | **Fastest** | Slow |
| Write speed | Moderate | **Fastest** | Very slow (ms) |
| Endurance | ∞ reads, ∞ writes | ∞ | ~10⁴–10⁵ P/E cycles |
| Standby power | **High (refresh)** | Low (leakage) | Very low |
| Cost per bit | **Lowest** | Highest | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** SRAM is ~5–10× larger per cell than DRAM, so on-chip caches are necessarily small (KB–MB). Main memory must therefore be DRAM — accepting the refresh penalty in exchange for density and cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit-Line Voltage Swing After Cell Access

**Problem context:** When the word-line is raised, the charge originally on the storage capacitor (C<sub>S</sub>, holding voltage V<sub>S</sub> ≈ V<sub>DD</sub> for a '1') is shared with the bit-line capacitance (C<sub>BL</sub>). Find the resulting bit-line voltage.

### Step 1 — Identify the initial conditions
- **Before access:** Storage capacitor holds charge Q<sub>i</sub> = C<sub>S</sub> · V<sub>S</sub>; bit-line is precharged to V<sub>BL,pre</sub> = V<sub>DD</sub>/2.
- **At access (word-line rises):** The access transistor turns ON, and the two capacitors become **parallel**, sharing total charge.

### Step 2 — Apply conservation of charge
Total charge is conserved because the word-line pulse is fast and no current source is connected (idealized):

$$Q_{initial} \;=\; Q_{final}$$

$$C_{S}\cdot V_{S} \;+\; C_{BL}\cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \;=\; (C_{S}+C_{BL})\cdot V_{BL,final}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the final bit-line voltage
Rearranging:

$$V_{BL,final} = \frac{C_{S}\cdot V_{S} + C_{BL}\cdot\frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_{S}+C_{BL}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the differential signal
The sense amplifier detects the **difference** between this bit-line and the reference bit-line (which stays at V<sub>DD</sub>/2):

$$\Delta V_{BL} = V_{BL,final} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{C_{S}\cdot(V_{S} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2})}{C_{S}+C_{BL}}$$

### Step 5 — Specialize for a stored '1' (V<sub>S</sub> = V<sub>DD</sub>)
$$\Delta V_{BL}\big\vert_{1} = \frac{C_{S}\cdot\frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_{S}+C_{BL}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{1+\frac{C_{BL}}{C_{S}}}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical evaluation
Let V<sub>DD</sub> = **1.2 V**, C<sub>S</sub> = **20 fF**, C<sub>BL</sub> = **200 fF**:

$$\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{1.2}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{1+\frac{200}{20}} = 0.6\cdot\frac{1}{11} = 0.6 \cdot 0.0909 \approx 0.0545\text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{BL} \;\approx\; 54.5\text{ mV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The signal is only ~55 mV out of 1.2 V — roughly **4.5 %** of full-swing. This is why sense amplifiers must have **< 20 mV offset** and why DRAM is sensitive to noise, VTT ripple, and temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Consumed Per DRAM Access

### Step 1 — Energy required to charge the bit-line from 0 to V<sub>DD</sub>
The bit-line capacitance C<sub>BL</sub> is charged through the sense amplifier. Energy stored in a capacitor charged to V is E = ½ C V². Half the energy drawn from the supply is dissipated in the supply resistance/PMOS, half remains in the capacitor.

$$E_{BL,charge} = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot C_{BL}\cdot V_{DD}^{\,2}$$

### Step 2 — Numerical evaluation
Let C<sub>BL</sub> = **200 fF**, V<sub>DD</sub> = **1.2 V**:

$$E_{BL,charge} = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 200\times10^{-15}\cdot (1.2)^{2} = 100\times10^{-15}\cdot 1.44 = 144\times10^{-15}\text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{BL,charge} \;\approx\; 144\text{ fJ per bit-line precharge}}$$

### Step 3 — Total energy per read (per row activation, per bank)
Modern DDR4 banks have 1–2 Kb rows, so for an 8-bank chip the energy per **row activation** is:

$$E_{ACT} \approx N_{rows}\cdot E_{BL,charge} \approx 1024 \cdot 144\text{ fJ} \approx 147\text{ pJ}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to SRAM access energy
A typical 6T SRAM read at the same node consumes **~5–10 fJ per bit**, but since SRAM arrays are much smaller, the per-access energy is comparable. DRAM wins on **energy per bit stored**, SRAM wins on **energy per random access at small scale**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Refresh Power Budget

### Step 1 — Define variables
- N<sub>rows</sub> = number of rows per bank
- I<sub>leak</sub> = average leakage current per cell
- V<sub>DD</sub> = supply voltage
- t<sub>REF</sub> = refresh interval (64 ms standard)

### Step 2 — Charge lost per cell per refresh interval
The charge that leaks away in time t<sub>REF</sub> is:

$$\Delta Q_{leak} = I_{leak} \cdot t_{REF}$$

The energy needed to restore this charge to V<sub>DD</sub> is:

$$E_{refresh,\,per\,cell} = \Delta Q_{leak} \cdot V_{DD} = I_{leak}\cdot t_{REF}\cdot V_{DD}$$

### Step 3 — Aggregate over the entire chip
For an N-bit memory:

$$E_{refresh,\,total} = N \cdot I_{leak}\cdot t_{REF}\cdot V_{DD}$$

The average **refresh power** is:

$$P_{refresh} = \frac{E_{refresh,\,total}}{t_{REF}} = N \cdot I_{leak}\cdot V_{DD}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example
A 8 Gb DDR4 chip with average cell leakage of **5 fA** at V<sub>DD</sub> = 1.2 V:

$$P_{refresh} = 8\times10^{9}\cdot 5\times10^{-15}\cdot 1.2 = 48\times10^{-6}\text{ W} = 48\text{ µW}$$

This is ~10–25 % of total DRAM power in typical workloads — explaining why **LPDDR** uses *low-power self-refresh modes* for IoT sleep states.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DRAM vs. SRAM vs. NAND Comparison (Step-Form)

| Parameter | DRAM | SRAM | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell structure** | 1T + 1C | 6T (cross-coupled inverters) | 1T + floating-gate MOSFET |
| **Storage mechanism** | Charge on capacitor | Loop of two inverters | Trapped electrons on FG |
| **Data retention without power** | **No** (capacitor leaks) | **No** (needs power to hold state) | **Yes** (years) |
| **Refresh required?** | **Yes — every 64 ms** | No | No |
| **Destructive read?** | **Yes (auto-restored by sense amp)** | No | No |
| **Read latency** | 50–100 ns | 1–5 ns | 25–100 µs |
| **Write latency** | 50–100 ns | 1–5 ns | 200 µs – 1 ms |
| **Erase latency** | N/A | N/A | 1–2 ms (block) |
| **Endurance (cycles)** | ∞ | ∞ | 10⁴ – 10⁵ |
| **Density** | High (Gb–multi-Gb) | Low (Mb) | Very high (multi-Gb, 3D stacked) |
| **Cell area** | 6 F² | 120 F² | 4 F² (planar), smaller in 3D |
| **Cost / bit** | Lowest | Highest | Low (in bulk) |
| **Typical use** | Main memory (PC, IoT gateways) | CPU caches, registers | SSDs, eMMC, SD cards in IoT |
| **IoT use case** | Raspberry Pi / gateway RAM | MCU on-chip cache | Firmware, log storage on edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Analytical Insight:** The IoT "memory triad" in a typical edge device is **SRAM (inside the MCU) + DRAM (external, if any) + Flash (firmware + data logging)**. The 2024-scheme syllabus expects students to justify *which memory is appropriate for which IoT sub-system* (e.g., real-time sensor data → SRAM, large ML model weights → DRAM, persistent device identity → Flash/EEPROM).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 1T1C DRAM Cell — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL["WORD LINE WL"] -->|gate signal| TR["ACCESS TRANSISTOR NMOS"]
    TR -->|drain source| C["STORAGE CAPACITOR CS"]
    C -->|holds charge| GND1["GROUND PLATE"]
    TR -->|bit line contact| BL["BIT LINE BL"]
    BL --> SA["SENSE AMPLIFIER"]
    SA --> RB["ROW BUFFER LATCH"]
    RB --> IO["COLUMN MULTIPLEXER"]
    IO --> DQ["EXTERNAL DQ PINS"]
    SA -.->|feedback restore| C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **word-line (WL)** is the row-select signal. When it rises, the access transistor conducts.
- The **bit-line (BL)** is the column-sense wire running vertically across the array.
- The **storage capacitor C<sub>S</sub>** is the actual bit-storage element.
- The dashed arrow from **Sense Amplifier → Capacitor** represents the **auto-restore** after a destructive read.

## 4.2 DRAM Chip Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTRL["MEMORY CONTROLLER"] -->|ADDR CMD| DEC["ADDRESS DECODER"]
    DEC --> RA["ROW ADDRESS LATCH"]
    DEC --> CA["COLUMN ADDRESS LATCH"]
    RA --> WLDRV["WORD LINE DRIVER"]
    WLDRV --> ARRAY["CELL ARRAY MATRIX OF 1T1C CELLS"]
    ARRAY --> BL_SA["BIT LINE + SENSE AMPLIFIERS ROW BUFFER"]
    BL_SA --> COLMUX["COLUMN MULTIPLEXER"]
    CA --> COLMUX
    COLMUX --> IO["I/O BUFFERS"]
    IO --> DQ["DQ PINS EXTERNAL BUS"]
    CTRL -.->|REFRESH| REFCTL["REFRESH CONTROLLER"]
    REFCTL --> WLDRV
    REFCTL --> BL_SA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Memory Controller** (often inside the SoC, e.g., BCM2711 in Raspberry Pi 4) issues RAS, CAS, WE, and CKE signals.
- **Address Decoder** splits the address into row and column components (multiplexed on the same bus to save pins).
- **Refresh Controller** periodically issues auto-refresh commands (typical in DDR — denoted "REF" in JEDEC spec).
- For LPDDR, the refresh controller may be **inside the DRAM chip** (self-refresh mode, used when the IoT device sleeps).

## 4.3 Read / Write / Refresh State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> ACTIVATED : ACT command row address valid
    ACTIVATED --> READING : READ CAS command
    ACTIVATED --> WRITING : WRITE CAS command
    READING --> ACTIVATED : burst continues
    WRITING --> ACTIVATED : burst continues
    ACTIVATED --> IDLE : PRE precharge closes row
    IDLE --> REFRESHING : REF auto refresh command
    REFRESHING --> IDLE : one or more rows restored
    REFRESHING --> ACTIVATED : continue with row open after REF
    IDLE --> SELFREFRESH : enters low power mode
    SELFREFRESH --> IDLE : controller wakes chip
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **IDLE** state has all banks precharged.
- The **ACTIVATED** state means a row is open in the row buffer.
- **SELF-REFRESH** is critical for battery-powered IoT — the chip refreshes itself without external clock, consuming < 100 µA.

## 4.4 DRAM Placement in a Typical IoT Gateway Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR["SENSORS TEMP HUMIDITY MOTION"] --> MCU["MCU ARM CORTEX M4"]
    MCU --> BLE["BLE WIFI MODULE"]
    BLE --> GW["IoT GATEWAY RASPBERRY PI CLASS"]
    GW --> DRAM["LPDDR4 DRAM 2 8 GB MAIN MEMORY"]
    GW --> FLASH["eMMC NAND FLASH 16 64 GB STORAGE"]
    GW --> ETH["ETHERNET WI FI 6 RADIO"]
    ETH --> CLOUD["CLOUD MQTT BROKER"]
    GW --> EDGEAI["EDGE AI INFERENCE TFLITE MODEL"]
    EDGEAI --> DRAM
    EDGEAI --> FLASH
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **gateway** uses LPDDR4 DRAM for working memory (ML inference, MQTT buffers, OS).
- The **flash** stores the OS, TensorFlow Lite model, and log files persistently.
- The **MCU side** uses only SRAM/Flash — no DRAM, because of power constraints.
- This matches the KTU Module-4 emphasis on **edge-to-gateway memory hierarchy** in IoT.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is DRAM called "Dynamic" RAM? What is the typical refresh interval specified by JEDEC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DRAM stores each bit as **electrical charge on a tiny capacitor** inside the memory cell. Because the capacitor's dielectric and the access transistor's sub-threshold leakage form a finite resistance, the stored charge **leaks away** with time — even when the chip remains powered. To preserve the data, an external controller must **periodically read and rewrite** (sense-amplify) every row. This mandatory periodic restoration is the **"dynamic"** behaviour.

- **(1 mark)** Storing bit as charge on capacitor.
- **(1 mark)** Charge leaks → data would be lost without refresh.
- **(1 mark)** JEDEC refresh interval = **64 ms** (32 ms for LPDDR low-power modes), with all rows refreshed within this window.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List three differences between DRAM and SRAM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | DRAM | SRAM |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stores charge on **capacitor** (1T1C) | Stores state in a **6T latch** (cross-coupled inverters) |
| 2 | **Destructive read**; must restore after each read | **Non-destructive read** |
| 3 | **Needs refresh** every 64 ms | **No refresh** needed |
| 4 | Higher density, lower cost/bit | Lower density, higher cost/bit |
| 5 | Slower (~50–100 ns) | Faster (~1–5 ns) |

*(Any three correct differences earn 3 marks — 1 mark each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the structure and operation of a **1T1C DRAM cell**. Discuss why the read operation is destructive and how data is restored.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **voltage developed on the bit-line (ΔV<sub>BL</sub>)** when a DRAM cell storing a '1' is accessed, given C<sub>S</sub> and C<sub>BL</sub>. Compute ΔV<sub>BL</sub> for V<sub>DD</sub> = 1.2 V, C<sub>S</sub> = 20 fF, C<sub>BL</sub> = 200 fF, and comment on the design implications.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Structure (3 marks):** A 1T1C cell consists of:
- **One NMOS access transistor** — gate connected to the **word-line (WL)**, drain to the **bit-line (BL)**, source to the storage node.
- **One storage capacitor C<sub>S</sub>** — between the storage node and the cell plate (typically tied to V<sub>DD</sub>/2 to maximize write margin).

**Write operation (1 mark):** The WL is raised to V<sub>DD</sub> + V<sub>TH</sub> (boosted). The bit-line is driven to V<sub>DD</sub> (write '1') or GND (write '0'). Charge flows into / out of the capacitor through the access transistor.

**Read operation (2 marks):** The bit-line is first precharged to V<sub>DD</sub>/2. The WL is then raised; the cell's charge redistributes between C<sub>S</sub> and C<sub>BL</sub>, creating a small ΔV<sub>BL</sub>. The **sense amplifier** detects this and amplifies to full-swing. Crucially, after charge sharing, the capacitor's voltage is **no longer the original '1'** — it has been partially discharged to ≈ V<sub>DD</sub>/2. This is the **destructive read**.

**Restore (1 mark):** Immediately after sensing, the sense amplifier drives the bit-line to the correct full-swing voltage, which overwrites the (disturbed) capacitor with the original value. This is the **auto-restore** step — no extra command is required.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (5 marks):** Using conservation of charge (already shown in Section 3.1):

$$\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{1+\frac{C_{BL}}{C_{S}}}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{1.2}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{1+\frac{200}{20}} = 0.6\cdot\frac{1}{11}$$

$$\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{0.6}{11} = 0.0545\text{ V} = 54.5\text{ mV}$$

**Design implications (2 marks):**
- The signal is only **~4.5 % of full-swing** — extremely small. Sense-amplifier input offset must be **< 20 mV** to avoid misread.
- The ratio C<sub>BL</sub>/C<sub>S</sub> dominates — designers use **3D capacitor structures** (trench, pillar, deep-trench) to maximize C<sub>S</sub> in a small footprint.
- The small signal also explains why DRAM is **sensitive to VTT noise, temperature, and process variation**, unlike SRAM.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **DRAM refresh mechanism**. Why is refresh needed? How does JEDEC specify the refresh interval? Discuss the impact of refresh on power consumption in IoT edge devices.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **DRAM, SRAM, and NAND Flash** along the following axes: cell structure, volatility, density, read/write latency, endurance, and typical IoT use case. Recommend the appropriate memory for: (i) a battery-powered temperature sensor MCU, (ii) an IoT gateway running an MQTT broker + ML inference, (iii) persistent storage of sensor logs on an edge device.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Why refresh is needed (2 marks):** Charge on the storage capacitor leaks away through (i) sub-threshold leakage of the access transistor, (ii) junction leakage of the source/drain diodes, and (iii) gate-oxide tunneling. Leakage current is on the order of **fA per cell**, but accumulated over **64 ms** this is enough to corrupt the data.

**JEDEC refresh interval (2 marks):** The JEDEC standard requires that **every row be refreshed at least once every 64 ms** (t<sub>REF</sub> = 64 ms) at normal operating temperature. For temperatures above 85 °C, the interval shortens to **32 ms**. In **LPDDR** low-power modes, the chip enters **self-refresh** and the internal oscillator refreshes rows autonomously, allowing the external controller to be powered down.

**Refresh mechanism (2 marks):** The memory controller issues a **REFRESH (REF)** command. Internally, an address counter sequentially activates one row, runs the sense amplifier (which reads and rewrites the cell), then precharges. The cycle repeats until all rows are covered. The number of REF commands needed is:

$$N_{REF} = \left\lceil \frac{t_{REF}}{t_{RFC}} \right\rceil$$

where t<sub>RFC</sub> is the **refresh cycle time** (typically **350–500 ns** for DDR4).

**Impact on IoT power (1 mark):** Refresh accounts for **~10–25 %** of total DRAM power in active mode, and is the **dominant power consumer in standby**. For battery-powered IoT gateways, **LPDDR4/5** chips support partial-array self-refresh (PASR), refreshing only the banks holding critical data, cutting standby current to **< 100 µA**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Axis | DRAM | SRAM | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell structure | 1T + 1C | 6T | 1T + floating gate |
| Volatility | Volatile (refresh) | Volatile | **Non-volatile** |
| Density | High (Gb) | Low (Mb) | **Very high** (multi-Gb, 3D) |
| Read latency | 50–100 ns | **1–5 ns** | 25–100 µs |
| Write latency | 50–100 ns | 1–5 ns | 200 µs – 1 ms |
| Endurance | ∞ | ∞ | 10⁴–10⁵ P/E cycles |
| IoT use case | Gateway main memory, ML weights | MCU on-chip cache, registers | Firmware, log storage, eMMC/SD |

**Recommendations (2 marks):**

- **(i) Battery-powered temperature sensor MCU** → **SRAM** (on-chip) for transient data + **Flash** (on-chip) for firmware and log retention. No DRAM — refresh current would drain the battery.
- **(ii) IoT gateway with MQTT broker + ML inference** → **DRAM** (LPDDR4, 2–8 GB) for OS, MQTT buffers, and TensorFlow Lite working memory. The ML model itself is loaded into DRAM from Flash at boot.
- **(iii) Persistent storage of sensor logs** → **NAND Flash** (eMMC or SD card), because the data must survive power-off and the write volume is low (logs only, not high-frequency writes).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Ways Students Lose Marks on DRAM Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing t<sub>REF</sub> with t<sub>RFC</sub>:** t<sub>REF</sub> = 64 ms is the *total* window to refresh all rows; t<sub>RFC</sub> is the *time per single refresh command*. Do not interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting "auto-restore"** when describing destructive read — the read itself is destructive, but the sense amplifier immediately rewrites the value, so the **net** data integrity is preserved.
> 3. **Stating "DRAM does not need a controller"** — false. DRAM requires an external **memory controller** (or on-die controller in LPDDR) for refresh, address multiplexing, and timing.
> 4. **Writing "DRAM is non-volatile"** in the comparison table — instant 0 for that row. DRAM is **volatile**.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps in the ΔV<sub>BL</sub> question** — KTU examiners specifically allocate 5 of 7 marks to the **derivation**; merely quoting the formula earns ≤ 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing LPDDR with DDR** — LPDDR (Low-Power DDR) is for mobile/IoT; DDR is for desktops/servers. They differ in supply voltage, prefetch, and self-refresh support.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — DRAM (OEERT614 / Module 4):**

- **DRAM = Dynamic RAM.** Stores bit as **charge on a capacitor** in a **1T1C cell**.
- **Why "Dynamic"?** Capacitor charge **leaks** → must be **refreshed every 64 ms** (JEDEC).
- **Read is destructive**, but the **sense amplifier auto-restores** the value immediately.
- **Bit-line voltage swing** after access: 
  $$\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{1+C_{BL}/C_{S}}$$
  Typical value ~50 mV for V<sub>DD</sub> = 1.2 V.
- **DRAM hierarchy:** Cell → Mat → Bank (8–16) → Channel. Row buffer holds an **open page** of 1–8 Kb.
- **Refresh power** = N · I<sub>leak</sub> · V<sub>DD</sub>, contributing **10–25 %** of total DRAM power.
- **Variants in IoT context:** LPDDR4/4X (Raspberry Pi 4), PSRAM (ESP32), eMMC NAND (firmware).
- **Comparison trifecta:**
  - **SRAM** → fastest, on-chip cache, no refresh.
  - **DRAM** → dense, cheap, main memory, needs refresh.
  - **NAND Flash** → non-volatile, slow writes, finite endurance (10⁴–10⁵ P/E).
- **KTU-mandated IoT use cases:**
  - Sensor node → **SRAM + Flash** (no DRAM).
  - Edge gateway / SBC → **LPDDR + Flash** (or eMMC).
  - Cloud-side AI aggregator → **DDR5 + HBM** for model parallelism.
- **Command set to memorize:** **ACT** (activate row), **READ/WRITE** (CAS), **PRE** (precharge/close row), **REF** (refresh), **SREF** (self-refresh for IoT sleep).
- **Energy per read** ≈ ½ C<sub>BL</sub> V<sub>DD</sub>² ≈ **100–150 pJ** per bit-line precharge event.
- **Golden rule for KTU:** "**DRAM trades simplicity for density; the refresh burden is the price of that density.**"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
