<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the IBM Watson IoT Platform. List any four of its key features.*

**Model Answer:**

The IBM Watson IoT Platform is a fully managed, cloud-hosted service provided by IBM that enables IoT devices, gateways, and applications to connect, communicate, and exchange data securely over the Internet. It is part of IBM Cloud (Bluemix) and is built on a publish-subscribe messaging model using MQTT and HTTP protocols.

**Four Key Features:**

1. **Secure Device Connectivity** using TLS-encrypted MQTT on port 8883 with certificate-based authentication.
2. **Device Management** including bulk registration, firmware updates, and device shadowing for state synchronization.
3. **Real-time Event Processing** via a built-in rule engine that triggers actions when telemetry thresholds are met.
4. **Cognitive Integration** with IBM Watson AI services (Machine Learning, NLU, Vision) for intelligent analytics.

(Any four valid features accepted.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a Device Shadow in IBM Watson IoT? Why is it useful?*

**Model Answer:**

A **Device Shadow** is a virtual JSON document maintained in the Watson IoT cloud for each registered device. It stores two key fields:

- **reported state** — the last known state reported by the device.
- **desired state** — the state requested by the application.

It also contains a **version** number for conflict resolution.

**Usefulness:**

1. It enables **communication with offline devices** — the application can update the desired state in the cloud, and the device receives it upon reconnection.
2. It provides **state synchronization** without requiring persistent device connectivity.
3. It supports **last-write-wins conflict resolution** using the version counter.
4. It decouples the application from real-time device availability, enabling robust asynchronous control.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of the IBM Watson IoT Platform with a neat diagram. Discuss the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the MQTT topic structure used in IBM Watson IoT. Explain the publish–subscribe mechanism with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Layered Architecture — 7 marks**

The IBM Watson IoT Platform follows a 4-layer architecture aligned with the standard IoT reference model:

1. **Perception Layer (1 mark):** Contains physical sensors (temperature, humidity, motion) and actuators (motors, relays). Generates raw analog/digital data.

2. **Network Layer (2 marks):** Includes gateways (e.g., Raspberry Pi, ARM Cortex boards) that aggregate sensor data. Uses **MQTT (port 8883)** and **HTTP/HTTPS** to securely transmit data to the cloud. TLS encryption ensures confidentiality.

3. **Platform Layer — Watson IoT Core (3 marks):** The heart of the platform. It performs:
   - Device authentication via token.
   - Event ingestion through the MQTT broker.
   - Rule engine evaluation.
   - Device shadow management.
   - Data persistence (Cloudant / Db2).
   - Integration with cognitive services.

4. **Application Layer (1 mark):** User-facing applications built using **Node-RED**, REST APIs, or mobile SDKs. These consume processed data for visualization, alerting, and decision-making.

*(Include the Mermaid architecture diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)*

**(b) MQTT Topic Structure & Pub–Sub Mechanism — 7 marks**

**Topic Format (3 marks):**
The IBM Watson IoT MQTT topic follows a strict 5-segment structure for publishing:

$$
\texttt{iot-2/evt/}\langle \texttt{event\_id} \rangle\texttt{/fmt/}\langle \texttt{format} \rangle
$$

**Example:** A temperature sensor publishing JSON data uses the topic:

$$
\texttt{iot-2/evt/temperature/fmt/json}
$$

**Pub–Sub Mechanism (4 marks):**

- **Publisher:** The IoT device publishes messages to a topic.
- **Broker:** The Watson IoT MQTT broker receives the message, validates ACLs, and routes it.
- **Subscriber:** Applications subscribe using specific or wildcard topics. The wildcard `+` matches a single segment.

**Subscription Examples:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{Subscribe to all temperature events:}\quad & \texttt{iot-2/evt/temperature/fmt/json} \\
\texttt{Subscribe to all events in JSON:}\quad & \texttt{iot-2/evt/+/fmt/json} \\
\texttt{Subscribe to everything:}\quad & \texttt{iot-2/+/fmt/+}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Workflow:**

$$
\text{Device} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH}} \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{Forward}} \text{Subscriber App}
$$

This decoupled architecture enables **horizontal scalability** and **real-time fan-out** of telemetry data to multiple consumers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the Device Shadow concept in IBM Watson IoT. How does it resolve conflicts when the device is offline? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a rule in IBM Watson IoT that triggers an email alert when temperature exceeds 80°C **AND** humidity falls below 20% in a refrigerator device type. Compute the required bandwidth if 2000 such refrigerators each publish a 200-byte JSON packet every 3 seconds. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Device Shadow — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Device Shadow is a virtual JSON document stored in the IBM Watson IoT cloud for each device. It contains:

$$
S = (\texttt{reported}, \texttt{desired}, \texttt{version})
$$

**Example (3 marks):** A smart AC has shadow:
- `reported: {temperature: 24, mode: "cool"}`
- `desired: {temperature: 22, mode: "cool"}`

The application updates the *desired* state, and on reconnection, the AC adjusts itself to match.

**Conflict Resolution (2 marks):** When a device is offline:
1. Application updates `desired` field; version increments.
2. Device reconnects and fetches shadow delta.
3. Device updates its internal state to match `desired`.
4. Device publishes new `reported` state.
5. Cloud merges and increments version.

This implements **eventual consistency** via last-write-wins.

**(b) Rule Design & Bandwidth Calculation — 7 marks**

**Rule Design in JSON (3 marks):**

```json
{
  "name": "RefrigeratorAlert",
  "deviceType": "refrigerator",
  "condition": {
    "and": [
      {"property": "temperature", "op": ">", "value": 80},
      {"property": "humidity", "op": "<", "value": 20}
    ]
  },
  "action": {
    "sendEmail": "ops@factory.com"
  }
}
```

The Boolean fire condition is:

$$
F = \big(\text{temp} > 80\big) \land \big(\text{humidity} < 20\big)
$$

**Bandwidth Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 2000$ devices, $S_{\text{pkt}} = 200 \, \text{bytes}$, $T_{\text{int}} = 3 \, \text{s}$.

**Step 1:** Events per second per device.

$$
f_{\text{device}} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333 \, \text{events/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Total events per second.

$$
f_{\text{total}} = 2000 \times 0.3333 = 666.67 \, \text{events/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Required bandwidth in bytes/sec.

$$
B_{\text{req}} = 666.67 \times 200 = 133{,}333.33 \, \text{bytes/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to kbps.

$$
B_{\text{req}} = \frac{133{,}333.33 \times 8}{1000} \approx 1066.67 \, \text{kbps} \approx 1.07 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

**Result:** The platform requires approximately **1.07 Mbps** of bandwidth to support 2000 refrigerators.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IBM Watson IoT Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `iot-2` prefix in MQTT topic** — students often write `iot/evt/...` and lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Confusing Device Shadow with device firmware** — Shadow is a *state JSON*, not code. Clearly mention `reported`, `desired`, and `version`.
> 3. **Writing MQTT port as 1883** — for IBM Watson IoT, the production port is **8883 (TLS)**. Plaintext 1883 is rarely used.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical problems** — bandwidth answers without `kbps` or `Mbps` lose 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not mentioning Rule Engine** — when asked about "real-time processing", always cite the rule engine explicitly.
> 6. **Mixing up layers** — Watson IoT Core is the *platform layer*, not the *application layer*. Keep the four layers distinct.
> 7. **Skipping authentication** — any architecture diagram without `orgID + token` and TLS loses marks.
> 8. **Confusing `+` and `#` wildcards** — In IBM Watson IoT, only `+` (single-level) is used. The `#` multi-level wildcard is part of the generic MQTT 5 spec, not Watson's typical pattern.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- IBM Watson IoT Platform is a **PaaS** on IBM Cloud, built on a **pub–sub model** over **MQTT (port 8883)** and **HTTP**.
- A device is uniquely identified by the **3-tuple (orgID, deviceType, deviceID)** and authenticated with a **12-character token**.
- The MQTT topic for publishing is exactly: **`iot-2/evt/\<event_id\>/fmt/\<format\>`**.
- The **Device Shadow** stores `(reported, desired, version)` JSON and supports **offline state synchronization** with **last-write-wins** conflict resolution.
- The **Rule Engine** uses Boolean conditions (`AND` / `OR`) on telemetry fields to fire actions like email, webhook, or device command.
- Data is stored in **Cloudant (NoSQL)** or **Db2** and integrated with **Watson AI services** for cognitive analytics.
- Applications are built using **Node-RED**, **REST APIs**, and **SDKs (Python, Node.js, Java, C#)**.
- Bandwidth for an IoT deployment is computed as: $B = N \times \frac{1}{T} \times S \times 8 \, \text{bits}$.
- Watson IoT follows a **4-layer architecture**: Perception → Network → Platform → Application.
- Authentication is **one-way TLS** — the device authenticates to the cloud; the cloud holds a CA-signed certificate.
- The platform is **eventually consistent** and is designed for **horizontal scalability** to millions of devices.
- For KTU 2024 exams, remember the formula for **per-device event rate**: $f = 1/T_{\text{interval}}$ and platform total: $f_{\text{total}} = N \cdot f$.
- Watson IoT Core was **deprecated by IBM in 2023** and migrated to **IBM IoT Platform-as-a-Service successor** — but for KTU syllabus purposes, the architecture and concepts remain the same.
- The **default event format** in Watson IoT examples is `json` — always specify the format explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT614 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Analytics** is the systematic application of data analysis, statistical algorithms, and machine learning techniques on the massive volumes of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data generated by IoT devices, sensors, and actuators, in order to extract actionable insights, detect anomalies, predict future events, and automate decision-making processes in real-time or near-real-time.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, IoT Analytics is positioned as the *intelligence layer* of the IoT reference architecture. It sits on top of the data acquisition and networking layers and bridges the gap between raw sensor data and business intelligence. The KTU syllabus explicitly highlights it as the "value-creation phase" of the IoT data lifecycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Used in KTU 2024 Module 4:**
> - **Descriptive Analytics** – What happened?
> - **Diagnostic Analytics** – Why did it happen?
> - **Predictive Analytics** – What will happen?
> - **Prescriptive Analytics** – What should be done?

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **IoT Analytics as a hospital's Intensive Care Unit (ICU) for a smart city**:

- **Sensors (heart rate monitors, temperature probes, traffic cameras)** are continuously streaming data from the "body" of the city.
- **The data center** is like the hospital's centralized monitoring room where all readings pour in.
- **Analytics** is the team of doctors and nurses who:
  - *Descriptively* note: "The patient's temperature is 102°F."
  - *Diagnostically* figure out: "It's an infection caused by bacteria X."
  - *Predictively* warn: "If untreated, the temperature will spike to 105°F in 2 hours."
  - *Prescriptively* advise: "Administer antibiotic Y immediately."

Without analytics, IoT is just a *data dump*. With analytics, IoT becomes a *decision engine*.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** The **4 D's of Analytics** — **D**escribe, **D**iagnose, **D**etect/Predict, **D**ecide/Prescribe. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are **bold-highlighted** as they are routinely tested in KTU examinations:

- **Velocity of Data**: Typically **> 1 GB/s** in large-scale IoT deployments.
- **The 5 V's of Big Data in IoT**: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value**.
- **Latency Tolerance**: 
  - Real-time analytics: **< 100 ms**
  - Near-real-time: **100 ms – 5 s**
  - Batch analytics: **> 5 s (often minutes/hours)**
- **Edge Analytics Response Time**: **< 10 ms** (processed locally on the device/gateway).

### 1.4 Why IoT Analytics Matters (Engineering Perspective)

In modern Industry 4.0 ecosystems, raw sensor data alone is **inert**. The economic value is unlocked only when analytics converts data into:
- Predictive maintenance schedules (saving **15–30%** in maintenance costs).
- Energy optimization in smart grids.
- Anomaly detection in security systems.
- Customer behavior insights in retail IoT.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Paradigms of IoT Analytics (Hierarchical Model)

IoT Analytics is built upon a **four-tier analytical hierarchy**, often represented as the *Analytics Maturity Model*:

#### Tier 1: Descriptive Analytics (The "What" Layer)
- Aggregates historical IoT data to summarize past behavior.
- **Core techniques**: Mean, median, mode, standard deviation, count, sum, min/max.
- **Tools in IoT context**: SQL queries on time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB).
- **Example**: "Last Tuesday, the average temperature in greenhouse zone 3 was 28.4°C between 2 PM and 4 PM."

#### Tier 2: Diagnostic Analytics (The "Why" Layer)
- Performs root-cause analysis using drill-down, correlation, and data mining.
- **Core techniques**: Correlation coefficients, regression analysis, decision trees (for explanation).
- **Example**: "The temperature spike was caused by a malfunctioning exhaust fan (Fan ID: F-12) detected via cross-correlation with vibration data."

#### Tier 3: Predictive Analytics (The "What Next" Layer)
- Uses historical and real-time data to forecast future events.
- **Core techniques**: Time-series forecasting (ARIMA, LSTM), regression, classification models.
- **Example**: "Based on current vibration patterns, the motor will fail within 14 days with 87% confidence."

#### Tier 4: Prescriptive Analytics (The "What Should Be Done" Layer)
- Recommends optimal actions using optimization, simulation, and reinforcement learning.
- **Core techniques**: Linear programming, genetic algorithms, Markov Decision Processes (MDP).
- **Example**: "Reduce motor RPM to 1800 and schedule maintenance in 12 days to maximize operational life and minimize cost."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cognitive Level Mapping (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy):**
> Descriptive → **Remember/Understand**
> Diagnostic → **Analyze**
> Predictive → **Apply/Evaluate**
> Prescriptive → **Create/Evaluate**

### 2.2 Architectural Components of an IoT Analytics Pipeline

A standard KTU-recommended IoT Analytics pipeline consists of **five sequential stages**:

1. **Data Ingestion Layer**: MQTT brokers, Kafka streams, REST APIs.
2. **Data Storage Layer**: 
   - *Hot storage* (Redis, Cassandra) for real-time queries.
   - *Cold storage* (HDFS, S3) for historical batch analytics.
3. **Data Processing Layer**:
   - *Stream processing*: Apache Flink, Spark Streaming.
   - *Batch processing*: Apache Hadoop, Spark.
4. **Analytics & ML Layer**: Model serving via TensorFlow Lite, ONNX runtime, or cloud ML services.
5. **Visualization & Action Layer**: Grafana, Kibana, Power BI, custom dashboards.

### 2.3 Edge Analytics vs. Cloud Analytics (Critical Comparison for KTU)

| Feature | **Edge Analytics** | **Cloud Analytics** |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing Location** | On device / gateway | Centralized data center |
| **Latency** | **< 10 ms** | **100 ms – seconds** |
| **Bandwidth Usage** | Minimal (only insights sent) | High (raw data transmitted) |
| **Privacy** | High (data stays local) | Lower (data in transit) |
| **Compute Power** | Limited (constrained devices) | Virtually unlimited |
| **Use Case Example** | Autonomous vehicle obstacle detection | City-wide traffic pattern analysis |
| **Failure Recovery** | Local fallback | Centralized redundancy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often claim "cloud is always better". The correct answer depends on the **latency-criticality** and **data volume** of the application. For time-sensitive IoT apps (e.g., industrial safety), **edge analytics is mandatory**.

### 2.4 The CRISP-DM Methodology in IoT Context

The **Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM)** is the de-facto KTU-recommended framework:

1. **Business Understanding** → Define IoT use case.
2. **Data Understanding** → Sensor characteristics, sampling rates.
3. **Data Preparation** → Cleaning, normalization, feature engineering.
4. **Modeling** → Apply ML/statistical models.
5. **Evaluation** → RMSE, accuracy, F1-score, precision/recall.
6. **Deployment** → Stream ML models into production pipelines.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize each formula, its notation, and its units.

| **#** | **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean of sensor readings | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $n$ = number of samples (dimensionless), $x_i$ = sample value (sensor unit) | Baseline temperature/pressure calculation |
| 2 | Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ | $\sigma^{2}$ = variance (unit²) | Measure of sensor data spread |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | $\sigma$ = std deviation (sensor unit) | Anomaly detection threshold (±3σ rule) |
| 4 | Pearson Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}}$ | $r \in [-1, +1]$ (dimensionless) | Diagnostic analytics: linking two sensor streams |
| 5 | Simple Linear Regression | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$ | $\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | Predictive baseline modeling |
| 6 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$ | $y_i$ = actual, $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted | Model evaluation in predictive analytics |
| 7 | Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Same units as $y$ | More interpretable error metric |
| 8 | Data Reduction Ratio | $DRR = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Compressed Size}}{\text{Original Size}}$ | $DRR \in [0, 1]$ (dimensionless) | Edge analytics efficiency |
| 9 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling Hz, $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq Hz | Ensuring faithful sensor data capture |
| 10 | Information Entropy (Shannon) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $H$ in bits, $p$ = probability | Measuring information content of IoT stream |
| 11 | Z-Score for Anomaly | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \bar{x}}{\sigma}$ | $z_i$ dimensionless | Flag data point if $\vert z_i \vert > 3$ |
| 12 | Throughput Requirement | $T = N \times S \times f$ | $N$ = devices, $S$ = sample size (bytes), $f$ = freq (Hz) | Bandwidth planning for IoT analytics |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever using the pipe symbol for absolute value (e.g., $|z| > 3$), write it as $\vert z \vert > 3$ in LaTeX to avoid markdown table rendering errors.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing**: Predictive maintenance using vibration + temperature analytics reduces downtime by **up to 50%**.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil moisture analytics optimizes irrigation, saving **20–40% water**.
- **Healthcare IoT (IoMT)**: Wearable analytics detect arrhythmias with **> 95% accuracy** using ECG time-series models.
- **Smart Cities**: Traffic analytics reduce congestion delays by **15–25%** in pilot deployments (e.g., Singapore, Barcelona).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Z-Score Anomaly Detection Threshold

**Problem Context:** A temperature sensor in an industrial IoT setup generates readings. We need to mathematically justify why a $\pm 3\sigma$ rule flags only **0.27%** of points as anomalies (assuming Gaussian distribution).

**Step 1: Recall the Standard Normal Distribution**
A sensor reading $x$ is considered normal if it lies within a confidence interval around the mean $\bar{x}$:

$$x \in [\bar{x} - k\sigma, \ \bar{x} + k\sigma]$$

**Step 2: Express in terms of the Z-Score**
Dividing both sides by $\sigma$ and subtracting the mean:

$$z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{\sigma}$$

The interval becomes $z \in [-k, +k]$.

**Step 3: Apply the Empirical (68-95-99.7) Rule**
For a Gaussian distribution, the cumulative probability within $k$ standard deviations is:

$$P(\vert z \vert \leq k) = \begin{cases} 0.6827 & \text{if } k = 1 \\ 0.9545 & \text{if } k = 2 \\ 0.9973 & \text{if } k = 3 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Outlier Probability**
The probability of a point being an outlier is:

$$P(\text{anomaly}) = 1 - P(\vert z \vert \leq k)$$

For $k = 3$:

$$P(\text{anomaly}) = 1 - 0.9973 = 0.0027 = 0.27\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result for KTU Exam:** "Using the $\pm 3\sigma$ rule, only 0.27% of IoT sensor readings are flagged as anomalies, corresponding to a 99.73% confidence interval."

### 3.2 Derivation of Optimal Sampling Rate (Nyquist-Shannon)

**Problem:** An IoT vibration sensor monitors a motor rotating at 3000 RPM. Determine the minimum sampling rate to faithfully capture the vibration signal.

**Step 1: Convert Rotational Speed to Frequency**

$$f_{max} = \frac{3000 \text{ RPM}}{60} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Nyquist Theorem**
To avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the Values**

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 100 Hz sampling rate is the *theoretical minimum*. In practice, IoT engineers use **$f_s = 5 \times f_{max} = 250$ Hz** to provide a safety margin and account for higher harmonics.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Predictive Analytics with Linear Regression

**Problem:** An IoT weather station has collected the following data on humidity (input) and soil moisture (output):

| Humidity (%) $x_i$ | Soil Moisture (%) $y_i$ |
|---|---|
| 30 | 15 |
| 50 | 25 |
| 70 | 40 |
| 90 | 55 |

**Predict the soil moisture when humidity = 60% using simple linear regression.**

**Step 1: Compute the Means**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{30 + 50 + 70 + 90}{4} = \frac{240}{4} = 60$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{15 + 25 + 40 + 55}{4} = \frac{135}{4} = 33.75$$

**Step 2: Compute the Required Sums**

We need $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ and $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$.

For each data point, compute the deviations:

- $x_1 - \bar{x} = 30 - 60 = -30$, $y_1 - \bar{y} = 15 - 33.75 = -18.75$
- $x_2 - \bar{x} = 50 - 60 = -10$, $y_2 - \bar{y} = 25 - 33.75 = -8.75$
- $x_3 - \bar{x} = 70 - 60 = +10$, $y_3 - \bar{y} = 40 - 33.75 = +6.25$
- $x_4 - \bar{x} = 90 - 60 = +30$, $y_4 - \bar{y} = 55 - 33.75 = +21.25$

**Step 3: Compute the Products and Squares**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) &= (-30)(-18.75) + (-10)(-8.75) + (10)(6.25) + (30)(21.25) \\
&= 562.5 + 87.5 + 62.5 + 637.5 \\
&= 1350
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} &= (-30)^{2} + (-10)^{2} + (10)^{2} + (30)^{2} \\
&= 900 + 100 + 100 + 900 \\
&= 2000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Slope $\beta_1$**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}} = \frac{1350}{2000} = 0.675$$

**Step 5: Compute the Intercept $\beta_0$**

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 33.75 - (0.675)(60) = 33.75 - 40.5 = -6.75$$

**Step 6: Form the Regression Equation**

$$y = -6.75 + 0.675 x$$

**Step 7: Predict for $x = 60$**

$$y = -6.75 + 0.675 \times 60 = -6.75 + 40.5 = 33.75$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** At $x = 60$ (the mean of $x$), the predicted value should equal the mean of $y$ (33.75). The result confirms our arithmetic is correct.

### 3.4 Derivation of Data Reduction Ratio (DRR) for Edge Analytics

**Problem:** An edge gateway receives 10,000 sensor readings per second (each 4 bytes). After applying a sliding-window aggregation (mean over 10 readings), only 1,000 values per second are sent. Calculate the DRR.

**Step 1: Compute Original Data Rate**

$$R_{orig} = 10{,}000 \text{ samples/s} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 40{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Compressed Data Rate**

$$R_{comp} = 1{,}000 \text{ samples/s} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the DRR Formula**

$$DRR = 1 - \frac{R_{comp}}{R_{orig}} = 1 - \frac{4{,}000}{40{,}000} = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A DRR of **0.9 (or 90%)** means edge analytics reduced the bandwidth requirement by 90%, conserving network and energy resources — a critical metric for battery-powered IoT deployments.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Analytics Type

| **Decision Criterion** | **Descriptive** | **Diagnostic** | **Predictive** | **Prescriptive** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Volume Required** | Small to Medium | Medium | Large (historical) | Very Large (historical + simulated) |
| **Computational Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Time Horizon** | Past | Past | Future (short-term) | Future (long-term) |
| **Typical Algorithm** | SQL aggregation | Correlation, drill-down | ARIMA, LSTM, Random Forest | Optimization, RL, MDP |
| **KTU Marks Weightage** | 2–3 marks | 3–5 marks | 5–7 marks | 7–14 marks |
| **Real-World Example** | Daily production count | Why did yield drop? | Forecast next month's demand | Recommend optimal inventory reorder |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End IoT Analytics Reference Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete flow of data from sensor to insight, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["DATA SOURCES LAYER"]
        s1["SENSOR NODE A"]
        s2["SENSOR NODE B"]
        s3["ACTUATOR NODE C"]
        s4["MOBILE IOT DEVICE"]
    end

    subgraph ING["INGESTION LAYER"]
        m1["MQTT BROKER"]
        m2["KAFKA STREAM"]
    end

    subgraph STG["STORAGE LAYER"]
        h1["HOT STORE: INFLUXDB"]
        h2["COLD STORE: HDFS S3"]
    end

    subgraph ANA["ANALYTICS PROCESSING LAYER"]
        a1["STREAM PROCESSOR FLINK"]
        a2["BATCH PROCESSOR SPARK"]
        a3["EDGE ANALYTICS ENGINE"]
    end

    subgraph ML["ML AND MODEL LAYER"]
        ml1["ANOMALY DETECTION MODEL"]
        ml2["TIME SERIES FORECASTING LSTM"]
        ml3["PRESCRIPTIVE OPTIMIZER"]
    end

    subgraph VIS["VISUALIZATION AND ACTION LAYER"]
        v1["GRAFANA DASHBOARD"]
        v2["ALERT NOTIFICATION SERVICE"]
        v3["ACTUATOR CONTROL SIGNAL"]
    end

    s1 --> m1
    s2 --> m1
    s3 --> m2
    s4 --> m2
    m1 --> h1
    m2 --> h1
    h1 --> h2

    h1 --> a1
    h2 --> a2
    a1 --> a3

    a1 --> ml1
    a2 --> ml2
    a3 --> ml1

    ml1 --> v1
    ml2 --> v1
    ml1 --> v2
    ml3 --> v3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the IoT analytics architecture", reproduce this 5-layer model. The **arrows must be unidirectional** (data flows left-to-right), and the **layers must be clearly demarcated**.

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selecting Edge vs. Cloud Analytics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START: NEW IOT ANALYTICS USE CASE"] --> q1{"IS LATENCY BELOW 100 MS REQUIRED"}
    q1 -- YES --> edge["USE EDGE ANALYTICS"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"IS DATA VOLUME GREATER THAN 10 GB PER DAY"}
    q2 -- YES --> q3{"IS BANDWIDTH LIMITED OR COSTLY"}
    q3 -- YES --> hybrid1["USE HYBRID: EDGE PREPROCESSING PLUS CLOUD ML"]
    q3 -- NO --> cloud["USE CLOUD ANALYTICS"]
    q2 -- NO --> q4{"IS REAL TIME PROCESSING NEEDED"}
    q4 -- YES --> edge2["USE EDGE OR STREAM PROCESSING"]
    q4 -- NO --> batch["USE BATCH CLOUD ANALYTICS"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** If a KTU question describes a **self-driving car** or **industrial safety shutoff**, the answer is **edge analytics**. If it describes **monthly sales forecasting** or **city-wide pollution trends**, the answer is **cloud analytics**.

### 4.3 The 4-D Analytics Maturity Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p4["PRESCRIPTIVE ANALYTICS: WHAT SHOULD WE DO"] --> p3["PREDICTIVE ANALYTICS: WHAT WILL HAPPEN"]
    p3 --> p2["DIAGNOSTIC ANALYTICS: WHY DID IT HAPPEN"]
    p2 --> p1["DESCRIPTIVE ANALYTICS: WHAT HAPPENED"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Pyramid:** The bottom is the **foundation** (most organizations start here) and the top is the **most advanced** (fewer organizations reach this level). Each layer **builds upon** the one below it. KTU expects students to explain this **hierarchy** in 7–10 mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

Define **IoT Analytics**. List and briefly explain the **four types of analytics** used in IoT systems.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** IoT Analytics refers to the process of analyzing the vast amount of data generated by IoT devices and sensors using statistical, mathematical, and machine learning techniques to derive meaningful insights, detect patterns, predict future events, and support decision-making.

**Four Types:**

1. **Descriptive Analytics** – Summarizes historical data to answer *"what happened"*. Uses mean, median, count, and aggregation queries. Example: Daily average temperature report from a weather IoT system.

2. **Diagnostic Analytics** – Performs root-cause analysis to answer *"why it happened"*. Uses correlation, drill-down, and data mining. Example: Identifying that a machine failure was caused by abnormal vibration patterns.

3. **Predictive Analytics** – Forecasts future outcomes using historical data to answer *"what will happen"*. Uses regression, time-series models (ARIMA, LSTM). Example: Predicting equipment failure 14 days in advance.

4. **Prescriptive Analytics** – Recommends optimal actions to answer *"what should be done"*. Uses optimization, simulation, reinforcement learning. Example: Recommending the best time to schedule maintenance to minimize cost and downtime.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

Differentiate between **Edge Analytics** and **Cloud Analytics** in IoT. Mention **two use cases** for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Edge Analytics** | **Cloud Analytics** |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | On the device or local gateway | Centralized data center / cloud server |
| **Latency** | Very low (< 10 ms) | Higher (100 ms – seconds) |
| **Bandwidth** | Low (only processed insights sent) | High (raw data transmitted) |
| **Privacy** | High (data stays local) | Lower (data in transit) |
| **Compute Power** | Limited | Virtually unlimited |

**Use Cases of Edge Analytics:**
1. Autonomous vehicle collision avoidance (sub-millisecond decisions).
2. Industrial safety shutoff systems in chemical plants.

**Use Cases of Cloud Analytics:**
1. City-wide air quality trend analysis over months.
2. Monthly sales forecasting for retail IoT networks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **IoT Analytics Architecture** with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **predictive analytics** is applied in an **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** scenario for predictive maintenance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The IoT Analytics Architecture consists of **five distinct layers**, each with a specialized role:

1. **Data Sources / Perception Layer:** Contains IoT sensors, actuators, RFID tags, and smart devices that generate raw data (temperature, pressure, motion, etc.). Example: Vibration sensors mounted on a motor.

2. **Ingestion / Network Layer:** Responsible for transporting data using lightweight protocols like **MQTT, CoAP, AMQP** or stream-based platforms like **Apache Kafka**. Acts as the data highway.

3. **Storage Layer:** Divided into:
   - *Hot storage* (Redis, InfluxDB) for time-sensitive real-time queries.
   - *Cold storage* (HDFS, Amazon S3) for historical batch analytics.

4. **Analytics & Processing Layer:** The *brain* of the system. Performs:
   - *Stream processing* (Apache Flink, Spark Streaming) for real-time events.
   - *Batch processing* (Hadoop MapReduce, Spark) for offline analysis.
   - *Edge analytics* for sub-10ms decisions.

5. **Visualization & Action Layer:** Presents insights via **dashboards (Grafana, Kibana)** and triggers **actuator commands** (e.g., shut down a faulty machine, send SMS alert to operator).

> **Diagram Required:** Draw the 5-layer block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). The diagram itself is worth 3 marks; the explanation carries 4 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Predictive Analytics in IIoT for Predictive Maintenance:**

**Step 1: Data Collection** – Vibration, temperature, and acoustic sensors are installed on rotating machinery (e.g., motors, turbines). Data is sampled at 1 kHz to capture high-frequency fault signatures.

**Step 2: Data Preprocessing** – Raw signals are filtered (low-pass filter to remove noise), normalized, and segmented into windows of 1024 samples each.

**Step 3: Feature Engineering** – Statistical features are extracted:
- Root Mean Square (RMS) value.
- Peak-to-Peak amplitude.
- Spectral kurtosis (from FFT analysis).

**Step 4: Model Training** – A **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** neural network or **Random Forest classifier** is trained on historical data labeled as "healthy" or "faulty". Features are inputs; remaining useful life (RUL) is the output for regression.

**Step 5: Real-Time Prediction** – New sensor data is fed to the deployed model, which outputs:
- **Failure probability** in the next 7/14/30 days.
- **Confidence interval** for the prediction.

**Step 6: Prescriptive Action** – When the failure probability exceeds 80%, the system:
- Issues a work order to maintenance crews.
- Adjusts the machine's operating parameters (e.g., reduce RPM).
- Schedules spare part procurement.

**Engineering Benefit:** Reduces unplanned downtime by **up to 50%**, cuts maintenance costs by **15–30%**, and extends asset life by **20–40%** in IIoT deployments.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **CRISP-DM methodology** for IoT analytics. List all six phases with their significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor in a smart building reports the following 10 readings (in °C): 22, 24, 23, 25, 24, 26, 25, 23, 24, 22. 
  Compute: 
  (i) the **mean** and **standard deviation**,
  (ii) determine whether the reading **27°C** (assumed new) is an **anomaly** using the **3σ rule**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** is the industry-standard framework for executing analytics projects, including IoT. The six phases are:

1. **Business Understanding:** Define the IoT use case clearly. Example: "Reduce HVAC energy consumption by 20% while maintaining occupant comfort."

2. **Data Understanding:** Identify available sensor data sources, their formats, sampling rates, and quality. Example: 10 temperature sensors reporting every 5 minutes.

3. **Data Preparation:** Clean the data (handle missing values, outliers), normalize, and engineer features. This is the most time-consuming phase (~60–70% of project effort).

4. **Modeling:** Select and apply appropriate algorithms (e.g., LSTM for time-series, Random Forest for classification). Tune hyperparameters.

5. **Evaluation:** Assess model performance using metrics like **RMSE, MAE, F1-score, ROC-AUC**. Validate against held-out test data.

6. **Deployment:** Integrate the model into the live IoT pipeline, monitor drift, and retrain periodically. Often uses **MLOps** practices for continuous delivery.

**Key Significance:** CRISP-DM is **iterative** (not linear) — insights from later phases can send you back to earlier ones. It is **vendor-neutral** and widely adopted in IoT analytics deployments.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given Data:** $\{22, 24, 23, 25, 24, 26, 25, 23, 24, 22\}$, $n = 10$.
**New Reading:** $x_{new} = 27°C$.

**Step (i)(a): Compute the Mean $\bar{x}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 22 + 24 + 23 + 25 + 24 + 26 + 25 + 23 + 24 + 22 = 238 \\
\bar{x} &= \frac{238}{10} = 23.8°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (i)(b): Compute the Standard Deviation $\sigma$**

First, compute $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ for each data point:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 22 | −1.8 | 3.24 |
| 24 | +0.2 | 0.04 |
| 23 | −0.8 | 0.64 |
| 25 | +1.2 | 1.44 |
| 24 | +0.2 | 0.04 |
| 26 | +2.2 | 4.84 |
| 25 | +1.2 | 1.44 |
| 23 | −0.8 | 0.64 |
| 24 | +0.2 | 0.04 |
| 22 | −1.8 | 3.24 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} &= 3.24 + 0.04 + 0.64 + 1.44 + 0.04 + 4.84 + 1.44 + 0.64 + 0.04 + 3.24 = 15.60 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{15.60}{10}} = \sqrt{1.56} \approx 1.249°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Apply the 3σ Anomaly Detection Rule**

The acceptable range is:

$$[\bar{x} - 3\sigma, \ \bar{x} + 3\sigma] = [23.8 - 3(1.249), \ 23.8 + 3(1.249)] = [20.05, \ 27.55]$$

Since $x_{new} = 27°C$ lies **within** the interval $[20.05, 27.55]$, the reading is **NOT an anomaly** by the 3σ rule.

> **Equivalent Z-Score Verification:**
> $$z = \frac{27 - 23.8}{1.249} = \frac{3.2}{1.249} \approx 2.56$$
> Since $\vert z \vert = 2.56 < 3$, the reading is **not anomalous**.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in IoT Analytics Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing analytics types:** Students often write "predictive" when the question demands "diagnostic". Memorize the **4-D framework** explicitly.
> 2. **Omitting the diagram:** A 7-mark architecture question without a diagram typically gets capped at 4–5 marks. **Always draw a labeled block diagram.**
> 3. **No units in numerical answers:** A standard deviation of "1.249" loses marks — write "**1.249 °C**".
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps in regression:** The KTU checker expects at least the **deviation table** for a 7-mark regression problem. Jumping directly to $\beta_1$ forfeits process marks.
> 5. **Mixing up stream vs. batch processing:** Stream processing handles **unbounded, real-time data**; batch processing handles **bounded, historical data**. Examiners deduct marks for this interchange.
> 6. **Forgetting CRISP-DM is iterative:** Stating it is "linear" is a factual error worth 1–2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for IoT Analytics:**

- ✅ IoT Analytics = the **intelligence layer** of the IoT stack; it converts raw sensor data into actionable insights.
- ✅ The **4-D framework**: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (memorize the sequence and example for each).
- ✅ **Edge analytics** = low latency (< 10 ms), local, privacy-preserving; **Cloud analytics** = high latency, scalable, suitable for historical trends.
- ✅ The **IoT Analytics pipeline** has 5 layers: Sources → Ingestion → Storage → Analytics → Visualization/Action.
- ✅ **CRISP-DM** is the standard methodology with 6 phases: Business → Data → Preparation → Modeling → Evaluation → Deployment (iterative, not linear).
- ✅ **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - Mean: $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$
  - Std Dev: $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$
  - Z-Score: $z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{\sigma}$, anomaly if $\vert z \vert > 3$
  - Linear Regression: $\beta_1 = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$, $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$
  - Nyquist Rate: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
  - Data Reduction Ratio: $DRR = 1 - \frac{\text{Compressed}}{\text{Original}}$
  - Shannon Entropy: $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i)\log_2 p(x_i)$
- ✅ **5 V's of Big Data in IoT:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- ✅ **Stream processing tools:** Apache Flink, Spark Streaming. **Batch tools:** Hadoop, Spark.
- ✅ **Visualization tools:** Grafana, Kibana, Power BI.
- ✅ **Predictive maintenance** in IIoT can reduce downtime by **up to 50%** and maintenance costs by **15–30%** — a frequently cited KTU statistic.
- ✅ The **3σ rule** corresponds to a **99.73% confidence interval**; only **0.27%** of data points are flagged as anomalies in Gaussian IoT streams.
- ✅ **Always include units, a labeled diagram, and a sanity check** in numerical answers to score full marks.
